There is uncertainty if Pseudophilotes jordanicus is a  subspecies of P sinaicus or a separate species. Described in the year 2,000  by D. Benyamini, the species was separated from P. sinaicus on the basis of morphology, genitalia and a large geographic separation. However, Tshikolovets (2011) have questioned this separation and suggested only subspecies status. In this Red List assessment we treat both as separate species.

In some published literature, this taxon is referred to as a subpopulation or subspecies of the European Cisco (Coregonus albula).

At the regional scale, the systematics of European ciscoes and whitefishes (Coregonus spp.) has been the subject of considerable debate since the turn of the 21st century, with little indication of a definitive outcome.

It is widely accepted that non-anadromous members of this group have repeatedly undergone adaptive radiations in boreal, subarctic and perialpine lakes (Douglas et al. 1999, Østbye et al. 2005, Kahilainen and Østbye 2006, Harrod et al. 2010, Hudson et al. 2011).

Subpopulations inhabiting these systems largely diversified in the wake of the most recent glacial period 10,000-15,000 years ago. They are typified by parallel patterns of divergence in traits associated with foraging (i.e., gill raker counts, benthic vs. pelagic feeding ecology), physiology (i.e., growth rate, habitat depth partitioning) and reproductive ecology (i.e., reproductive timing and spawning habitat).

Up to six different sympatric forms sometimes referred to as “morphs”, "ecomorphs" or "ecotypes" have been recorded in perialpine lakes, and these radiations might be even more diverse in some large systems of northeastern Europe (Præbel et al. 2013, Doenz et al. 2018, Bitz‐Thorsen et al. 2020, Öhlund et al. 2020).

However, there exist significant differences in opinion regarding the taxonomic status of these forms.

In Northern Europe, including the United Kingdom, all except a handful of Irish subpopulations are usually regarded as belonging to two widely-distributed species; Coregonus albula (ciscoes) and C. lavaretus (whitefishes), with the latter also frequently referred to as the "Coregonus lavaretus species complex” (Etheridge et al. 2012, Wanke et al. 2017, Häkli et al. 2018, Crotti et al. 2020). Within this comparatively uniform taxonomic concept, the catch-all English vernacular name “Vendace” is typically used for C. albula while "Common Whitefish" or "European Whitefish" are applied to C. lavaretus.

Conversely, researchers and fisheries authorities based in Central Europe have tended to treat the different forms as distinct taxa, leading to the recognition of more than 60 species across the European region (Kottelat and Freyhof 2007, Selz et al. 2020, De-Kayne et al. 2022, Selz and Seehausen 2023). Under this diverse taxonomic concept, the name “Vendace” is restricted to its original usage for the United Kingdom endemic Coregonus vandesius and the native range of C. lavaretus is limited to its type locality of Lake Bourget in France.

With the above in mind, it appears unlikely that the striking phenotypic diversity exhibited by members of this genus will ever be adequately represented by a single accepted taxonomic system. In the absence of a region-wide consensus, the IUCN Red List broadly follows the taxonomy provided by Fricke et al. (2024).

Genus Fijiopoma was erected by Clench (1949) in a revision of Caroline, Fijian and Samoan cyclophoroideans. However, there appears to be considerable conchological variability within, and overlap amongst, the nominal Fijian species, indicating the need for reassessment of their taxonomic status.

The Fijian members of genus Gonatorhape von Mollendorff were revised by Clench (1949). However, there appears to be considerable conchological variability within and overlap among the nominal Fijian species, indicating the need for reassessment of their taxonomic status.

The Fijian members of genus Gonatorhape von Moellendorff were revised by Clench (1949). However, there appears to be considerable conchological variability within and overlap among the nominal Fijian species, indicating the need for reassessment of their taxonomic status. Clench (1949) and Solem (1978) suggested that G. stricta may be synonymous with G. intercostata (Mousson, 1870) which has been recorded widely in the Lau Group.
Pacific Diplommatinidae are in urgent need of systematic revision. The Fiji species have not been reviewed since Kobelt (1902).
Pacific Diplommatinidae are in urgent need of systematic revision. The Fiji species have not been reviewed since Kobelt (1902).
Fourteen species of Placostylus are recorded from Fiji (Barker et al. 2005; Barker and Bouchet 2010).
A record from the island of Koro (Parkinson 1987) is considered by Barker and Bouchet (2010), as probably a mis-identification.

Fourteen species of Placostylus are recorded from the Fijian archipelago (Barker et al. 2005; Barker and Bouchet 2010).

Placostylus seemanni has in the past been considered to consist of two subspecies, Placostylus seemanni seemanni and Placostylus seemanni mbengensis. In this assessment we follow the treatment of Barker et al. (2005) and consider these taxa as full species, i.e., Placostylus seemanni  and Placostylus mbengensis. Barker et al. (2005) consider that species status should be afforded based on differences in niche (arboreal vs ground-dwelling), habitat (lowland vs montane), and conchological features noted by Cooke, 1942.

Placostylus ochrostoma is endemic to the islands of Rabi, Taveuni, Gamea and northeastern Vanua Levu. At approximately 35- 40 mm in adult shell height P. ochrostoma is most likely the smallest of Fiji’s Placostylus species. The shell aperture and lip are a striking saffron colour.

Placostylus seemanni has in the past been considered to consist of two subspecies, Placostylus seemanni seemanni and Placostylus seemanni mbengensis. In this assessment we follow the treatment of Barker et al. (2005) and consider these taxa as full species, i.e., Placostylus seemanni  and Placostylus mbengensis.

Barker et al. (2005) consider that species status should be afforded based on differences in niche (arboreal vs ground-dwelling), habitat (lowland vs montane), and conchological features noted by Cooke (1942).
Pacific Endodontidae were revised by Solem (1976).
Fijian Trochomorphidae were last revised by Baker (1941), but with strong reliance on BPBM specimen material.
Fijian Trochomorphidae were last revised by Baker (1941), but with strong reliance on BPBM specimen material.
The taxonomic limits within this genus are poorly understood and this species may not be endemic to the Seychelles islands.
This is considered to be a species complex, and it is likely that the population from Rotuma is a separate species.
Synonym = Rhodacme filosa (Conrad, 1834)

This is considered species inquirenda under Cephalocassis (Kottelat 2013). The genus placement is tentative, based on the treatment in Desoutter (1977) as a valid species of Hemipimelodus, and the uncertain status of the species in Kailola (2004) (Ferraris 2007).

Although classified as one species, the two geographic populations have been ranked separately in the National listings carried out by the New Zealand Department of Conservation, as they are more genetically distinct and more divergent than some described sister species of galaxiids (Waters and Craw 2008). Taxonomic research is needed to determine whether this species should actually be split into two (R. Hitchmough pers. comm. 2011).
Recent studies show that North and South Island populations are genetically divergent, but these differences are not considered enough to warrant separate species status (NIWA 2013).
The taxonomic status of this species requires clarification.

Originally, this species was described as Partula montana Cooke & Crampton, 1930 and was later revised to Eua montana (Cooke & Crampton, 1930) by Pilsbry and Cooke (1934). Cowie (1998) noted that the name montana Cooke & Crampton, 1930 is a junior primary homonym of montana Moellendorff, 1900 (originally proposed as Partula rufa ssp. montana Moellendorff 1900). Cowie (2019) published the replacement name for Eua montana (Cooke & Crampton, 1930): Eua mauga Cowie, 2019.
This species may be closely related to Samoana abbreviata and S. conica, its shells being a greenish-yellow colour. It is dextral (see Cowie 2005, unpub. report).
This probably is not a Theganopteryx species; its generic placement is uncertain.
This probably is not a Theganopteryx species; its generic placement is uncertain.
This probably is not a Theganopteryx species; its generic placement is uncertain.
This probably is not a Theganopteryx species; its generic placement is uncertain.
This species is similar to Zarceus fallaciosus and the taxonomy of the two species needs re-examination.
This probably is not a Theganopteryx species; its generic placement is uncertain.
An undescribed species of Scottiola is also present in Seychelles (Mahé and Silhouette islands); its range is included within that of S. salticiformis and it has the same status.

Semper (1873) described Vitrinoconus turritus from the Philippines. Baker (1941) subsequently described "Coneuplecta (s.s.) turrita palauensis from Palau (Belau) (C. Christensen, personal communication). Baker moved Semper's turritus to Coneuplecta, which was presumed to be conspecific with Semper's taxon (an idea that itself may be doubtful) but subspecifically distinct from it. Thus "Coneuplecta turrita (Semper, 1873)" is a polytypic species with two included subspecies:  Coneuplecta turrita turrita (Semper, 1873) from the Philippines and Coneuplecta turrita palauensis Baker, 1941, from Belau (C. Christensen, pers. comm.).

Based on current knowledge of Pacific island land snail biogeography, it is doubtful whether the poorly known “subspecies” turrita from the Philippines is conspecific with the “subspecies” palauensis from Palau. The conservation status of the Philippines “subspecies,” if still extant, is currently unknown. Therefore the assessment for Coneuplecta turrita is essentially identical to the assessment for Coneuplecta turrita palauensis, until: 1) additional data can be gathered for Coneuplecta turrita turrita, and/or 2) it is resolved whether these two “subspecies” actually represent two distinct species. Until further land snail fieldwork in the Philippines is undertaken and museum research on the type specimens of these snails is accomplished, these taxonomic and conservation questions will remain unresolved. Since the species falls into the category of “CR (Possibly Extinct)” (at least based on Palau data), it may already be too late for taxonomic questions to have much practical relevance for Coneuplecta turrita.

Molecular and morphological evidence indicates that this newly-discovered phenotype belongs to the "Madascincus polleni species complex", rendering M. polleni as defined by Miralles et al. (2011) paraphyletic with regard to this species and Madascincus stumpffi. It is treated here as a distinct species following Miralles et al. (2011), as it is morphologically distinct and strongly genetically divergent from the other three clades identified for this complex.

This species has been elevated from being a subspecies of Eleutherodactylus zugi (Hedges et al. 2008). Its taxonomic status, however, remains uncertain. Díaz and Cádiz (2008) treated this taxon as a subspecies of Eleutherodactylus zugi. However, this assessment follows Frost (2021) in recognizing it as a valid species.
Two of the subspecies of Eleutherodactylus varians (olibrus and staurometopon) have been elevated to the species level.

In the Eleutherodactylus alfredi species group according to the original description (Canseco-Márquez and Smith 2004). This species has since been transferred over to the genus Craugastor (Frost 2011). The holotype was originally considered to be a specimen of E. spatulatus (Canseco-Márquez and Smith 2004), although it does not seem to have been considered in the latter species' 2004 assessment.

authority: Dobriyal, Singh, Uniyal, Joshi, Phurailatpam & Bisht, 2004

taxonomicNotes: Pinniwallago kanpurensis was described from a small lake 'Bara Tal' near village of Bhitargaon, Kanpur, Uttar Pradesh, India (Gupta et al. 1981). This unique genus and species was disregarded as based on anomalous specimens and synonymized to Wallago attu by Menon (1999). However, other authors (Talwar and Jhingran 1991, Ferraris 2007) have considered this monotypic genus valid.
The only living member of the genus; the most "primitive" living member of the Centrarchidae (Lee et al. 1980).

The types Kingston used for her description of Skiffia francesae (1978) were collected by Miller and Fitzsimons in 1970 from the Río Teuchitlán E of Teuchitlán. Two decades later, in 2006, a stock of Skiffia was found in the Molino pond near Cuyacapán, Laguna de Sayula drainage. Fish from this locality are regarded to belong to the Golden Skiffia. According to these two drainages, two subpopulations are recognized: The Upper Río Ameca subpopulation (type subpopulation) and the Laguna de Sayula subpopulation. Both are regarded Extinct in the Wild.

McCranie and Wilson (1996) regarded C. atitlanensis as a synonym of C. bivittatus. Characteristics of recently acquired specimens from extreme southwestern Honduras (J. R. McCranie) also suggest that the two taxa may be conspecific (L. Porras, pers. comm. 2013).
Recent molecular work sequencing Jamaican species of Celestus has confirmed the validity of this species (S.B. Hedges unpubl. data).
Prior to its description by Köhler (2009), this species was referred to as a population of Anolis altae.
The original name is listed as Norops pijolense (McCranie et al. 1993). The unjustified emendation pijolensis has  sometimes been used for this species (McCranie and Köhler 2015).

No reports of Trachyboa gularis have appeared in the scientific literature since the species was first described, and it has rarely been mentioned. The name has, however, been used in pet trade articles ( Arnett 1992, Burger 2017) to refer to a species from coastal Ecuador that "does well" in certain captive conditions, suggesting a known extant population. The basis for this is not clear and no diagnosis is provided to clarify whether this represents "true" T. gularis. It is probable that any animals in trade marketed as T. gularis in fact belong to T. boulengeri.

Zaher et al. (2024) synonymised Trachyboa within Tropidophis.

The holotype of this species was for some time transferred to an American institution in Washington DC before being returned to Bogota by J. Lynch, and it is possible that reports of a second record refer to the holotype in two separate collections (C. Arredondo, J. Daza and R.Caicedo pers. comms. 2013)
The taxonomic validity of this species is unclear. Experts proposed this species may be a colour variation of Trimetopon slevini, however the known ranges do not overlap directly.

The subgenus Chamaeleo (Trioceros) was elevated to generic rank by Tilbury and Tolley (2009). Barej et al. (2010) elevated the former subspecies to species rank based on molecular and morphological differences from the nominate taxon. Accepted as Trioceros perreti in Tolley and Herrel (2014) and Glaw (2015).
Malonza and Bauer (2022) resurrected Ancylodactylus to encompass African geckos previously included in Cnemaspis.

Anderson (1875) described it as Trionyx nigricans from the pond at the Hazrat Sultan Bayazid Bostami Shrine in Nasirabad near Chittagong, Bangladesh. Subsequent authors believed that the species either became extinct in the wild (Pritchard 1979, Groombridge 1982) or that it ‘descended from introduced individuals of the more widespread Trionyx gangeticus’ (Groombridge 1982, Khan 1987) or of N. hurum (Rashid 1990). Using mainly skull osteology as well as coloration and pattern characters, Praschag and Gemel (2002) recognized it from northeast India as Aspideretes nigricans. Based on molecular data, Engstrom et al. (2004) and Praschag et al. (2007) transferred the genus Aspideretes Hay, 1903 into synonymy of Nilssonia (Gray, 1872). See Praschag and Gemel (2002) for discussion of taxonomic distinction from other large Asian softshell species.

The female Holotype of this species was collected by W. L. Tower in August 1903 in the Río Verde and described by S. E. Meek in 1904 as Goodea toweri. in 1939, Hubbs & Turner placed the Bluetail Splitfin in the monotypic genus Ataeniobius. No subpopulations are distinguished.

The types Hubbs used in 1924 to describe Goodea captiva from Jésus María were collected in 1907 by S.E. Meek but regarded as Goodea atripinnis. In 1937, Hubbs and Turner transfered the species into the new erected genus Xenoophorus and described a second species in this genus, Xenoophorus erro from the Río Santa María. In 1939 they described from endorheic streams near Venado and Moctezuma a third one, Xenoophorus exsul. J. Fitzsimons (1979) finally synonymized the last two species with Xenoophorsu captivus as he couldn't find any differences. According to three separate drainages, three subpopulations can be distinguished: The Río Santa María subpopulation (type subpopulation), the Río Matehuala subpopulation from Venado and Moctezuma and the Illescas subpopulation, that inhabited the endorheic springs near Illescas and which is regarded Extinct in the Wild.

Praschag et al. (2007, 2008) demonstrated that the widespread species Batagur baska comprised at least two genetically distinct species, and separated out B. affinis for the southern species occupying southern Thailand, Peninsular Malaysia, and Sumatra in Indonesia, retaining the name B. baska for the more northern species from India, Bangladesh, and Myanmar.
Was under Iguanidae.
This species is the sole member of its genus.

The Mona Rhinoceros Iguana is currently recognized as a distinct species, Cyclura stejnegeri (ITWG 2016). Prior to 2000, it was considered a subspecies in the group including Cyclura cornuta cornuta from Hispaniola (Dominican Republic and Haiti), Cyclura cornuta stejnegeri from Mona Island, Puerto Rico, and Cyclura cornuta onchiopsis, which presumably went extinct on Navassa Island in the mid-19th century (Powell 1999).

Prior to analysis by Keogh et al. (2008), all the banded iguanas were lumped as this single species. The Lau Banded Iguana (Brachylophus fasciatus) is now recognized as distinct from the Fiji Banded Iguana (Brachylophus bulabula). Most previous references pertaining to iguanas in central Fiji are now attributed to B. bulabula. This taxonomic change includes references to the introduced population on Vanuatu, now described as B. bulabula (Bauer 1988, Keogh et al. 2008). The introduced population on Tonga remains as B. fasciatus.

 

Recent unpublished data finds some morphological and colour variability between islands in the Lau Group, but genetically these populations have less inter-island variability than within the other two species of Brachylophus. The systematics of the iguanas on the islands in the northern Lau Group (north of Tuvuca) is unknown, but for now they will be retained within this taxon.

Recent genetic data (Keogh et al. 2008) have shown that each subpopulation (island) of this species is genetically unique.  This data, along with many morphological characters, clearly define this species, although the observed genetic inter-island variability might be consistent with subspecific differences after further assessment.
The genus Canthigaster is hypothesized to undergo fast morphological/color differentiation and slow mtDNA evolution (Randall et al. 2008).

The Dusky Shark is extremely similar genetically and morphologically to the Galapagos Shark (Carcharhinus galapagensis). These species share mitochondrial DNA haplotypes but are distinguishable based on single nuclear polymorphisms (SNPs) (Corrigan et al. 2017) and also occupy different habitats (insular vs. continental).

Carettochelys insculpta is the only surviving extant member of the genus Carettochelys and the family Carettochelyidae, a formerly widespread turtle family dating back to the Eocene and previously occurring across Asia and much of the rest of the globe. As such, it is considered a deep phylogenetic lineage with high-priority conservation needs, a so-called EDGE species (Evolutionarily Distinct, Globally Endangered). The common name in Indonesian Papua is Kura-kura Moncong Babi and the common name in Papua New Guinea is Piku.

There is some controversy concerning the taxonomy and nomenclature of Squatina species of the Southwest Atlantic. Squatina occulta described by Vooren and da Silva (1991) has been regarded as being a junior synonym of S. guggenheim by Soto (2001) in a species-level checklist of the region. Although evidence was not given to support this statement, Bernardes et al. (2005) followed Soto’s proposal. However, later studies have re-evaluated the species’ validity. The validity of S. occulta Vooren and da Silva, 1991 as a distinct species has been confirmed through the study of mitochondrial DNA by Stelbrink et al. (2010) and in a comparative study of Southwest Atlantic Squatina neurocrania (Carvalho et al. 2012). Both studies also recognized S. argentina, S. guggenheim, and S. occulta as the only valid species in the Southwest Atlantic (preceding the description of new species since that time, S. david and S. varii).

Some confusion exists regarding the publication date of the description of S. occulta (Faria et al. 2014). Some taxonomic literature sources list 1991 as the publication date while others list 1992. This confusion is probably due to the distribution of pre-prints of the article in 1991 before the full issue of the journal was printed in 1992. According to The International Commission on Zoological Nomenclature, the publication date of the S. occulta description is 1991. There has also been discrepancy in referencing the species authority for S. occulta, particularly the name of the second author (Faria et al. 2014); K.G. da Silva’s surname has been spelled ‘da Silva’, ‘Da Silva’, and ‘Silva’. Faria et al. (2014) suggested using the one in the revisions by Vaz and Carvalho (2013) and in online taxonomic databases. Thus, the recommended citation is S. occulta Vooren & da Silva, 1991.

Himantura signifer, H. kittipongi, and H. oxyrhynchus were placed within the newly described genus Fluvitrygon (Last et al. 2016a). This species is most similar to H. kittipongi, from which it differs in having no thorny denticles on the tail and a pale edge to the disc (Last et al. 2016b).
Appeared in IUCN Red Lists prior to 2007 under the generic names Heosemys and Geoemyda.

Le et al. (2014) concluded that the Yangtze and Red River subpopulations of Rafetus swinhoei do not show significant genetic divergence and do not warrant recognition as separate evolutionary significant units for conservation management. However, it appears that Le et al. (2014) did not actually have any samples from the Yangtze subpopulation, only samples from the Vietnamese and Chinese parts of the Red River catchment. Chen (2015) found two distinct mtDNA lineages of R. swinhoei which are roughly 2.8% divergent from each other (Cytochrome B and NADH dehydrogenase 4), one represented by Red River specimens and the second one by a Shanghai museum specimen of unknown origin. In the meantime, tissue samples from the male of Suzhou Zoo and the female of Changsha Zoo proved to be concordant with the Shanghai museum specimen (H. Chen pers. comm.). This points to a possibility that the Yangtze and the Red River subpopulations may represent separate taxa at least at the level of subspecies. In that case the Yangtze subpopulation would represent the nominate form Rafetus swinhoei swinhoei and the Vietnamese and Chinese Red River subpopulation the subspecies Rafetus swinhoei leloii.

Was treated under the generic name Geoemyda in the 1996 IUCN Red List. The genus was changed to Heosemys in the 2000 IUCN Red List. Now reassigned to a new monotypic genus, Leucocephalon (A. Rhodin pers. comm.).
Last et al. (2016) revised the genus Rhinobatos, transferring glaucostigma, horkelii, lentiginosus, leucorhynchus, percellens, planiceps, prahli, and productus to the new genus Pseudobatos.
Genetics studies have led some researchers to suspect that there may be an undescribed species of Echinorhinus off Oman and elsewhere in the Arabian Sea region (Henderson et al. 2016).

Squalus mitsukurii was once considered a globally distributed species, but recent morphological and genetic work suggests the species is restricted to temperate waters of Japan and Korea, and is possibly endemic to the Northwest Pacific (Pfleger et al. 2018, Ziadi-Künzli et al. 2020). The resolution of the species-complex is ongoing with further species likely being described in the future.
Himantura oxyrhynchus, H. signifer, and H. kittipongi and were placed within the newly described genus Fluvitrygon (Last et al. 2016a).

Originally described as a subspecies of the Cuban Rock Iguana (Cyclura macleayi lewisi Grant, 1940; Cyclura nubila lewisi sensu Schwartz and Thomas, 1975), this iguana was elevated to species-level by Burton (2004), supported by Malone et al. (2000) and S. Funk (unpublished data 2008).
This species has recently been described by Ng and Sparks (2003a). This new taxon comprises the formerly referenced Arius sp. Ankofia and Arius sp.Lac Andrapongy.

The male Holo- and female Allotype of this species were collected by R.R. Miller et al. in March 1982 at the type location, a pond at the Ojo de Agua de las Mujeres spring near El Toboso. Several Paratypes were collected with the both types and in 1985. The species was named Characodon sp. "Coahuila" before the description by A. Radda and M. Meyer to curtain the real locality of this species (Radda, pers. comm.). Several phylogenetic studies since about the late 1990's revealed, that Characodon populations N of the El Saltito falls, historically numbered among Characodon lateralis,  are closer related with Characodon audax and belong to this species. Taking in consideration its affiliation to different isolated springs and creeks, seven subpopulations can be distinguished: The El Toboso subpopulation (type subpopulation), the Los Pinos subpopulation, the Laguna Seca subpopulation, the El Garabato subpopulation, the Río La Sauceda subpopulation, the Pino Suarez subpopulation and the Victoria de Durango subpopulation. The last one is regarded Extinct.

The specimens A. Günther used for his description of Characodon lateralis in 1866 go back to fish being part of collections of the British Museum: Four females from a collection from B. Seemann, that he should have had "obtained in Southern Middle America" (Günther, 1969) and three females respectively two males from the Haslar collection, also with location data "Middle-America". Characodon garmani, described from a single female by Jordan and Evermann (1898) had been seen as synonym of lateralis by S.E. Meek in 1904,  but again accepted as separate species by Hubbs and Turner in 1939. Since about the late 1990's, it has become clear, that red finned Characodon populations north of the El Saltito falls, historically seen as Characodon lateralis, are closer related with the black finned Characodon audax from El Toboso and therefore belong to this species. Two subpopulations, the Los Berros subpopulation (regarded as type subpopulation) and the Amado Nervo subpopulation are distinguished. The second one is regarded Extinct in the Wild.
There are three subspecies, namely babelthuapi Solem, 1976; constricta s. str.; and komakanensis Solem, 1976

Molecular data and morphological, ecological, and demographic analyses suggest that the Panamanian golden frogs and their kin (the Atelopus varius-zeteki clade) are comprised of five distinct forms (Zippel et al. 2006). The species has a variable appearance and color and pattern are poor diagnostic characteristics for identification of this species (Richards and Knowles 2007). Some subpopulations have a very yellow color, resembling Atelopus zeteki, which is why both Atelopus varius and Atelopus zeteki are sometimes referred to as golden frogs in Panama (Estrada et al. 2014). Genetic analysis of mitochondrial DNA reveals support for recognizing A. varius and A. zeteki as separate clades, but sorting of haplotypes was imperfect and Costa Rican samples sorted into a third outlying group (Richards and Knowles 2007). Genetic examination of the phylogeny of this group would be helpful, but the extreme declines of this group will make sourcing of fresh, genomic quality tissues challenging.
This is a restricted concept of this species following the split of the broader concept into this and Leptophryne javanica (Hamidy et al. 2018).

Morales (2002 "2000") noted that earlier records from Ecuador, Suriname, Venezuela, French Guiana, and Peru apply to other species. Frogs known as Allobates brunneus from northern Orinoco in Venezuela have been separated as A. pittieri (La Marca et al. 2004). All reports of Allobates brunneus from Venezeula are considered A. aff. marchesianus rather than aff. brunneus as the marchesianus type locality is closer to the Venezuelan southern border (Barrio-Amoros et al. 2019). Records of this species from Mount Duida, Venezuela is probably applied to an undescribed species (Barrio-Amorós and Santos 2009). Reports from the Guianas and Brazilian state of Paraná, probably refer to other species (Santos-Pereira et al. 2018).

It has been suggested that this species is not native to Martinique, but is synonymous with another species from South America (M. Breuil pers. comm., Breuil 2009); however, following its rediscovery in October 2012 (Dewynter et al. 2012), new genetic material and phylogenetic analysis confirmed that it is nested within a clade of lowland Amazonian Allobates but nonetheless distantly related to any known species of the group (Fouquet et al. 2013). In addition, the arrival of the species in Martinique likely corresponds to an overseas dispersal from South America during the late Miocene, as previously hypothesized for Bothrops lanceolatus and Leptodactylus fallax, two other species endemic to Martinique and surrounding islands (Fouquet et al. 2013).
This species was previously included in the genus Hyla but has recently been moved to the new genus Isthmohyla (Faivovich et al. 2005).

This species was previously within the genus Phyllomedusa but was moved to the genus Hylomantis by Faivovich et al. (2005). Subsequently, it was moved from Hylomantis to Agalychnis by Favivovich et al. (2010).
Molecular data suggest that there is more than one species under this name (S. Loader pers. comm. June 2012).
Molecular data suggest that there are more than one species under this name (S. Loader pers. comm. June 2012).
Genetic data indicate that this is a complex of species occurring in isolated, montane areas, each highly threatened (S.B. Hedges pers. comm. 2021).

This species was previously within the genus Eleutherodactylus (Crawford and Smith 2005). The current taxonomic concept probably contains several cryptic species (Crawford et al. 2013; R. Ibanez pers. comm June 2018).

This species was previously included in the genus Eleutherodactylus (Crawford and Smith 2005). Two new species were described from the species group to which Craugastor coffeus (C. laticeps group) belongs, it is possible, albeit unlikely based on distinguished external morphological characteristics, there is additional taxonomic change yet to be determined (McCranie 2018).

There is doubt about the validity of this species as it was described from a single specimen based on a variable trait (Honduras Red List Assessment Workshop March 2019).
A specimen recorded from Forêt des Pins National Park in Haiti may belong to this taxon, but further research is needed to clarify this (C. Martínez Rivera pers. comm. 2020).

Recent DNA and morphological studies suggest that this species belongs in its own monotypic genus, Mecistops (McAliley et al. 2006).  This finding has been supported by all subsequent phylogenetic studies (e.g., Hekkala et al. 2011, Meredith 2011, Oaks 2011,Shirley et al. 2013) and Mecistop has gained wide use and acceptance in the literature and is hence used here.

A recent study (Shirley 2013, Shirley et al. 2013) has found significant molecular and morphological support for two divergent taxa in this genus - one distributed entirely in West Africa and the other in Central Africa. Formal taxonomic revisions and recognition of these two unique taxa are expected soon.
Schwartz and Henderson (1991) considered some subpopulations of this species to refer to Eleutherodactylus darlingtoni, but Hedges (1992) redefined the range, and this definition is used here.

The only known extant population of C. siamensis outside mainland Southeast Asia, located in East Kalimantan Province (Indonesian Borneo), is genetically distinct and considered an ‘Evolutionarily Significant Unit’ (Gratten 2003).

The species was named by Schmidt in 1935 from specimens from Najuan Lake, Mindoro. The species has been considered a subspecies of Crocodylus novaeguineae but currently it is accorded full species status on the basis of unique morphology, distribution and genetic separation (see Uetz et al. 2016).

This species was previously included in the genus Eleutherodactylus (Crawford and Smith 2005). Genetic analysis of animals in the captive collection may represent disparate cryptic species (Crawford et al. 2013).
This taxon is presently considered valid, but is very likely to be a junior synonym of Eleutherodactylus weinlandi based on genetics and morphology (S.B. Hedges pers. comm. 2021).
The following species were previously considered to be subspecies of Eleutherodactylus ruthae but have since been recognized to be full species: E. aporostegus, E. bothroboans and E. tychathrous.
This taxon is very likely a junior synonym of Eleutherodactylus glandulifer (Hispaniola Red List Assessment Workshop 2020; S.B. Hedges pers. comm. 2021).
This species was previously included in the genus Eleutherodactylus (Crawford and Smith 2005).
This species was included within Oreobates considering that it shares morphological characters with species of this genus (Hedges et al. 2008).

Telmatobius punctatus was previously considered to be a subspecies of Telmatobius brevirostris; it was elevated to specific status by Lehr (2005) based on differences in morphological features and colour patterns.
A specimen collected by J. Cadle in the 1980s (MHNSM 960) from Chorro Blanco in the province of Santa Cruz, was later assigned to this species.

This is a restricted concept of this species following the split of the broader concept into this and Megophrys elfina (Poyarkov et al. 2017).

This species has become Megophrys, following Ophryophryne becoming a subgenus of Megophrys (Mahony et al. 2017).

Lyu et al. (2021) listed this species in Ophryophryne without comment, and this work was not a revision of Megophrys. This assessment retains it in Megophrys until generic boundaries within the Megophryinae are more stable.
The northernmost part of its range has been attributed to A. vallani by Vences et al. (2010).

We follow Poynton (2003b) and Channing and Howell (2006) in considering Probreviceps rungwensis to be distinct from P. macrodactylus. In addition, we follow Mkonyi et al. (2004) and Channing and Howell (2006) in considering Probreviceps loveridgei to be distinct from P. macrodactylus. Furthermore, molecular data suggest that there are more than one species under the P. macrodactylus name (S. Loader pers. comm. June 2012).

Matsui (2007) determined that Rana okinavana (now Nidirana okinavana) is a senior synonym of R. psaltes that occurs in the Southern Ryukyus and Taiwan. This meant that the brown frogs of the Central Ryukyus that had been referred to as R. okinavana, no longer had a name. Further taxonomic study determined that there were in fact two genetically and morphologically distinct lineages, and hence they were described as two new species, Rana kobai and Rana ulma (Matsui 2011). 

Lyu et al. (2020) revised the taxonomy of Nidirana and determined that populations from Taiwan, Province of China, previously assigned to this species are actually N. adenopleura. However in this study, the sampling is limited and the possibility remains that N. okinavana is still present in Taiwan, Province of China (M. Matsui pers. comm. September 2020).
Specimens refered to this species from Ponmudi and surroundings in India, are now included in the newly described species, Philautus sp. nov. 'Ponmudi Hills' (S.D. Biju pers. comm.).

Allozyme data (Shaffer 1984) seem to confirm the original morphological description of this as a distinct species (Dixon 1963); mitochondrial DNA sequence data indicate a very close relationship to several Mexican populations of Ambystoma velasci (Shaffer and McKnight 1996).
This taxon, originally known as Margaritifera monodonta, was removed from the 1996 Red List but kept on the Mollusc SG database as Not Evaluated.
This species is under taxonomic review and it is likely to be split into five species (P. Praschag pers. comm. 2018).
Two subspecies have been described, namely: fuscozonata s.str. and depressa Solem 1976.

This species has been mistaken mainly for Dasyatis (=Hypanus) say but also for Dasyatis pastinaca in the past. The specific name has been misspelled as hipostigma, including in the original description.
The scientific name for this species is often misspelled as Carcharhinus perezii. Carcharhinus springeri Sadowsky & Amorim 1977 is considered a synonym (Soto 2001).
Castro (2011) resurrected Carcharhinus cerdale, considering it distinct from C. porosus. Carcharhinus cerdale is restricted to the Eastern Pacific and C. porosus to the Western Atlantic.

The previous assessment of the Turks and Caicos Rock Iguana (Gerber 2004) recommended additional genetic studies to fully evaluate the subspecific designation of Cyclura carinata bartschi (Cochran 1931) on Booby Cay, located 0.5 km off the east end of Mayaguana Island, The Bahamas. Two independent population genetic studies have now demonstrated that iguanas on Booby Cay are not genetically different from C. carinata in the Turks and Caicos Islands, and therefore should not be considered as a separate subspecies (Bryan et al. 2007, Welch et al. 2017). Further, the Booby Cay population is genetically most similar to populations from the eastern side of the Caicos Bank, rather than the most geographically proximate populations on the western and northern sides of the Caicos Banks (Welch et al. 2017). Since it is likely that humans introduced iguanas to Booby Cay, this population is not included in the species’ extent of occurrence.

Welch et al. (2017) describe the existence of two distinct Evolutionary Significant Units (ESUs, sensu Moritz 1994) for C. carinata: one inhabiting islands on the western end of the Caicos Bank, and one inhabiting the remaining islands of the Caicos Bank and islands of the Turks Bank. Multiple management units (MUs) are also identified within each ESU (Welch et al. 2017). Considering the severe geographic and genetic fragmentation of iguanas across the archipelago these authors recommended the two ESUs be elevated as different subspecies.

Fossils from Puerto Rico were originally described by Barbour (1919) and named Cyclura portoricensis. These and additional fossils from Puerto Rico were re-examined by Pregill (1981) and attributed to Anegada Rock Iguanas, Cyclura pinguis.

Prior to 2000 three subspecies were commonly recognized: Cyclura cornuta cornuta from Dominican Republic and Haiti, Cyclura cornuta stejnegeri from Mona Island, Puerto Rico, and the extinct Cyclura cornuta onchiopsis from Navassa Island (Schwartz and Carey 1977). That taxonomy has subsequently been followed by some authors (for example, Malone et al. 2000, Pérez-Buitrago et al. 2007, and references therein). However, all three taxa are now recognized as species (ITWG 2016).

Original population in Utah Lake may be extinct. When numbers were low, Chasmistes liorus hybridized with Catostomus ardens, which apparently led to introgression of new characters into the population; now this form (described as a new subspecies, Chasmistes liorus mictus), is abundant, according to Miller and Smith (1981). Ongoing research may reveal if any unhybridized Chasmistes liorus remain.

Catostomus fecundus is an invalid taxon because it was based on hybrids between Catostomus ardens and Chasmistes liorus.

Chasmistes and Deltistes are closely related to the older, more diverse, widespread genus Catostomus; Chasmistes species are distinctive in having branched gill rakers and a terminal mouth (Scoppettone and Vinyard 1991).

Harris and Mayden (2001) used molecular data to examine phylogenetic relationships of major clades of Catostomidae. In all trees, Scartomyzon was paraphyletic and embedded in Moxostoma, and Catostomus was never recovered as monophyletic (Xyrauchen was embedded within Catostomus). They concluded that the phylogenetic relationships and taxonomic composition of taxa presently included in Moxostoma and Scartomyzon are in need of further study, as are the relationships and composition of the genera Catostomus, Chasmistes, Deltistes , and Xyrauchen, and the phylogenetic affinites of Erimyzon and Minytrema.

See Smith (1992) for a study of the phylogeny and biogeography of the Catostomidae.

This species is comprised of several recognized forms (torulosa, gubernaculum, rangiana, and cincinnatiensis), but it is not clear whether these forms represent ecophenotypic variation, true subspecies, or a species complex. The entire species group exhibits considerable ecophenotypic variation. Epioblasma was formerly placed in the genera Unio, Truncilla, and Dysnomia (Johnson 1978).

A list of synonyms for this species can be found on The MUSSEL project web site (Graf and Cummings 2011).

The spellings Dipturus trachyderma and Dipturus trachydermus have been used for this species. The basionym is Raja trachyderma and the valid name is Dipturus trachydermus following grammatical rules. While Fricke et al. (2019) use Dipturus trachyderma, we follow Last et al. (2016) which correctly uses Dipturus trachydermus.

Dipturus diehli Soto & Mincarone, 2001, as known only from the holotype taken off Santa Catarina, Brazil (29°39´S, 47°50´W) at a depth of 480 m, is likely a synonym of D. mennii and is treated here as such.
Without knowledge of calls or habitat this species cannot be readily separated from Eleutherodactylus gossei (Schwartz and Fowler 1973).

During recent surveys in Palau, the single specimen found was larger in shell diameter than the paratypes in the Bishop Museum (Honolulu, Hawaii) with which it was compared. Nonetheless, the shell sculpture of the specimen suggests placement in Aaadonta kinlochi and is treated as such in this assessment.

The Holotype of this species was collected by Robert R. Miller and J. Fitzsimons in the Laguna Magdalena in February 1970, Paratopoytpes were added from maintained and raised fish fom the same collection from the University in Michigan, Ann Arbor. Paratypes go back to a collection of Charles Turner from the Hacienda San Sebastián in 1939. All these collection sites belong to the endorheic Laguna Magdalena basin and hence to the Laguna Magdalena subpopulation. Fish E of San Marcos  from the Río San Marcos (Canal del Bajío) are part of the Río Ameca fish fauna and belong therefore to a different subpopulation, the Río San Marcos subpopulation. This subpopulation might go back to a quite recent stream capture.
Sayyadzadeh et al. (2023) treat G. sindhae and G. smartae as junior synonyms of G. dunsirei. We do not follow this opinion, which is based on a missunderstanding of species concepts.
Appeared in IUCN Red Lists prior to 2007 under the generic names Geoemyda and Heosemys. No subspecies or synonymised data.

The description by Meek (1904) as Characodon multiradiatus is based on specimens collected by J.N. Rose in the Lerma Lake E of Toluca at the beginning of the 20th century and referred to Girardinichthys innominatus (a synonym of Girardinichthys viviparus) by Evermann and Goldsborough (1902). The species was transfered into the new genus Lermichthys by Hubbs in 1926, temporally overlapping with the description by Jordan and Evermann (1927) as Girardinichthys limnurgus. Finally, Miller and Fitzsimons (1971) placed this species within the genus Girardinichthys. According to its appearance in four different drainages, four subpopulations can be distinguished: The Río Lerma subpopulation (type subpopulation), the Río Pánuco subpopulation, the Río Cutzmala subpopulation and the Lagunas de Zempoala subpopulation.

The Chapultepec Splitfin was the first cyprinodont fish where giving birth to live fish had been observed as early as 1769. However, the description took place much later when Bustamante y Septién descibed Cyprinus viviparus from Mexico City in 1837. The Dutch ichthyologist Pieter Bleeker, who was not aware of this description, described the same species in 1860 as "Girards unnamed fish": Girardinichthys innominatus, Günther in 1866 as Limnurgus variegatus to replace "the barbarous name proposed by Bleeker, who never saw the species". Charles T. Regan finally added with Characodon geddesi (1904) and Limnurgus innominatus (1907) two more synonyms to the list. It took until the year 1957 when José Álvarez del Villar discovered the description of Bustamante and the fish got his final name: Girardinichthys viviparus. No subpopulations are distinguished.

The Desert Tortoise was previously considered to be a single wide-ranging species, Gopherus agassizii (sensu lato), inhabiting the Mojave and Sonoran Desert regions of the southwestern USA and northwestern Mexico (Iverson 1992). The species was eventually found to be polytypic, and Murphy et al. (2011) split out the morphologically and genetically distinct Sonoran Desert subpopulations as Gopherus morafkai, the Sonoran Desert Tortoise. Further analysis demonstrated that G. morafkai was also polytypic and therefore split further to separate and describe the Sinaloan Thornscrub Tortoise further to the south as G. evgoodei (Edwards et al. 2016). This taxonomy of three species of desert tortoises has been accepted by TTWG (2017) and Berry and Murphy (2019).

The taxon Hardella indi (Gray, 1870) was traditionally considered a subspecies, Hardella thurjii indi, but was most recently synonymized as insufficiently distinct from the typical form to warrant recognition (Praschag et al. 2007).
The relationship between Hirthia and Lavigeria is under discussion. Species in this genus may be members of the Lavigeria clade (E. Michel, pers. comm.).
Was incorrectly listed on Red Lists prior to 2008 as Hobbseus valobushensis.
Haase (1995) considered this species to be a 'problematic taxa' known from few specimens all taken from a 'poorly defined locality'.
Was recorded as Ampelita (Eurystyla) julii in the 1996 and 2000 Red Lists.
Was recorded as Ampelita (Xystera) fulgurata in the 1996 and 2000 Red Lists.

The genus Leptoxis was formerly called Anculosa (Goodrich, 1922). The genus Leptoxis is a difficult group. There is considerable disagreement in regard to the number of valid species (Goodrich 1922, Burch 1982). Dillon and Lydeard (1998) found high levels of genetic divergence among populations of Leptoxis praerosa and Leptoxis plicata and from all other populations of Leptoxis studied, indicating they are distinct species. In a preliminary analysis of molecular phylogeny of Mobile River basin pleurocerids, Lydeard et al. (1997) concluded that Leptoxis picta and Leptoxis plicata are quite distinct from one another and all other pleurocerids studied, while Leptoxis taeniata and Leptoxis ampla are sister taxa, and Leptoxis picta the most basal of the group.
Original as Barilius polylepis, but changed in 1935 to Anabarilius polylepis by Chu.

There has been much disagreement concerning the relationships of the one family placed in order Mugiliformes (Nelson 2006). Although reasons exist for regarding mugilids as perciform, rather than being related to the Atherinomorpha, there seems to be insufficient reason to change the classification from that given in Nelson (1994), based on Stiassny (1993) and Johnson and Patterson (1993) (Nelson 2006).

This species is treated by some authors as being a synonym of the more widespread Liza melinoptera (Valenciennes, 1836).
Bott (1969) described this taxon as Globonautes macropus edeaensis. This species was revised by Cumberlidge (1994, 1998) as Louisea edeaensis.
Might be Austropyrgus petterdianus (Brazier, 1875).
Variously cited as a species, subspecies of Cryptomastix mullani, or synonym of Cryptomastix sanburni. The problem has not been effectively addressed in the literature.

There are problems with the Order name and the correct placement of the family. The placement in the Order: Nerillida follows the 'Updated Pettibone' which is modified and adapted from J.A. Blake (1994): 50 [Taxonomic Atlas of the Benthic ... Santa Barbara Channel vol.4]. See:  http://biodiversity.uno.edu/~worms/pc-order.html.

This species was formerly included in the Parathelphusidae but has recently been reassigned to the Gecarcinucidae; Parathelphusidae is now regarded as a junior synonym of Gecarcinucidae (Klause et al. in press).

Subfamily Pseudothelphusinae; tribe Pseudothelphusini.

This species was formerly included in the Parathelphusidae but has recently been reassigned to the Gecarcinucidae; Parathelphusidae is now regarded as a junior synonym of Gecarcinucidae (Klause et al. in press).
Subfamily Potamiscinae. This species was assigned to Indochinamon by Yeo & Ng (2007).

Subfamily Potamiscinae. This species was assigned to Iomon by Yeo & Ng (2007).

Bott (1970) included this species in the subfamily Liotelphusinae. Yeo et al. (2007) transferred it to the subfamily Gecarcinucinae Rathbun, 1904.

This species was formerly included in the Parathelphusidae but has recently been reassigned to the Gecarcinucidae; Parathelphusidae is now regarded as a junior synonym of Gecarcinucidae (Klause et al. in press).

Subfamily Potamiscinae. This species was assigned to Doimon by Yeo & Ng (2007).

This species was formerly included in the Parathelphusidae but has recently been reassigned to the Gecarcinucidae; Parathelphusidae is now regarded as a junior synonym of Gecarcinucidae (Klause et al. in press).
Subfamily Potamiscinae. This species was assigned to Doimon by Yeo & Ng (2007).

Bott (1959) described this taxon as Globonautes balssi, and later as Globonautes macropus balssi. This species was revised by Cumberlidge (1994, 1998, 1999) as Louisea balssi.
Subfamily Potamiscinae.
