Subfamily Potamiscinae. This species was assigned to Indochinamon by Yeo & Ng (2007).

This species was formerly included in the Parathelphusidae but has recently been reassigned to the Gecarcinucidae; Parathelphusidae is now regarded as a junior synonym of Gecarcinucidae (Klause et al. in press).
Originally Oreinus grahami by Regan (1904), but changed in 1964 to Schizothorax grahami by Cao and in 1992 to Racoma grahami by Wu and Wu (1992). Valid as Schizothorax grahami (Regan, 1904).
Originally Barbus grahami, but changed in 1963 to Percocypris grahami by Wu, and in 1977 to Sinocyclocheilus grahami
Originally Barilius alburnops, but changed in 1943 to Ischikauia alburnops by Nichols, and in 1958 to Anabarilius alburnops by Cheng.

This doubtful taxon, described by Bernasconi, has never been found again despite more recent searches at the type locality in Corsica (see Bertrand 2004).

Initially described as Moitessieria corsica, Falkner et al. 2002 placed the species in the genus Spiralix. Then, Boeters and Falkner (2009) restudied the type specimen and proposed to place the species within a new genus, the genus Corseria. This latter placement has been followed by MolluscaBase (2022).
The taxonomic position of this species is currently under review, and it may be transferred to Fusconaria as F. rotulata (Wright, 1899).

Eschmeyer records this species as a synonym of Oncorhynchus masou. FishBase records the species as O. masou formosanus following Shen (1993). Kottelat (pers. comm., 2001) treats this as a good species for the reasons discussed in Kottelat (1997) for European salmonids. Treated as subspecies by Taiwanese authors, but does not make biological sense. It is a diagnosable lineage, endemic to Taiwan. So, left as a valid species.
This species was originally described as a subspecies of Orconectes propinquus, but was elevated to a full species by Fitzpatrick (1967).
This subspecies was raised to species status by Buhay and Crandall (2008).
Plagigeyeria deformata is considered as a valid species, but has never been collected alive, so the genus name is uncertain.

The species was described by Falniowski (1987). Mazan and Szarowska (2000) queried the distinctness of B. austriaca, B. cylindrica and B. zyvionteki, noting that ecologically, Bythinella zyvionteki resembles B. austriaca, which may be only a geographically restricted form of the latter. However, these remarks need to be verified by future studies.
The attribution to the genus Graziana is provisional based on shell characters and requires confirmation (Reischutz and Reischutz, 2009).
Generic placement follows MolluscaBase (2022), however, the generic position of this species is uncertain and its placement under Hauffenia questionable (Georgiev and Hubenov 2013).

This species is currently under review taxonomically. Richling et al. (2017) suggested that it forms part of their Bythiospeum Clade 1 and considered it as part of a species complex that has a more extensive range in southwestern Germany.

However, Glöer (2022:116 and p.168) rejected the molecular data of Richling et al. (2017), noting that "...barcoding with COI is not suitable in many cases for prosobranch Molluscs..." and retained it as a distinct species with a distribution in three localities. Two subspecies are currently recognised: the nominate subspecies and Bythiospeum sterkianum ssp. lauterborni (F. Haas, 1936)

Bythiospeum moenanum (Flach, 1886) is now considered to be a subspecies of Bythiospeum rhenanum, although in the past it was considered to be a subspecies of B. sterkianum.

This species lies within one of the groups of spring-snails that require further research using molecular systematics to determine the taxonomic identity, and the current species limits are doubted by some researchers.
Nobre (1930) considered this species to be conspecific with Belgrandia gibba Morelet (1845), but not B. gibba Draparnaud (1805) from France in Fauna Europea.

Phylogenetically, the species of Eupaludestrina show low COI divergence distances (uncorrected p-distances < 2%), but in combination with other morphological characters Miller (2023) considered that there was enough to consider Eupaludestrina foxianensis a distinct species

Bank (2006, Fauna Europea project) list two synonyms: O.prlitchevi Hadžišče, 1959, and O.sandanskii Hadžišče, 1959. Bank in Fauna Europea is considering it as part of the Genus Ohrigocea, whereas other authors classified it into the Genus Dolapia.
This species lies close to a sister species Dianella schilickumi, the only two species known in the genus, which is restricted to freshwater lakes in Greece (Albrecht et al. 2009).
This species lies close to a sister species Prespolitorea valvataeformis Radoman 1973, the only two species known in the genus, which is restricted to freshwater Lake Prespa.

According to Szarowska (2006) this species belongs to the European genus Marstoniopsis.

This species lies close to the sister (sub)species Marstoniopsis macedonica malaprespaensis (Malo Prespa) and Marstoniopsis macedonica graeca (Vegoritis), the only three species known in the former genus of Parabythinella Radoman, 1973, which is restricted to freshwater lakes in Greece, Albania and Macedonia.

According to Szarowska (2006) this species belongs to the European genus Marstoniopsis, but it is currently retained in Parabythinella by MolluscaBase (2022).

This species lies close to the sister (sub)species Marstoniopsis macedonica macedonica (Lake Prespa) and Marstoniopsis macedonica graeca (Vegoritis), the only three species known in the former genus of Parabythinella Radoman, 1973, which is restricted to freshwater lakes in Greece, Albania and Macedonia.

This species is one of many small spring snails that can be most easily be identified using anatomical characters of the reproductive system. Listed as Belgrandiella galaica Boeters & Rolan, 1988 in the Spanish Red List (Verdú and Galente 2006).

Richling et al. (2017) suggested that this species forms a separate Bythiospeum Clade, noting that it was a range-restricted species, in contrast to most other German putative Bythiospeum species which they found to cluster with B. acicula. Glöer (2022) rejected their molecular data but also retained it as a distinct species known from the type locality. Richling et al. (2017) suggests that further molecular work will be ongoing on this family.

authority: Manganelli, Bodon, Cianfanelli, Talenti & Fo. Giusti, 1998

taxonomicNotes: Radoman (1985) considered the species to be close to Ohridohauffenia depressa, which is lives sympatrically within part of the range.
Although the shells look similar to Horatia (Daphniola) exigua, this species is retained as a separate species on the basis of anatomical differences (Reischutz 1988).

This species is one of many small spring-snails that can be most easily be identified using anatomical characters of the reproductive system. There is an ongoing Molecular systematics project in Austria collecting fresh material of the spring-snails, but as yet there are no wide scale reports that include this species (Schubert et al 2023).

Described in 1854, identified by Paladilhe in 1870, but never found again since. This species has not been studied properly since then and its taxonomic status remains doubtful. Could be in fact Belgrandia marginata.

This is one of five spring snails described in this genus, and restricted to Lake Ohrid. These species are all lacustrine-spring species and occur sympatrically. It is difficult to distinguish the different species of this genus and more than one species might be conspecific.

Some Marstoniopsis species from western Europe were synonymised by Falniowski and Wilke (2001) under M. scholtzi. That study did not include any specimens of Marstoniopsis armoricana, however, it is likely that further studies will show that it is also a synonym of M. insubrica (now considered the senior name for M. scholtzi), and molecular analysis of the material is required.

This is one of five spring snails that is described in this genus and is restricted to Lake Ohrid. These species are all lacustrine-spring species and occur sympatrically. It is difficult to distinguish the different species of this genus and more than one species might be conspecific (C. Albrecht et al. pers. comm. 2009).
This species was considered as a valid species by Haase (V. Prie, pers. comm. 2010)

This species was long believed a member of Melanopsis genus. Recently, Smoleń and Falniowski (2009), based on mitochondrial DNA sequence analysis, claimed that this species is closely related to Microcolpia daudebartii (='Fagotia acicularis') and phylogenetically is distant from other Melanopsis species. It is supported by Sümegi et al. (2012a,b, 2018) and Gulyás et al. (2023) who have found that the present-day parreyssii morph evolved during the Holocene from a smooth, Microcolpia daudebartii-like morph that was present in the fossil records since the late Pleistocene. Neubauer et al. (2014), therefore, suggest using the name Microcolpia parreyssii and this is followed also by MolluscaBase (2023).
This species lies close to a sister species Prespolitorea malaprespaensis Radoman 1973, the only two species known in the genus, which is restricted to freshwater Lake Mikri Prespa.

MolluscaBase (2021) maintains as Ampullaceana relicta ssp. pinteri (Schütt, 1974). This subspecies has been characterised both morphologically and genetically in a series of papers (Schütt 1974; Albrecht et al. 2008; Welter-Schultes 2012). 
Other authors regard this taxon as a separate species. For example, Albrecht et al. (2008) considered A. r. relicta and A. r. pinteri as two sister species from two ‘sister’ lakes, Ohrid and Prespa (but see Aksenova et al. 2018a). According to the anatomical data presented in Albrecht et al. (2008), the morphology of the copulatory apparatus in A. r. pinteri is virtually identical with that of the nominative subspecies (Vinarksi et al, 2016)
The name of the genus was previously Orientalina.

There are four subspecies known for Plagigeyeria zetaprotogona in addition to the nominate race: Plagigeyeria zetaprotogona pageti Schutt, 1960; Plagigeyeria zetaprotogona vitoja Reischutz & Reischutz, 2008, Plagigeyeria zetaprotogona zetadidyma Schutt, 1960. Most of these subspecies are restricted to single sites in Montenegro, although one P. z. zetatridyma Schutt, 1960 extends to Bosnia and Herzegovina.

Falkner and Niederhöfer (2008) discuss the nomenclatural issues and difficulties with defining the types of this species.

According to Richling et al. (2017), this species is a junior synonym of Bythiospeum acicula. However, Glöer (2022: 138) accepts it as a 'good' species, a position currently followed by MolluscaBase (2025).

Originally regarded as a subspecies of Bythinella cebennensis (Dupuy 1851), Falkner et al. (2002) followed Bernasconi (2000), who separated this taxon from Bythinella cebennensis. Falkner et al. (2002) also placed Paludinella anianensis (Paladihle 1870) as a synonym of this species.

Material from the Paladilhe collection is polytypic (V. Prie pers. comm. 2009): the shells from the Hérault basin seem to belong to B. eurystoma, while the shells from the River Lez seem to belong to a different species. Bythinella eutrepha should then be considered as a distinct species endemic to the Lez drainage.

Falkner and Niederhöfer (2008) discuss the problems with the taxonomy of the species and various issues with the nomenclature of the taxon. "Vitrella franconica" Geyer, 1904 was a common species name for the varieties of various French species, and so according to Falkner, the name Vitrella franconica "...is thus regarded as a name for a hypothetical concept" and has no status in the Zoological Nomenclature (Code Article 1.3.1).

The following nominal taxa are interpreted here as synonyms of Bythiospeum clessini clessini (Weinland, 1883):

  • Vitrella kraussii Weinland, 1883: type locality is identical to that of Vitrella clessini;
  • Vitrella franconica var. spirata Geyer, 1904: 325; described from 14 springs; Lectotypus by Zilch (1970: 325, Plate 17 Fig 15) "tailor Fountain at Adolzhausen";
  • Vitrella franconica var. spirilla Geyer, 1904:326-327; described from five springs; Lectotypus locus typicus by Zilch (1970:326; Plate 17 Fig 13) in a small spring below the small pond; Geyer (1907a:412-413) himself synonymised "Franconica" kraussi [sic] and spirilla already clessini. Since Geyer (1907a:415) from "between multiple sources Vorbachand Jagst" also the common occurrence of spirata boasts of "transitional forms clessini" and real clessini is also synonymous spirata.

This species lies within one of the groups of spring-snails that is currently being researched using molecular systematics to determine the taxonomic status and the species limits are doubted by some researchers (Benke et al. 2009). A conservation assessment is made based on data from shell morphology pending the results for full analysis for the group.

Two subspecies are currently recognised by MolluscaBase (2023); clessini (Weinland, 1883) and posterum (Geyer, 1904). Former included subspecies, moenanum (Flach, 1886) and septentrionale (Schütt, 1960) are now placed as subspecies of Bythiospeum rhenanum (Lais, 1935) (MolluscaBase 2023).

Bythiospeum clessini was treated as a synonym of B. alcicula (Held, 1838) by Richling et al. (2017) based on molecular studies and whilst this treatment was initially followed by MolluscaBase, this was later reversed following Glöer (2022).

There are currently two subspecies listed for this species: putei (Geyer, 1904) and roesleri (Geyer, 1904).

The taxonomic status of this species has been in dispute since Richling et al. (2017) treated both subspecies as potential synonyms of Bythiospeum acicula, based on their geographical grouping. Glöer (2022) maintained both subspecies as distinct but did not provide any new data to reject the findings of Richling et al. (2017) keeping both on shell characters.

This species lies within one of the groups of spring-snails that are currently being researched using molecular systematics to further determine the taxonomic status which is disputed but the species is maintained by MolluscaBase (2023).

This species has been formerly regarded as a subspecies of Bythiospeum tschapecki (Clessin, 1882) (Stojaspal 1978).

This species lies within one of the groups of spring-snails that is currently being researched using molecular systematics to determine the taxonomic status and the species limits are doubted by some researchers (Benke et al. 2009). A conservation assessment is made based on data from shell morphology, pending the results of the full analysis for the group.

According to Szarowska (2006) this species belongs to the European genus Marstoniopsis.

This species lies close to the sister (sub)species Marstoniopsis macedonica malaprespaensis (Mikri Prespa) and Marstoniopsis macedonica macedonica (Prespa Lake), the only three species known in the former genus of Parabythinella (Radoman 1973), which is restricted to freshwater lakes in Greece, Albania and Macedonia.

The genus Graziana was proposed as a replacement name for Frauenfeldia Clessin, 1878, non Egger, 1863. [...It is] a junior synonym of Belgrandiella Wagner,1927."(Kabat & Hershler 1993). Close to the Belgrandiella species, the genus has been redefined by Haase (1994).

Considered previously (Boeters 1970) as a subspecies of Belgrandiella saxatilis, this taxon is considered as a valid species by Falkner et al. (2002).

The genus Graziana was proposed as a replacement name for Frauenfeldia Clessin, 1878, non Egger, 1863. "[...It is] a junior synonym of Belgrandiella Wagner, 1927." (Kabat and Hershler 1993). Belgrandiella species, the genus was redefined by Haase (1994).

Only four species are currently placed in this genus. Three of these lie in the Lake Ohrid catchment, two in springs and the other in the lake. The fourth species is from Lake Prespa, which feeds the aquifer supplying the water to Ohrid. This species is undergoing a systematic review and might be merged with P. grochmalickii and P. sanctinaumi, in which case its Red List status will need to be reviewed.

Bolling (1965) considered just three species of this genus for Germany. By 2001, CLECOM (Check List of European Continental Mollusca; Falkner et al. 2001) listed 70 species and subspecies for Germany in this problematic genus, noting that as long as identification was based solely on shell morphology, there will be no progress on resolving the taxonomic issues. Glöer and Zettler (2005) noted that for Germany nearly 70 different "species" of this genus were originally described.

A revision of this genus based on DNA analysis was urgently needed and Richling et al. (2017) published data which reduced this (as B. pellucida) and a number of other species to synonymy with B. acicula. However, Glöer (2022) re-established the species, as he considered that the molecular data was insufficiently robust.

Originally placed in the subfamily Orientaliinae (Orientaliidae) (Radoman 1973).

Originally described as Zavalia vjetrenicae Radoman, 1973 (e.g. Radoman 1983, Glöer 2022), MolluscaBase (2022) however placed Zavalia as a synonym of Narentiana.

Two subspecies are known for this species, both of which have been assessed separately for the Spanish National Red List:

  • Spiralix valenciana valenciana Boeters, 2003.
  • Spiralix valenciana castellonica Boeters, 2003.

The two assessments are merged herein for the European Red List project, but their data are given separately, should there be cause at a later stage to consider that both taxa may be distinct species.

This species was previously considered as a synonym of B. acicula and it lies within one of the groups of spring-snails that are currently being researched using molecular systematics to determine the taxonomic status. The species limits are doubted by some researchers (Benke et al. 2009; M. Haase pers. comm. 2010). A conservation assessment is made based on data from shell morphology pending the results of a full analysis for the group.

Richling et al. (2017) again reduced this species to a synonym of B. acicula, a decision later reversed by Glöer (2022).

The Nova spring population (Benifaió, Valencia) was tentatively assigned to Tarraconia gasulli, and if subsequent research shows that it belonged to a different species, then this would reduce the number of subpopulations. This site has been lost, and the subpopulation is thought to be possibly extinct.

This is a recently described taxon which is endemic to type locality. It has never been found alive. It was previously described in the genus Moitessieria,  then a new genus Henrigirardia was created by Boeters & Falkner (2003).

The genus Graziana was proposed as a replacement name for Frauenfeldia Clessin, 1878, non Egger, 1863. "[...It is] a junior synonym of Belgrandiella Wagner, 1927." (Kabat and Hershler 1993). Close to Belgrandiella species, the genus was redefined by Haase (1994).

This is one of five spring snails that described in this genus, and restricted to Lake Ohrid. These species are all lacustrine-spring species and occur sympatrically. It is difficult to distinguish the different species of this genus and more than one species might be conspecific. It is most closely related to O. miladinovorum (Radoman 1985).
Although the species was listed in Bole and Velkovrh (1986), it was not considered to be published sensu ICZN until the subsequent paper in Bole and Velkovrh (1987).

This species lies within one of the groups of spring-snails that is currently being researched using molecular systematics to determine the taxonomic status and the species limits are doubted by some researchers (Benke et al. 2009). A conservation assessment is made based on data from shell morphology, pending the results of the full analysis for the group.
Radoman (1978) established the genus Kerkia Radoman, 1978.
This species is one of many small spring-snail species that can be most easily be identified using anatomical characters of the reproductive system.
Radoman (1985) considered the species to be close to Ohridohauffenia rotonda, with which it lives sympatrically within part of the range.

This species lies within one of the groups of freshwater snails that is currently being researched using molecular systematics to determine the taxonomic status and the species limits are doubted by some researchers (Benke et al. 2009). A conservation assessment is made based on data from shell morphology pending the results for full analysis for the group.

Richling et al. (2017) reduced this species to a synonym of B. acicula, a decision reversed by Glöer (2022) without discussion.

Falkner and Niederhöfer (2008) discuss the problems with the taxonomy of the species and various issues with the nomenclature of the taxon.

This species lies within one of the groups of freshwater snails that is currently being researched using molecular systematics to determine the taxonomic status and the species limits are doubted by some researchers (Benke et al. 2009).

A conservation assessment is made based on data from shell morphology pending the results for full analysis for the group. Richling et al. (2016) reduced this species to a synonym of B. acicula, a decision reversed by Glöer (2022).

This is one of five spring snails that described in this genus, and is restricted to Lake Ohrid. These species are all lacustrine-spring species and occur sympatrically. It is difficult to distinguish the different species of this genus and more than one species might be conspecific. It is most closely related to O. karevi (Radoman 1985).

This species is one of many small spring-snails that can be most easily be identified using anatomical characters of the reproductive system. There is an ongoing Molecular systematics project in Austria collecting fresh material of the srping-snails, but as yet there are no wide scale reports that include this species.
This species lies close to its sister species at the Ohrid Lake (Wilke et al., 2007).
Taxonomic research is required to clarify the relationship with M. arguineguinesis.

The taxon has been re-assigned as subspecies of H. cincta. The shell variability of the Syrna population, ranging from typical H. valentini shells to typical H. cincta shells and their intermediates, plus the similarities of their genitalia and the small genetic distance between them (Psonis et al. 2015), leave no doubt that there is only one species, H. cincta. The populations of Kalymnos and nearby islets must thus be reassigned to the subspecies H. cincta valentini (Mylonas et al. 2022).
Research on the species taxonomy is necessary to prove its distinctness from Napaeus osoriensis.
The family Vitrinidae in the Canary Islands was revised by Valido et al. (1999).
This species has been revalidated by Falkner et al. (2002). Its taxonomic validity needs to be fully investigated, especially at the molecular level.
Should possibly be listed as O. haydeni kanabensis.
This species has two subspecies, the nominate subspecies and subspecies P. c. hermeli Nuyda 1992 (Page and Treadaway 2003).
Listed in IUCN Red Lists from 1996 to 2006 under the generic name Paralabidochromis.
Appeared in the IUCN Red Lists from 1996 to 2006 under the generic name Paralabidochromis.

A poorly known species; the only published records are from a small area of west Java. G.T. Reels and this assessor (R. Dow) found what appears to be this species at Bako National Park in west Sarawak, Malaysia in 2005 (R. Dow pers. obs. 2008). Confirmation of the identity of material from Bako awaits detailed comparison against the type series, but agreement with the original description is good, except in size and for minor details of colouration; such differences might be expected given the distance between the populations.
Part of the material published under the name of Procordulia irregularis (Martin, 1907), P. sambawana (Martin, 1907) or P. sambawana (Lieftinck, 1953) refers to this species (van Tol, 1997).

authority: Baran, Kumlutaş, Lanza, Sindaco, Ilgaz, Avci & Crucitti, 2005

taxonomicNotes: We provisionally follow Melnikov et al. (2008) in considering Phrynocephalus horvathi to be distinct at the species level pending additional taxonomic studies.
This species was elevated to species status from Vipera ursinii anatolica by Nilson and Andrén (2001).

Although some may consider that the nomenclature of the species is wrongly typed in the SIS and that kathrini should be the name, Glöer (pers. comm. 2010) notes that because it is named after Katrin (female) the name must be katrinae and a correction published in Mollusca 26(1):2.

Based on molecular data, some authors raise the possibility that this species is conspecific with T. danubialis (Sands et al. 2020, Bunje and Lindberg 2007). Others argue - based on their different ecology and morphology - that until we have more profound knowledge about these species their formal synonymisation is premature (Fehér et al. 2009). They are treated as a separate species in MolluscaBase (2022) and this assessment is made accordingly.
Records of this species from other regions of Spain (Balearic Islands, Murcia and Valencia) are considered to be potentially different species.

Martínez-Ortí (2014), Martínez-Ortí et al. (2015) and Martínez-Ortí and Osca (2023) point to the taxonomic validity of T. valentinus.

Sands et al. (2020) found specimens resembling T. valentinus (collected from near the type locality) to group with T. baeticus in molecular analyses. In contrast, Martínez-Ortí (2014) found some degree of molecular differentiation between these species based on museum type material. Until more material is studied through more extensive methods, the status of T. valentinus remains uncertain.
Glyptothorax poonaensis was described by Hora (1938) as Glyptothorax conirostre poonaensis.

Schismatorhynchos nukta was originally described as Cyprinus nukta by Sykes (1839) from Indrayani river near Mahalunge, Maharashtra, India. Day (1872) changed the generic status of the fish to Labeo, while Hora (1942) changed the generic status of the fish to Schismatorhynchos.
Barman (1983) described Danio horai from Namdapha National park. Fang (2001) treated it under the genus Devario.

Pillaia indica was described from Sumer stream, about 22 km north of Shillong, Khasi and Jaintia Hills, Meghalaya, India. In addition, there is one paratype from Umsing at an altitude of 1524 m (Jazdani, 1972).

Originally described as Noemacheilus arunachalensis (Datta and Barman 1984) from Namdapha River, Namdapha Wildlife Sanctuary, Tirap District, Arunachal Pradesh, India, the species is currently placed in the genus Lepidocephalichthys.
Hora (1921) described the species from Kanjupkhul Village, Manipur from where Nambul River originates, the type locality of the fish.
Two subspecies have been described: P. n. niger (Dufo, 1840) and P. n. subfuscus Gerlach, 1994.
This species recently changed genus, from Gulella to Glabrennea (Schileyko 2000).
This species recently changed genus, from Gulella to Glabrennea (Schileyko 2000).
This is an undescribed species restricted to Aldabra atoll; its generic placement is uncertain.
This species is also described as Unio sp. A in Tchang-Si (1948).

Tchang-Si and Tsi, Chung-Yen named this species in their 1949 paper, regarding it as a subspecies. Xia (1982) reviewed the Margarya species in Lake Dianchi, and believed this to be a separate species. Xia is followed here.

Clarias magur was described by Hamilton (1822) from gangetic provinces.  The identity of the southern form of Clarias requires verification.  The range of distribution of Clarias batrachus is now restricted to Sunda Islands (type locality Java) (Ng and Kottelat 2008).

Pterocryptis barakensis was described from the Barak River at Vanchengphai Village, Tamenglong District, Manipur, northeast India by Vishwanath and Nebeshwar in Jayaram (2006).  The original description was scanty and the species was redescribed by Vishwanath and Nebeshwar (2009).

Amblyceps arunachalensis was described from the Dikrong River in Arunachal Pradesh, northeastern India (Nath and Dey 1989). This species was considered a junior synonym of A. mangois (Hamilton 1822) by Ng (2005) before Vishwanath and Linthoingambi (2007) revalidated the species.
Badis tuivaiei was described from the Tuivai River, Churachandpur District and Irang River, Tamenglong District,  Manipur, northeast India (Vishwanath & Shanta 2004).
Vishwanath & Nebeshwar Sharma (2004) described Schistura reticulata from Maklang River (Chindwin basin), Manipur in India.

Schistura minuta was described from the Iyei River (Barak-Surma-Meghna drainage), Noney District, Manipur (Vishwanath & Shanta Kumar 2006). The species was originally named as Schistura minutus but changed to Schistura minuta by Kottelat (2012).
Schistura tigrinum was described by Vishwanath and Nebeshwar 2005 from Barak River at Khunphung, Tamenglong District, Manipur, India.
Kottelat et al. (2007) described the species from Synrang Pamiang cave system, Meghalaya, India.

Vishwanath & Manojkumar (1995) described Psilorhynchus microphthalmus from Chakpi River at Mombi, Manipur, India.  The type locality seems to be erroneous, and the exact type locality seems to be 2kms downstream of Mombi at Pamtujang (also a tributary of the Chapki River) (B. Shangningam pers. comm.).
Schizothorax integrilabiatus was described from Xigong Lake, an endorheic mountain lake in the lower Yarlung Zangbo River (Brahmaputra in India) drainage (Wu et al. in Wu and Wu 1992).
Botia striata was described by Rao (1920) from Tunga River at Shimoga, Karnataka, India.
This species is unique in the world by the extreme length of the male cerci, almost 30% of the abdomen length and 5 to 6 times as long as epiproct.
Subgenus (Chromaphyosemion).
Glyptothorax davissinghi was described by Manimekalan and Das (1998) from the Karimpuzha and Paanapuzha tributaries of River Chaliyar, Kerala, India.
Garra hughi was described by Silas (1955) from streams in the Lower Vauguvarrai Estate in the High Ranges of Travancore, Kerala, India.
Labeo potail was originally described as Cyprinus potail Sykes (1839) from Temburni on Bhima river, Maharashtra.
Monopterus fossorius was described by Nayar (1951) from Karamana, Trivandrum.
Nemacheilus petrubanarescui was described by Menon (1984) from Nethravati River, Karnataka, India.
This species was described from the Hanoi area and northern Viet Nam. It may be only a variety of Cristaria plicata but more work needs to be done.

Simpson (1900) erected the genus Gibbosula with this species as the type species. Simpson (1914) also recognized Gibbosula crassa (Wood, 1815). Haas (1969) recognized Lamprotula crassa (Wood, 1815) placing Gibbosula as as junior synonym of Lamprotula and included Unio (Quadrula) mansuyi Dautzenberg and H. Fischer (1908) as a junior synonym. Thannh et al. (1980) used the combination Gibbosula crassa and also included U. mansuyi as a synonym.

Unio (Quadrula) leai var. ponderosa Dautzenber & H. Fischer, 1905 was erected as a variety of Lamprotula leai (Gray, 1834) and is not the same as Mya pondersoa Dellwyn, 1817 which is considered by Haas (1969) as a junior synonym of Lamprotula crassa (Wood, 1815). Dang et al. (1980) elevated the  variety to species status and considered it separate from L. leai. Although this assessment treats it as a valid species, it it likely that this is probably only a variety of L. leai.

Originally placed Arconada et al. (2007) as part of Alzoniella pyrenaica. The species has similar conchological characters, but differs anatomically in characters of the bursa copulatrix and the receptaculum (Arconada et al. 2007).

Horalabiosa joshuai was first described by Silas (1953) from the rainforest streams of Tambraparni River, Singampatti, Tirunelveli, Tamil Nadu, India. Talwar and Jhingran (1991) placed it under Garra lamta. The taxonomic identity was in question until it was revalidated by Devi (1992).

Nemachilichthys shimogensis was described by Rao (1920) from Tunga River, Shimoga, Karnataka. There is an ambiguity regarding the taxonomic validity of this species. Menon (1987, 1999) and Talwar and Jhingran (1991) synonymised the species with Nemchilichthys ruppelii (Sykes 1841). However, Banarescu and Nalbant (1995) treated the species as seperately as N. shimogensis.
Travancoria elongata was described by Pethiyagoda and Kottelat (1994) from Vettilapara, 26 km upstream of Chalakkudy town, Kerala, southern India.
Homaloptera montana was first described by Herre (1941) from a brook flowing through the Puthuthottam estate in Valparai, Anamalai Hills, Tamil Nadu, India.

Batasio sharavatiensis was described by Bhat and Jayaram (2004) from Joginmatha, 1 km upstream of the Jog Falls in the west flowing Sharavati River, Karnataka. Arunachalam and Muralidharan (2007) reported a range extension of this species from the east flowing river Tunga. However, a comparison of their morphometric data and the photograph raises a doubt whether their specimens are conspecific with B. sharavatiensis sensu stricto. Until further taxonomic studies, including molecular methods, are available the report from Tunga is not considered for the assessment.
Puntius deccanensis was described by Yazdani and Rao (1976) from Nalla near Katraj Tank, Pune, Maharashtra, India.

Nemacheilus pulchellus is described by Day 1873 from the Bhavani River at the base of the Nilgiris (India). In recent publications it has also placed in the genus Mesonemacheilus (e.g., Rema Devi and Indra 2002, Shaji 2002).
Crossocheilus periyarensis was described by Menon and Jacob (1996) from the upper reaches of the Periyar River at Thanikkudy, Kerala, India.
Hypselobarbus periyarensis was described by Raj (1941) from Periyar Lake, Kerala, India
Homaloptera santhamparaiensis was described by Arunachalam et al (2002) from Panniyar stream of River Periyar, Idukki District, Kerala, India.
Horabagrus nigricollaris was described by Pethiyagoda and Kottelatt (1994) from Vettilapara on the Chalakudy River, 26 km upstream of Chalakudy Town, Kerala, India.
Garra kalakadensis was described by Rema Devi (1993) from Pachaiyar, east of Sengaltheri, Kalakad Wildlife Sanctuary, Tirunelveli District, Tamil Nadu.

Glyptothorax madraspatanus was described as Glyptosternum madraspatanum by Day (1873) from Bhavani River, Nilgiris, India. Record of this species from Mula-Mutha rivers of Pune (Kharat et al. 2003) are wrong as their specimen do not match with the type specimens (ZSI Kolkata F1235 and F1313) (N. Dahanuker, pers. obs.) and is likely to be an hitherto undescribed species. It is therefore likely that the other records of this species from Mula-Mutha river (Suter 1944, Tonapi and Mulherkar 1963, Wagh and Ghate 2003) are also wrong. Since this species is habitat specialist, it is likely to be restricted in distribution and its reports from river systems other than Cauvery need to be validated. There is an urgent need to delimit the distribution of the species preferably by molecular methods.
Puntius cauveriensis was described by Hora (1937) from the Cauvery River at Coorg.
Hypselobarbus dubius was described by Day (1867) from Bhavani River at the base of Nilgiri hills, India.
Barilius canarensis was described by Jerdon (1849) from erstwhile Canara, southern India.
Glyptothorax anamalaiensis was described by Silas (1952) from a mountain stream flowing through the Anamalai Hills, Western Ghats, India.
The species was decribed as Glyptothorax housei by Herre (1942) from Puthuthottam Estate, Valparai, Tamil Nadu.
Osteochilus longidorsalis was first described from Vettilapara in Chalakudy River, Kerala, India (Pethiyagoda and Kottelat 1994).
The correct species name should be Neolissochilus wynaadensis following Ali et al. (2014). This species was described as Barbus wynaadensis by Day (1873).
Hemibagrus punctatus was described by Jerdon (1849) from Cauvery River and its principal tributaries in southern India.

Day described Paradanio neilgherriensis from Neilgherry hills in 1867 and he placed in the genus Paradanio. Later in 1878 he placed this species in the genus Danio. The scientific name was recently changed from Danio neilgherriensis to Devario neilgherriensis by Fang Fang Kullander in the doctoral dissertation "Phylogeny and Species Diversity of the South and Southeast Asian Cyprinid Genus Danio Hamilton (Teleostei, Cyprinidae)." in 2001.
Travancoria jonesi was first described by Hora (1941) from a mountain stream in Pampadumpara, Peeramedu, Kerala, India.

Sykes (1839) described Barbus mussullah from Ghod river, Sirur, Pune District. Maharashtra, India. There has been considerable debate on the generic status of the species. This species has been treated as Tor mussullah by Talwar and Jhingran (1991), Jayaram (1997, 2005, 2010) following the discussions given in Hora (1943). Menon (1992, 1999), however suggested that the species considered as Tor mussullah by Hora (1943) is same as Tor khudree and he placed T. mussullah under genus Hypselobarbus following Rainboth (1989). However, both the original suggestion of the genus Hypselobarbus by Bleeker (1860), with Barbus mussullah as its type species, and resurrection of the resurrection of genus Hypselobarbus by Rainboth (1989) is based on the illustration by Sykes (1839) and not by examining specimens.

Suggestions made by Hora (1943) that the species should be called Tor mussullah seems more valid as the species called as 'musunda' in the type locality of Barbus mussullah and neighbouring areas is indeed a Tor species (Neelesh Dahanukar pers. obs.).

The Malabar mahseer, Tor malabaricus, was described by the British zoologist, T.C Jerdon in 1849 (Jerdon 1849) from the mountain streams of Malabar (an erstwhile province of Southern India; currently in the northern part of Kerala State), India. The species had a confusing taxonomy, as some authors considered it a synonym of the Deccan mahseer, T. khudree (Menon 1992; 1999), while others believed it to be a valid sub species, T. khudree malabaricus (Indra 1993). Tor malabaricus can be easily identified from its congeners in having a lesser number of lateral line scales (23-24), and a body with a pale brown colour on its upper half, silver on its lower half and reddish fins (Jerdon 1849).

Puntius crescentus was described by Yazdani and Singh (1994) from Kali Nadi at Sunkeri, Karnataka, India. There is a need to establish the precise taxonomic status of the species as is has several characters similar to another species P. vittatus.
Puntius fraseri was originally described as Barbus fraseri by Hora and Misra (1939) from Darna river, Deolali, Nashik, Maharashtra.
Parapsilorhynchus prateri was first described from Darna River in the upper Godavari River basin, Deolali, Nasik District, Maharashtra State, India (Hora and Misra 1938).
Pterocryptis wynaadensis was first described from Wynaad, Kerala, India by Day (1873).
Hamilton (1822) described this species as Cyprinus curmuca from Vedavathi River in the Tungabhadra drainage. Barbus kolus Sykes, 1841 was synonymised with C. curmuca by Menon (1992).
Parapsilorhynchus elongatus was described by Singh (1994) from Ghod River, Khondwal village, Ambegaon taluka, Pune District, Maharashtra, India.

authority: Sreekantha, Gururaja, Rema Devi, Indra & Ramachandra, 2006
taxonomicNotes: Schistura nagodiensis was described by Sreekantha et al. (2006) from Nagodi tributary of Sharavathi river, Karnataka, southern India.
Mesonemacheilus herrei Nalbant & Bănărescu, 1981 was treated as a synonym for Noemacheilus guentheri Day, 1867 (Menon 1987). But, Eschmeyer and Fricke (2010) treat it as a separate species.
Lepidopygopsis typus was described by Raj (1941) from Periyar Lake, Kerala, India.
Glyptothorax kudremukensis was described by Gopi (2007) from the headwaters of the Thunga River, Muduba, Kudremukh National Park, Karnataka, India.
Garra surendranathanii was first described by Shaji et al. (1996) from the Orukomban tributary of Chalakudy River, Kerala, India.

Crottini et al. (2009) considered Cryptoscincus, of which C. minimus was the only member, a junior synonym of Paracontias based on molecular evidence. Specimens from Baie de Sakalava, noted by Glaw and Vences (2007) as needing further study to verify their assignment to this species, have now been confirmed as P. minimus (M. Vences pers. comm. May 2011).

This species was synonymized with B. dentata by Raxworthy and Nussbaum (1995). This synonymy was questioned by Glaw et al. (1999), and the validity of this species was supported by Townsend et al. (2009)

This species was until recently thought to be known only from the holotype (Glaw and Vences 2007); however animals found in the Fort Dauphin area are considered to represent this species (C. Raxworthy pers. comm. January 2011). This requires clarification, and the taxonomic status and relationships of this skink are in need of further study (Glaw and Vences 2007).
Stenophis guentheri was assigned to Lycodryas by Nagy et al. (2010) in their revision of these genera.

Nagy et al. 2010 removed Stenophis tulearensis from synonymy with this species (where it had been placed by Vences et al. 2004).The same authors elevated the subgenus Phisalixella to a full genus. The taxonomic identity of both P. tulearensis and P. variabilis requires further clarification (M. Vences pers. comm. January 2011).
The taxonomy of this species is in need of revision (Glaw and Vences 2007).
Alain has data on this species - include him in the reassessment.

Records of this species from northern Madagascar, noted by Glaw and Vences (2007) as requiring further taxonomic study, may represent C. parsonii, C. oshaugnessyi or, in Montagne d'Ambre, C. ambreensis (C. Raxworthy pers. comm. June 2011), although there are no confirmed records of true C. oshaugnessyi from northern Madagascar (F. Glaw pers. comm. July 2011). A record from Tsaratanana requires further investigation to clarify its specific identity (F. Glaw pers. comm. July 2011).

This chameleon was formerly considered a subspecies of C. gastrotaenia, but was elevated to species status by Böhme (1997). Individuals from Andohahela in the southeast require additional study to verify their taxonomic status (Andreone et al. 2001, Glaw and Vences 2007).

The Furcifer verrucosus, F. nicosiai, F. oustaleti group is in need of major revision and probably contains as yet undescribed species (Glaw and Vences 2007). Previous records of this species from the Menabe Region appear to be a previously undescribed taxon (Raselimanana 2008).
Specimens resembling this species have been recorded from near Makira at approximately 50 m asl., but these require  confirmation (F. Glaw pers. comm. January 2011).
This species shows a strong similarity to B. thieli (Andreone et al. 2009). The B. thieli/B. lineata complex is in need of revision and may contain several new species (Andreone et al. 2009).

Calumma hilleniusi has variously been considered either a full species or a subspecies of C. brevicorne (Raxworthy and Nussbaum 2006). The identity of southern subpopulations currently assigned to this species requires verification (Vences et al. 2002).

Recently-discovered specimens from Baie de Sakalava are provisionally assigned to X. grandidieri, a species previously known only from a single specimen with the type locality "Madagascar" (Glaw and Vences 2007; F. Glaw pers. comm.April 2011), on the basis that two other restricted-range species described by Mocquard have been found at the same locality, providing evidence that collections took place within or near this site (F. Glaw pers. comm. April 2011). These records were treated as belonging to X. mocquardi in Glaw and Vences (2007). Xenotyphlops mocquardi is itself likely to be a junior synonym of this species, as it falls within the range of variation observed within more recently-collected Baie de Sakalava specimens assigned to X. grandidieri (M. Vences pers. comm. January 2011; F. Glaw pers. comm. April 2011). If this is the case, this will represent a slight range extension to the south (M. Vences pers. comm. January 2011).

This species is clearly distinct from any other Malagasy Lygodactylus. It is presumed that the village of Mandritsara where the species has been recorded represents the type locality. However, as the type locality is given as "Mt. Mandritsara, northwest Madagascar" in the original description (Pasteur, 1965), and no mountain exists near the village (which is in northeast Madagascar), this cannot be verified with certainty.

Along with other members of the Liopholidophis stumpffi group, this species was briefly included within the new genus Bibilava (Glaw et al. 2007, Glaw and Vences 2007). Cadle and Ineich (2008) later resurrected Thamnosophis for this species group.
This species is also known as Prisodontopsis johnstoni (E.A. Smith 1893) in recent literature, but that name is a primary homonym of Unio johnstoni Etheridge, 1881.
Gabbia alticola was described as Amnicola alticola (Subba Rao 1989).
Platyops sterreri is a stygobitic mysid, endemic to anchialine caves in Bermuda and the only species in the genus.

Thomas Say originally described Unio cicatricosus in 1829. Isaac Lea described Unio varicosus later the same year. Both names were widely used in the literature; however, varicosus Lea was pre-occupied by varicosus Lamarck, 1819. Frierson (1911) synonomized Unio cicatricosus Say with Unio aesopus Green, 1827 (= Plethobasus cyphyus Rafinesque, 1820) and renamed Lea's shell as Unio detectus Frierson, 1911. He also named two additional species Unio cicatricoides and Unio comptertus as being part of this complex. In 1919, Ortmann synonomized all of Frierson's names under cicatricosus Say. Three years later Ortmann and Walker (1922) sunk cicatricosus into cyphyus. Recent authors have not followed Ortmann and Walker (1922) and recognize Plethobasus cicatricosus as distinct (Parmalee 1967, Bogan and Parmalee 1983, Cummings and Mayer 1992, Turgeon et al. 1998, Williams et al. 2008).

A list of synonyms for this species can be found on The MUSSEL project web site (Graf and Cummings 2011).
This newly described species was discovered in 2005. It is the single representative of the family Symphytognathidae in Europe with its closest relatives occurring in Cote D’Ivoire.

Aipysurus fuscus is a small to moderately sized venomous snake, typically reaching a length of 60 cm and maximum total length of 98 cm (Cogger 2014, Francis 2006, Mirtschin et al. 2017, Storr et al. 2002). Adults are brown, blackish-brown or purplish-brown, but often have obscure, paler cross bands on the lower flanks. Sometimes the lateral scales will have a darker patch at the centre, resulting in faint, longitudinal striations; younger snakes are typically lighter and dark banded.

Aipysurus fuscus can be differentiated from closely related A. laevis by its smaller size, darker colouration and by the number of scales on belly and mid-body (155 – 172 ventral scales and less than 21 scale rows around the midbody). Other scale features used in taxonomy of A. fuscus include: head shields are regular with some fragmentation of scales occasionally (prefrontals, frontal and supraoculars) especially the parietals; upper labials six to nine, with the first three or four sometimes divided horizontally; anal scales are divided, while ventral scales are broad with a medium keel and no posterior notch. Body scales are smooth with some lowest rows weakly keeled in males. Body scales are imbricate (overlapping) and there are typically 19 scale rows around the neck and 19 around the mid-body. Females have between 24 to 33 subcaudal scales, while males have 30 to 37 subcaudal scales (Francis 2006, Rasmussen et al. 2014).

Microsatellite markers for A. fuscus and A. laevis sampled across four Timor Sea reefs were analysed to assess evidence for recent inter-specific gene flow and historical introgression (Sanders et al. 2014). The data fit an Isolation–Migration model, which showed significant and asymmetrical levels of gene flow following species divergence, and highest rates of introgression from the large A. laevis population into the much smaller A. fuscus population(Sanders et al. 2014). Population assignment analyses recovered two ancestral clusters that broadly corresponded to morphological species designations, but revealed high frequencies of hybrids on all four reefs and individuals of pure A. fuscus ancestry only at Scott and (historically) Ashmore. Most unexpectedly, 95% of snakes sampled at Hibernia were hybrids that resembled A. laevis in phenotype, revealing a collapse of reproductive barriers (‘reverse speciation’) at this reef. These results have dire implications for the conservation status of A. fuscus, and highlight the fragility of reproductive barriers in a recent marine radiation (Sanders et al. 2014).
Generic name was entered incorrectly in 1996 as Antriscopia.

Chlorocnemis maccleeryi Pinhey, 1969 was described as a subspecies of C. montana St. Quentin, 1942. It differs substantially in coloration and size. Moreover the appendages are more like those of C. abbotti (Calvert, 1892) than C. montana. The differences are sufficient to regard the taxon as a good species (Dijkstra and Clausnitzer in press).
Described by Boulenger (1898) based on specimens from the Chao Phraya River, Thailand.
This species was recognised as conspecific with the Indonesian Balantiocheilos melanopterus Bleeker, 1851 for a long time. It was described as a separate species by Ng and Kottelat (2007).
Its generic level was validated as Devario by Fang (2001) and and Fang et al. (2009).

This species was originally described as Nemachilus spilopterus var. nasifilis by Pellegrin 1936 from a specimen collected from Song Ko and Song Tan in Central Viet Nam. Since the collection of the types in 1936, this species has not been collected.
Subgenus (Mesoaphyosemion).

This species is one of several troglobitic species in the subgenus Ortmannicus. It is highly distinctive, and its closest affinities are with Procambarus delicatus, which appears to be on a line leading to Troglocambarus (J. Cordeiro, T. Jones and R. Thoma pers. comm. 2009).
Subgenus (Pauciradius).
Subgenus (Fundulopanchax).
Subgenus (Diapteron).
Although the species has been assumed to be an inhabitant of rapids Teugels et al. (1992) have collected it in both rapids and in non-rapidly flowing stretches of the Cross River.

From a study conducted by Buhay and Crandall (2009) Procambarus pecki has been transferred from the subgenus Remoticambarus of the genus Procambarus, to the subgenus Aviticambarus of the genus Cambarus. This has been based on genetic studies which more accurately reflect its evolutionary history.
This species is now places in Aphaniops.

Subgenus (Paraphyosemion).

Brycinus bartoni is close to Brycinus longipinnis. The two species are distinguished by predorsal scale counts (13 in B. bartoni vs. 8 1/2-11 1/2 in B. longipinnis); it is also similar to B. intermedius and B. tholloni, but has fewer scales in transverse row between lateral line and dorsal fin origin (5 1/2 vs. 6 1/2).
Subgenus (Chromaphyosemion).
Subgenus (Paludopanchax).
It is currently under revision. Teleogramma brichardi is possibly a complex.
Subgenus (Chromaphyosemion).

Subgenus (Chromaphyosemion): Aphysemion (Chromaphyosemion) melinoides (Sonnenberg, 2007) is distinguished from its close relative Aphysemion (Chromaphyosemion) lugens by the orange colour on the throat and belly, orange in basal half of anal and dorsal fins and orange fin streamer on dorsal fin in males and orange in ventral part of caudal fin in females; the anterior or complete dorsal fin orange, orange hue on flanks of females. A. melinoides is known from several collection localities along the road from Kribi to Ebolowa between the villages Akok and Akom II and on the road from Akom II to Bipindi in southwestern Cameroon.
Appeared in the IUCN Red Lists from 1996 to 2006 under the generic name Prognathochromis.

This species lies within one of the groups of subterranean snails that need researched using molecular systematics to determine the taxonomic status and the species limits. A conservation assessment is made based on data from shell morphology pending the results for full analysis for the group. However, given the characteristic shell, this is undoubtedly a valid species.

Applying the methodology of Heller et al. (2005) to differentiate Melanopsis species, M. brevicula has to be considered as a distinctive Melanopsis species characterised by its minute size, flattened sides, low spire and small mouth aperture.
This species is described in the PhD dissertation of Ghamizi (1998) (nomen nudum). Type specimen in Musee de Paris.
Nomen nudum for a new species described by Ghamizi (1998) in his PhD thesis. Type specimen in the Musee de Paris.
There are many forms of M. magnifica. It's a very beautiful shell. It's possible that it's many species combined.
The generic position of this new species is still uncertain. The name 'knidirii' is a nomen nudum as the species is only described in the PhD dissertation of Ghamizi (1998).

The generic position of this new species is still uncertain, anatomical data lacking. The name 'valai' is a nomen nudum since the species is only described in the Ph D dissertaion of Ghamizi (1998). Type specimen is in Musee de Paris.
This species is described in the PhD dissertation of Ghamizi (1998) (nomen nudum). Type specimen in Musee de Paris.

Kristensen (1985) and Ramdani et al. (1987) consider the Maghrebian M. scalaris as a distinct species. Ghamizi (1998) suggests that it may belong to the Circummediterranean M. dufouri Férusac, 1823. Applying the methodology of Heller et al. (2005) to differentiate Melanopsis species, M. scalaris has to be considered as a distinctive Melanopsis species characterised by its strong subsutural shoulder and turriform shell.

This species is sympatric with Maroccopsis agadirensis, and is found in the same well. Nomen nudum for a new species described by Ghamizi (1998) in his PhD thesis. Type specimen in the Musee de Paris. Very distinct species.
Formerly placed in family Thiaridae, this genus is now placed in Paludomidae.

Formerly placed in family Thiaridae, but this genus is now tentatively placed in Paludomidae along with Cleopatra until further genetic analysis can confirm the appropriate family for this genus.

Originally described by Bédé (1932) as a new subgenus, Liouvillea, on the basis of anatomic differences. Subsequently considered as synonymous with the European Anodonta cygnaea Lake (see Daget, 1998), from which it differs by its smaller size, broad umbo and umbonal ornamenation. Further molecular and taxonomic research is necessary to establish its degree of taxonomic distinction.

For the moment it should not be considered as A. cygnea because the Iberian populations are not considered A. cygnea which would imply a jump, so almost certainly not.
Nomen nudum for a new species described by Ghamizi (1998) in his PhD thesis. Type specimen in the Musee de Paris.
Both Mandahl-Barth (1988) and Daget (1998) regarded Mutela langi as a synonym of M. rostrata. However, Graf regards Mutela langi as morphologically distinct, but based on limited shell data.

Considered by Ghamizi (1998) as a distinct morphotype. Applying the methodology of Heller et al. (2005) to differentiate Melanopsis species, M. chlorotica has to be considered as a distinctive Melanopsis species characterised by its small size, elongate form and the presence of several dark coloured bands.

These Moroccan populations were identified by Kristensen (1985) and Ramdani et al. (1987) as Lymnaea glabra. Ghamizi (1990) forwards conclusive arguments that the species originally described by Pallary is distinct and endemic to Morocco.
Nomen nudum for a new species described by Ghamizi (1998) in his PhD thesis. Type specimen in the Musee de Paris.

The Maghrebian Anodonta populations were ranged by all modern malacologists (e.g., Daget 1998) under the European A. cygnaea and A. anatina. Recent molecular studies have revealed however the existence of 2 cryptospecies of Anodonta in Mediterranean Europe. According to the present assessors, taking into account the above and the conchyologic distinctive features the Anodonta populations that occur in the marshes of the border region of Algeria and Tunisia should be considered as a separate taxon.
It is sympatric with G. gofasi. Nomen nudum for a new species described by Ghamizi (1998) in his PhD thesis. Type specimen in the Musee de Paris.

The generic position of this new species is still uncertain. The name 'salahi' is a nomen nudum as the species is only described in the PhD dissertation of Ghamizi (1998). Voucher specimens deposited in Paris Museum.
Formerly placed in family Thiaridae, this genus is now placed in Paludomidae.
Nomen nudum for a new species described by Ghamizi (1998) in his PhD thesis. Type specimen in the Musee de Paris.
Nomen nudum for a new species described by Ghamizi (1998) in his PhD thesis. Type specimen in the Musee de Paris.
This species is possibly related to M. recticosta (Brown 1994), but Brown maintained it as a separate species.

Applying the methodology of Heller et al. (2005) to differentiate Melanopsis species, M. letourneuxi has to be considered as a distinctive Melanopsis species characterised by strong ribs with two rows of noduli separated by a marked indentation.

Highly distinctive small species. Applying the methodology of Heller et al. (2005) to differentiate Melanopsis species, M. saharica has to be considered as a distinctive Melanopsis species characterised by its squat extremely globose form.

In his revision of the Paleartic unionids, Haas (1940) considered the three Moroccan species described by Pallary (1918, 1923 and 1928) to belong to a single subspecies of the European Psilunio auricularius (Spengler, 1792 ), namely P. auricularius marocanus. Some authors (Van Damme 1984; Ghamizi 1998) retained the Moroccan form as distinctive while others (Mandahl-Barth 1988; Daget, 1998) did not. Recent molecular and conchiological investigation show that this species is a separate species, differing from the European species e.g., by its smaller size, nearly black outer test and differences in the hinge.

Considered erroneously by Van Damme (1984), Daget (1998) and Ghamizi (1998) as a synonym of Unio elongatulus durieui Deshayes, 1847. Unio foucauldianus was considered a synonim of U. gibbus by Araujo et al. (2009). However, based on morphological and genetic analyses, Froufe et al., 2016 recognize U. foucauldianus not as U. gibbus but as the valid name for the other single Unio species found in Morocco. Pallary first described this species in 1936 from specimens collected at the Aït Melloul bridge, in the lower section of the Sous River, which now dries completely in the summer and where the species has not been recorded for decades. Unfortunately, the present location of the type specimens of U. foucauldianus is unknown.

Melanopsis mourebeyensis is considered to be a distinct morphotype by Ramdani et al. (1987) and by Ghamizi (1998). Applying the methodology of Heller et al. (2005) to differentiate Melanopsis species, M. mourebeyensis has to be considered as a distinctive Melanopsis species characterised by a nodular carena and a swollen body whorl.
Nomen nudum for a new species described by Ghamizi (1998) in his PhD thesis. Type specimen in the Musee de Paris.
Nomen nudum for a new species described by Ghamizi (1998) in his PhD thesis. Type specimen in the Musee de Paris.

Iberus is an Iberian Peninsula endemic genus, that comprised 20 morphospecies of uncertain validity (traditional classifications conflicted with each other mostly in the number of species), some of them occupying a very restricted geographical area. They were characterized mostly on the basis of the shell morphology because the reproductive system shows no diagnostic features at the specific level (Puente 1994, Arrébola 1995). Elejalde et al. (2008) studied partial DNA sequences of two mitochondrial genes (COI and 16S rRNA) from different specimens of Iberus, to test the validity of the described morphospecies of the genus. The analyses have revealed 11 main phylogroups, being genetically unique lineages that are allopatrically distributed and considered to have full species status. One of them is I. gualtieranus, composed by three subspecies:  I.g. gualtieranus, I.g. mariae and I.g. ornatissimus. The first has a keeled and flattened shell with marked ornamentation of spiral and radial costulae (synapomorphy), while the other two show rounded and globose shells with soft ornamentation. I.g. mariae and I.g. ornatissimus have distinct and very restricted range and their shells clearly differ one from another. Both taxa were phylogenetically very closely related to I. g. gualtieranus, in contrast with the opinion of other authors who considered them to be more closely related to I. alonensis s.l., on the basis of shell morphology (Alonso and Ibáñez 1981).

There is a general agreement that the undescribed form (Haplochromis sp. nov. 'nigrofasciatus') is a clearly circumscribed species. The formal description for this species was published in 2010 (Niemantsverdriet and Witte in de Zeeuw et al. 2010).

There is a general agreement that the undescribed form (Haplochromis sp. nov. 'citrus') is a clearly circumscribed species. The formal description for this species was published in 2010 (ter Huurne and Witte in de Zeeuw et al. 2010).

There is a general agreement that the undescribed form (Haplochromis sp. nov. 'Dusky Wine-red Fin') is a clearly circumscribed species. The formal description of this species was published in 2010 (Meites and Witte in Zeeuw et al. 2010).

There is a general agreement that the undescribed form (Haplochromis sp. nov. '75') is a clearly circumscribed species. The formal description of this species was published in 2010 (ter Huurne and Witte in de Zeeuw et al. 2010).

There is a general agreement that the undescribed form (Haplochromis sp. nov. 'Yellow') is a clearly circumscribed species. The formal description for this species was published in 2010 (Mietes and Witte in de Zeeuw et al. 2010).
Appeared in the IUCN Red Lists from 1996 to 2006 under the generic name Ptyochromis.
There are two recognised subspecies: D. c. caspia (Eichwald, 1855) and D. c. pallasi (Andrusov, 1897).

This species may be in need of taxonomic revision (F.Köhler pers. comm. 2010), since Strong and Köhler (2009) suggest placement of this species in the family Semisulcospiridae. Some authors consider Biwa as a synonym or subgenus of Semisulcospira.
Note that this species should not be confused with Physa arachleica Starobogatov & Prozorova, 1989.

The genus Marstonia formerly was relegated to synonymy with Pyrgulopsis by Hershler and Thompson (1987). But Thompson and Hershler (2002) later re-evaluated eastern North American species and assigned to Pyrgulopsis and recognized them as distinct species of the genus Marstonia (J. Cordiero pers. comm. 2010).
A photograph of the only known specimen of this species was included by Glaw and Vences (2007) as Paracontias hildebrandti, with the note that this identification was tentative.

It is likely that an unidentified species reported from the Ankarana Massif in the 1980s represents P. roesleri, but this is not known from specimens and so its identity cannot be confirmed (Glaw et al. 2010).

Belgrandia alcoaensis was considered as a subspecies of Belgrandia heussi by Haase (2000: 185) based on an examination of one shell (a paratype). Rolan and Oliveira (2009) have examined many specimens and consider it very different from the other Portuguese species due to the almost flat whorls, especially the last one, the prominent spiral cord and lack of any axial thickening.
Two subspecies were described: I. henrici henrici (Córdoba subpopulations) and I. henrici giennensis (Jaén subpopulations).

According to Fernholm (1986, 1998), E. octatrema was only known from the 300 mm holotype. In the original description, Barnard (1923) apparently described only one specimen, but he stated “…ventral fin ending only a short distance (8-10 mm) behind last gill-opening”. The range “8-10 mm” could be evidence that there were at least two specimens used for the description. In addition, the only other known specimen BMNH 1927.12.6.1 (formerly SAM 13031) is listed as "co-type" in the original register. According to the SAM database (M. Bougaardt pers. comm.), the station data for these specimens are: SAM 13030, Cape Saint Blaize, North 1/4 North, 6.5 miles, 36 fm (66 m), 28 June 1899, shrimp trawl, RV Pieter Faure; and BMNH 1927.12.6.1 [ex SAM 13031], Cape Saint Blaize, West 3/4 North, 4 miles, 27 fm (49 m), 25 January 1900, trawl, RV Pieter Faure. Based on this evidence, both specimens are considered here as syntypes of E. octatrema. Also, the type locality provided in Barnard’s (1923) original description (“Agulhas Bank, 25-40 fm”) is an assumed approximation.
Giant sea bass was originally assigned to the grouper family, Serranidae, but was later placed in a new family called the Percichthyidae (Domeier 2001).

This species was previously known as Arkansia wheeleri, but changed to Arcidens wheeleri in Clarke (1981) and Graf and Cummings (2007).

A list of synonyms for this species can be found on The MUSSEL project web site (Graf and Cummings 2011).

Known as Folifer yunnanensis by some researchers. It was originally described as F. yunnanensis. The genus is in need of further taxonomic investigation. It may be closer to Spinibarbus, and at present it would be better considered within Folifer (M. Kottelat pers. comm. 2011).
The taxonomic validity of this species needs to be fully investigated, especially at the molecular level.

Was under the family Iguanidae.

Based on electrophoretic analyses, Adest (1977) concluded that Uma scoparia, Uma notata, and Uma inornata comprise a single species (Uma notata) (Adest 1977). Stebbins (1985, 2003) maintained the three taxa as distinct species. Phylogenetic relationships inferred from mtDNA sequences indicate that Uma notata rufopunctata is more closely related to Uma inornata than to Uma notata notata (Wilgenbusch and de Queiroz 2000, Trepanier and Murphy 2001). Trepanier and Murphy (2001) concluded that for the northern species of Uma existing data support either a two-species arrangement (Uma scoparia, Uma notata) or five-species classification (Uma scoparia, Uma notata, Uma inornata, and Uma rufopunctata, plus an undescribed species from Mohawk Dunes, Arizona). Trepanier and Murphy prefered the latter arrangement and stated that a description of the Mohawk Dunes species is in progress. Crother et al. (2003) adopted the taxonomy preferred by Trepanier and Murphy.

This species is included in Montivipera following Nilson et al. (1999). Further taxonomic research is needed to clarify the validity of this species (W. Böhme pers. comm.). It may represent a synonym of M. xanthina (see Schatti et al., 1991), however recent electropheoretic analysis of blood-sera (Göçmen in press) support the recognition of M. albizona at the species level.
This species is sometimes considered to be conspecific with V. raddei (see McDiarmid et al. 1999).
This species is within the subgenus Pelias (Nilson et al. 1999).

According to Gittenberger (1993), the similar Xerosecta species living in the southern half of the Iberian Peninsula (X. cespitum, X. promissa, X. reboudiana and X. adolfi) can be identified by some shell features. Puente (1994) and Arrébola (1995) indicated that sometimes reproductive system studies are required for an unambiguous identification.

Some consider Austropotamobius pallipes as a species complex comprised of two genetically distinct species; A. pallipes and an Italian species for which the name is being discussed. The Italian species is thought to be comprised of a number of subspecies, though this depends on the author. Both the Italian form and A. pallipes can be found in Spain, France, Italy and Switzerland. It is also suggested that there are two subspecies of A. pallipes: A. pallipes pallipes which exists in France, the British Isles, Spain, Switzerland, and Germany, and A. p. subsp. nov. which is known from Liguria in Italy and the Alpes Maritimes region of France. There still exists some debate as to whether the Italian form should be raised to species level, though recent genetic work (Grandjean et al. 2000a, Fratini et al. 2005, Bertocchi et al. 2008) would support a separate species, Austropotamobius italicus with 4 subspecies.
Appeared on Red Lists prior to 2008 as Bayadera brevicauda ishigakiana.

It is likely that there are more Beddomeia taxa in Tasmania and the Australian state of Victoria than are currently classified (Ponder et al. 1993). The order may be Neotaenioglossa (Inland Fisheries Service 2004).

This species is one of many small spring-snails that can be most easily identified using anatomical characters of the reproductive system. Originally, this species was found to be living with another spring snail Graziana lacheineri (Gloer 2002).

This species is one of many small spring-snails that can be most easily identified using anatomical characters of the reproductive system. There is an ongoing Molecular systematics project in Austria collecting fresh material of the srping-snails, and this confirms the taxonomic status of this species (Schubert et al, 2023).

This is one of many small spring-snail species that can be most easily be identified using anatomical characters of the reproductive system. The spring snails from this region are being collected and researched as part of a major review (Schubert et al, 2023) but there is no data on this species that has been published.

This species is one of many small spring-snails that can be most easily identified using anatomical characters of the reproductive system. There is an ongoing Molecular systematics project in Austria collecting fresh material of the spring-snails, but as yet there are no wide scale reports that include this species (Schubert et al 2023).

This species is one of many small spring-snails that can be most easily be identified using anatomical characters of the reproductive system. The species has been reviewed by the Austrian Bar-Code of Life project and is found to be distinct using molecular data (Schubert et al, 2023)

This is one of many small spring-snail species that can be most easily identified using anatomical characters of the reproductive system. It is close in shell characters to Belgrandiella parreysi, the distribution records based on shells alone are therefore questionable (Gloer 2002).

One of many small spring-snail species that can be most easily be identified using anatomical characters of the reproductive system.  It has been confused with Belgrandiella fuschi Boeters, 1970, so distribution records based on shells alone are questionable.
Yasuhikotakia sidthimunki changed its generic name from Botia which is an Indian subcontinent taxon.
The specific epithet was incorrectly spelt in the 1996 Red List as verticornis according to the Odonata Specialist Group.
This species is described as Mulleria dalyi. Subba Rao (1989) refers to this as Acostaea (Pseudomulleria) dalyii.

Some authors treat the name Naesiotus as subgeneric status for this species, occasionally given as generic name. This treatment follows previously listing for Galapagos species, which followed Coppois (1985).
Synonym = Naesiotus sculpturatus.
Synonym = Naesiotus wolfi.

This species is one of many small Hydrobids that can be most easily be identified using anatomical characters of the reproductive system, however, these species are in urgent need of a review incorporating conchological, anatomical and molecular characters in order to understand the relationships with other taxa (Haase et al. 2007).

This species is one of many small spring-snails that can most easily be identified using anatomical characters of the reproductive system. The taxonomic status remains uncertain, as Benke et al (2011) considered that this may be a synonym of B. austriaca, but Schubert et al (2023) noted the presence of molecular material pertaining to this name in Barcode of Life project and it is still listed in MolluscaBase and Glöer (2022).

This species lies within one of the groups of Hydrobids that is currently being researched using molecular systematics to determine the taxonomic status and the species limits are doubted by some researchers. This species lies within one of the groups of spring-snails that is currently being researched using molecular systematics to determine the taxonomic status. Schubert et al. (2023) and Richling et al. (2017) have not as yet reviewed the material of this species, possibly due to sampling issues.

Whilst there are no new data for this species, there are tentative recent records, however, it is not confirmed whether it is the same species, so further research is required to determine the status of this species. Duda et al. (2023) continue to note some uncertainty about the species limits in this species group in Austria following the results noted by Richling et al. (2017) in Germany and differ from Glöer (2022) who consider it a valid species from a single location.

This taxon is regarded as a subspecies of splendens Harris, 1782 by several authors (Schmidt 1938, 1954; Buchholtz 1955; Rueppell et al. 2005; Kalkman 2006). Calopteryx syriaca is an endemic of the southern Levant (Schneider 1986, Dumont 1991) and overlaps in its northernmost range with Calopteryx hyalina Martin, 1909. In their area of sympatry both taxa exhibit 'character displacement', which is another reason to give them full specific rank (Schneider 1986).
This species is allopatric with Cambarus aculabrum and C. setosus. It was originally identified as C. setosus.
Following J. Marshall (Natural History Museum, London) Neduba longipennis was transferred to Aglaothorax longipennis.

An isolated population of putative hybrid (G. sila x G. wislizenii) origin has been reported in the Cuyama River drainage system southwest of the southern end of the San Joaquin Valley; apparently hybrids are no longer extant, as a result of habitat loss and local extirpation (Jennings 1995).

But the missing species need to be added to the Red List.

The number of salmonid taxa within some genera (e.g. Salmo, Salvelinus, and Coregonus) listed in various databases (e.g. Fishbase or Eschmeyer's Catalog of Fishes) has increased very rapidly over the past 15-20 years and IUCN policy has promoted conservation assessments of all such taxa. Some of these taxa are so-called “resurrected” valid names, or species-level assignments that have been given to taxa historically assigned to, or first described as, subspecies, or intra-subspecific “forms”. Following a recent trend in Eurasia, subspecific names have been purged, often without any biological investigation. Most involve new descriptions of populations from a single location, or a very restricted geographic area. For such new descriptions, in general, there is little or only a superficial effort at a meta-analysis across the genus, whether or not phenotypic or genetic data are included. Phenotypic diagnosis is very often made based on a suite of characters, with no single character being unique in the genus. Many of these taxa are not widely accepted across the international community, and are associated with very sparse data, and/or little or no data available in the English language. It has become an intractable problem for the IUCN Species Survival Commission (SSC) Salmon Specialist Group (SSG) to professionally comment on the Red List Assessments of all of these taxa. The SSG strongly recommends a rigorous evaluation of the validity of these species designations. An example in the UK revealed no robust support for taxonomic subdivision among UK European coregonids (Etheridge et al. 2012). This example, and many others like it throughout Eurasia, have real implications where the legal conservation status is reflected by use of binomial taxonomic names.
Prior to 2008, the generic name was incorrectly spelt on the IUCN Red List.

According to the New World Orthoptera Hypolestes clara should be transferred to Ortholestes clara. However, Paulson and von Ellenrieder (2005 submissions) note that this should be Hypolestes clara: originally described as Ortholestes clara. There is confusion about whether any Hispaniolan populations are this species, H. trinitatis of Cuba, or a third unnamed species (Dunkle 1991). Hypolestes occupies a somewhat isolated position in its family, and other family placement has been suggested, even a family of its own. It may be a sister group to most other modern damselflies (Rehn 2003).
Originally described under genus Eurypetalia; von Ellenrieder (in prep.) is currently reviewing taxonomy of South American austropetaliids.

This Pemba Island species is nearly identical to some species of the Malagasy radiation of Platycnemis, but distant from the Guineo-Congolian species that have tropical Asian affinities. The species is likely to be a long-distance wind-borne arrival from Madagascar, which survived due to favourable climatic conditions on Pemba (Dijkstra et al. 2007).

The original description contained the descriptions of two varieties, which occur on different islands. Solem (1979) considered these varieties as conspecific. By contrast, Criscione & Köhler (2015) demonstrated that both represent distinct species, which belong to different genera.

Martin (1909) described this taxon as a subspecies of Calopteryx splendens (Harris, 1782) and was followed by several authors (Morton 1924, Schmidt 1954, Buchholtz 1955). Bartenef (1912) regarded it as a subspecies of Calopteryx syriaca Rambur, 1842. Although already Dumont (1975, 1977) treated the taxon - with some doubts - as a valid species, it was Schneider (1986) who could show conclusively that C. hyalina merits full specific rank: C. hyalina and C. syriaca are sympatric but never syntopic. In addition to sympatry (upper Orontes, Nahr al-Kabir (S), Litani, Lake Hula) they exhibit reproductive character displacement. Where C. hyalina comes into contact with C. intermedia Selys, 1887 (lower Orontes, Nahr al-Kabir (S)) hybrids occur, and these were described by Buchholtz (1955) as Calopteryx splendens "pseudosyriaca".
Genus name should probably be Tomichia.

In 2004, Andrew Smith discovered a new species Poecilotheria hanumavilasumica from a sacred grove of the Hanumavilasum Temple on Rameshwaram Island. This species was earlier misidentified by Simon (1885), and Annandale (1907), as Poecilotheria fasciata from the Island and later Gravely (1915) misidentified this as Poecilotheria striata from Pamban on Rameshwaram Island.
May belong to the genus Thrigmopoeus. The description of the species is based only on the female.
