This species appeared on the IUCN Red List as Pachypanchax sp. nov. 'Anjingo' until 2016. This species was formally described as Pachypanchax sparksorum in Loiselle (2006). An updated Red List assessment has been created to reflect this change.

This species was submitted as Pachypanchax sp. nov. 'Menambery' and subsequently changed to Pachypanchax sp. nov. 'Varatraza' on recommendation of Paul Loiselle and published on The IUCN Red List in 2004. The species was subsequently formally described and named (Loiselle 2006).
Note the species name is maromandia not maromamdia (see FishBase).
Voucher specimens held at the American Museum of Natural History, University of Michigan Museum of Zoology, and Museum d'Histoire Naturelle, Geneva.
Voucher specimens held at the American Museum of Natural History.

Voucher specimens have been deposited with the American Museum of Natural History. Submitted as Paretroplus sp. nov. 'Mahajamba' and subsequently changed to Paretroplus sp. nov. 'Giant Lamena' on recommendation of Paul Loiselle.

This species appeared on the IUCN Red List as Pachypanchax sp. nov. 'Talio' until 2016. This species was formally described as Pachypanchax patriciae in Loiselle (2006). An updated Red List assessment has been created to reflect this change.

This species appeared on the IUCN Red List as Ptychochromis sp. nov. 'Joba mena' until 2016. This species was formally described as Ptychochromis insolitus in Stiassny and Sparks (2006). An updated Red List assessment has been created to reflect this change.
This species was included as Ptychochromoides sp. nov. in earlier versions of the IUCN Red List.

H. desfontainii is the type species of the genus Astatotilapia, the current concept of which is widely regarded as polyphyletic. It is therefore retained within the catch-all genus Haplochromis here, pending a conclusive taxonomic review of African haplochromine cichlids. A desert-dwelling haplochromine cichlid population reported to inhabit Oued Arak, a tributary of Oued Botha in Tamanrasset Province, southern Algeria, remains in need of identification and may represent an additional subpopulation.
Nothobranchius symoensi was established as a species by Wildekamp in 1978, and that status has since been accepted by all authors.
This species previously appeared on the IUCN Red List in the genus Tilapia but is now considered valid in the genus Coptodon (Eschmeyer et al. 2018).

Populations of this species originating from the upper Lufira system were first identified as N. brieni, which species was the first and, for 40 years, the only Nothobranchius species described from the Congo and upper Zambezi river drainages, and the populations were also referred to as N. aff. taeniopygus, based on the presence of broad light subdistal band in the caudal fin. Finally, Nothobranchius polli was established as a distinct species by Wildekamp in 1978, and that status has since been accepted by all authors.
This species previously appeared on the IUCN Red List in the genus Tilapia but is now considered valid in the genus Coptodon (Eschmeyer et al. 2018).
This species previously appeared on the IUCN Red List in the genus Tilapia but is now considered valid in the genus Coptodon (Eschmeyer et al. 2018).
This species previously appeared on the IUCN Red List in the genus Tilapia but is now considered valid in the genus Coelotilapia (Eschmeyer et al. 2018).
This species is a member of the genus Luciobarbus following Geiger et al. (2014).
Nothobranchius hengstleri was established as a species by Valdesalici in 2007, and that status has since been accepted by all authors.
Nothobranchius oestergaardi was established as a species by Valdesalici & Amato in 2011, and that status has since been accepted by all authors.
Previously assessed on the IUCN Red List under the temporary name Bedoti sp. nov. 'Vevembe'
This species was previously assessed on the Red List under the temporary name of Bedotia sp. nov. 'lazana'.

The eight species within the genus Pseudoplatystoma form a monophyletic group with two clades: one clade (P. metaense and P. tigrinum) is restricted to the Orinoco and Amazon basins; and the other clade (P. corruscans, P. fasciatum, P. magdaleniatum, P. orinocoense, P. punctifer, and P. reticulatum) is found in the Guyanas, Orinoco, Amazon and Paraná basins (Buitrago-Suárez and Burr 2007).

This is a restricted concept of Philautus everetti (as Rhacophorus everetti) following the split of the broader concept into this and Philautus macroscelis by Hertwig et al. (2012) and genetic evidence showed unequivocally that it belongs to Philautus.

Even though Congeria has long been thought to be a single pan-Dinaric species (Congeria kusceri Bole, 1962), phylogenetic analyses have recently clarified that it consists of three distinct species, namely Congeria kusceri Bole, 1962, Congeria mulaomerovici Morton and Bilandžija, 2013, and Congeria jalzici Morton and Bilandžija, 2013 (Bilandžija et al. 2013).

The genus Congeria is endemic to Europe and known from only 51 sites in the Dinaric karst (Bilandžija et al. 2022).

Even though Congeria has long been thought to be a single pan-Dinaric species (Congeria kusceri Bole, 1962), phylogenetic analyses clarified that it consists of three distinct species, namely Congeria kusceri Bole, 1962, Congeria mulaomerovici Morton and Bilandžija, 2013, and Congeria jalzici Morton and Bilandžija, 2013 (Bilandžija et al. 2013).

The genus Congeria is endemic to Europe and known from only 51 sites in the Dinaric karst (Bilandžija et al. 2022).

This taxon was regarded by MolluscaBase (April 2023) as interim unpublished as it failed to fulfil ICZN Article 8 regarding publishing species description in an electronically issued article (M. Lopes-Lima pers. comm. 2021), but the species was formally described by Morton and Bilandžija (2023).

There have been no recent taxonomic changes (cf. Iredale 1934, 1943; McMichael & Hiscock 1958; Smith 1992).

This species is related to Hyridella narracanensis, found at scattered localities in SE South Australia, coastal Victoria and northern Tasmania, but is distinguished by more extensive shell sculpturing (McMichael and Hiscock 1958). Playford and Walker (2008) speculated that H. glenelgensis and H. narracanensis (specifically, shells from nearby Eight Mile Creek, South Australia), could prove to be ecophenotypes of one species. Preliminary analysis of mitochondrial DNA in mantle-tissue snips from the two taxa showed 2% divergence in cytochrome c oxidase subunit I (COI), but this was considered inconclusive due to small sample sizes.
Further work is needed for a taxonomic review of the genus Evynnis (Iwatsuki et al. 2007).

authority: Nuñez, Veloso, Espejo, Veloso, Cortes & Araya, 2010

taxonomicNotes: This species was originally described as a subspecies Antipodogomphus neophytus hodgkini Watson, 1969.

Hypselobarbus micropogon was originally described as Barbus micropogon from Mysore, Karnataka, India (Cuvier and Valenciennes 1842). Barbus conirostris Günther 1868, Barbus gracilis Jerdon 1849 and Barbus mysorensis Jerdon 1849 have been considered as junior subjective synonyms of Hypselobarbus micropogon by most authors (e.g. Jayaram 1991; Menon 1999; Knight et al. 2013, 2016). Arunachalam et al. (2016) resurrected Barbus gracilis as Hypselobarbus gracilis and considered Barbus conirostris and Barbus mysorensis as its synonyms. However, the resurrection is not done properly and there are several lacunae with respect to the description, diagnosis and improper comparison with the congeners because of which the validity of H. gracilis is in question. This issue was also pointed out by Knight et al. (2016). As a result, Hypselobarbus gracilis, Barbus conirostris and Barbus mysorensis are retained as synonyms of H. micropogon.

Jerdon (1849) described Systomus assimilis from rivers of Canara, the erstwhile British Indian region, now comprising several districts in coastal Karnataka. The species was considered as a synonym of Dawkinsia filamentosa (Jayaram 1991, Menon 1999) until it was resurrected by Pethiyagoda and Kottelat (2005) who considered the species from Nethravati River, Karnataka, as conspecific to Jerdon's description. However, they also tentatively assigned and identified the populations from Chalakudy and Kallada River to D. assimilis and kept D. lepida under its synonymy. Katwate et al. (2020) designated a neotype for Dawkinsia assimilis from Nethravati River at Dharmasthala (12.966 N, 75.370 E), Karnataka and suggested that populations from Chalakudy comprise of two other species, D. lepida and D. austellus; and from Sowparnika and Sita rivers comprise the new species D. apsara.
Was under Iguanidae.

Records presently assigned to this species from Argentina are very unlikely to represent this species, as the Argentinian and Chilean populations are separated by mountains up to 5,000 m high, and the Argentinian records are themselves from valleys more than 400 km from one another (L. Avila pers. comm. 2016). Resolution of this issue is complicated as the few known Argentinian specimens are poorly-preserved (L. Avila pers. obs.). For the purposes of this assessment L. gravenhorstii is considered to be endemic to Chile.

This species was tentatively assigned to the Hypsiboas punctatus group in the original publication, diagnosed by its morphological, ecological and advertisement call features (Kok 2006); it was previously moved to the genus Boana by Dubois (2017), and has now been moved to Myersiohyla by Pinheiro et al. (2018).

No entry for this species in Eschmeyer 91998). The only entry for totae in Eschmeyer is Pygidium totae valid as Trichomycterus totae both from the same family, Trichomycteridae, as Rhizosomichthys. FishBase records R. totae as a synonym of T. totae based on Burgess (1989). Stiassny and Harrison (1999) indicate that this may just be a "fat form of Trichomycterus".
The family is undergoing extensive revision likely to be published in 2007. It is therefore uncertain to what entity the species name currently refers to.

The eight species within the genus Pseudoplatystoma form a monophyletic group with two clades: one clade (P. metaense and P. tigrinum) is restricted to the Orinoco and Amazon basins; and the other clade (P. corruscans, P. fasciatum, P. magdaleniatum, P. orinocoense, P. punctifer, and P. reticulatum) is found in the Guyanas, Orinoco, Amazon and Paraná basins (Buitrago-Suárez and Burr 2007).

This is a well defined species (Grubb 1982, 2005; Groves et al. 1995). The form sifanicus was included in M. moschiferus as a subspecies by Ellerman and Morrison-Scott (1951) and considered an independent species by Gao (1963). However, Groves et al. (1995) included it in M. chrysogaster as subspecies. The chrysogaster of Cai and Feng (1981) is subspecifically or specifically distinct, and the available name for this taxon may be leucogaster Hodgson, 1839 (Groves et al. 1995, Grubb 2005). Some Chinese authors continue to recognize M. sifanicus as a full species (e.g., Yang et al. 2003, Zhou et al. 2004).

Currently, two subspecies are recognized in China:

1) M. c. chrysogaster (Hodgson, 1839) in southeastern and southern Tibet;
2) M. c. sifanicus (Büchner, 1891) in Qinghai, Gansu, Ningxia, western Sichuan and northwestern Yunnan.

There does not appear to be documentation of a clear delineation between these two forms.

Moschus saturatus Hodgson, 1839 may be a prior name for this species. Gao (1985) treated fuscus as a subspecies of M. chrysogaster, i.e. M. chrysogaster fuscus. But Groves et al. (1995) considered it as valid species; Su et al. (2001) demonstrated its phylogenetic status as a valid species by analysis of Cytochrome b.

This taxon is sometimes treated as a Himalayan subspecies of Alpine Musk Deer M. chrysogaster (e.g. Grubb 1990), but it was separated by Groves and Grubb (1987) and by Groves et al. (1995) on the basis of different skull proportions.

This taxon was recently separated by Meijaard and Groves (2004) as a species distinct from Tragulus napu, in agreement with Sanborn (1952) and Rabor (1977). The population of Pulau Bangi (Malaysia), which lies mid-way between Balabac and the Bornean mainland, and which was almost certainly (and perhaps repeatedly) connected to both by former (late Pleistocene) land bridges (Heaney 1986), might belong to this species and requires further taxonomic studies.
This species was formerly treated as a subspecies of Moschus berezovskii and Moschus moschiferus, but is now regarded as a valid species (see Li et al. 1999, Su et al. 2001ab)

This species was originally described as a subspecies of Alpine Musk Deer M. chrysogaster. It is very similar to Himalayan Musk Deer M. leucogaster, but Groves et al. (1995) suggested that it might be a separate species, a course followed by Grubb (2005) and it is so treated here.

This species was synonymized with Brookesia minima by Raxworthy and Nussbaum (1995) but later resurrected (Schimmenti and Jesu 1996, Glaw et al. 1999). The taxonomy of the B. minima group is in need of revision and the distribution of all species needs to be re-evaluated following thorough morphometric and molecular studies.
Genetic data indicate that Eleutherodactylus principalis is nested within one of the four lineages of E. auriculatus and a thorough taxonomic revision of this group is needed (Rodríguez et al. 2010).
This is a restricted concept of Eleutherodactylus zugi following the split of the broader concept into this and E. erythroproctus (Hedges et al. 2008).

authority: Acevedo, Wake, Márquez, Silva, Franco & Amézquita, 2013

taxonomicNotes: This species was synonymized with Pelophylax nigromaculatus by Fei, Ye and Huang (1990), and this synonymy was accepted by Dubois (1992) and Dubois and Ohler (1996). However, Zhao and Adler (1993) still consider this to be a valid species and hence it is included here pending a definitive decision on its taxonomic status.
Amolops daiyunensis was considered a synonym of A. hongkongensis by Yang (1991), but we follow Fei et al. (1999), Jin et al. (2005) and Ngo et al. (2006) in treating it as a separate species.
This is a restricted concept of this species which excludes subpopulations which are now assigned to other segregate species in the Amolops monticola group (Patel et al. 2021).
We will not be following the taxonomy proposed by Lyu et al. (2021), and will retain this species in Megophrys until there is more stability for the genus.
Inger et al. (1990) considered Oreolalax puxiongensis to be synonymous with O. schmidti.
Zhao and Adler (1993) considered this species to be synonymous with Oreolalax major but did not give any reason.
Zhao and Adler (1993) regarded this species to be synonymous with Scutiger ruginosus (= S. mammatus) without discussion.

This is a restricted concept of this species following the split of the broader concept into this, Pachytriton granulosus and Pachytriton inexpectatus (Nishikawa et al. 2011). Paramesotriton ermizhaoi has also been synonymised with this species (Nishikawa et al. 2011).
We consider Cynops chenggongensis to be separate from C. cyanurus, following Zhao and Adler (1993) and Fei et al. (1999).
Inger et al. (1990) regarded this taxon as a junior synonym of Batrachuperus pinchonii. It was removed from the synonymy of B. pinchonii by Ye et al. (1993).
This species was first described by Tian, Gu, Sun and Li (1998), who are the valid authors of this species. It was redescribed by Tian, Li and Gu (2006).
This species has been moved from Rhacophorus to Zhangixalus (Jiang et al. 2019).
This species has been moved from Rhacophorus to Zhangixalus (Jiang et al. 2019).

authority: Taylor, Stoffberg, Monadjem, Schoeman, Bayliss & Cotterill, 2012
taxonomicNotes: This species formerly was included in Rhinolophus hildebrandtii, but acoustic, biogeographical, morphological and molecular data show it to be a distinct species (Taylor et al. 2012).

No taxonomic problems with the species. The species is of special interest as it is the only one of the genus Eostemmiulus, itself phyletically very isolated in the Southeast Asian fauna (Golovatch et al. 2010).

This species was originally identified from Daegu, Republic of Korea, under the name of Gomphus coreanus Doi & Okumura, 1937. It was later recombined as Anisogomphus coreanus Doi & Okumura, 1943 and again recombined as Asiagomphus coreanus Asahina, 1985 (Bae and Lee 2012).

authority: (Monadjem, L.R. Richards, P.J. Taylor & S. Stoffberg, 2013)
taxonomicNotes: This species is genetically and morphologically distinct from the closely related Neoromicia brunnea (Monadjem et al. 2013).

The evolutionary phylogeny of the Armadillidae requires further study. As presently defined, the genus Pseudolaureola Kwon, Ferrara & Taiti is comprised of just four species with a remarkably disjunct distribution across humid forests of the tropical Afro-Australasian zone; In addition to the Atlantic outpost occupied by P. atlantica, the other taxa are found in Madagascar, New Caledonia and western Australia (Schmalfuss, 2003). Related genera (e.g. Laureola Barnard) have a predominantly southern African distribution but extend as far as south-east Asia.

This rare moth was only discovered in 2009 (Arenberger et al., 2012). Previously, only a single Agdistis species was known from St Helena (the locally much more widespread endemic, A. sanctaehelenae Wollaston), although some taxonomic confusion remains over the genus and it is possible that further St Helena species await recognition. Agdistis marionae is superficially close to its more common sister taxon and the adults are distinguished on relatively subtle morphological characters, but the larvae presumably display strong physical and behavioural differences. Agdistis sanctaehelenae occurs in many habitats and is thus assumed to be less host-specific.
This form might be confused with Gephyromantis thelenae, G. blanci and G. leucocephalus (F. Glaw and M. Vences pers. comm.).
This species occurs in sympatry with Gephyromantis eiselti from which it is distinguished mainly by its call, so museum records might confuse these two species (F. Glaw pers. comm. November 2015).
This species' taxonomy is unclear.
Sherwood et al. (2024) placed Theridion sciaphilum Benoit, 1977 and Theridula huberti Benoit, 1977 in the newly described genus Helenidion.
Sherwood et al. (2024) placed Theridion solium Benoit, 1977 in the newly described genus Trust.
Sherwood et al. (2024) placed Theridula huberti Benoit, 1977 and Theridion sciaphilum Benoit, 1977 in the newly described genus Helenidion.
Trioza barrettae has recently been described (Taylor and Moir 2014) and was previously known as Trioza sp. 03 (Moir et al. 2012a).
Dawson (1984) and most later authors consider Oostethus to be a subgenus of Microphis based on morphology and meristics. Kottelat (2013) resurrected Oostethus with no annotation or justification.

authority: Ndriantsoa, Riemann, Vences, Klages, Raminosoa, Rödel & Glos, 2013

taxonomicNotes: This species was recently described as distinct from the very similar E. evelynae and E. genie based on results from DNA barcoding (Victor 2014).
This species was recently described based on results from DNA barcoding (Victor 2014) and resembles T. multifasciatus.

This species was recently described based on results from DNA barcoding. It had been considered to be conspecific with E. horsti and also closely resembles E. louisae (a sympatric species) in appearance (Victor 2014).
There is uncertainty about the taxonomy of this species, as it is similar morphologically to Pseudodiploexochus leleupi.
This species shares characteristics with Pseudodiploexochus tabularis and has also similar morphology to P. insularis.
There is a possibility that this species may be the same as Pseudodiploexochus tabularis. Some taxonomic investigation is required for this species.

authority: Penny, Andreone, Crottini, Holderied, Rakotizafy, Schwitzer & Rosa, 2014

taxonomicNotes: Carcharhinus dussumieri is part of the Carcharhinus seali-dussumieri group that has been revealed to consist of five species: C. dussumieri and C. sealei have recently been re-described, C. coatesi and C. tjutjot have been resurrected as valid species, and a new species, C. humani has been described (White 2012, White and Weigmann 2014).

Recent changes to the systematics of Rhinobatos have elevated the subgenus Glaucostegus to full generic status and placed this genus into a family of its own: Glaucostegidae (Compagno 2005, Last et al. 2016a).

Glaucostegus microphthalamus is currently considered a synonym of G. typus (W.T. White and D.A. Ebert pers. comm. 2016).

The Dwarf Sawfish (Pristis clavata) is similar to the Smalltooth Sawfish (Pristis pectinata), with which it may have been previously confused, but it has fewer rostral teeth than the Smalltooth Sawfish (Last and Stevens 2009).

Rhynchobatus australiae is part of the Indo-West Pacific Rhynchobatus djiddensis species-complex. The name R. djiddensis has previously been used widely, leading to poorly-defined taxonomy and distributions for members of the complex (in particular, R. australiae, R. djiddensis, and R. laevis). Further investigation into the taxonomy of 'whitespotted wedgefish' is required, and additional species are likely to be described in the future.

Revision of Squalus in the Indo-Australasian region resulted in the resurrection of S. montalbani (Philippines, Indonesia, Australia) and S. griffini (New Zealand) and the description of S. chloroculus Last, White & Motomura, 2007. These species were previously considered to be conspecific with S. mitsukurii, which this work has revealed, does not occur in Australasian waters. Continued investigation of Squalus mitsukurii from around the world is resulting in more taxa being recognized.
Pseudococcus markharveyi has recently been described (Gullan et al. 2013) and has been previously referred to as Pseudococcus sp. 15 in Moir et al. (2012).
Acizzia mccarthyi has recently been described (Taylor & Moir, 2014). No other names exist for the species and the taxonomy has not been questioned.
Was under Iguanidae.
Was under Iguanidae.
Was under Iguanidae.

Castro and Tobar-González (2014) suggested there may be differences between high-elevation and low-elevation populations of this species on Cantillana, but had access only to lower-elevation specimens that agree with their recently-collected specimen.

Liolaemus stolzmanni is a member of a poorly-characterized radiation of species whose systematics have long been complicated by restricted access to the remote areas where these lizards occur (Langstroth 2011). Confusion has surrounded the correct attribution of records from northern Chile and adjacent regions of Argentina, Bolivia and Peru) to either Liolaemus stolzmanni or Liolaemus reichei, the oldest available names for taxa from this region, due to inadequacies in or misunderstandings of the original descriptions by subsequent authors (Langstroth 2011). This situation was further complicated by the descriptions of multiple additional taxa from Chile, Peru and Bolivia, with several specimens previously reported as L. reichei being properly attributable to L. poconchilensis and Bolivian material described as L. stolzmanni having been referred to L. pachecoi (Langstroth 2011). Examining photographs of the type series of L. stolzmanni, Langstroth (2011) synonymized L. reichei (as now understood, a species restricted to northern Chile) with this older name and proposed emending the type locality from "Hoch-Peru" to "Deserts of Iquique, Tarapaca Region, Chile", on the basis that this species is not known to occur anywhere within the territory of modern Peru and indeed appears to be endemic to this area of northern Chile.
Puntius denisonii was described by Day (1865) from Mundakkayam, Kerala, southern India.

Menon, Rema and Vishwanath (2000) described this species (as Puntius manipurensis) from Loktak Lake, Manipur, India. This species considered as valid by Kullander and Fang (2005), Vishwanath and Laishram (2004), Kullander and Britz (2008).

Jerdon described this species from Srirangapattana in 1849 as Systomus arulius. Subsequently, the species was reported from Kottayam, Thenmalai, Kulathupuzha and Periyar Lake (Menon 1999). However, a new species, Dawkinsia exclamatio was later described from Thenmalai and Kulathupuzha as a different species (Pethiyagoda and Kottelat 2005). But Knight et al. (2011) speculates that D. exclamatio are possible hybrids between D. arulius with D. filamentosa. Since there are no recent records of D. arulius from either Kallada or Periyar there is a need to check for existing voucher specimens if any, to validate the historic occurrence (and their later extirpation) of the species in this region.
Sahyadria chalakkudiensis was described (as Puntius chalakkudiensis) by Menon, Remadevi and Thobias (1999) from the Chalakudy River, Kerala, India.
This species most probably represents a senior synonym of Demirsoyus salmani Sirin and Ciplak, 2004 in Yalim et al. (2004) (F. Willemse pers. comm. 2005).

Puntius ophicephalus was described by Raj (1941) from Kallar, a tributary of Pampa River, a few miles south of Periyar Lake, Kerala, Travancore, India. This species was re-discovered after half a century at Mlappara inside the Periyar Tiger Reserve (Zacharias et al 1996). This fish was placed in the genus Eechathalakenda by Menon (1999).
This species is considered to possibly represent a local form of C. dorsalis by Willemse (1984). Taxonomic research is necessary to clarify the status of this species.

The large gap between the northern and southern subpopulations and the high degree of long-term isolation in different mountain ranges suggest a considerable infraspecific variability which requires detailed taxonomic research.
This species was moved to Pethia following Pethiyagoda et al. (2012). Pethia pookodensis was first described by Mercy and Jacob (2007) from Pookode Lake, Wayanad, Kerala, India.
Pethia sharmai was described (as Puntius sharmai)by Menon and Rema Devi (1993) from Mogappair, West Annanagar, Madras, Tamil Nadu, India. The species was moved to Pethia following Knight (2013).

This species was transferred to Dawkinsia following Pethiyagoda et al. (2012). Dawkinsia tambraparniei was described as Puntius arulius tambraparniei from Tambraparini River, Tamil Nadu by Silas (1954). The species status was validated by Pethiyagoda and Kottelat (2005) in their revision of the Puntius filamentosus complex.

The Horrid Ground-weaver (Nothophantes horridus Merrett & Stevens, 1995) is a recently described species of small linyphiid spider (total body length of just 2.5 mm). It is also the only known representative of the genus.

Glyptothorax naziri which was considered a synonym of G. kashmirensis by Javed et al. (2013) is now considered valid and the species has been assessed separately. Therefore the current assessment of G. kashmirensis does not include information on G. naziri
Brown (1965) described two subspecies within Platymantis parkeri: P. p. parkeri from Bougainville; and P. p. bukanensis from Buka.

The subspecies Alsophis antiguae sajdaki is thought by some to represent a full species (Hedges et al. 2009), which would render A. antiguae extinct. There is however evidence against this proposition from a study of more than 400 live specimens from Great Bird Island which concluded that, apart from some geographical variation in ventral counts, the proposed diagnostic characters represented sexual dimorphism. On this basis this account considers that A. antiguae is still extant, represented by the surviving form A. a. sadjaki (J. Daltry pers. comm. 2010, 2015).

There are currently two recognized subspecies:
A. hyporissor hyporissor
A. hyporissor leberi.

This species has two subspecies: A. sanctonum sanctonum Barbour, 1915 and A. s. danforthi Cochran, 1938. Zaher et al. (2009) recognized A. danforthi as a distinct, separate species, but this was a broad molecular treatment of advanced snakes with an emphasis on South America and did not provide detailed justification for this assignment. The subspecific arrangement has been retained by subsequent authors (Wallach et al. 2014) and is preferred pending a phenotypic and genetic review of snakes from Iles des Saintes (R. Gomes pers. comm. 2019).

Two subspecies are recognized:
S. torrei spielmani Grant, 1958
S. torrei torrei Barbour, 1914.

Helgen et al. (2009) suggested that two different species were previously considered as 'Mountain Coati' N. olivacea; one endemic to Colombia and Ecuador, N. olivacea, and one restricted to Venezuela, N. meridensis.
Taxonomic treatment follows Solem (1983)

Mivart (1890) distinguished two species of Cuon, the northern Dhole (C. alpinus) and the southern Dhole (C. javanicus), based on differences in body size and the second upper and second lower molars. Dholes were later recognized as a single species (Ellerman and Morrison-Scott 1966), but separated into as many as 11 subspecies based on differences in coat length and colour (Durbin et al. 2004). The validity of many of these subspecies is doubtful, and a genetics study found no clear subspecies distinctions (Iyengar et al. 2005). However, samples used by Iyengar et al. (2005) came exclusively from the southern half of the Dhole distribution, so the genetic distinctiveness between northern and southern Dholes remains unknown. Examination of the auditory bulla showed at least one northern subspecies, from the Tian Shan Mountains, to be morphologically distinct from other subspecies (Ivanoff 2007). Further research is needed to clarify the morphological and genetic differences between northern and southern dholes, especially because conservation efforts would need to employ different strategies for the two groups, as they occupy vastly different habitats and prey on different species.

The putative northern-Dhole group historically occurred throughout East Asia to as far south as the Himalayan Mountains and the Yangtze River, and would include the following five subspecies described by Durbin et al. (2004): C. a. alpinus, C. a. primaevus, C. a. laniger, C. a. hesperius, and C. a. fumosus. The putative southern-dhole group would include the remaining six subspecies described by Durbin et al. (2004): C. a. lepturus, C. a. dukhumensis, C. a. adjustus, C. a. infuscus, C. a. sumatrensis, and C. a. javanicus. Within the southern-Dhole group, Iyengar et al. (2005) found two major phylogeographic genetic groupings on the mainland, and stated that the subspecific status of Dholes on Sumatra and Java was unclear and requires further study.
This species was originally described from pre-Hispanic and subfossil remains. It was subsequently discovered alive (Wetzel et al. 1975; Wetzel 1977a, b, 1981).

Gittenberger (1985) after anatomical examination of two specimens of Xeroplexa setubalensis (type species of Xeroplexa, Monterosato1892) from the locus typicus noted that it corresponded to Candidula (Kobelt 1871), so Xeroplexa must be considered a junior synonym of Candidula. Molecular studies carried out by Chueca et al. (2018) confirm the validity of Candidula setubalensis, assigned to the genus Candidula, and that it constitutes a monophyletic clade, being the sister group of the rest of the Candidula species belonging to the C. intersecta complex.

Bawean Deer has sometimes been included in Hog Deer Axis porcinus (Haltenorth 1963), but is better considered a full species (Groves and Grubb 1987, Grubb 2005). With Hog Deer it was often placed in the genus Cervus (e.g. Chasen 1940, van Bemmel 1944), until Groves and Grubb (1987) strongly argued for placement in Axis. The most recent analyses indicate that these two species, together with calamianensis, constitute a genus distinct from Axis, Hyelaphus (Meijaard and Groves 2004, Pitra et al. 2004). The species name is sometimes mis-spelled kuhli, but the correct original spelling, which must be used today, is kuhlii.
Gracixalus waza has been synonymised with this species (Nguyen et al. 2020).

authority: (Poyarkov, Vassilieva, Orlov, Galoyan, Tran, Le, Kretova & Geissler, 2014)

taxonomicNotes: This species was split from the broader concept of Leptopelis modestus (Portillo and Greenbaum 2014). It is part of the Leptopelis modestus complex, together with a currently unnamed species, the sister taxon of Leptopelis modestus sensu stricto, according to the original publication.
This species was split from the higher elevations of Raorchestes tinniens (S.R. Chandramouli and S.R. Ganesh pers comm. October 2020).

Chaitanya et al. (2018) assigned to Micrixalus cf. adonis specimens found in Meghamalai Wildlife Sanctuary, although precise recording sites were not provided and they are not included in the distribution map or extent of occurrence (EOO) until further taxonomic work has been carried out.

This is a restricted concept of this species following the split of the broader concept into this and Micrixalus sali (Biju et al. 2014). According to Biju et al. (2014), subpopulations from Siruvani and surrounding regions now belong to M. thampii.
This is a split from the broader concept of Micrixalus nudis (Biju et al. 2014).
This is a split from the broader concept of Micrixalus saxicola (Biju et al. 2014).
This is a restricted concept of this species following the split into this and Micrixalus herrei (Biju et al. 2014).
This species has been removed from the synonymy of Micrixalus fuscus (Biju et al. 2014).
The status of the subspecies Asiotmethis t. steppensis and A. t. flavipes needs to be studied.
A recent study by López et al. (2013) showed that the former records from North Lanzarote and Montaña Clara belong to a different species, Purpuraria magna.

A female record from Troglav Mountain is quite similar to what is regarded as R. buchichii in the literature. However, the true identity is uncertain as far as the subpopulation from Troglav and the subpopulations from islands are not compared morphologically on both sexes and bioacoustically, both on song characters and stridulatory area morphology.
Taxonomic limits of Umma and Sapho must be reassessed, but this species is closer to Sapho (Dijkstra, unpubl.).

Pinhey (1967) regarded C. hasta Pinhey, 1960 as a subspecies of C. molindica Fraser, 1948. Dijkstra (2007) considers C. hasta as a good species and as closely related to C. tenuis Longfield, 1936, of which it might even be a synonym.

Wallach et al. (2014) follow Henderson and Powell (2009) in recognizing Boa orophias and B. nebulosa as full species, however, some other authors (e.g. Uetz and Hallerman 2015) still treat both as subspecies of Boa constrictor.

Reynolds (2016) summarizes evidence from mitochondrial DNA and biogeography that Anolis ernestwilliamsi represents a very recent adaptation from the widespread Anolis cristatellus found on neighbouring islands. He suggests, based on evidence that the species exhibits a low or nonexistent level of ecological separation from a nearby population of A. cristatellus and that observed morphological differences are likely to reflect a combination of genetic drift and very recent adaptations to a non-natural substrate, that the Carrot Rock population is unlikely to be sufficiently divergent to justify continued recognition as a distinct species (Reynolds 2016 and refs therein).
This species was changed from the genus Steropleurus Bolívar, 1878 to the new genus Conacinotus by Barat (2012).
Ephippigerida marceti is probably a synonym of E. asella or of E. areolaria (Barat 2012, Olmo 2006, Poniatowski et al. 2010)
The species was moved from the genus Ephippigerida Bolívar, 1903 by Barat (2012),
Taxonomy is clarified by Dijkstra (2005).
A-group. Pseudagrion is likely to be split into two genera at some point. Until this taxonomic split occurs, the candidates for these two groups are highlighted by "group A" and "group B".

This species has been treated as a subspecies of Leiocephalus carinatus, but is treated as a full species in recent literature (McCranie 2011, 2015, 2018; Hedges et al. 2019). McCranie (2018) notes that the species exhibits diagnostic features which do not overlap with other members of the L. carinatus group, supporting its recognition as a full species.

Hecht (1951) treated Aristelliger nelsoni as a subspecies of A. praesignis in a distribution map of the species' range but provided no supporting justification. This scheme has nonetheless been widely followed since, although distinct morphological differences between A. nelsoni and the nominate form were highlighted by Bauer and Russell (1993). McCranie (2018) corroborated this finding, and noted that the deep sea separating the Swan Islands from the Cayman Islands and Jamaica further supports the recognition of the two taxa as distinct species. This author recommended treating A. nelsoni as a full species pending molecular research to clarify its status, a recommendation that had previously been followed by Powell and Henderson (2012). On this basis, and noting that the proposed subspecific status has never been adequately justified, this account follows McCranie (2015, 2018) in treating A. nelsoni as a full species.

Records from Hellshire have been sequenced and identified as a separate, undescribed species (S.B. Hedges, unpubl. data), confirming that Tropidophis stullae is known only from Portland Ridge and likely to be endemic to this area.

There are tadpoles further north that could be Telmatobufo bullocki or Telmatobufo ignotus (C. Castro, F. Rabanal, J.C. Ortiz, A. Charrier pers. comm. July 2015). These samples are not included in this assessment until such time as their identities can be verified.

This species was under the generic name Amietophrynus but is now treated under Sclerophrys (Frost 2025). Although the sister taxon (Eastern Leopard Toad, Sclerophrys pardalis) is known from Plettenberg Bay eastwards, there have always been records of toads between the two distributions (Stillbaai and Wilderness), but these have yet to be ascribed to one or the other taxon. These could be part of the distribution of a single species (Sclerophrys pardalis sensu lato) or translocations of one or the other. Genetic studies are required for these subpopulations. There are reports of this species occurring as far east as Agulhas National Park, with the sister taxon Sclerophrys pardalis reportedly occurring as far west as Sedgefield. Additionally, there is an unconfirmed but newly discovered breeding site far to the west at Stillbaai (https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/178736533). Moreover, as the two species are known to be closely related (for mtDNA: Cunningham and Cherry 2000, Eick et al 2001), the status of each subpopulation and the breeding sites between these need to be resolved.
This species is closely related to Afrixalus spinifrons (Cope 1862).

There are serious taxonomic problems with the genus Arthroleptis through much of Africa. In many cases, the available names can be referred only to museum specimens, not to animals in the field. This is because the identification of these species frequently depends more on their vocalizations than their morphology.

Known as Tympanoctomys loschalchalerosorum (Dunnum 2015).
This is a restricted concept of Crinia riparia following the split of the broader concept into this and Crinia flindersensis (Donnellan et al. 2012).

We depart from the taxonomy of Pelodryadidae proposed by Dubois and Frétey (2016) and Duellman et al. (2016) and retain the composition of Litoria, Nyctimystes and Cyclorana as previously recognised. This taxonomy is currently used in all Australian lists (e.g., ASH list, State and Federal government lists) and field guides (e.g., Hoskin et al. 2015, Cogger 2018, Clulow and Swan 2018).
This species was reassigned from Geocrinia to Anstisia (Webster and Bool 2022).
This is a restricted concept of this species following the split of the broader concept into this and Litoria watsoni (Mahony et al. 2020).

We depart from the taxonomy of Pelodryadidae proposed by Dubois and Frétey (2016) and Duellman et al. (2016) and retain the composition of Litoria, Nyctimystes and Cyclorana as previously recognised. This taxonomy is currently used in all Australian lists (e.g., ASH list, State and Federal government lists) and field guides (e.g., Hoskin et al. 2015, Cogger 2018, Clulow and Swan 2018).

There is currently uncertainty regarding the taxonomic status of this species. It occurs in two disjunct isolates separated by 500 km, but the most recent taxonomic study by Thomson et al. (1996) suggests that they represent one species. More recently the genetic distinctness of this species from closely related Litoria species, in particular L. raniformis, is also being questioned and additional taxonomic research is required (Voros et al. 2010 in Threatened Species Scientific Committee 2019). For the purposes of this assessment, the species is considered to be valid and contain a northern and southern subpopulation, pending further taxonomic resolution.
The northern and southern populations could comprise different species (Penman et al. 2004, Hunter et al. 2018), but this requires further taxonomic research.

Zozaya and Hoskin (2015) discuss the taxonomic status of this species relative to Pseudophryne major, and recommend a phylogenetic analysis be done to resolve whether or not it may be a synonym of this wide ranging species.

We depart from the taxonomy of Pelodryadidae proposed by Dubois and Frétey (2016) and Duellman et al. (2016) and retain the composition of Litoria, Nyctimystes and Cyclorana as previously recognised. This taxonomy is currently used in all Australian lists (e.g., ASH list, State and Federal government lists) and field guides (e.g., Hoskin et al. 2015, Cogger 2018, Clulow and Swan 2018).

We depart from the taxonomy of Pelodryadidae proposed by Dubois and Frétey (2016) and Duellman et al. (2016) and retain the composition of Litoria, Nyctimystes and Cyclorana as previously recognised. This taxonomy is currently used in all Australian lists (e.g., ASH list, State and Federal government lists) and field guides (e.g., Hoskin et al. 2015, Cogger 2018, Clulow and Swan 2018).

Previously recognised as an outlying population of frogs in the Litoria phyllochroa group – specifically L. pearsoniana and L. barringtonensis (Hoskin et al. 2013).

We depart from the taxonomy of Pelodryadidae proposed by Dubois and Frétey (2016) and Duellman et al. (2016) and retain the composition of Litoria, Nyctimystes and Cyclorana as previously recognised. This taxonomy is currently used in all Australian lists (e.g., ASH list, State and Federal government lists) and field guides (e.g., Hoskin et al. 2015, Cogger 2018, Clulow and Swan 2018).

Rulyrana saxiscandens, Cochranella croceopodes and Rulyrana tangarana are considered to be synonymous based on available morphological, bioacoustic, and phylogenetic evidence (Twomey et al. 2014). Under the Principle of the First Reviser of the ICZN (article 24.2.2) precedence is given to saxiscandens, rendering R. tangarana and C. croceopodes junior synonyms of R. saxiscandens.

Geiger and Owen (2012) observe that owing to long-term consolidation of this species with H. madaka (previously referred to as ‘Giant Abalone’ but since accepted as a separate species), there are no medium to long term unambiguous data on H. gigantea that are known and that "segregation of the data among those two taxa is impossible after the fact". The common-name species of abalone, Megai-awabi, was previously described as H. sieboldii, but Habe (1983) confirmed that the type specimens of H. gigantea should instead be attributed to Megai-awabi and its name changed to H. gigantea, while that of Madaka-awabi changed to H. madaka from H. gigantea. Haliotis gigantea shares a sympatric distribution with two other abalone, H. madaka and H. d. discus. With regard to the relationship of these three species: “population genomic analyses revealed that although the three species are genetically distinct, there is evidence for historical and ongoing gene flow among these species” (Hirase et al. 2021).

Haliotis madaka was, until recently, considered to be a synonym of H. gigantea, and most ecological and biological data of both species are therefore confused, restricting accurate assessment of bathymetry, habitat, diet etc from the literature (Geiger and Owen 2012).

The scientific name for Madaka-awabi was previously H. gigantea, but Habe (1983) confirmed that the type specimens of H. gigantea were Megai-awabi (H. sieboldii at that time). Therefore, the scientific name of Madaka-awabi changed to H. madaka from H. gigantea, and that of Megai-awabi changed to H. gigantea from H. sieboldii (Habe 1983).  H. madaka shares sympatric distribution with two other abalone, H. gigantea and H. d. discus. In terms of the relationship of these three species: “population genomic analyses revealed that although the three species are genetically distinct, there is evidence for historical and ongoing gene flow among these species” (Hirase et al. 2021).
This is a restricted concept of Batrachoseps relictus following the split of the broader concept into this and B. altasierrae (Jockusch et al. 2012).

Specimens from the Upper Kern River were originally assigned to Batrachoseps simatus by Brame and Murray (1968), who noted that there were morphological differences. Jockusch and Wake (2002) and Jennings (2004) treated these specimens as an undescribed lineage, until Jockusch et al. (2012) described them as B. bramei. Additionally, subpopulations from Breckenridge Mountain that were tentatively assigned to this species by Stebbins (1985) are now recognized as B. relictus (Jockusch et al. 2012).

Crossodactylus boulengeri was removed from the synonymy of this species and C. werneri was split from this species by Pimenta et al. (2014). A single specimen from 1961 in the Argentinean province of Misiones previously assigned to this species is now recognised as Crossodactylus schmidti (Pimenta et al. 2014, Ferraro et al. 2018). Records from Itatiaia in Pimenta et al. (2014) are now treated as belonging to C. werneri (Brazilian Red List Assessment Workshop 2021).

The Kyushu populations formerly assigned to this species have now been described as new species; Hynobius amakusaensis, Hynobius osumiensis and Hynobius shinichisatoi by Nishikawa and Matsui 2014. Hynobius hirosei, from Shikoku, was removed from the synonymy of this species by Nishikawa et al. 2007.

This taxon is a complex currently under revision. Records of this species from Anjanaharibe and Tsaratanana (Raxworthy et al. 1998), Andringitra (Raxworthy and Nussbaum 1996), and Ambolokopatrika (Andreone et al. 2000) are highly likely to refer to other species, e.g. R. ornata, R. tany, R. vaventy and other undescribed species (Scherz et al. 2014). In the framework of ongoing revision of the Rhombophryne serratopalpebrosa (Scherz et al. 2014, 2015), all available specimens of this complex in European and Malagasy collections have been examined and found only a single specimen was found to correspond to the R. serratopalpebrosa holotype and it is thus considered to be restricted to Marojejy. For this reason, we exclude all uncertain records of this species (F. Glaw and M.D. Scherz pers. comm. November 2015). Records from further south in Madagascar might refer to Plethodontohyla coronata.
Morgan (1997) named the species "in respect of Ellen Clark’s pioneering parastacid studies".

authority: Veloso, Sallaberry A., Navarro, Iturra-C., Valencia, Penna & Díaz, 1982

taxonomicNotes: There are tadpoles further north that could be Telmatobufo bullocki or Telmatobufo ignotus (C. Castro, F. Rabanal, J.C. Ortiz, A. Charrier pers. comm. July 2015). These samples are not included in this assessment until such time as their identities can be verified.

Its tadpole was briefly described as belonging to Telmatobius montanus (Cei and Roig 1965), but was later re-assigned as this species (Cei 1976).

There may be an additional subpopulation of this species occurring 60 km south of the Pehuenche Valley, Cajón de Plaza in Chile (C. Correa pers. comm. December 2017). Mitochondrial sequences identify these individuals as belonging to both this species and Alsodes hugoi, despite resembling this species (Correa et al. 2018). It may be a hybrid population, however further taxonomic work is required to confirm its identity (C. Correa pers. comm. December 2017). For the purposes of this assessment, it has not been included within this species' distribution map.

It is possible that Argentinean subpopulations of Alsodes from the forests of Neuquén and Río Negro Provinces might belong to A. verrucosus, although until this can be verified these records have not been included in attempts to map its range.

Only the biological subpopulation at the type locality is known for certain as Eupsophus contulmoensis (C. Castro and F. Rabanal pers. comm. 2015). Biological subpopulations outside of the type locality have varying morphs and their identity is unclear. However, for the purposes of this assessment and until such time as these subpopulations' identities are resolved, they are considered under the nominal form of Eupsophus contulmoensis.

It was removed from the synonymy of Eupsophus roseus by Suárez-Villota et al. (2018), where it had been placed by Correa-Quezada et al. (2017).
Removed from the synonymy of Eupsophus roseus by Suárez-Villota et al. (2018), where it had been placed by Correa-Quezada et al. (2017).

Diagnosis of Telmatobius chusmisensis is based on external morphology and osteology of adults, external morphology of tadpoles, karyotype, chromosomal C-banded patterns and Ag-NOR (nucleolar organizer region) position (Formas et al. 2006).
This species was moved into Neolissochilus following Pethiyagoda et al. (2012). The species was described by Day (1877) from Bhavani river at the base of Nilgiri hills.

The species was identified by Ng (2002) after it was realized that the type series of crabs described as Karstama emdi (Ng and Whitten 1995) contained this additional species. The species was reassigned from Sesarmoides to Karstama by Davie and Ng (2007).

The species was redefined by Ng (2002) when a second species, K. balicum, was recognized from within the original type material, and the species was reassigned from genus Sesarmoides to Karstama by Davie and Ng (2007).
Adenomus dasi has been synonymized under this species by Meegaskumbura et al. (2015).

The taxonomy of untufted capuchin monkeys (Cebus spp.) has recently been debated and although there is a better understanding of its phylogeny, some phylogenetic and biogeographical aspects of this genus remain unresolved (Ruiz-Garcia et al. 2010; Boubli et al. 2012; Lynch Alfaro et al. 2012). The Santa Marta white-fronted capuchin (Cebus malitiosus) comprises of all populations of untufted capuchins that occur in the north-western region of the Sierra Nevada de San Marta, in northern Colombia (Defler 2003, 2004). Both Hershkovitz (1949) and Hernadez-Camacho and Cooper (1976) consider the Santa Marta white-fronted capuchin a subspecies of C. albifrons, while Groves (2001) considers it as one of the six forms of varied white-fronted capuchins (Cebus albifrons versicolor) in northern Colombia. The only molecular study that has included a single sample of capuchin monkeys from the northern region of Sierra Nevada de Santa Marta found that C. malitiosus had a completely different haplotype compared to those from other northern Colombian populations (C. a. versicolor, C. a. pleei and C. a. cesarae) and actually nested in a basal branch with capuchin monkeys from the Colombian and Peruvian Amazon (Ruiz-García et al. 2010). The validity of C. malitiosus as a separate species on the northwestern foothills of the Sierra Nevada de Santa Marta still needs further validation.

The taxonomy of gracile capuchin monkeys (Cebus spp.) continues to be debated and despite increasing understanding of its phylogeny, many phylogenetic and biogeographical aspects of this genus remain unresolved (Ruiz-Garcia et al. 2010, Boubli et al. 2012, Lynch Alfaro et al. 2012, Lima et al. 2017). Genetic analyses by Boubli et al. (2012) have indicated that the following forms of white-fronted capuchins that were recognized as subspecies of Cebus albifrons by Hershkovitz (1949) should be provisionally considered distinct species: albifrons, yuracus, unicolor, cuscinus, cesarae, malitiosus, leucocephalus, trinitatis, aequatorialis, and versicolor. The Varied White-fronted Capuchin (Cebus versicolor) comprises all populations of gracile capuchins that occur in northern Colombia in the Cauca-Magdalena interfluvium, on both banks of the Magdalena River and in the middle Magdalena River valley, excluding those populations on the western slope of the Eastern Andes and east of the eastern Andes Mountains into Venezuela (Cebus leucocephalus) and those in northern Cesar department and north to the Rio Ranchería (Cebus cesarae) and the Magdalena Department northern coast (C. malitiosus). An alternative hypothesis, by Hernandez-Camacho and Cooper (1976), considered a C. versicolor “complex” to include three taxa proposed by Hershkovitz (1949): C. albifrons versicolor, C. a. pleei (described from Mompos in Bolivar department) and C. a. leucocephalus, primarily because those authors had observed one population of capuchins in the Barrancabermeja region of the middle Magdalena with individual variation that seemed to encompass morphotypes of all three described subspecies; this points to one of the ongoing challenges of taxonomy for capuchin monkeys, the extreme individual morphological variation even within a single population (Rylands et al. 2005). However, mitochondrial genetic analyses support Cebus cesarae as the sister taxon to C. versicolor, with C. leucocephalus as a separate lineage (Lima et al. 2017). Ruiz-García et al. (2010) using mtDNA analyses also found that C. a. versicolor, C. a. cesarae, and C. a. pleei were genetically closely related to one another.

Here we note that the holotype (and only known locality) for Cebus albifrons pleei (sensu Hershkovitz, 1949) is found within our distribution of C. versicolor; the phenotype of C. pleei is more similar to C. versicolor than to C. cesarae, however, the genetic evidence is currently inconclusive to whether C. a. pleei should be synonymized with C. versicolor or instead with C. cesarae, extending the latter’s range south. Here we consider C. a. pleei a junior synonym of C. versicolor. We also note that the holotype for Cebus leucocephalus falls at the eastern edge of our C. versicolor range, and the C. versicolor holotype is listed as Bogotá, which is outside its distribution. These discrepancies are subjects of current research.
This is a newly described species belonging to the Rhinolophus maclaudi group (Kerbis Peterhans et al. 2013).
This is a newly described species belonging to the Rhinolophus maclaudi group (Kerbis Peterhans et al. 2013).

The previous Red List assessments for Galápagos tortoises treated the various allopatric island populations as subspecies of Chelonoidis nigra (now named Chelonoidis niger), as did several authors (Pritchard 1996, Caccone et al. 1999, Beheregaray et al. 2003, Fritz and Havas 2007,TTWG 2007, Rhodin et al. 2008). However, other authors have considered them as full species based on morphology (Bour 1980, Fritts 1983, Ernst and Barbour 1989) and the more recent consensus among researchers (Caccone et al. 2002; Russello et al. 2005, 2007; Poulakakis et al. 2008, 2012, 2015; Chiari et al. 2009) is to treat most as full species based on congruent patterns of mitochondrial and nuclear variation. This elevated species-level taxonomy has been largely accepted by TTWG (2009, 2014) and TEWG (2015) for most, but not all, phylogenetic lineages of Galápagos tortoises. This Red List assessment therefore now treats C. chathamensis as a full species, rather than retaining its previous subspecies ranking from earlier Red List assessments.

Pritchard (1996) restricted the name chathamensis to the now-extirpated tortoise populations of southwestern and central San Cristóbal, but excluded the eastern San Cristóbal population, considering this population to represent a possibly distinct subspecies for which no name was available. For purposes of this Red List Assessment, the eastern San Cristóbal population is considered part of chathamensis until taxonomic clarity emerges.

Testudo wallacei Rothschild, 1902, was described with type locality “Chatham Island?”, but its type locality was designated Jervis Island [Rábida] by Van Denburgh (1914); Rábida tortoises were hypothesized to be an introduced population (Pritchard 1996), later confirmed by genetic analysis (Poulakakis et al. 2012) to represent tortoises from southern Isabela, and the name wallacei is considered a nomen dubium and synonym of C. vicina (Fritz and Havas 2007, TTWG 2014).

The previous Red List assessments for Galápagos tortoises treated the various allopatric island populations as subspecies of Chelonoidis nigra (now named Chelonoidis niger), as did several authors (Pritchard 1996, Caccone et al. 1999, Beheregaray et al. 2003, Fritz and Havas 2007,TTWG 2007, Rhodin et al. 2008). However, other authors have considered them as full species based on morphology (Bour 1980, Fritts 1983, Ernst and Barbour 1989) and the more recent consensus among researchers (Caccone et al. 2002; Russello et al. 2005, 2007; Poulakakis et al. 2008, 2012, 2015; Chiari et al. 2009) is to treat most as full species based on congruent patterns of mitochondrial and nuclear variation. This elevated species-level taxonomy has been largely accepted by TTWG (2009, 2014) and TEWG (2015) for most, but not all, phylogenetic lineages of Galápagos tortoises. This Red List assessment therefore now treats C. hoodensis as a full species, rather than retaining its previous subspecies ranking from earlier Red List assessments.

The previous Red List assessments for Galápagos tortoises treated the various allopatric island populations as subspecies of Chelonoidis nigra (now named Chelonoidis niger), as did several authors (Pritchard 1996, Caccone et al. 1999, Beheregaray et al. 2003, Fritz and Havas 2007,TTWG 2007, Rhodin et al. 2008). However, other authors have considered them as full species based on morphology (Bour 1980, Fritts 1983, Ernst and Barbour 1989) and the more recent consensus among researchers (Caccone et al. 2002; Russello et al. 2005, 2007; Poulakakis et al. 2008, 2012, 2015; Chiari et al. 2009) is to treat most as full species based on congruent patterns of mitochondrial and nuclear variation. This elevated species-level taxonomy has been largely accepted by TTWG (2009, 2014) and TEWG (2015) for most, but not all, phylogenetic lineages of Galápagos tortoises. This Red List assessment therefore now treats C. porteri as a full species, rather than retaining its previous subspecies ranking from earlier Red List assessments.

The name C. porteri now refers only to the species of tortoise found on western Santa Cruz Island in the area known as La Reserva (the originally designated Tortoise Reserve). A second tortoise species on eastern Santa Cruz Island from the Cerro Fatal region has recently been described as C. donfaustoi (Poulakakis et al. 2015), thus separating what was formerly considered one species into two. A third very small group of tortoises occurs in the northwestern region of Santa Cruz near Cerro Montura. The tortoises found at Cerro Montura have been identified as genetically indistinguishable from the Pinzón tortoise, C. duncanensis (Russello et al. 2005), and it is considered that they were probably introduced from Pinzón to Santa Cruz by humans.

Some recent literature suggests that the valid name for the Western Santa Cruz tortoise may be C. nigrita (e.g., EMBL database). However, the Tortoise and Freshwater Turtle Specialist Group follows the nomenclature of Pritchard (1996), Fritz and Havas (2007), TTWG (2014), and TEWG (2015), who concluded that nigrita is a nomen dubium and placed it in questionable synonymy under C. porteri.

There is evidence to suggest that populations from Lake Enriquillo represent at least one separate species that has not yet been described (Smith 1989, Marmolejo 2011). Further molecular analysis suggests that these two lineages may be the result of colonization by at least two separate progenitors (Echelle et al. 2006). Therefore, subpopulations in Lake Enriquillo are not considered under the scope of this assessment.

The name Tympanocryptis condaminensis was erected by Melville et al. (2014) for the subpopulation of T. pinguicolla found in the Darling Downs, on the basis of genetic evidence that the two taxa are specifically distinct.

Parabotia curtus was originally described as Cobitis curta by Temminck & Schlegel (1846). After changes in the generic position, the species is included in Parabotia in the latest references. Because the gender of Parabotia is determined as masculine (see Kottelat 2012), Parabotia curtus (not curta) is currently used. Parabotia curtus is undoubtedly endemic to Japan, although an uncertain record of this species exists from southern China (see Watanabe et al. 2009). The species is included in the subfamily Botiinae of the family Cobitidae. Some recent references use the family name of Botiidae, based on the morphology and molecular phylogeny (e.g., Nalbant 2002, Šlechtová et al. 2006, Watanabe et al. 2009).

The taxonomy of Varanus timorensis and V. auffenbergi from Roti Island is unresolved, and the occurrence of V. timorensis on this island is in need of clarification (E. Arida and S. Reilly pers. comm. 2019). Unpublished data suggest that V. auffenbergi represents a subspecies of V. timorensis (A. Koch pers. comm. 2019).
This taxon could be considered a subspecies of Varanus melinus due to its morphological similarity and parapatric distribution (A. Koch pers. comm. 2019).
Genetic studies are in need for confirmation of the conspecificity of the Omanian (U. thomasi) and Somalian (U. t. aethiopica) populations.

The relationship between this species and Boophis microtympanum has not yet been clarified. Boophis laurenti might simply be a colour morph, but a distinct call has been recorded. More species could be involved, and the whole complex needs revision (M. Vences pers. comm.).

Records of Mantella expectata or M. betsileo from the arid west and southwest Madagascar (including from from Tsingy de Bemaraha) probably refer to a separate, undescribed, species that is not characterized by blue legs (F. Andreone pers. comm., Rabemananjara et al. 2007). Here we consider as Mantella expectata to refer only the populations from Isalo and the thin band further south (F. Glaw pers. comm. May 2016).
This species is genetically very closely related to Mantella baroni (Vences et al. 2004).

This species and Mantella crocea might represent colour variants of the same species (M. Vences and F. Glaw pers. comm. August 2016, Chiari et al. 2004). Subpopulations with intermediate characteristics between M. crocea and M. milotympanum have been recorded from north of Fierenana, Andriabe, Savakoanina and the Zahamena reserve (Chiari et al. 2004).
This species was redefined, and two new species, Mantidactylus charlotteae and M. zipperi, were described by Vences and Glaw (2004).

This recently described species is similar to Spinomantis peraccae and S. elegans. There is confustion regarding the taxonomy of this Group (additional species might be involved), and a revision is needed.
Records from Tsingy de Bemaraha now refer to Gephyromantis atsingy, records from Analavelona and the Kelifely Plateau are in need of confirmation (F. Glaw pers. comm. November 2015).
The taxonomic distinction between Mantidactylus madecassus and M. pauliani has recently been corroborated on the basis of genetic studies (M. Vences pers. comm.).
This species is here considered to belong to the genus Spinomantis, rather than Boophis, following Glaw and Vences (2006).

This species was synonymized with Stumpffia psologlossa by Blommers-Schlösser and Blanc (1991). However, we follow Glaw and Vences (1994) in treating it as a separate species. D'Cruze et al. (2007) recorded Stumpffia cf. roseifemoralis from Montagne des Français; the identity of these animals requires further study and they are not included as part of this assessment.
We follow Glaw and Vences (2006) in separating this species from Mantidactylus curtus.
We follow Glaw and Vences (2006) in removing this species from the synonymy of Mantidactylus brevipalmatus.

taxonomicNotes: Bott (1955) treated this species as a junior synonym of Potamonautes (Rotundopotamonautes) emini emini. It is treated here as the valid species Potamonautes mutandensis.

This species was originally considered by Hill (1959) to constitute a subspecies of Rhinolophus arcuatus Peters, 1871. Based on morphological analyses with other specimens of R. “arcuatus” from throughout its range, Patrick and co-workers (2013) suggested that the taxon on Borneo be raised to species status.

We follow Pickersgill (2007) in removing this species from the synonymy of Afrixalus uluguruensis. However, molecular data show that this might represent more than one species (S. Loader pers. comm. Oct 2015).

This tube-nosed bat is closely related to Murina chrysochaetes from southern China and northern Vietnam (Son et al. 2015) suggesting that both species are relics within of the former large distribution range of their ancestor. Previously this bat was once attributed to as M. aurata Milne-Edwards, 1872 (Borissenko and Kruskop 2003).

For this species we have followed the proposal of Holyoak and Holyoak (2012), who consider Cryptazeca kobelti Gittenberger, 1983 a synonymous with C. monodonta. The former name has been used by some authors to designate populations of C. monodonta living in Spain.

The eight species within the genus Pseudoplatystoma form a monophyletic group with two clades: one clade (P. metaense and P. tigrinum) is restricted to the Orinoco and Amazon basins; and the other clade (P. corruscans, P. fasciatum, P. magdaleniatum, P. orinocoense, P. punctifer, and P. reticulatum) is found in the Guyanas, Orinoco, Amazon and Paraná basins (Buitrago-Suárez and Burr 2007).
Some authors have placed this as a synonym of the widespread species C. acicula, however Stamol (2010) gives shell characters that separate the two taxa.
This species was reassigned from Pristimantis to Tachiramantis (Arroyo et al. 2022).
This species was reassigned from Pristimantis to Serranobatrachus (Arroyo et al. 2022).
This taxon is probably a complex of 2–3 species (D. Cisneros-Heredia pers. comm. January 2021).
This species was previously included in the genus Eleutherodactylus (Crawford and Smith 2005).
This species was mistakenly reported as Pristimantis cabrerai by Kattan (1984).

Records from the western slope of the Serranía de los Paraguas in Chocó Department and Valle de Cauca Department (Castro-Herrera and Vargas-Salinas 2008) might actually refer to another species, P. molybrignus (G. Gonzalez pers. comm. June 2018), and therefore are excluded from this species' extent of occurrence (EOO) until further taxonomic work is carried out.
This species was previously within the genus Eleutherodactylus (Crawford and Smith 2005).
This species was reassigned from Pristimantis to Serranobatrachus (Arroyo et al. 2022).
This species was previously included in the genus Eleutherodactylus (Crawford and Smith 2005).
This is the sister species of Pristimantis bellona according to Lynch and Ruíz-Carranza (1996b).
The records reported from Chocó Department are not reliable and require confirmation (Colombia Red List Assessment Workshop August 2016).

Due to a typographic error on the title of the paper describing this species (Lynch 1995), the name was given as P. lasallorum. However, the correct spelling is P. lasalleorum. Lynch (1995) gives the correct spelling elsewhere in the original description.
This taxon has previously been confused with Pristimantis calcaratus (Lynch 1998). It was previously assessed under the name Pristimantis kelephas; this was an incorrect subsequent spelling.

Duellman (1981) originally placed the taxon in the genus Centrolenella, which was subsequently designated to Cochranella by Ruiz-Carranza and Lynch (1991) based on the absence of humeral spines in males from specimens collected at Campamento Chancos, Valle del Cauca. However, Cisneros-Heredia and McDiarmid (2006) verified the presence of humeral spines in the material described by Duellman (1981), thus combining the taxon with Centrolene; the Colombian material reported by Lynch and Ruiz-Carranza (1996) were therefore presumed to be of a distinct lineage (Cisneros-Heredia and McDiarmid 2006). The lack of clarity regarding Colombian specimens is reflected in many published accounts (e.g. Acosta-Galvis 2000, Cisneros-Heredia and Yánez-Muñoz 2007, Guayasamin and Frenkel 2018).

Later, in a monographic revision of the glassfrog family, Centrolenidae, Guayasamin et al. (2009) underscored the difficulty of substantively designating the taxon at the generic level, and regarded the taxon as incertae sedis within the subfamily Centroleninae. Finally, after incorporating mitochondrial sequences into a phylogenetic analysis of the family, Guayasamin et al. (2019) determined its placement within Nymphargus based on recent material from the Río Manduriacu Reserve, Imbabura, Ecuador (Maynard et al. 2020). New material for N. balionotus from Colombia has not been reported in recent decades. Thus, it is not currently possible to resolve whether or not the subpopulation from Campamento Chancos is representative of a distinct lineage from those in Ecuador, as suggested by Cisneros-Heredia and McDiarmid (2006). However, it is plausible that this scenario is correct based on available information; in addition to lacking humeral spines, the records from Campamento Chancos are well separated from, and substantially lower in elevation than all other specimens (Maynard et al. 2020).
Guayasamin et al. 2009 considered this species to be incertae sedis within Centroleninae.
Cisneros-Heredia and McDiarmid (2007) noted that this species may occur in Ecuador.
This is the sister species of Rhinella acrolopha, according to the original description.
This species has not been included in phylogenies based on molecular characters, so its evolutionary relationships are still uncertain (Yánez-Muñoz et al. 2018).

This is a species complex that requires formal subdivision (Guayasamín et al. 2006, 2020; Amador et al. 2018). Amador et al. (2018) provided molecular evidence that nominal Centrolene buckleyi is composed of at least three species, none of which is each other's closest relative, one in southern Ecuador, two (including Centrolene buckleyi sensu stricto) in northern Ecuador, and another in central Andean Colombia, with the edges remaining unknown (Frost 2021).

Two specimens from Huancabamba in Piura Department in northern Peru (Duellman and Wild 1993) previously assigned to this species are actually metamorphs of another species (Amador et al. 2018; D. Cisneros-Heredia pers. comm. 2021). All records of this species from Venezuela formerly assigned to this taxon are now assigned to Centrolene venezuelense.
In spite of its Amazonian type locality, this species is in fact known only from the Andes.
This species was reassigned from Pristimantis to Serranobatrachus (Arroyo et al. 2022).
This species is considered to be a derivative of Oophaga histrionica by Myers and Daly (1976).

Lötters (1992) doubted the distinction of this species from Oophaga histrionica.

Records from the Serrania de Baudo and Alto del Oso in the Department of Chocó that were previously questioned to belonging to this species have now been verified and assigned as Oophaga solanensis (Posso-Terranova and Andrés 2018).
The validity of this species was previously in question (Lynch pers. comm; Grant and Myers 2013). However, Grant et al. (2017) recognize this taxon as a distinct species.
There is ongoing debate about whether this is a valid species or whether it is a synonym of Hyloxalus pulchellus (T. Grant pers. comm., G. Pisso pers. comm. October 2018).
This genus has recently been moved from the family Hylidae (Faivovich et al. 2005).
This genus has recently been moved from the family Hylidae (Faivovich et al. 2005).

This form is probably a complex of several species. Brazilian populations assigned to this species presumably belong to another species. This genus has recently been moved from the family Hylidae (Faivovich et al. 2005).

This species was previously included in the genus Hyla but was moved to the resurrected genus Hyloscirtus by Faivovich et al. (2005) and then to Colomascirtus by Duellman et al. (2016), but later retained as Hyloscirtus by Rojas-Runjaic et al. (2018). It is probably a synonym of Hyloscirtus lindae.

This species was previously included in the genus Hyla, but was moved to the resurrected genus Hyloscirtus by Faivovich et al. (2005) and then to Colomascirtus by Duellman et al. (2016), but later retained as Hyloscirtus by Rojas-Runjaic et al. (2018).

This species was previously included in the genus Hyla, but was moved to the resurrected genus Hyloscirtus (Faivovich et al. 2005) and then to Colomascirtus by Duellman et al. (2016), but later retained as Hyloscirtus by Rojas-Runjaic et al. (2018).
Bolitoglossa tatamae can be distinguished from other similar species by a combination of morphological features (Acosta and Hoyos 2006).
This species was first assessed under the generic name Phyllomedusa, but has since been transferred to the genus Callimedusa (Duellman et al. 2016).
The taxonomy of this species' genus is in need of revision (M. Wilkinson pers. comm.).
In the genus Pristimantis according to Frost (2007). Recognition of Pristimantis zoilae was based on morphological features and colour patterns (Mueses-Cisneros 2007).

Colostethus ucumari can be distinguished from other Colostethus by means of morphological differences and colour patterns; it is also the second species of Colostethus known to possess skin toxins (Grant 2007).

Páez-Moscoso and Guayasamin (2012) suggested that this taxon is part of an Osornophryne bufoniformis species complex that is poorly elucidated (Frost 2021). It has been suggested that this species is actually a junior synonym of O. bufoniformis (L. Coloma pers. comm. 2019).

This taxon is believed to be the nominal subspecies A. g. guerianus whilst the fossil subspecies calathoides was distributed in the Ilhas Desertas on Deserta Grande and Bugio. In the opinion of K. Groh (pers. comm. 2016) the latter has to be treated as a good species.

It has been confirmed as a valid species through a morphological (shell characters and anatomy of the reproductive system) and genetic (sequencing of a fragment of the mitochondrial large ribosomal subunit 16S rRNA, and the nuclear internal transcriber spacer 1, ITS-1 rRNA) study (Giusti et al. 1995).

Originally placed in genus Imitatrix, this genus was later found to be so close to Lampedusa that no generic dichotomy was needed. It is distinguished from the other species of the genus by virtue of its peculiar buccal pleats and lamellae arrangement. The species has been cited with two more subspecies (L. i. gattoi and L. i. emersoniana) that are now considered as junior synonyms (Giusti et al. 1995).
This is a split from the broader concept of Rhinella crucifer (Roberto et al. 2014).
This species has been assigned to the Scinax catherinae clade (Cruz et al. 2011).

Species described in 2015, allocated in the group of Brachycephalus pernix, a group composed of 18 formally recognized species (Monteiro et al. 2018). Based on molecular data they confirmed the species delimitation (Firkowski et al. 2016). However, Condez et al. (2017) discuss the need for a taxonomic re-evaluation of B. mariaeterezae, due to problems in its diagnosis and original description. Pie et al. (2017) refute the arguments in Condez et al. (2017).
This species has been assigned to the Scinax catharinae group (Cruz et al. 2011).

The Crossodactylus dispar complex is thought to contain may microendemics, such that subpopulations assigned to this species but recorded outside Santa Teresa Massif (e.g. Cachoeiro de Itapemirim), probably belong to other undescribed species (J.V. Lacerda pers. comm. 2021).
Removed from the synonymy of Brachycephalus ephippium by Pombal Jr. and Izecksohn (2011).
Removed from the synonymy of Brachycephalus ephippium by Pombal (2010).

According to Conndez et al. (2017), this species belongs to the Brachycephalus pernix group and requires a taxonomic reassessment due to errors present in the original diagnosis and description considering the use of doubtful diagnostic characters, such as body colour and skin roughness; characteristics that can vary intra-specifically as a consequence of a polymorphism or even be an artefact of specimen preservation.

Pristimantis sp. “J” from the summit of Mount Roraima in Venezuela (see McDiarmid and Donnelly 2005) could be P. aureoventris, but tissue samples and specimens are necessary to corroborate this assumption (Jablonski et al. 2017).
Removed from the synonymy of Crossodactylus dispar by Pimenta et al. (2014).
