taxonomicNotes: This species was provisionally allocated to Aparasphenodon when first described by de Assis et al. (2013). Blotto et al. (2021) redeﬁned Nyctimantis to include Aparasphenodon.

Bokermannohyla napolii is a newly described species on the basis of adult morphology, morphometrics, and mainly vocalizations. Adult specimens of B. napolii are most closely related to those of B. luctuosa and B. circumdata.

authority: Peloso, Sturaro, Forlani, Gaucher, Motta & Wheeler, 2014

taxonomicNotes: This species was reported as Leptobrachium pullum by Inger et al. (1999).

Hamlet species are defined primarily on differences in colour patterns. They are known to interbreed and hybridize freely with very little consistent genetic differentiation amongst them. Prior to its description, it was confused with the phenotypically-similar Hypoplectrus gemma (Domeier 1994). A recent genomic analysis of H. maya confirms it is a distinct evolutionary lineage from other Caribbean hamlet species, including H. nigricans, H. puella, H. unicolor and H. gemma (Moran et al. 2019).
This is a split from the broader concept of Limnonectes kuhlii, for which it was previously confused (Matsui et al. 2014).

The genus Lucifuga is one of the most diverse stygiobiotic genera of fishes to have been described. The taxonomy of Lucifuga species may still be incomplete due to relatively common discoveries of new species. García-Machado et al. (2011) concluded that Lucifuga spp. are genetically divergent, but most closely related lineages are morphologically indistinguishable and recommend revisiting the taxonomy of the genus. Since the distribution of certain morphological characters among these species is disjointed, they possibly may not be used for classification in the future. A description of a new species, Lucifuga gibarensis of Cuba, was recently published (Hernández et al. 2020).

The genus Lucifuga is known as one of the most diverse of stygiobiotic fishes. The systematics of Lucifuga may still be incomplete due to the relatively common discoveries of new species. Garcia-Machado et al. (2011) concluded that Lucifuga spp. are genetically divergent, but with some of the species being morphologically indistinguishable and recommends revisiting the taxonomy of the genus. A revision of the Lucifuga spp. of Cuba with the description of a new species is currently in preparation by Hernández et al. (Hernández, D., Casane, D., García, A., Chevalier-Monteagudo, P., Møller, P. R. and García-Machado, E.). Recently a species from eastern Cuba, formerly described as a variety of L. dentata, was described as a new species, L. gibarensis (Hernández et al. 2020). The species appears to be more closely related to Bahamian species, than to the other Cuban species. 

There are no species of Lucifuga spp. on Little Bahama Bank other than L. lucayana and the other Bahamian species L. spelaeotes separated from L. lucayana by the deep waters of the Providence Channels (Møller et al. 2006).
This species was split from Eleutherodactylus wetmorei (Hedges et al. 2008).

This species was split from Eleutherodactylus audanti (Hedges et al. 2008). On the basis of preliminary molecular data, Díaz et al. (2018) suggested that E. melatrigonum may be conspecific with E. notidodes.
This species was split from Eleutherodactylus wetmorei (Hedges et al. 2008).

The taxonomic position of the genus Schurmannia is unclear but is most probably a synonym of Alocerus, as Švácha and Danilevsky (1987) first confirmed the strong similarity between the larvae of A. moesiacus and S. sicula. Then Holzschuh (1995), proposed a new combination, Alocerus siculus, but this was not considered by subsequent authors.
Nososticta pilbara was originally described as a subspecies of N. solida in Watson (1969) and later elevated to a full species in Watson (1974).

Specimens recorded at Estação Biológica de Boracéia, in the municipality of Salesópolis, São Paulo State, were assigned to Paratelmatobius gaigeae by Heyer et al. (1990). Later, Pombal and Haddad (1999) reallocated these specimens to P. poecilogaster (1990).

This genus was recently transferred to the Potamonautidae: Deckeniinae: Hydrothelphusini by Cumberlidge et al. (2007).

This species was originally described as Boreas uglowi Cumberlidge & Sternberg, 2002. However, this genus name proved to be occupied, so the taxon was given the replacement name of Boreathelphusa uglowi (Cumberlidge & Sternberg, 2002) by Cumberlidge (2010).
This species was split from Incilius valliceps and Incilius macrocristatus (Mendelson III et al. 2012).
This is a split from the broader concept of Thorius schmidti (Rovito et al. 2013) where it had been placed by Hanken and Wake (1998).

It has been suggested that this species is a synonym of Phrynobatrachus acridoides, but recent surveys indicate that it differs both in vocalizations and ecology (M. Pickersgill pers. comm.). Phrynobatrachus nigripes, described by Pickersgill (2007), is in fact a synonym of this species (M. Pickersgill pers. comm.).

In the Rhacophorus calcaneus group of Orlov et al. (2012), who noted extensive confusion with Rhacophorus robertingeri in the literature and who discussed previous confusion in the range. Rhacophorus chuyangsinensis was also synonymised with this species by Orlov et al. (2012).
We follow Stuart (2008) in assigning this species to the genus Amolops.

This species was originally identified from Jeongseon, Republic of Korea, under the name of Galloisiana biryongensis Namkung, 1974. It was later recombined as Namkungia biryongensis Storozhenko & Park, 2002.

Note, for the birdwing butterflies, genera Ornithoptera, Trogonoptera and Troides, specific and subspecific status follows D'Abrera. Subsequent revisions have been referenced but only totally new taxa have been added. Subspecies are listed where possible as they may occasionally be listed as full species.
Genus Lokia is most likely a synonym to Aethiothemis (Dijkstra unpublished).

Reynolds and Foster (1992) indicated that Atelopus rugulosus is a synonym of A. tricolor. Subsequently, Lötters and De la Riva (1998) confirmed this and pointed out that in evaluating the types of A. rugulosus from south-east Peru, and A. wilimani from Bolivia, both are synonyms of A. tricolor.
This species was considered to be a subspecies of Rhinella limensis by Leviton and Duellman (1978).

The status of Ameerega rubriventris as a valid species is still in dispute by some, though the unusual ventral colors and genetic distinctness suggest that this species is indeed different from A. altamazonica (Twomey and Brown 2017).
Prior to the official description in 1979, this species was known to many people but under incorrect names, such as Phyllobates bicolor.
This genus has recently been moved from the family Hylidae (Faivovich et al. 2005).
This genus has recently been moved from the family Hylidae (Faivovich et al. 2005).
Pristimantis vermiculatus is considered to be a junior synonym of Pristimantis petrobardus (Duellman and Lehr 2009).

According to Lehr and Catenazzi (2009), Psychrophrynella bagrecito (Lynch 1986) seems to be related to Noblella rather than to Psychrophrynella (Lehr 2006, De la Riva et al. 2008).

This species was reassigned from Psychrophrynella to Noblella (von May et al. 2024).
The specimen from Cajamarca, Peru previously attributed to Lynchius parkeri by Lehr (2006) has been assigned to Lynchius oblitus based on phylogenetic analyses (Motta et al. 2016).

The biological identities of some of the Peruvian subpopulations need to be clarified (A. Catenazzi pers. comm. February 2014). Until such time, all subpopulations currently allocated to Telmatobius timens are encompassed in this assessment.

In the genus Nymphargus according to Cisneros-Heredia and McDiarmid (2007). Nymphargus mixomaculatus is diagnosed from other similar glassfrogs by a combination of morphological features and colour patterns (Guayasamin et al. 2006).
Atelopus pyrodactylus can be distinguished from other similar species by a combination of morphological features and colour patterns (Venegas and Barrio 2006).
Microkayla boettgeri can be distinguished from other similar species by a combination of morphological characters (Lehr 2006).

In the Eleutherodactylus unistrigatus group according to the original publication. In the genus Pristimantis according to AmphibiaWeb (2008). Pristimantis wagteri is distinguished from other similar species by a combination of morphological features and colour patterns (Venegas 2007).
This species was reassigned from Phrynopus to Oreobates by Padial et al. (2012).

In the Eleutherodactylus (Eleutherodactylus) orestes group according to the original publication. In the genus Pristimantis following Heinicke et al. (2007). Pristimantis cordovae can be distinguished from other similar species by a combination of morphological features and different colour patterns (Lehr and Duellman 2007).
This species was formerly included within Ranitomeya fantastica (Brown et al. 2008).

Historically, there has been some doubt over the distinctiveness of this taxon from Rhinella spinulosa (Gorham 1974, Córdova 1999). However, it is considered a distinct species from the broader concept of Rhinella spinulosa (Cei 1972, Cei 1980). 

Morphological and molecular studies are needed to clarify the distribution of Rhinella in southern Peru (R. Santa Cruz pers. comm. August 2018).
Named as Philautus decoris by Manamendra-Arachchi and Pethiyagoda in 2005, the genus has been revised and replaced with Pseudophilautus by Li et al. in 2009.

The genus Delamarephorura contains few species and occurs in Africa and in Viet Nam (Janion et al. 2013). Delamarephorura tami is one of the two Vietnamese species of the genus in Viet Nam, the other one being a littoral form described known from Binh Chau beach. The genus has so far not provided other species from Viet Nam, contrary to several other Tullbergiidae genera.
Eustra honchongensis belongs to a genus including species which are all rare and narrowly endemic in Southeast Asia (Deuve, 1996).
Groves (2002) provisionally listed two subspecies of Grevy's Zebra. However, Groves and Bell (2004) concluded that the species is indeed monotypic. Reviewed by Churcher (1993).

Gnomulus bedoharvengorum is a species of the large Indo-Malayan genus Gnomulus. The Hon Chong species is biologically of interest because of its occurrence in caves and rarer occurrence in surface litter under bushes, while most other species of the genus live in rainforest litter. It appears as a relictual form in this respect.

Isometrus deharvengi belongs to the rather large genus Isometrus, which is known from Vietnam and surrounding countries by several species (Lourenço & Duhem, 2010). All species of the genus except one have reduced distribution areas. The presence of I. deharvengi in caves is unusual for the genus Isometrus, though the species is not troglomorphic.

scientificName: Burmoniscus sp. nov. 'HC - first segment white'
taxonomicNotes: Species under description by Stefano Taiti and Giovanna Monticelli Cardoso (May 2016), vouchers in Firenze (1 specimen), and 2 specimens to be deposited in MNHN Paris (Taiti 2016, pers. comm.).

Species under description by Louis Deharveng and Anne Bedos, vouchers in MNHN Paris (6 specimens), to be deposited in Ho Chi Minh City University and MNHN Paris.

Ceratophysella sp. nov. 'HC' is a new undescribed species of Hypogastruridae Collembola, assumed to be related to temperate region species of the genus. Its is a remarkable relict restricted to one cave of the Hon Chong hills, and the only non-guanobitic cave Hypogastruridae known from the tropics.
Formerly included in the genus Salamandra, this taxon was assigned to the genus Lyciasalamandra by Veith and Steinfartz (2004).
We follow Mattoccia et al. (2005) and Canestrelli et al. (2006) in separating Salamandrina perspicillata from S. terdigitata.

Until 2003, all Scleropages from Southeast Asia were considered to belong to the same species; S. formosus. In 2003 Pouyaud et al., considered there to actually be four species, three of which they described as new (S. aureus, S. legendrei, S. macrocephalus). Kottelat and Widjanarti (2005) reviewed the published data and did not reach the same conclusions from the quality of the data presented, and in order for more than one species to be recognised, a professional standard study would need to be presented. In 2012, a new species (S. incriptus) was described from Myanmar (Roberts 2012).
This species was previously included in the genus Eleutherodactylus (Crawford and Smith 2005). It was removed from synonymy of Craugastor punctariolus (Campbell and Savage 2000).

Mokodongan et al. (2018) found that the Oryzias woworae species group (comprising of O. asinua, O. wolasi, and O. woworae) could not be separated by their body shape using geometric morphometrics and that their phylogenetic relationships did not support the presence of three species. As such, they recommend a taxonomic re-examination of the O.woworae species group.

The previous Red List assessments for Galápagos tortoises treated the various allopatric island populations as subspecies of Chelonoidis nigra (now named Chelonoidis niger), as did several authors (Pritchard 1996, Caccone et al. 1999, Beheregaray et al. 2003, Fritz and Havas 2007,TTWG 2007, Rhodin et al. 2008). However, other authors have considered them as full species based on morphology (Bour 1980, Fritts 1983, Ernst and Barbour 1989) and the more recent consensus among researchers (Caccone et al. 2002; Russello et al. 2005, 2007; Poulakakis et al. 2008, 2012, 2015; Chiari et al. 2009) is to treat most as full species based on congruent patterns of mitochondrial and nuclear variation. This elevated species-level taxonomy has been largely accepted by TTWG (2009, 2014) and TEWG (2015) for most, but not all, phylogenetic lineages of Galápagos tortoises. Chelonoidis donfaustoi is a recently described species (Poulakakis et al. 2015), previously included within C. porteri. The name C. porteri is now restricted only to the species of tortoise found on western Santa Cruz Island in the area known as La Reserva (the originally designated Tortoise Reserve). This Red List assessment treats this newly described taxon, C. donfaustoi, as a full species.
This species may be a species complex, and further taxonomic work is required to verify this (K.O. Chan and E. Quah pers. comm. January 2018).

Telmatherina bonti is the only valid name currently available for riverine Telmatherina (Kottelat 1990, Miesen et al. 2015). Nevertheless, Telmatherina cf. bonti has been used for different riverine populations in the Malili Lakes System and several separate adjacent drainages due to high level of phenotypic and genotypic diversity recorded among them, as comprising thirteen geographically fragmented morphotypes following Herder et al. (2006b) that can be genetically assorted more inclusively into three strongly supported groups (Herder et al. 2006a). Although the two studies (Herder et al. 2006a, 2006b) seem to abandon the status of T. bonti by referring to all the pertinent riverine populations—except the one in the Petea River—together with the lacustrine one as T. cf. bonti, it is necessary to reserve the name T. bonti in the strict sense for the group that includes any populations readily assigned to the identity as redescribed by Kottelat (1990). Therefore, all the populations embedded in the larger riverine clade of Herder et al. (2006a) need to be treated as T. bonti, while leaving the other two stream-dwelling groups as taxa, the status of which needs to be resolved taxonomically.

This species previously appeared on the IUCN Red List in the genus Weberogobius but is now considered valid in the genus Mugilogobius. It most closely resembles Mugilogobius hitam described by Larson et al. in 2014 from Lake Towuti; but the two species can be discriminated based on lateral scale count — 52-65 in Mugilogobius amadi and 31-34 in Mugilogobius hitam (Larson et al. 2014).
This species previously appeared on the IUCN Red List as Tamanka sarasinorum but is now valid in the genus Mugilogobius.

This species was previously thought by Berry (1975) and Kiew (1975) to include Limnonectes tweediei. However, Dring (1979), Grandison (1982) and Leong Tzi Ming (pers. comm.) consider the two forms to be distinct.
In literature this species was often placed under the genus Erinna which is found in Hawaii in similar habitat. However, this is a case of convergence and not relationship.
According to recent molecular research (Pigneur et al. 2014) is Corbicula madagascariensis a distinct species and not a form of C. fluminalis.

Neritopsine populations from Madagascar and the Mascarenes have been often described as Clithon corona (Linnaeus, 1758) or as C. longispina (Récluz, 1841) and C. brevispina (Riech, 1937), which should be considered synonyms of the first.

Clithon corona does occur in the eastern Indo-Pacific, in Papua New Guinea, Guam, Caroline Islands, Solomon Islands, Vanuatu, Samoa, Taiwan and New Caledonia. All above references from Madagascar and the Mascarenes should be attributed to Clithon coronatus (Leach, 1815). Inversely, references of C. corona from the eastern Pacific are erroneous.

The shells described as C. longispina (Récluz, 1841) and Clithon brevispina described from the island  of Madagascar do come from other localities in the Indo-Pacific according to Fischer-Piette and Vulkadinovic (1973).
This species is presently placed in the new genus Madagisikara Köhler & Glaubrecht, 2010. It previously appeared on the IUCN Red List in the genus Melanatria.

In Hylarana signata complex, this is a split from the broader concept of Hylarana siberu (Chan et al. 2014). Currently known as Pulchrana centropeninsularis after a phylogenetic review by Oliver et al. (2015).

The validity of this species was questioned by Golubev in Ščerzbak (2003), who considered it an isolated population attributable to Phrynocephalus maculatus. According to Barabanov and Ananjeva (2007) P. golubewii is presently considered a valid species. It was treated within P. maculatus by Sindaco and Jeremčenko (2008), who however, noted that it appeared to be well-separated in the phylogeny provided by Arnold (1999).

taxonomicNotes: This species was assessed in 2008 as a subspecies of Cercocebus atys, but is now raised to a species. This species was also formerly treated as a subspecies of C. torquatus.

Groves (2001), Grubb et al. (2003), and Kingdon et al. (2008) treat Erythrocebus patas as a monotypic species in a monotypic genus. Schwarz (1927), Isbell (2013), and De Jong and Butynski (2018) recognize baumstarki as one of the three subspecies within E. patas (patas, pyrrhonotus, baumstarki). Verheyen (1962), Hill (1966), Napier and Napier (1967), Dandelot (1968, 1974), and Kingdon (1971, 1997, 2015) recognize an additional subspecies, E. p. villiersi.

Erythrocebus baumstarki was described by Matschie (1906) as a species (type locality: Ikoma, western Serengeti, Tanzania). Here, baumstarki is, once again, treated as a species. This restoration to species status is based on baumstarki’s considerable geographic isolation and distinctive coloration and pattern of the pelage. The distance between the nearest confirmed E. baumstarki record (Northern Extension of Serengeti National Park, north central Tanzania; G. Schaller pers. comm.; number 15 in Figure 1 and Table 1 of De Jong et al. 2009) and the nearest confirmed E. p. pyrrhonotus record (Kiganjo, central Kenya; H. Douglas-Dufresne pers. comm.; number 47 in Figure 2 and Table 3 in De Jong et al. 2008) is ca. 260 km. There are, however, several records between these two localities for Erythrocebus that have not been identified as to species (De Jong et al. 2008). It appears that these two species are separated by a forest barrier and by very dry habitat in the Eastern (Gregory) Rift Valley (De Jong et al. 2008, 2009, De Jong & Butynski 2014, Butynski & De Jong 2017). Erythrocebus baumstarki is also phenotypically distinct (e.g., black nose, lack of white moustache, pale red crown, white neck, and dark grey upper limbs, whereas pyrrhonotus has a white nose, white moustache, dark red crown, rufous neck, and lacks dark grey on the upper hind legs). Resurrecting baumstarki to species status is in agreement with Gippoliti (2017), who suggests treating baumstarki, as well as poliophaeus and pyrrhonotus, as species.

Cercopithecus diana and C. roloway comprise the diana species group. In the 2008 IUCN Red List these taxa were treated as subspecies (C. d. diana and C. d. roloway) of a broader species-concept following Kingdon (1997) and Grubb et al. (2003). Groves (2005) elevated both to full species and that treatment is now followed here (based on Mittermeier et al. 2013). Not all assessors agree with this classification; Oates (2011) recognizes one species with two subspecies: diana and roloway.

Cercopithecus diana and C. roloway comprise the diana species group. In the 2008 IUCN Red List these were treated as subspecies (C. d. diana and C. d. roloway) of a broader species-concept following Kingdon (1997) and Grubb et al. (2003). Groves (2005) elevated both to full species and that treatment is now followed here (as in Mittermeier et al. 2013). Not all assessors agree with this classification; Oates (2011) treats roloway as a subspecies of C. diana.
Chan et al. (2010), suggested that the dubious records of Leptolalax gracilis from Gunung Tahan might actually represent this species.

Modern taxonomic arrangements of the colobus monkeys either distinguish the red colobus monkeys and the Olive Colobus as separate genera, Piliocolobus and Procolobus, respectively (e.g., Groves 2001, 2005, 2007; Roos et al. 2011, Kingdon 2015), or consider them to belong to one genus Procolobus, with two subgenera (Procolobus for the Olive Colobus and Piliocolobus for the red colobus) (Oates et al. 1994, Grubb et al. 2003, 2013; Oates 2011). Ting (2008) placed all the red colobus as subspecies of Procolobus (Piliocolobus) badius. The 2008 IUCN Red List assessments followed the taxonomy of Grubb et al. (2003).

The arrangement of two genera in Groves (2001, 2005, 2007) is followed here: a monotypic Procolobus de Rochebrune, 1887 (Olive Colobus) and polytypic Piliocolobus de Rochebrune, 1887 (red colobus).

Preuss’s Red Colobus was treated as a subspecies of Procolobus (Piliocolobus) pennantii by Grubb et al. (2003), but as a distinct species by Kingdon (1997, 2015), Groves (2001, 2005, 2007), and Butynski and Kingdon (2013).

Modern taxonomic arrangements of the colobus monkeys either distinguish the red colobus monkeys and the Olive Colobus as separate genera, Piliocolobus and Procolobus, respectively (e.g., Groves 2001, 2005, 2007; Roos et al. 2011, Kingdon 2015), or consider them to belong to one genus Procolobus, with two subgenera (Procolobus for the Olive Colobus and Piliocolobus for the red colobus) (Oates et al. 1994, Grubb et al. 2003, 2013; Oates 2011). Ting (2008) placed all the red colobus as subspecies of Procolobus (Piliocolobus) badius. The 2008 IUCN Red List assessments followed the taxonomy of Grubb et al. (2003).

The arrangement of two genera in Groves (2001, 2005, 2007) is followed here: a monotypic Procolobus de Rochebrune, 1887 (Olive Colobus) and polytypic Piliocolobus de Rochebrune, 1887 (red colobus).

This taxon was formerly the nominate subspecies of a wider concept of P. pennantii that also included as subspecies parmentierorum, bouvieri, tholloni, powelli Matschie, nigrimanus Trouessart, lulindicus, langi, foai and semlikiensis (Gautier-Hion et al. 1999). Kingdon (1997), Groves (2001, 2005) and Butynski et al. (2013) considered bouvieri and epieni to be subspecies of pennantii; Grubb et al. (2003) likewise, but also including preussi. Kingdon (2015) considered epieni to be a distinct species but maintained bouvieri as a subspecies of P. pennantii. Piliocolobus pennantii is here treated as a distinct monotypic species following Groves (2007).

Modern taxonomic arrangements of the colobus monkeys either distinguish the red colobus monkeys and the Olive Colobus as separate genera, Piliocolobus and Procolobus, respectively (e.g., Groves 2001, 2005, 2007; Roos et al. 2011, Kingdon 2015), or consider them to belong to one genus Procolobus, with two subgenera (Procolobus for the Olive Colobus and Piliocolobus for the red colobus) (Oates et al. 1994, Grubb et al. 2003, 2013; Oates 2011). Ting (2008) placed all the red colobus as subspecies of Procolobus (Piliocolobus) badius. The 2008 IUCN Red List assessments followed the taxonomy of Grubb et al. (2003).

The arrangement of two genera in Groves (2001, 2005, 2007) is followed here: a monotypic Procolobus de Rochebrune, 1887 (Olive Colobus) and polytypic Piliocolobus de Rochebrune, 1887 (red colobus).

Kingdon (1997), Groves (2001, 2005) and Butynski et al. (2013) considered bouvieri and epieni to be subspecies of pennantii; Grubb et al. (2003) likewise, but also including preussi. Gautier-Hion et al. (1999) considered that the Central African parmentierorum, bouvieri, tholloni, powelli Matschie, nigrimanus Trouessart, lulindicus, langi, foai and semlikiensis were also subspecies of pennantii. Kingdon (2015) treated epieni as a distinct species (but maintained bouvieri as a subspecies of P. pennantii). Groves (2007) treated epieni and bouvieri as distinct species, and pennantii as a monotypic. The study by Ting (2008) on vocalizations and mitochondrial DNA confirmed that P. epieni is not closely related to its closest geographic relatives (P. pennantii and P. preussi). The Niger Delta Red Colobus is here treated as a distinct monotypic species following Groves (2007).

Modern taxonomic arrangements of the colobus monkeys either distinguish the red colobus monkeys and the olive colobus as separate genera, Piliocolobus and Procolobus, respectively (e.g., Groves 2001, 2005, 2007; Roos et al. 2011; Kingdon 2015), or consider them to belong to one genus Procolobus, with two subgenera (Procolobus for the olive colobus and Piliocolobus for the red colobus) (Oates et al. 1994; Grubb et al. 2003, 2013; Oates 2011). Ting (2008) placed all the red colobus as subspecies of Procolobus (Piliocolobus) badius. The 2008 IUCN Red List assessments followed the taxonomy of Grubb et al. (2003).

The arrangement of two genera in Groves (2001, 2005, 2007) is followed here: a monotypic Procolobus de Rochebrune, 1887 (Olive Colobus) and polytypic Piliocolobus de Rochebrune, 1887 (red colobus).

Piliocolobus lulindicus was recognised as a distinct taxon by Colyn (1991), who reviewed its nomenclatural history and resurrected the forms foai and lulindicus as subspecies of Colobus badius. He argued that the montane form foai (sensu stricto) in the east and lowland form lulindicus (sensu stricto) in the west are separated by an intermediate and possible hybrid lowland form, kabambarei Matschie. However, Groves (2007) considered both lulindicus and kabambarei as junior synonyms of Piliocolobus foai, noting continuous variation among these forms. The consensus at a workshop held in Rome in 2016 to update the African primate Red List assessments was that it would be expedient (precautionary) to continue to recognise lulindicus as a distinct taxon, considering the threatened status of all the red colobus monkeys. We include in this species Colyn’s (1991) lulindicus (sensu stricto) as well as the intermediate and potential hybrid populations that exist in the lowland regions to the west, with the western boundary being the Albertine rift where it is replaced by the montane form P. foai. Further research is needed to resolve this taxonomy.

Unio populations from western Iberia were previously thought to belong to the widespread and more common European Unio pictorum and the species name Unio delphinus to be its junior synonym (Haas 1969). However, subsequent integrative molecular and morphological work recognised U. delphinus as a valid species and distinct from U. pictorum (Araujo et al. 2009, Lopes-Lima et al. 2017).

Unio populations from Morocco, previously thought to be U. delphinus, belong to a separate species, the Moroccan endemic Unio foucauldianus Pallary, 1936 (Froufe et al. 2016).

Due to advances in the study of the genus Potomida, the species Potomida littoralis now strictly refers to the southwest European (France, Spain and Portugal) and northwest African (Morocco, Algeria and Tunisia) Potomida subpopulations.

Traditional conchological characters, which are of limited use for identifying taxonomic units due to the high phenotypic plasticity of unionoid bivalves, are responsible for the more than 90 synonyms described for P. littoralis (Cuvier, 1798) before the mid-20th century (Graf 2010). Of the eight P. littoralis subspecies recognised by Haas (1969), the Iberian P. l. umbonata (Rossmässler, 1844) and the North African P. l. fellmanni (Deshayes, 1848) have been retained as synonyms of P. l. littoralis (Froufe et al. 2016, Araujo et al. 2017).

Based on molecular and biogeographical arguments, the Greek P. l. acarnanica and the Turkish and Middle Eastern P. l. semirugata have recently been considered as distinct species, i.e. P. acarnanica and P. semirugata, respectively (Froufe et al. 2016). P. l. delesserti from the Middle East was synonymised with P. semirugata.

Based on an extensive comparative morphological and anatomical study, P. l. homsensis (Lea, 1865) was correctly reassigned to a different family (Margaritiferidae) and is now Pseudunio homsensis (Lea, 1865) (Smith 2001, Lopes-Lima et al. 2018).

Finally, P. l. komarowi (O. Boettger, 1880) has recently been synonymised under Unio bruguierianus but subsequently reassigned to Unio mardinensis (Lopes-Lima et al. 2021, 2023).

This species was previously included in the circum-Mediterranean Potomida littoralis. However, recent phylogenetic studies (Froufe et al. 2016) recognised the distinction of three species within the genus Potomida, namely P. littoralis in north-western African and south-western European countries (Algeria, Tunisia, Morocco, Spain, Portugal and France), P. acarnanica in Greece, and P. semirugata in Türkiye and the Middle East.
This species was previously included in Hipposideros ater, but is now considered distinct following Douangboubpha et al. (2011).
This species has been split from Rhinopoma muscatellum by Hulva et al. (2007) and Benda et al. (2009).

Rhinolophus belligerator (Patrick, McCulloch, and Ruedas 2013) previously was included with R. arcuatus proconsulis by Hill (1988) based on a single specimen from the Tengah Province of Sulawesi. Subsequent analyses by Patrick and co-workers (2013) elevated this taxon to species status.
Records of Cardioglossa pulchra from Tchabal Mbabo (Amiet 1972) now refer to this species (Herrmann et al. 2004).

Lontra provocax had been considered a subspecies of L. canadensis (Davis 1978). It was placed in the genus Lontra by van Zyll de Jong (1987). Koepfli and Wayne (1998) and Bininda-Emonds et al. (1999) supported the separation of New World otters into genus Lontra from Lutra, except Pteronura.

Modern taxonomic arrangements of the colobus monkeys either distinguish the red colobus monkeys and the Olive Colobus as separate genera, Piliocolobus and Procolobus, respectively (e.g., Groves 2001, 2005, 2007; Roos et al. 2011; Kingdon 2015), or consider them to belong to one genus Procolobus, with two subgenera (Procolobus for the Olive Colobus and Piliocolobus for the red colobus) (Oates et al. 1994, Grubb et al. 2003, 2013; Oates 2011). Ting (2008) placed all the red colobus as subspecies of Procolobus (Piliocolobus) badius. The 2008 IUCN Red List assessments followed the taxonomy of Grubb et al. (2003).

The arrangement of two genera in Groves (2001, 2005, 2007) is followed here: a monotypic Procolobus de Rochebrune, 1887 (Olive Colobus) and polytypic Piliocolobus de Rochebrune, 1887 (red colobus).

The form lulindicus, recognised by Colyn and Verhayen (1987), Colyn (1987, 1991), Grubb et al. (2003), and Struhsaker and Grubb (2013) (the last as a subspecies of Procolobus rufomitratus) was considered by Groves (2001, 2007) to be a junior synonym of Piliocolobus foai. Gautier-Hion et al. (1999) placed all the Central African red colobus as subspecies of Procolobus pennanti [sic], including lulindicus. Kingdon (1997, 2015) treated the Central African red colobus, including lulindicus, as subspecies of oustaleti. The consensus at a workshop held in Rome in 2016 to update the African primate Red List assessments was that it would be expedient (precautionary) to continue to recognise this species, considering the threatened status of all the red colobus monkeys.

Studies are needed to determine whether or not specimens from Cameroon and Togo belong to the same species (M.-O. Rödel pers. comm.). It is currently unclear whether there are any Werneria species in Togo, but it has been suggested that if there is one, it could be either W. preussi or alternatively a different, distinct species, Werneria africanus, which would be an endemic species to Togo (see Segniagbeto et al. 2007).

Following Amiet and Dowsett-Lemaire (2000), Leptodactylodon bueanus is here considered a separate species from L. albiventris (as opposed to a subspecies of L. albiventris following Amiet (1987)). Rödel and Pauwels (2003) provisionally assigned records of L. albiventris from Equatorial Guinea (De la Riva 1994) to L. stevarti.
Iskandar (pers. comm.) has examined the type specimen, and considers that this species does not belong to the genus Oreophryne.
We follow Rödel and Pauwels (2003) in provisionally assigning records of Leptodactylodon albiventris from Equatorial Guinea (De la Riva 1994) to this newly described species.
The Atelognathus praebasalticus complex needs to be re-examined taxonomically. There are four known subspecies, some of which may constitute new species. Some of these subspecies may be extinct.

authority: Evans, Carter, Tobias, Kelley, Hanner & Tinsley, 2008
taxonomicNotes: This species is considered to be the sister taxon of Xenopus wittei (Evans et al. 2008).

This taxon was previously considered as belonging to the otherwise central Ryukyu species Takydromus smaragdinus (Boulenger 1887), but is now recognized to be a distinct, phylogenetically rather distant species (see Lin et al. 2002, Ota et al. 2002).

This species is valid in the genus Haplochromis in FishBase (Froese and Pauly 2016) based on Van Oijen (1996). It is valid in the genus Pundamilia in Catalog of Fishes (Eschmeyer et al. 2016) based on Seehausen et al. (1998). Previously, this species appeared on the Red List in the genus Pundamilia but the taxonomy of FishBase is now followed.

Current scientific review of the taxonomy of wild equids (Groves 1986) places Przewalski's Horse as a subspecies of the extinct Equus ferus. Although Przewalski's Horse (Equus ferus przewalskii) can hybridize with domestic horses (Equus ferus caballus) to produce fertile offspring (Ryder et al. 1978, Trommerhausen-Smith et al. 1979), the existence of 2n = 66 chromosomes in Przewalski's Horse identifies it as being more different from its domestic relatives (2n = 64) than are any two breeds of domestic horse (Ryder 1994). Furthermore, mitochondrial DNA research has shown that the Przewalski's Horse is not the ancestor of modern domestic horses (Vilà et al. 2001). Przewalski's Horse also show a number of consistent differences in their appearance as compared to domestic horse breeds: the mane is short and erect when in good body condition; forelocks are nearly nonexistent; the upper part of the tail has short guard hairs; a dark dorsal stripe runs from the mane down the spine to the tail; several dark stripes can be present on the carpus and generally the tarsus (Groves 1994). Przewalski's Horses grow a thick mane in winter, which contrary to domestic horses they shed each spring with the rest of their winter coat.

Other studies of the genetic differences between Przewalski's and domestic horses have indicated very little genetic distinction between them. Only four alleles at four separate serological marker loci have been identified as specific to Przewalski's Horse (Bowling and Ryder 1987); the vast majority of blood protein variants are present in both Przewalski's and domestic horses and even the fastest evolving DNA region known in mammals (the mitochondrial DNA control region), does not show significant differences between the two types of horse (Ishida et al. 1995, Oakenfull and Ryder 1998). Thus it is clear that Przewalski's and domestic horses are very closely related and have in the past interbred, but the fixed chromosomal number difference between them indicates that they are distinct populations (Oakenfull et al. 2000). A variety of molecular studies support their phylogenetic relationship as sister taxa (Steiner et al. 2012, Côté et al. 2013) diverging between 150,000 and 250,000 years ago (Goto et al. 2011, Steiner and Ryder 2011).
Mouldingia orientalis was assessed as Endangered in 1996. Köhler and Criscione (2013) transferred this species to Nanotrachia.

Thomson and Georges (2009) placed the taxon bellii in the genus Myuchelys, which they separated from Elseya, a classification followed in the revision by Georges and Thomson (2010) and Fielder et al. (2015). Molecular and morphological assessments have found no substantive differences among populations from the Gwydir, Namoi, and Border Rivers catchments (Fielder et al. 2012; Fielder 2013), with little evidence to support any taxonomic subdivision of M. bellii at the species or subspecies level, as suggested by Cann (1998) and Wells (2007). The generic name Wollumbinia (Wells 2002) has been rejected as unavailable for this taxon (Georges and Thomson 2010; Fielder et al. 2015).

This taxon was listed as Elseya sp. 1 in the 1996 and 2000 IUCN Red Lists. It was incorrectly spelled Elseya belli on the IUCN Red List from 2002, but corrected in 2009 to E. bellii following Rhodin et al. (2008).

Mesoclemmys dahli (Zangerl & Medem, 1958) was originally described in the genus Phrynops and subgenus Batrachemys, and was for a short while considered a subspecies of Phrynops nasutus, but has been considered a full species by most authors, including TTWG (2021). It was placed in the genus Mesoclemmys by Bour and Zaher (2005). Based on both mitochondrial and nuclear gene sequence variation, Vargas-Ramírez et al. (2012) confirmed the species distinction of Mesoclemmys dahli and M. zuliae, and identified two geographically isolated, weakly divergent populations within M. dahli (Córdoba and Cesar Departments, Colombia). Gallego-García et al. (2018) documented significant genetic structuring of M. dahli, with at least four subpopulations. Subsequently, Gallego-García et al. (2019) identified six genetically isolated populations divided among three higher-level genetic clusters. Given the results of both papers, they hypothesized that these population structures might be the result of habitat fragmentation and strong natural selection for adaptation to anthropogenic landscape changes, particularly forest clearing.

Pseudemys alabamensis has generally been considered a distinct species or subspecies since its description by Baur (1893). However, it was for a while synonymized under Pseudemys floridana by Carr (1952), until subsequently elevated again as a distinct species by Carr and Crenshaw (1957), which was further corroborated by Seidel (1994) using morphology and electrophoresis. Morphological analysis of the separate meta-populations in the Mobile-Tensaw Delta of Alabama and the Pascagoula and Biloxi Deltas in Mississippi by Leary et al. (2003) indicated that they are distinct from each other, but not enough to warrant taxonomic recognition. In a phylogeographic study using mitochondrial and nuclear markers, Moreno et al. (2022) found that these separate populations shared the same mitochondrial haplotype and had low genetic diversity, but that nuclear microsatellites indicated the populations to be distinct, which they concluded to be the result of genetic drift in isolated populations.

Genetic analysis of Trachemys taxa throughout Central and South America have suggested that T. adiutrix should perhaps be considered a subspecies of T. dorbigni (Fritz et al. 2011; Rodrigues et al. 2016; Fritz et al. 2024). However, this arrangement has not been universally accepted, and the TTWG (2021) considers dorbigni and adiutrix as separate species, not subspecies, while acknowledging that both arrangements are under consideration. Unpublished genetic analyses (Baretto, unpubl. data) do not support the proposal to consider T. adiutrix as a subspecies of T. dorbigni. This Red List assessment covers T. adiutrix as a separate species from T. dorbigni.

Cuora flavomarginata is generally considered to include the form evelynae as a subspecies (Ota et al. 2009), though Ernst et al. (2008) retained evelynae as a full species. The name sinensis has been applied to mainland Chinese populations as a subspecies, with the typical flavomarginata restricted to Taiwan, but recent reviews (Fritz and Havas 2007; Ota et al. 2009; TTWG 2010) have not recognised Taiwanese and mainland populations as taxonomically distinct.

Cuora yunnanensis has been suggested to be an old example of an inter-specific hybrid, but a 1908 museum specimen was found to have unique mt-DNA indicating it to be a valid species status (Parham et al. 2004), and recent trade specimens of unreported origin agreed genetically with the 1908 specimen (He et al. 2007).
Historically placed in Podocnemis. No subspecies are currently recognized.
Placed by some as a subspecies under C. chitra.

The species Dermatemys mawii possesses 16 mitochondrial haplotypes and three major lineages throughout its distribution range. The first lineage, with a restricted distribution, is located at the Mexico-Guatemala border in the Sartún and Salinas rivers (upper Usumacinta River basin) and has an approximate origin of one million years ago (Martínez-Gómez 2017). The second group is restricted to the Papaloapan River in Veracruz, Mexico. It is surrounded by two geographical barriers (the Isthmus of Tehuantepec in Oaxaca and the Sierra de Santa Martha in Veracruz). With the widest distribution, the third group spans the lower part of the Grijalva-Usumacinta system in the southeastern Gulf of Mexico to the Caribbean region, between Quintana Roo (Mexico) and Belize. Genetic differentiation among these D. mawii populations has been observed, and genetic diversity values range from low to high depending on the locality (HO = 0.158–0.609) (González-Porter et al. 2013; Gallardo-Alvarez et al. 2019). It has been suggested that the lineage located in the Sartún and Salinas rivers shows significant genetic differences, indicating possible species-level diversity; however, morphological differences have not been found (González-Porter et al. 2011), and no taxonomic change has been recommended or recognized (TTWG 2021).

The Spotted Turtle has been classified as the sole member of the genus Clemmys since the turn of the 21st century, when the genus was critically examined and revised to resolve paraphyly with respect to the clade encompassing Emys, Emydoidea, and Actinemys and possibly Terrapene (Bickham et al. 1996; Burke et al. 1996;  Lenk et al. 1999; Holman and Fritz 2001; Feldman and Parham 2002; Stephens and Wiens 2003). Clemmys is one of two monotypic genera within the subfamily Emydinae—the other being Emydoidea (Lipps 2021; TTWG 2021, 2025).

Glyptemys insculpta was historically primarily known as Clemmys insculpta. This nomenclature was used in previous IUCN Red List assessments (1996, 2006, 2011). Although for a long time allocated to the genus Clemmys, the paraphyletic nature of that genus required reclassifying both the Bog Turtle (Glyptemys muhlenbergii) and the Wood Turtle (Glyptemys insculpta) into genus Glyptemys (Holman and Fritz 2001; Parham and Feldman 2002).

Previously this species was known as Clemmys muhlenbergii, under which name it appeared in IUCN Red Lists until 2008. Paraphyly of the traditional genus Clemmys (sensu McDowell 1964) required placement of the species muhlenbergii and insculpta into Glyptemys.

Scientific debate of the taxonomy and number of species present in the genus Graptemys continues (Praschag et al. 2017) in contrast to Lindemann (2013) or the conclusions of Thomson et al. (2018). However, since its description there has been little debate regarding the unique characters and appropriate status as a distinct species for Graptemys caglei.

After its initial description as Terrapene coahuila by Schmidt and Owens (1944), Mertens and Wermuth (1955) designated the taxon a subspecies of Terrapene ornata, but all subsequent authors have considered it a separate species, including Wermuth and Mertens (1961, 1977). Iverson et al. (2007) proposed T. coahuila as the sister taxon to T. carolina. There are no subspecies and no synonymized taxa. Martin et al. (2021) interpreted their genetic results as showing T. coahuila to be a full species deep within the T. carolina clade, most closely related to the western population of T. c. major. TTWG (2021) agrees that T. coahuila is a distinct species.

This taxon was considered a lineage of Pseudemys scripta, but noted by Legler (1990) to be sufficiently distinct to warrant subspecies rank as P. s. taylori, and formally elevated to full species in the genus Trachemys by Seidel (2002). Molecular analyses of the genus Trachemys was performed by Jackson et al. (2008), Spinks et al. (2009), Parham et al. (2015), Seidel and Ernst (2016) and they were consistent in finding T. taylori to be monophyletic. However, recent work by Fritz et al. (2024) found little genetic differentiation from Trachemys venusta and proposed reassigning the taxon as Trachemys venusta taylori. This suggested change is still under consideration by the taxonomic community; for now we assess it as a species, T. taylori.

Traditionally placed in Chinemys, reevesii has in recent years been widely accepted as a species of Mauremys (Fritz and Havas 2007, TTWG 2010). The species includes the forms Chinemys megalocephala Fang, 1934, Geoclemys grangeri Schmidt, 1925, and Damonia unicolor Gray, 1873, which have on occasion been considered as valid species or subspecies, but have in recent years been treated consistently as synonyms of reevesii (Guo et al. 1997, Fritz and Havas 2007, TTWG 2010, Rhodin et al. 2021).

The Green Sturgeon was once considered conspecific with the Sakhalin Sturgeon (Acipenser mikadoi) and the two forms were synonymized (Berg 1948). However, recent molecular data on three mitochondrial genes and morphometric studies show large differences between North American and Asian forms (Birstein and DeSalle 1998, North et al. 2002). Sakhalin Sturgeon is now believed extinct in Japan, Korea and China and the species sustains only a remnant population in Russia's Tumnin River where there is a hatchery (Adams et al. 2002).

The genus Nesolestes may be a synonym of Neurolestes (Gambles 1970). Similar specimens from adjacent Cameroon may pertain to another species (Vick unpubl). The original 2009 Red List assessment included this species in Nesolestes. In 2017, it was moved to genus Neurolestes.

authority: Barej, Schmitz, Penner, Doumbia, Brede, Hillers & Rödel, 2015
taxonomicNotes: This is a split from the broader concept of Odontobatrachus natator (Barej et al. 2015).

Feldhamer et al. (1988) and Geist (1998) included Dama mesopotamica as a subspecies of Dama dama, though it was regarded as a separate species by Haltenorth (1959), Ferguson et al. (1985), Uerpmann (1987), and Harrison and Bates (1991). We follow Pitra et al. (2004) and Randi et al. (2001) in treating D. mesopotamica as a separate species, based on a major study on the evolution and phylogeny of old world deer.
Prior to its naming, this species was confused with Epipedobates boulengeri and Epipedobates espinosai according Cisneros-Heredia and Yánez-Muñoz (2011 "2010").
This species was previously included in the genus Hyloscirtus, then to moved to the genus Colomascirtus by Duellman et al. (2016), and later retained as Hyloscirtus by Rojas-Runjaic et al. (2018).

authority: Guayasamin, Rivera-Correa, Arteaga-Navarro, Culebras, Bustamante, Pyron, Peñafiel, Morochz, & Hutter, 2015
taxonomicNotes: This is a split from the broader concept of Hyloscirtus alytolylax (Guayasamín et al. 2015). Museum specimens recorded as H. alytolylax should be reviewed as some may pertain to this species.

authority: Coloma, Carvajal-Endara, Dueñas, Paredes-Recalde, Morales-Mite, Almeida-Reinoso, Tapia, Hutter, Toral-Contreras & Guayasamin, 2012

taxonomicNotes: Brito et al. (2017) provided records of Pristimantis aff. gualacenio from Tinguichaca, Sambalán and Cisnian, Morona Santiago.
This species was previously considered as incertae sedis, however the validity of this species has now been confirmed (Jungfer et al. 2013).
The taxonomic status of this species is unclear.

Lötters et al. (2011) provided molecular evidence that this nominal species is a complex of species. The specimens from the Departamento Piura, Peru belong to an undescribed species (Coloma et al. 2000).
This species was removed from the synonymy of Atelopus ignescens by McDiarmid (1971).

Coloma, Lötters and Salas (2000) redefined Atelopus ignescens and restricted the species to the north-central Andes of Ecuador. Populations in northern Ecuador (in Cachi Province), and adjacent southern Colombia (Narino Department), previously assigned to Atelopus ignescens are now considered to belong to an undescribed species.

The specimens in the lowlands may be a different species as they are based on old museum specimens and doubtful collecting localities. Tapia et al. (2017) excluded these records from the current known range of the species and therefore they are considered as Presence Uncertain in this reassessment until they can be reassigned to another taxon. Reports from Valle del Cauca Department in Colombia, refer to an unidentified, probably unnamed species and are excluded from this assessment.
Records other than from the type locality (e.g. Peters 1973) are considered here to refer to Atelopus planispina.

The taxonomic status of Atelopus planispina and A. palmatus was discussed by Coloma (1997). Records of A. palmatus from Napo Province actually belong to this species (Ecuador Red List Assessment Workshop July 2016).

Gluesenkamp and Guayasamin (2008) this species is reported in the Sumaco Volcano, but in fact they are males of Osornophryne sumacoensis verified by Páez-Moscoso et al. (2011). The identity of the specimens from the northern part of Parque Nacional Llanganates requires confirmation.

Hyloxalus cevallosi and Leucostethus fugax are similar (as originally described in 1991 and 1993 respectively) and their type localities are from the same general area in Río Bobonaza, Provincia Pastaza. The description of H. cevallosi was based on a single male, with no mention of having a Finger III swollen and by having a brownish-grey venter. Whereas the L. fugax description was based on a series of specimens, of which the holotype was a male with a Finger III swollen and a uniform cream venter. After 1993, collections in the area have revealed specimens of only one species with a Finger III swollen in males and having tubercles in the eyelid (present also in other species of Leucostethus). The specimens with these unique two features have been identified in museum collections ambiguously either as L. fugax or H. cevallosi (e.g. QCAZ Bioweb). Until further verification of the identity of H. cevallosi is undertaken we treat H. cevallosi as occurring only from the localities mentioned in the original description (Coloma and Duellman in prep.).
The subpopulation from Pastaza Province reported by Coloma (1995) belongs to an undescribed species (L. Coloma pers. comm.)
This form probably contains a number of undescribed species, according to Rivero (1991).
This species needs a more detailed taxonomic review, as it may be a species confused with Hyloxalus maculosus (J.C. Sánchez pers. comm. 2021).

Its possible that this is a species complex, with the northern-most subpopulation appearing different from the subpopulation at the type locality in the Cordillera del Condor (D.F. Cisneros-Heredia and M. Ortega-Andrade pers. comm. 2021).

The assignment of the specimens from San Francisco de Las Pampas to this species is tentative, but is justified by the absence of significant differences between the original description of Paruwrobates whymperi and these specimens. Paruwrobates erythromos (Vigle and Miyata 1980) is similar to P. whymperi and might be conspecific. However, comparison of P. erythromos and the type specimen of P. whymperi is complicated due to the bad preservation of the holotype of P. whymperi; differences in ventral colour patterns might be a preservation artefact. Comparison of P. erythromos and the specimens from San Francisco de Las Pampas reveal that they are not conspecific (Coloma 1995).

Santos et al. (2014) moved this species from Ameerega to Hyloxalus, then Grant et al. (2017) moved it to the current genus and suggest the possibility that it is the same species as Paruwrobates whymperi.
The genus Gastrotheca has recently been moved from the family Hylidae (Faivovich et al. 2005).
This species was previously within the genus Hyla but has recently been moved to the resurrected genus Hyloscirtus (Faivovich et al. 2005).

This species was previously included in the genus Hyla but was moved to the resurrected genus Hyloscirtus by Faivovich et al. (2005) and then to Colomascirtus by Duellman et al. (2016), but later retained as Hyloscirtus by Rojas-Runjaic et al. (2018).

This species was previously included in the genus Hyla but was moved to the resurrected genus Hyloscirtus by Faivovich et al. (2005) and then to Colomascirtus by Duellman et al. (2016), but later retained as Hyloscirtus by Rojas-Runjaic et al. (2018).
This species was previously within the genus Hyla but has recently been moved to the resurrected genus Hyloscirtus (Faivovich et al. 2005).

This is a species complex and the only confirmed localities are Sigsig in the province of Azuay and Urdaneta in the province of Loja (Székely et al. 2017, Urgilés et al. 2017; M.H. Yánez-Muñoz pers. comm. 2021). Urgilés et al. (2017) revised the species, recognizing Pristimantis cajanuma, from within the previous concept. Lynch (1979) states that this species occurs on the eastern Andean Cordillera from the Cuenca hoya to the Loja hoya in southern Ecuador. However, Urgilés et al. (2017). suggest that this distribution might be inaccurate and needs to be reviewed, as many of the records are probably erroneous belonging to very similar, but in fact different species.
This is a restricted concept of Pristimantis ornatissimus following the split of the broader concept into this and Pristimantis ecuadorensis (Guayasamin et al. 2017).

The forms in the north (Imbabura and Pichincha) and south (Bolivar) of this range pertain to undescribed species (Rojas Runjaic 2012, Franco Mena 2020, Ecuador National Red List Assessment Workshop 2020).

Pristimantis riveti was described from a single specimen collected in Ecuador, El Mirador (Despax 1911). This species name was not associated with any population until Lynch (1969) incorrectly identified subpopulations of P. curtipes as “P. riveti” (Lynch 1979). Subsequently, Lynch (1979) provided a species account based on specimens collected near Cuenca, Azuay Province, in southern Ecuador, but remarkably, excluded the type locality from his account. Subsequent references to the species (e.g., Almendáriz and Orcés 2004, Székely et al. 2016) relied on the morphological characterization provided by Lynch (1979) and ascribed the binomen to subpopulations from Azuay, Cañar and Loja Provinces. 

Various species (e.g., P. gloria, P. lutzae, P. multicolor and P. chomskyi) have been mistakenly identified as P. riveti (Páez and Ron 2019). Therefore, this is a restricted concept of Pristimantis riveti following the split of the broader concept into this, P. gloria, P. lutzae, P. multicolor and P. chomskyi (Páez and Ron 2019).

Some previous authors referred to this species as Eleutherodactylus trepidotus (e.g., Lynch and Duellman 1980, Lynch 1984, Almendáriz et al. 2004, Stuart et al. 2008) until Pristimantis festae was deemed the senior synonym (Heinicke et al. 2007, Hedges et al. 2008).
It is a species complex.
In the Duovox clade of Ron et al. (2006).
The northern subpopulation could belong to an undescribed species.

In the genus Nymphargus according to Cisneros-Heredia and McDiarmid (2007). Nymphargus buenaventura is distinguished from other species of glassfrogs by a combination of morphological features and colour patterns (Cisneros-Heredia and Yánez-Muñoz 2007).

In the genus Nymphargus following Cisneros-Heredia and McDiarmid (2007). Guayasamin et al. (2006) indicate that the species can be distinguished from other similar species by a combination of morphological and colouration differences and allopatric distributions. Guayasamin et al. (2006) adopt the Evolutionary Species Concept in their description of Nymphargus wileyi.

Frost (2021) indicates that this taxon was removed from the synonymy of Colostethus bocagei by Santos et al. (2003), by implication of their placement of this taxon in a phylogenetic tree away from C. bocagei, where it had been placed by Coloma (1995); but Coloma in Duellman (2004) suggested that C. maculosus remains a synonym of C. bocagei, which is contrary to the results of Santos et al. (2003). Páez-Vacas et al. (2010) considered this species to be part of the Hyloxalus bocagei complex, distinct from H. bocagei.
Atelopus petersi can be distinguished from other similar species by a combination of morphological features and different colour patterns (Coloma et al. 2007).
Formerly included within Leptodactylus pentadactylus; separated by Heyer (2005).
Atelopus onorei can be distinguished from other similar species by its colour patterns (Coloma et al. 2007).
It is possible that this species could be a taxonomic complex, however the current data cannot confirm this (Guayasamin et al., 2015).

authority: Arteaga, Pyron, Peñafiel, Romero-Barreto, Culebras, Bustamante, Yánez-Muñoz & Guayasamin, 2016
taxonomicNotes: Some subpopulations of Pristimantis walkeri in northwestern Ecuador belong to P. nietoi (Arteaga-Navarro et al. 2016).

authority: Arteaga, Pyron, Peñafiel, Romero-Barreto, Culebras, Bustamante, Yánez-Muñoz & Guayasamin, 2016
taxonomicNotes: Subpopulations of Pristimantis walkeri in southwestern Ecuador belong to P. buenaventura (Arteaga-Navarro et al. 2016).

This is a split from the broader concept of Pristimantis calcarulatus (Hutter and Guayasamin 2015). Despite being nearly morphologically identical, these taxa have considerable genetic distance between them.

This is a split from the broader concept of Pristimantis calcarulatus (Hutter and Guayasamin 2015). The Río Guayllabamba south of the two reserves appears to serve as a geographical barrier separating P. cedros from P. calcarulatus and its sister taxon, P. pahuma (Hutter and Guayasamin 2015). Despite being nearly morphologically identical, these taxa have considerable genetic distance between them.

Páez and Ron (2019) identify that Pristimantis hampatusami (Yánez-Muñoz et al. 2016) and Pristimantis prometeii (Székely et al. 2016) correspond to the same species and propose that P. prometeii is a junior synonym of P. hampatusami.

DNA-based studies have shown that there are two genetic lineages within Sandelia bainsii; a southern lineage that is distributed in the Kowie, Great Fish and Keiskamma river systems, and a northern lineage that occurs in the Igoda and Buffalo river systems (Roos 2005, Chakona et al. unpublished. Samples from the Xulu and Nahoon river systems have not yet been analysed genetically. Morphological assessments and additional genetic analyses are being done to determine whether or not the northern and southern lineages of S. bainsii represent two distinct species. Both lineages will be included in this assessment, pending findings from the ongoing taxonomic evaluation of this species.
Formerly placed in family Bagridae, but recognized in a separate family by Mo (1991) and de Pinna (1998) (Nelson 2006).

Serranochromis robustus and Serranochromis jallae were previously considered to be subspecies of Serranochromis robustus. They are now both considered to be valid at the species level - this is therefore the more restricted concept of Serranochromis robustus, which was previously assessed at the subspecies level.

authority: Wood, Grismer, Grismer, Ahmad, Chan & Bauer, 2009
Grismer, Quah, Wood, Anuar, Muin, Davis, Murdoch, Grismer & Cota, 2016
Grismer, Wood, Anuar, Riyanto, Ahmad, Muin, Sumontha, Grismer, Chan, Quah & Pauwels, 2014
taxonomicNotes: Cnemaspis temiah was described based on a population of Cnemaspis from the Cameron Highlands that was long recognized but previously considered to belong to C. flavolineata (Grismer et al. 2014).

It must be noted that P. capensis formerly belonged to the genus Barbus, but following the work of Yang et al. (2015), Pseudobarbus was expanded to include additional species resulting in the renaming of the Breede-Berg River whitefish to ‘Pseudobarbus’ capensis (Skelton 2016). These nomenclatural changes were made because the type specimen on which the name Barbus capensis was based was re-identified as the Cape Whitefish or Witvis, and not the Clanwilliam Yellowfish, as previously thought (Skelton 2016). The name ‘Pseudobarbus’ capensis (Smith, 1841) for the Berg-Breede whitefish is in tune with the generic alignment of Yang et al. (2015).

It must be noted that 'Pseudobarbus' erubescens formerly belonged to the genus Barbus, but following the work of Yang et al. (2015), the genus Pseudobarbus was expanded to include additional species resulting in the renaming of the Twee River redfin to ‘Pseudobarbus’ erubescens (Skelton 2016).

It must be noted that Enteromius treurensis formerly belonged to the genus Barbus, but following the work of Yang et al. (2015), the genus Enteromius was expanded to include additional species resulting in the renaming of the Enteromius treurensis (Skelton 2016).

Most recently, Yang et al. (2015) proposed that the genus Pseudobarbus should be expanded to include all tetraploid cyprinines from southern Africa. This suggestion was accepted pending a critical evaluation of the generic status of these additional tetraploid species (Skelton 2016). Barbus trevelyani is one of the species that had been transferred to the genus “Pseudobarbus” (Skelton 2016). Skelton et al. (2018) subsequently erected a new genus, Amatolacypris, and transferred “Pseudobarbus” trevelyani to this genus. Amatolacypris trevelyani was described from the Buffalo River System, with its distribution extending into the neighbouring Keiskamma River System. However, Ellender (2013) uncovered species level genetic divergences between the Buffalo population (Amatolacypris trevelyani sensu stricto) and the Keiskamma population (hereafter Amatolacypris ‘trevelyani sp. Keiskamma’). These two lineages will thus be assessed separately. This assessment is on Amatolacypris trevelyani sensu stricto.

Smith (1841) described the first redfin species as Barbus (Pseudobarbus) burchelli, but the origin of the specimens that were used for this description is unknown, and no type material exists (Barnard 1943). When Skelton (1988) defined a monophyletic redfin genus, he raised Pseudobarbus to full genetic status and assigned a specimen from the Tradouw catchment as the neotype for P. burchelli. For two decades following Skelton’s (1988) revision, redfins from the Breede and three adjacent river systems (Heuningnes, Duiwenhoks and Goukou) were all considered to belong to Pseudobarbus burchelli (Skelton 2001). Subsequently, DNA-based studies by Swartz et al. (2009) and Chakona et al. (2013) revealed existence of four genetically distinct lineages within P. burchelli (referred to as Pseudobarbus sp. ‘burchelli Giant’, Pseudobarbus sp. ‘burchelli Tradouw’,  Pseudobarbus sp. ‘burchelli Breede’, and Pseudobarbus sp. ‘burchelli Heuningnes’). The Giant Redfin was described as a distinct species, Pseudobarbus skeltoni (Chakona & Swartz, 2013). There are ongoing investigations to determine the taxonomic integrity of the other three lineages of P. burchelli. If these lineages are found to constitute novel species, the name P. burchelli will become restricted to the Tradouw lineage (due to designation of the neotype from this river), the Heuningnes lineage will require a new name, and one of the three synonyms of P. burchelli may need to be re-validated for the widespread Breede lineage. Pending further taxonomic investigation, the Tradouw lineage is being provisionally designated as P. burchelli sensu stricto. This assessment is therefore only based on the Tradouw population.
The Verlorenvlei population of this species had been described as a new species Pseudobarbus verloreni (Chakona et al. 2014)

Pseudobarbus quathlambae was originally described by Barnard (1938) based on specimens that were collected from the uMkomaas River System in KwaZulu Natal Province of South Africa. The species had not been recorded from South Africa since the original specimens were collected from the type locality in 1938, until a recent survey led to the rediscovery of this species in the uMzimkhulu River System which is adjacent to the uMkomaas River system (Kubheka et al., 2017). Previous and ongoing genetic analyses have revealed the existence of three genetic lineages within P. quathlambae (Swartz 2005, Swartz et al. unpublished data). Detailed morphological assessment are being done to determine whether these lineages constitute separate taxonomic entities.

The population of the cryptic Silhouettea sibayi seems to be restricted to the upper part of Maputaland in KwaZulu-Natal (Lakes Sibaya, Kosi (KwaZulu-Natal (KZN), South Africa) and Piti (Mozambique)) with evidence that populations may extend south towards the aMatikulu and Thukela River with unconfirmed anecdotal observations from KwaZulu-Natal Ezemvelo Wildlife collections. Potential relationships between similar gobies within Maputaland and larger KZN are still unknown.

Undescribed but clearly different from specimens collected near the type locality for K. auriculata in Mozambique (voucher specimen held by the South African Institute of Aquatic Biodiversity (SAIAB)). The Kneria sp. nov. 'South Africa' population exists in a small geographic range near Lydenberg, Mpumalanga, South Africa.
Work in progress (Bloomer et al.) suggests that Chiloglanis do not migrate very much. An analysis of population structuring would be valuable to nature conservators when developing managements plans.

We follow Veith et al. (2003), in considering this species to be distinct from Rana macrocnemis at the species level. It is confirmed through molecular data to occur in Çakıroluk (type locality) and Girdev Lake (U. Kaya pers. comm. December 2018).
This species is very similar to Liolaemus wiegmanni, and it has been suggested the two taxa may be conspecific (Etheridge 2000).

authority: D. Holyoak, G. Holyoak, Yanes, Santana, García, Castro, Artiles, Alonso & Ibáñez, 2014
taxonomicNotes: The genus Vermetum was moved from Gastrodontidae to Pristilomatidae (Holyoak et al. 2014).
This is a polymorphic species that presents different morphotypes adapted to aquatic or terrestrial environments (Cuello et al. 2008).
The species Zeuneriana amplipennis occurs in the eastern Balkans. As the species is morphologically quite similar to Z. marmorata, the status of both species needs to be studied.

authority: Grismer, Belabut, Quah, Chan, Wood & Hasim, 2014

taxonomicNotes: The taxonomic history of Cyrtodactylus pulchellus is summarized by Grismer et al. (2016). This species was described from Pulau Penang based on a small number of characters by Cantor (1847), and the name was subsequently widely applied for populations on nearby islands and the Malay Peninsula (Grismer et al. 2016). Over the past decade, subpopulations outside Penang have been described as distinct species (summarized in Grismer et al. 2016), restricting C. pulchellus sensu stricto to this island.

authority: Grismer, Wood, Quah, Anuar, Muin, Sumontha, Ahmad, Bauer, Wangkulangkul, Grismer & Pauwels, 2012

taxonomicNotes: Harvengia vietnamita is a remarkable blind species, very isolated phyletically, for which the genus Harvengia and the subtribe Harvengina has been erected (Ferrer 2004). It has therefore to be considered as one of the most remarkable endemic species of Hon Chong, and a major issue regarding biodiversity conservation. Further taxonomic analyses are needed to decide if the subpopulation in the northern hills are distinct of those from the southern hill from where holotype was described. For now they are considered the same species.

Currently this species belongs to the genus Chamalycaeus. A taxonomic revision of the family Alycaeidae is currently underway, which may result in C. micronus being moved to an independent genus (B. Páll-Gergely pers. comm. 2016).

Currently this species belongs to the genus Chamalycaeus. A taxonomic revision of the family Alycaeidae is currently underway, which may result in C. micronus being moved to an independent genus (B. Páll-Gergely pers. comm 2016).

The Bonobo is also known as the Gracile Chimpanzee; formerly it was known as Pygmy Chimpanzee and Dwarf Chimpanzee. Population genetics suggest that Bonobos have a stable population history (Eriksson et al. 2004, Schubert et al. 2013), and that gene flow between populations is constrained by the larger riverine barriers (although Bonobos regularly enter water, they do not swim).

Reviewed by McDowell and Bogert (1954), McDowell (1967), Mertens (1961) and Underwood (1957). Based on a study of the structure of the ears, McDowell (1967) considered the family to be closely related to the extinct Mosasauridae from the Cretaceous and Rieppel (1983) showed that the only living species in the family, Lanthanotus borneensis (Steindachner, 1878), exhibits no special similarities with primitive snakes in his study of cranial structure. Rieppel (1980) showed that the genus Lanthanotus Steindachner, 1878, forms the sister-taxon of Varanus, the two lineages together forming the sister-taxon of the New World Heloderma.

authority: Grismer, Huat, Siler, Chan, Wood, Grismer, Sah & Ahmad, 2011

taxonomicNotes: The species status needs to be revisited (P. Oliver pers. comm. 2017). It exhibits very low genetic divergence and few morphological differences from two species occurring in nearby areas (P. Oliver pers. comm. 2017).

Molecular studies have demonstrated that all shads endemic to the Aegean, Black and Caspian Sea basins, including Alosa macedonica, share close phylogenetic relationships (Faria et al. 2006, Geiger et al. 2014, Vernygora et al. 2018). Their taxonomy may therefore be revised in the future .

Lee (1959: 254) listed it as Octochaetus microchaetus "incertae sedis" (of uncertain status) although it was included in checklists by Blakemore (2004, 2008, 2010, 2012).   Benham's original 1950 paper is online here - http://rsnz.natlib.govt.nz/volume/rsnz_78/rsnz_78_02_003870.html (December, 2016).

In the conservation breeding programme, Partula garrettii and P. tristis were confused. Initially, breeding animals were identified as P. garrettii, then re-identified (incorrectly) as P. tristis. They are now confirmed to be P. garrettii; there are no captive populations of P. trisis.
The species is currently divided into two sub-species that differ only slightly, but both anatomically and gentically.

This species was unambigously defined when Blakemore (2010) tied morphology of its name-bearing holotype to its DNA barcode from a tissue sample, as per Blakemore et al. (2010).  Nevertheless, it yet requires comparison with all congeners.

Closest taxonomic affinites are indicated in Blakemore (2010, 2011, 2012).

DNA is available for this species which would confim its identity and relationships with other taxa within its eleven congeners (see Blakemore, 2012).  The closest taxonomic and toopical association unsurprisingly appears with Deinodrilus gorgon Blakemore, 2010 from the same type-locality.
Megascolex species from Australia and New Zealand are now placed in the genus Anisochaeta (Blakemore 2010).

Ude (1905) thought his species may be the same as T. orthostichon (Schmarda, 1861) but this was disputed by Blakemore (2000, 2012) and by the current study (Blakemore, unpublished).  Of note is that a revision based on types by Michaelsen (1917) did not mention this similarity and showed the male pores to be radically different, both to T. unipapillatus and to Lee’s (1962) revision of “Megascolides orthostichon” that Blakemore (2014) thought was T. maorica (see Blakemore 2014 fig. 12 comparisons with Benham’s original figures).  Seemingly, Lee (1962) was unaware of Michaelsen’s (1917) revision and also missed his own (Lee 1959) review as another apparently significant difference is the spermathecal pores being segmental in T. maorica rather than intersegmental as in T. unipapillata.

For T. orthostichon the spermathecal pore location was given by Lee (1959) as 7/8/9 the same as T. unipapillatus but, as already noted, Michaelsen (1917) found the male pores and markings, at least, to differ conclusively for these two taxa.



An alleged “Megascolides ?unipapillatus (WM11)” report by non-specialists has DNA similar or identical with several other “species” including “Megascolides repans (WM10)” (online at: doi:10.1016/j.ympev.2010.09.024 - as was noted by Blakemore 2010, 2011, 2012).  Most likely either/or both are actually of T. maorica which Edwards (1954) and Lee (1959) had already recorded from Little Barrier Island more than 50 years ago as also noted by Reid et al. (1982).  Further directed eco-taxonomic study by specialists is requried with recourse to DNA as per Blakemore et al. (2010), Blakemore (2010, 2016).

This species was revised by Wang et al. (1993). It is a well-defined species sharply distinct from the parapatric or marginally sympatric M. chrysogaster; see Grubb (1982).

Four subspecies are recognized by Grubb (2005):
M. b. berezovskii Flerov, 1929; Sichuan, Qinghai, Tibet
M. b. bjiangensis Wang & Li, 2003; northwestern Yunnan;
M. b. caobangis Dao, 1969; Yunnan, Guangxi, Guangdong, northern Viet Nam;
M. b. yunguiensis Wang & Ma, 1993; Yunnan-Guizhou highland, Hunan, Jiangxi; Wang (2003) lists an unnamed form as occurring in south Gansu, Ningxia, south Shaanxi, west Hubei and west Henan (Wang et al. 1993); Wemmer (1998).

Moschus anhuiensis has in the past been included in this species (Yang et al. 2003); it is still not clear whether or not the recognized subspecies are really part of this species. The subspecies M. b. caobangis is a good candidate for separate species status, but this has never been looked at (J.W. Duckworth pers. comm.). Until recently the Viet Namese population was considered to be M. moschiforus by Russian scientists (much literature follows this; J.W. Duckworth pers. comm.).
Cryptotettix macrophthalmus probably belongs to the genus Rhynchotettix (H. Devriese pers. comm. 2016).

This species was previously confused with other guitarfishes and erroneously considered to be widespread in the Indo-Pacific. Rhinobatos formosensis from Taiwan is now known to be a junior synonym of R. schlegelii (Last et al. 2016a, Last et al. 2016b).
The species is distinguished from its regional congeners, the Bottlennose Guitarfish (Rhinobatos schlegelii) by colour, morphology, and meristic characters (Last et al. 2014).

The taxonomy of the 'whitespotted wedgefish' species-complex (which includes several Indo-West Pacific wedgefishes) is not fully resolved, and the status of the Taiwanese Wedgefish is in need of critical re-evaluation by comparing it to other regional species.

authority: Ruocco, Lucifora, Díaz de Astarloa, Mabragaña & Delpiani, 2012

taxonomicNotes:
Recent changes to the systematics of Rhinobatos have elevated the subgenus Glaucostegus to full generic status and placed this genus into a family of its own: Glaucostegidae (Compagno 2005, Last et al. 2016a).

Séret and McEachran (1986) tentatively synonymised Glaucostegus petiti (reported from the Western Indian Ocean) with G. cemiculus because the morphometrics and the meristics of the holotype and only known specimen of G. petiti fell into the ranges of variation of G. cemiculus. Glaucostegus cemiculus is an Eastern Atlantic species, however, and does not occur in the Western Indian Ocean from which G. petiti is reported (Bernard Séret pers. comm. 2008). It is possible that there was a mistake in the origin of the type specimen, although the collector Georges Petit did explore the Tuléar region of Madagasar. No further specimens of Glaucostegus petiti have been found to date, despite the presence of an active research station in Tuléar for decades, although other guitarfishes (Acroteriobatus annulatus and Rhinobatos holcorhynchus) have been collected from the region (Bernard Séret pers. comm. 2008).

Glaucostegus petiti is considered a synonym of G. cemiculus (William T. White and David A. Ebert pers. comm. 2016).
Last et al. (2016a) resurrected the species Telatrygon crozieri (Blythe, 1886) from its former synonymy with Dasyatis (=Telatrygon) zugei.

The genus Hypanus formerly was a junior synonym of Dasyatis (Kottelat, 2013); it was resurrected by Last et al. (2016) in their revision of the family Dasyatidae.

This species was mistakenly named Dasyatis macrophthalma in the title of the original description. A correction note was provided by Rosa et al. (2000).

Last et al. (2016) placed Dasyatis colarensis, D. garouaensis, D. geijskesi, D. margarita, D. margaritella, and Urogymnus ukpan within their newly described genus Fontitrygon. The two Western Atlantic species (F. colarensis and F. geijskesi) differ significantly from the other taxa in this genus and may prove to be non-congeneric with the four species from West Africa (Last et al. 2016).

This species is often mistaken with Hypanus guttatus but snout length and pelvic fin shape differ.

This species was previously confused with Pastinachus sephen with which it co-occurs (Last and Manjaji-Matsumoto 2010).

Amarasinghe et al. (2014) recognized the Knuckles Range population of Calotes liocephalus as a new species C. pethiyagodai on morphological grounds. These authors cautioned that little material was available for either this species or C. liocephalus and that further research should be conducted to confirm this arrangement..
