This species was formerly included in the Parathelphusidae but has recently been reassigned to the Gecarcinucidae; Parathelphusidae is now regarded as a junior synonym of Gecarcinucidae (Klause et al. in press).
Squatina aculeata is difficult to identify to the species level, therefore many of the records from fisher reports are often assigned only to the genus level. Misidentification is common.
Squatina oculata is difficult to identify to the species level, therefore many of the records from fishermen reports are often assigned only to genus level.

The Chilean Angelshark (Squatina armata), has previously been considered to be conspecific with the Pacific Angelshark (S. californica). Recent molecular evidence places S. armata as the sister taxon to the Neotropical angel sharks, indicating it is a distinct species (Stelbrink et al. 2010). Given its prior taxonomic placement, some literature on the elasmobranch fauna of Peru, Ecuador, and Colombia list S. californica as part of their coastal fauna, while others only mention S. armata. To add to the confusion, some country checklists include both S. armata and S. californica (see Cornejo et al. 2015). These records of S. californica occurring sympatrically with S. armata are likely incorrect, thus for the purpose of this assessment all angel shark records in the Southeast Pacific are considered to be S. armata. Further research is needed to elucidate the distributions of S. armata and S. californica, particularly in the Pacific coast of Central America and the northwest coast of South America.

There is some controversy concerning the taxonomy and nomenclature of Squatina species of the Southwest Atlantic. Squatina occulta described by Vooren and da Silva (1991) has been regarded as being a junior synonym of S. guggenheim by Soto (2001) in a species-level checklist of the region. Although evidence was not given to support this statement, Bernardes et al. (2005) followed Soto’s proposal. However, later studies have re-evaluated the species’ validity. The validity of S. occulta Vooren and da Silva, 1991 as a distinct species has been confirmed through the study of mitochondrial DNA by Stelbrink et al. (2010) and in a comparative study of Southwest Atlantic Squatina neurocrania (Carvalho et al. 2012). Both studies also recognized S. argentina, S. guggenheim, and S. occulta as the only valid species in the Southwest Atlantic (preceding the description of new species since that time, S. david and S. varii).

authority: Korn, Machado, Cristo & Cancela da Fonseca, 2010

taxonomicNotes: Hypsipetes siquijorensis (del Hoyo and Collar 2016) was previously listed as Ixos siquijorensis.
This species previously appeared on the IUCN Red List as Tilapia kottae. It is now valid in the genus Coptodon as Coptodon kottae (Lönnberg 1904).

Angel sharks (Squatina spp.) in the Eastern Atlantic and Mediterranean are difficult to identify to species level, therefore many of the records from fishers in the Mediterranean Sea are often assigned only to the genus level. Squatina squatina is the only species to occur in the Northeast Atlantic from Portugal northwards.
Birds of Polillo, supposedly larger, occasionally separated as subspecies mcgregori, but doubtfully valid. Monotypic.

Acanthagrion franciscoi fits none of the species groups defined by Leonard (1977). It keys out to
couplet 7 in Leonard’s key that leads to the viridescens, adustum and chararum groups. It differs
from the species of the viridescens group by having the modifications of genital ligula segment
3 not visible in ectal view. It differs from the species of the adustum group because the distal
part of genital ligula segment 3 is not incised. From the species of the chararum group it differs
by having the cercus slanting forward relative to the paraprocts from base to apex. A. franciscoi
differs from all known species of Acanthagrion by its small size (Rw 13.0 mm, Abd 17 mm),
a character it shares only with A. minutum Leonard, 1977, regarded by Leonard (1977) as the
smallest species of Acanthagrion.
Erythrodiplax ana fits in Borror’s Basalis Group.
Oxyagrion franciscoi is easily distinguished from all other congeners by its dominantly blue colours, a character it shares only with Oxyagrion ablutum (Calvert, 1909).

Slapcinsky and Krause (2016) compared Partula calypso, P. leucothoe and P. thetis to their newly described genus and species Sphendone insolita, and to other partulids and found that these three species require their own genus because their morphology is quite different from that of true Partula and from that of all other genera. Therefore, they resurrected the genus Palaopartula Pilsbry for these three snails.

Slapcinsky and Krause (2016) compared Partula calypso, P. leucothoe and P. thetis to their newly described genus and species Sphendone insolita, and to other partulids and found that these three species require their own genus because their morphology is quite different from that of true Partula and from that of all other genera. Therefore, they resurrected the genus Palaopartula Pilsbry for these three snails.

Slapcinsky and Krause (2016) compared Partula calypso, P. leucothoe and P. thetis to their newly described genus and species Sphendone insolita, and to other partulids and found that these three species require their own genus because their morphology is quite different from that of true Partula and from that of all other genera. Therefore, they resurrected the genus Palaopartula Pilsbry for these three snails.

Ninox philippensis, N. spilocephala, N. leventisi, N. reyi, N. rumseyi, N. spilonotus and N. mindorensis (del Hoyo and Collar 2014) were previously lumped as N. philippensis following Sibley and Monroe (1990, 1993).

Chilabothrus argentum is a recently discovered species (Reynolds et al. 2016), unknown until July 2015. Species description was based on genetic and morphological analysis of six specimens, in the context of other Bahamian and West Indian boas. This species is divergent from other West Indian boas at an mtDNA locus and is readily distinguishable based on morphometric and morphological characteristics.

Previously (del Hoyo and Collar 2014) placed in the genus Alopecoenas and before that in the genus Gallicolumba but transferred to Pampusana on the grounds of the latter’s priority (Bruce et al. 2016).
Sittiparus varius, S. owstoni, S. olivaceus and S. castaneoventris (del Hoyo and Collar 2016) were previously lumped as Parus varius following Sibley and Monroe (1990, 1993).

Based on patterns of genetic and morphological variation, Pauly et al. (2007) concluded that Ambystoma cingulatum should be split into two species, A. bishopi west of the Apalachicola-Flint rivers and A. cingulatum east of those rivers.

Based on mtDNA evidence,  A. cingulatum (sensu stricto) inhabiting the eastern Gulf Coastal Plain and the Atlantic Coastal Plain in northern Florida are distinct lineages; the Suwannee River and a large distributional gap separate them (Pauly et al. 2012). Further data are needed to determine whether these two lineages warrant recognition as different species (Pauly et al. 2012).
This species is morphologically somewhat similar to Plethodon elongatus, however they are genetically distinct (Mahoney 2001, Mead et al. 2005).

Vredenburg et al. (2007) examined the phylogeography of Rana muscosa as defined by Stebbins (2003) and determined that R. muscosa occurs in the southern Sierra Nevada and in mountains to the south and that populations in the Sierra Nevada north of this range comprise a distinct species (Rana sierrae).

There has been historical confusion and controversy regarding the taxonomic status of various leopard frog populations in the region occupied by Lithobates onca (Jaeger et al. 2001). The Vegas Valley Leopard Frog, R. fisheri, (now considered extinct) previously was regarded as a subspecies of R. onca. Jennings et al. (1995) conducted morphological comparisons and concluded that R. fisheri and R. onca are distinct species. Jaeger et al. (2001) examined variation in mtDNA, genomic DNA, and morphology and concluded that certain leopard frog populations in the Virgin River/Black Canyon (Colorado River) region represent a species (Lithobates onca) distinct from the lowland leopard frog (L. yavapaiensis). The Relict Leopard Frog was also found to be quite distinct from the Las Vegas Valley Leopard Frog (L. fisheri; now considered extinct) based on a genetic analysis of archival samples of that species (Hekkala et al. 2011). The Relict Leopard Frog was moved from the genus Rana to Lithobates by Frost et al. (2006), although that taxonomic revision remains contentious.
This species is a member of the Typhlomolge clade of central Texas Eurycea (Hillis et al. 2001).

Genetic studies showed that this form is distinct from populations of Lithobates capito and L. areolata, and has therefore been established as a separate species (Young and Crother 2001). This species was placed into the genus Lithobates by Frost et al. (2006). This action has been disputed by Yuan et al. (2016) who retained the name Rana and lowered Lithobates to the level of subgenus. The genus name Lithobates is currently recognized by Amphibian Species of the World and has therefore been retained for the purposes of this assessment. Additional taxonomic work is recommended to clarify this ongoing discussion.

This species was included in Eurycea neotenes by Sweet (1978, 1982), but was regarded as a distinct species by Chippindale et al. (1994) and was formally described as Eurycea naufragia by Chippindale et al. (2000).

Most references to Eurycea neotenes in the literature involve populations that Chippindale et al. (2000) considered Fern Bank Salamanders (E. pterophila) or members of the E. latitans or E. troglodytes species complexes
This species was removed from synonymy with Eurycea neotenes by Chippindale et al. (2000). It remains poorly circumscribed taxonomically, and warrants further research.
This species was included in Eurycea neotenes by Sweet (1978, 1982) and in previous publications. It was formally described as a distinct species by Chippindale et al. (2000).

Gyrinophilus gulolineatus was formerly regarded as a subspecies of G. palleucus; it was treated as a species by Brandon et al. (1986), Collins and Taggart (2002) and Crother et al. (2000). Petranka (1998) maintained this taxon as a subspecies of G. palleucus.

Some authors have regarded this species as conspecific with Gyrinophilus porphyriticus (Blaney and Blaney 1978), but the current consensus is that it is a valid species, based on morphometric analyses (Green and Pauley 1987).
Phillips (2017) found multiple distinct lineages within Eurycea wallacei, including high genetic diversity within multiple sampling sites.
This species was separated from Plethodon jordani (Highton and Peabody 2000).
This species was separated from Plethodon jordani (Highton and Peabody 2000).

Based on patterns of genetic and morphological variation, Pauly et al. (2007) concluded that Ambystoma cingulatum should be split into two species, A. bishopi west of the Apalachicola-Flint rivers and A. cingulatum east of those rivers.
This is a split from the broader concept of Aneides flavipunctatus (Reilly and Wake 2019).
This is a split from the broader concept of Desmognathus fuscus (Titus and Larson 1996, Tilley et al. 2008, Beamer and Lamb 2020).
Schistolais leontica (del Hoyo and Collar 2016) was previously listed as Prinia leontica.
This species was reclassified by Crampton (1916) as Partula otaheitana affinis. However, it was reinstated as Partula affinis by Johnson, Murray and Clarke (1986).

Has always been considered a distinct species but DNA analysis suggests that it may be a colour morph of Partula hyalina and as such remains a candidate for merging. Populations in one valley are genetically distinct.
This is a newly discovered (2006) species, described as Samoana meyeri the following year (Burch 2007). The species was placed in genus Partula in 2016 (Gerlach 2016).

This species has controversial taxonomy, made more confusing by widespread extinctions of varieties. It is likely that more than one species is involved. Eight subspecies are recognised by Crampton (1916); maybe two are extant in the wild.
This species was proposed as Partula affinis nodosa (Kondo and Burch 1983), but has remained accepted as Partula nodosa.
Partula suturalis has two subspecies: P. s. suturalis and P. t. vexillum.
Partula taeniata is now considered monotypic (MolluscaBase 2022).

This species was recorded as Partula solitaria on Moorea by Crampton (1932) but reclassified by Kondo (1968). High altitude populations of Samoana attenuata turned out to be S. burchi (Ó. Foighi pers. comm.).
This high altitude species in the wild previously was misidentified as Samoana attenuata, but recently these were reclassified as S. burchi after molecular analysis (O'Foighil pers. comm.).
Molecular analysis has revealed the possible existence of Samoana diaphana, previously believed endemic to Moorea, on Tahiti (O'Foighil, pers. comm.).
Lybius leucocephalus, L. senex and L. leucogaster (del Hoyo and Collar 2014) were previously lumped as L. leucocephalus following Sibley and Monroe (1990, 1993).

The older species name, Tridacna mbalavuana Ladd, 1934, takes priority over the newer name, Tridacna tevoroa Lucas, Ledua & Braley, 1990 (Newman and Gomez 2000). Tridacna mbalavuana was first described from fossils in Viti Levu, Fiji (Ladd 1934) and was already commonly known to the locals as ‘tevoro’, the devil clam. After closer examination of their morphological characteristics, the two species are now considered synonymous, with T. tevoroa being the junior synonym of T. mbalavuana (Newman and Gomez 2000).

The older species name, Tridacna squamosina Sturany, 1899, takes priority over the newer name, Tridacna costata Roa-Quiaoit, Kochzius, Jantzen, Zibdah & Richter, 2008 (Huber and Eschner 2011).

Tridacna squamosina was initially collected during the ‘Pola’ expedition to the Red Sea in the 1890s (Huber and Eschner 2011). Sturany (1899) first published the results of this expedition, which noted the presence of three Tridacna species in the Red Sea: T. maxima, T. squamosa, and a new species, T. elongata var. squamosina. This species was first rediscovered in the late 2000s in the Red Sea region by Richter et al. (2008). The largest individual so far was recorded at 35.9 cm long (J.J. ter Poorten pers. comm. 2024). A morphological comparison of materials revealed that Tridacna costata is identical to T. elongata var. squamosina, hence the former is now considered a junior synonym of T. squamosina.
This species was previously confused with Hynobius leechii and H. quelpaertensis and finally described by Min et al. (2016).

authority: Parra-Olea, Rovito, García-París, Maisano, Wake & Hanken, 2016
Parra-Olea, Rovito, García-París, Maisano, Wake & Hanken, 2016
Parra-Olea, Rovito, García-París, Maisano, Wake & Hanken, 2016

taxonomicNotes:
This relict species is known as probably the oldest extant butterfly linage, commonly referred to as a “Living Fossil” (Collins and Morris 1985), estimated to have evolved between 65 and 75 million years ago (Covarrubias-Camarillo et al. 2016). The subfamily Baroniinae contains only this species, and on the basis of morphology, it has been suggested to be the sister lineage to all other Papilionidae (Léon-Cortés et al. 2004). While the position of Baronia as the basal lineage within the Papilionidae family seemed assured (Munroe 1961, Hancock 1983), comprehensive morphological analysis of butterfly phylogeny by De Jong et al. (1996) has suggested that Parnassius might instead occupy this position. Baronia does, however, resemble one of the oldest known fossil butterflies, Praepapilio colorado, which occurred during the Eocene, some 48 million years ago (Durden and Rose 1978). The resemblance offers no evidence of its phylogenetic placement (Caterino et al. 2001), yet the phylogenetic position of this species has important implications for understanding butterfly evolution, such as whether the use of a leguminous host represents the "original" butterfly condition (Scott 1986).

Due to the geographical isolation between the main populations and the Chiapas population, it had been suggested that there are two subspecies: the nominal B. brevicornis (Vázquez and Pérez-Ruiz 1961) and B. brevicornis rufodiscalis within Chiapas, Mexcio (De la Maza et al. 1987, Machkour-M’Rabet et al. 2014).
Closely related to Lanistes solidus and Lanistes nyassanus, but conchologically distinctive (Berthold in Brown 1994).
This species was redescribed by Kok et al. (2006).

Subpopulations from Suriname that were previously reported as belonging to this species, have now been assigned to Anomaloglossus surinamensis (Ouboter and Jairam 2012). Following genetic, morphological and bioacoustic analysis, a subpopulation from Itoupe Massif in French Guiana have been assigned to A. dewynteri, and subpopulations from northeast French Guiana have been assigned to A. blanci (Fouquet et al. 2018).
This species can be differentiated from other similar species by a combination of morphological features, tadpole morphology, advertisement call and ecological traits (Kok et al. 2006).

This species is known from the Cook Strait and the Coromandel Peninsula; genetic divergence and minor morphological differences suggest that more than one taxon my be included under the name Toropuku stephensi (R. Hitchmough pers. comm. 2019).

Recent genetic evidence suggests that the east and west populations of this species diverged in the Miocene (Chapple et al. 2012). While this is not thought to have taxonomic implications, this finding indicates that the two populations should continue to be treated as separate management units and needs to be accounted for in captive breeding programs (Chapple et al. 2012).

The taxonomy of Oligosoma infrapunctatum has until recently not been studied in detail, although multiple undescribed species have been informally recognized from within the complex (discussed in Melzer et al. 2019). Uncertainty surrounds the identity of 'true' O. infrapunctatum, whose type locality is unknown: an assumption that this was Stephens Island (Takapourewa) (McCann 1955) is now considered to be in error, as the species known from this island is not conspecific with the O. infrapunctatum holotype (Melzer et al. 2019). These authors combined morphological evidence with a previous molecular phylogeny of the species complex to revise the taxonomy of this group.
This is a recognized species complex; as presently defined a northern and southern clade are recognized as distinct (Whitaker et al. 2018).

The name Woodworthia brunnea (sometimes, incorrectly, W. brunneus) has been used informally to refer to a species recognized as distinct from Dactylocnemis pacificus and from Woodworthia maculata, for which the name Pentadactylus brunneus is thought to be available (Jewell 2008). This action was taken without providing the taxonomic justification for associating the new taxon with the P. brunneus holotype. Nielsen et al. (2011) describe the content of Woodworthia as including W. brunnea, but do not comment on this assignment and elsewhere refer to samples of this species as Hoplodactylus cf. brunneus. Subsequently Hitchmough et al. (2016) used the form Woodworthia cf. brunnea to refer to this taxon, and this usage is preferred pending a formal examination of the appropriate nomenclature (R. Hitchmough pers. comm. 2019).

authority: Chapple, Bell, Chapple, Miller, Daugherty & Patterson 2011

taxonomicNotes: Work is underway to clarify the systematics of this species complex (R. Hitchmough and D. van Winkel pers. comm. 2019).

authority: Chapple, Bell, Chapple, Miller, Daugherty & Patterson, 2011

taxonomicNotes: This species was previously within the genus Hyla was moved to the new genus Myersiohyla (Faivovich et al. 2005). It has now been moved to the new genus Nesorohyla (Pinheiro et al. 2018).
This species boundaries with other described species that are supposed to occur throughout Amazonia (i.e. the Scinax garbei complex) remain ambiguous (A. Fouquet pers. comm. 2022).
This genus has recently been moved from the family Hylidae (Faivovich et al. 2005).

authority: Thinh, Mootnick, Vu Ngoc Thanh, Nadler & Roos, 2010
taxonomicNotes: Recent genetic and vocal studies confirmed the specific identity of the species which separates it from its closest congener, Nomascus gabriellae (Thinh et al. 2010a, 2010b, 2010c; Blair et al. 2011; Roos et al. 2013).
Nineteenth century records previously assigned to Dasypeltis scabra were assigned to this newly-described species by Saleh and Sarhan (2016).

Rhynchobatus djiddensis is part of the Indo-West Pacific Rhynchobatus djiddensis species-complex. The name R. djiddensis has previously been used widely, leading to poorly-defined taxonomy and distributions for members of the complex (in particular, R. australiae, R. djiddensis, and R. laevis). Further investigation into the taxonomy of 'whitespotted wedgefish' is required, and additional species are likely to be described in the future.
Brachionichthys australis and B. hirsutus are similar in appearance, but are confirmed to be separate species (Last et al. 2007).

A member of the Hyla circumdata group according to the original publication. Bokermannohyla vulcaniae is diagnosed by a combination of morphological features and colour patterns (De Vasconcelos and Giaretta 2003).

authority: Grismer, Wood, Aowphol, Cota, Grismer, Murdoch, Aguilar & Grismer, 2016

taxonomicNotes: This was considered as a subspecies of B. unicolor by Mandahl-Barth (1954) and re-instated as a distinct species by Brown (1994). With its large size and thin shell, it differes from all other Victorian viviparids. It must probably be lowered in taxonomic rank to 'ecotype' or at best 'subspecies', as all other 'endemic' Victorian Bellamya species (see Sengupta et al. 2009), but  B. phtinotropis was not included in their molecular analysis because, in spite of intense research in the type area (A. Jorgensen pers. comm. 2014), no live specimens were found.

The Afrotropical genus Ceratophallus consists of 12 small planorbid snails, most of which are restricted to rift lakes (Brown 1994). The generic position of three of these Ceratophallus species remains uncertain. The highest species diversity is found in the Victoria Basin and Lake Victoria proper. In addition to four species that are endemic to the lake and lake shore, namely C. kisumiensis, C. subtilis, C. concavus and C. crassus, the widespread C. natalensis and C. kigeziensis also occur in the Lake Victoria basin.
Morphologically this is a very distinctive species.
This species was previously regarded as Boninagrion ezoin, but this genus has been regarded as synonymous with Ischnura since 2012 (Karube et al. 2012).

authority: Hou, Wu, Yang, Zheng, Yuan & Li, 2014

taxonomicNotes: Lyu et al. (2021) listed this species in Panophys without comment, and this work was not a revision of Megophrys. This assessment retains it in Megophrys until generic boundaries within the Megophryinae are more stable.
Lyu et al. (2021) listed this species in Panophrys without comment, and this work was not a revision of Megophrys. This assessment retains it in Megophrys until generic boundaries are more stable.
Pachytriton xanthospilos has been removed from synonymy of this species (Wu et al. 2012).

This is a split from the broader concept of Tylototriton wenxianensis. The subspecies of T. wenxianensis from the north Dabie Mountains in Henan Province has been elevated to T. dabienicus according to Shen et al. 2012. Tylototriton anhuiensis was previously confused with this species, so subpopulations from Anhui Province that were previously attributed to T. dabienicus are now assigned to T. anhuiensis (Qian et al. 2017).
This is a split from the broader concept of Pseudohynobius flavomaculatus (Wei et al. 2009).

authority: (Li, Liu, Chen, Wu, Murphy, Zhao, Wang & Zhang, 2012)
taxonomicNotes:
This is a split from the broader concept of Rhacophorus dugritei (Li et al. 2012).

It was moved from the genus Rhacophorus to Zhangixalus by Jiang et al. (2019).

This species was removed as a synonym of Bufo burmanus by Yang and Rao (2008) where it had been placed by Yang et al. (1996) (according to Rao et al. (1998)). Frost (2019) notes that these authors either disagreed with or missed that Bufo burmanus was placed in the synonymy of Bufo pageoti by Dubois and Ohler (1999). This species was previously listed on the IUCN Red List as a synonym of Bufo pageoti (J. Luedtke pers. comm. August 2015).
This is a split from the broader concept of Pachytriton changi, which has been removed from synonymy with this species (Wu et al. 2012).
Because this species was recently described, it is sometimes referenced in the literature as Ctenosaura bakeri or Enyaliosaura bakeri, the sister species it was split from.
This species has traditionally been ranked as subspecies Matrona basilaris japonica, but now it is ranked as good species since 2011 (e.g., Hamalainen et al. 2011,Ozono et al. 2012).

This species was previously considered part of the genus Atrophaneura (Hancock 1988). This species is very similar to Atrophaneura priapus and there were some doubts about its taxonomic status in the past (Collins and Morris 1985). Racheli and Cotton (2010) have now placed this species in the priapus group, alongside Atrophaneura priapus, A. hageni, and A. sycorax.

This species was considered to belong to the genus Atrophaneura (Hancock 1988). The subgenus Pachliopta has since been elevated to full genus level by many authorities (e.g. GLoBIS; LepIndex; Racheli and Cotton 2010), and we follow this here.
Has sinistral shell, small and slender in comparison to B. nyassanus. Obtuse spire and closed umbilicus.

Recent genetic studies have shown the New Caledonian scincid genus Lioscincus to be polyphyletic and that it comprises four distinct evolutionary lineages which should be recognized at generic level (Sadlier et al. 2015). As a result, Lioscincus greeri and L. nigrofasciolatus are now placed in the genus Epibator.
Populations in the north at Rivière Nehoué and the Kaala massif are genetically divergent and may represent distinct taxa.

The population of Kanakysaurus on Dôme de Tiébaghi is genetically very close to Kanakysaurus zebratus yet is morphologically indistinguishable and genetically highly divergent from the K. viviparus with which it is sympatric. Whether this represents a distinct taxon or is the result of mitochondrial introgression awaits further research (Sadlier et al. 2009).

Populations of Oedodera on the Poum and Kaala massifs are genetically and morphometrically divergent from O. marmorata from Dôme de Tiébaghi. They are treated as potentially distinct taxa under description and hence are not included in this assessment for Oedodera marmorata.
The low-elevation specimens (Vavouto) are likely to be another species (on-going studies by A. Bauer and R. Sadlier pers. comm.).

The population of Kanakysaurus on Dôme de Tiébaghi is genetically very close to Kanakysaurus zebratus yet is morphologically indistinguishable and genetically highly divergent from the K. viviparus with which it is sympatric. Whether this represents a distinct taxon or is the result of mitochondrial  introgression awaits further research (Sadlier et al. 2009).

Recent genetic data show that, as presently defined, Bavayia sauvagii sensu (Boulenger, 1883) comprises a complex of at least nine cryptic species that appear to mostly have relatively confined, allopatric ranges.

Recent genetic data show that, as presently defined, Bavayia cyclura sensu Günther 1872 comprises at least ten cryptic species all of which have relatively confined, largely allopatric ranges. The data also show that material from the Îles Loyauté that was previously assigned to Bavayia cyclura is in fact Bavayia crassicollis.
There is significant genetic divergence between the known populations on Mt Panié and Tchingou.

Recent genetic data shows that, as presently defined, Bavayia montana sensu Roux, 1913 comprises a complex of at least six cryptic species all of which have relatively confined, largely allopatric ranges.

Amarasinghe et al. (2014) recognized the Knuckles Range population of Calotes liocephalus as the new species Calotes pethiyagodai on morphological grounds. These authors cautioned that little material was available for either this species or C. liocephalus and that further research should be conducted to confirm this arrangement.

authority: Samarawickrama, Ranawana, Rajapaksha, Ananjeva, Orlov, Ranasinghe & Samarawickrama, 2006
taxonomicNotes: There has been considerable controversy over the priority of the names Cophotis dumbara Samarawickrama, Ranawana, Rajapaksha, Ananjeva, Orlov, Ranasinghe & Samarawickrama, 2006 and Cophotis dumbarae Manamendra-Arachchi, de Silva & Amarasinghe, 2006, with both names being published at very close dates. Hallermann and Böhme (2007) reviewed this issue, and determined that Cophotis dumbara (published on November 25, 2006) should take priority over Cophotis dumbarae (published on December 6, 2006). Following an application to the ICZN by the authors of C. dumbarae (considered to have been based on the absence of a reply) this issue now appears to be settled, with C. dumbara accepted as the correct name (A.A.T. Amarasinghe pers. comm. 2019).

This species was previously synonymized with Cyrtodactylus rubidus (Blyth, 1861) (which is now restricted to the Andaman Islands).
[C. camortensis and C. nicobaricus are later splits and Tax Note should be updated, but these pops were not included in the 2019 assessment so no new concept is needed]

Lygosoma ashwamedhi was originally described as Riopa ashwamedhi by Sharma in 1969. A molecular phylogentic analysis has confirmed the validity of this species but concluded that it should be transferred to Eutropis (Srinivasulu et al. 2016).
This species was formerly the only member of the genus Balanophis but is now treated under Rhabdophis.
Batuwita (2019) re-described this species, referring the majority of records to other species.
Previously incorrectly thought to be a separate species, Hypnale walli is now treated as junior subjective synonym of Hypnale nepa (Maduwage et al. 2009).

In a morphological review of Lankascincus, Batuwita (2019) found that characters described as being diagnostic for L. munindradasai could not reliably distinguish it from the similar L. taprobanensis. This author consequently synonymized L. munindradasai with L. taprobanensis on both morphological and biogeographic grounds.

Taxononomic uncertainty persists with regard to the relationship between this species and R. punctatus (Pyron et al. 2016 and references therein; M. Wickramasinghe and N. Wickramasinghe pers comm. 2019).

Taxononomic uncertainty persists with regard to the relationship between this species and R. punctatus (Pyron et al. 2016 and references therein; M. Wickramasinghe and N. Wickramasinghe pers comm. 2019).

Pyron et al. (2016) suggest a comprehensive phylogeographic study of this species, which has the imprecise type locality "Sri Lanka", and to clarify whether this apparently widespread species represents a species complex once its type locality has been restricted.
Records of Rhinophis blythii, a species otherwise known only from the Central Hills, from southwestern Sri Lanka are likely to represent this species (Wickramasinghe et al. 2009).
This name may be comprised of more than one species (M. Wickramasinghe pers. comm. 2019).
Historical records of this species outside the known range were found to represent different species by Maramendra-Arachchi et al. (2007), who also referred Cnemaspis ranwellai to its synonymy.
Cnemaspis ingerorum was previously confused with Cnemaspis kumarasinghei. It was found to be genetically distinct by Agarwal et al. (2017) and formally described by Batuwita et al. (2019)
Records of Cnemaspis aliwisi from the south of the species' currently known range represent a separate, recently-described species, C. hitihami.

No distinct morphological characters were provided in the original description that can reliably distinguish this species from the Calotes liolepis species complex, which which it is sympatric (A.A.T. Amarasinghe pers. comm. 2019). Based on molecular analysis C. desilvai is a valid species (S. Karunarathna pers. comm. 2019), but further taxonomic resolution within the C. liolepis complex is needed to clarify range limits within this group and identify diagnostic characters for C. desilvai (Karunararathna et al. 2012).

The species was long considered to be monotypic of the genus. Recently (2012) a northern outlier population thought to be the Cairns Rainbowfish Cairnsichthys rhombosomoides (Martin and Barclay 2013) was found to be genetically and morphologically unique and was formally described as part of a generic revision in 2018 (Hammer et al. 2018).

The species was previously considered two species C. dalhousiensis and C. gloveri (Crowley & Ivantsoff 1990), but recent phylogenetic analyses suggests only one species (Unmack and Dowling 2010). For the purpose of the assessment, they are treated as a single taxon.
A recent taxonomic revision (Coleman et al. 2015) has split this taxon into two species, revising Galaxiella pusilla and describing a new species Galaxiella toourtkoourt.

Formerly considered to be a colour variety of the Pacific Blue-eye (Pseudomugil signifer) (Allen & Ivantsoff, 1982), the Honey Blue-eye differs in size (3.8 cm as opposed to 8.8 cm for male and 6.3 cm for female Pacific Blue-eyes) and in colouring, with the Pacific Blue-eye having a semi-transparent body that can vary in colour from pale olive, yellow to bluish.

Until recently, known as Galaxias olidus Günther, 1866 which was found to be an unresolved species complex (Raadik, 2011; Adams et al., 2014) and revised, with this new taxon described (Raadik, 2014).

Until recently, known as Galaxias olidus Günther, 1866 which was found to be an unresolved species complex (Raadik, 2011; Adams et al., 2014) and revised, with this new taxon described (Raadik, 2014).

Until recently, known as Galaxias olidus Günther, 1866 which was found to be an unresolved species complex (Raadik, 2011; Adams et al., 2014) and revised, with this new taxon described (Raadik, 2014).

Until recently, known as Galaxias olidus Günther, 1866 which was found to be an unresolved species complex (Raadik, 2011; Adams et al., 2014) and revised, with this new taxon described (Raadik, 2014).

Until recently, known as Galaxias olidus Günther, 1866 which was found to be an unresolved species complex (Raadik, 2011; Adams et al., 2014) and revised, with this new taxon described (Raadik, 2014).

Until recently, known as Galaxias olidus Günther, 1866 which was found to be an unresolved species complex (Raadik, 2011; Adams et al., 2014) and revised, with this new taxon described (Raadik, 2014).
Until recently, known as Galaxias olidus Günther, 1866 which was found to be an unresolved species complex (Raadik 2011, Adams et al. 2014) and revised, with this new taxon described (Raadik 2014).

Until recently, known as Galaxias olidus Günther, 1866 which was found to be an unresolved species complex (Raadik 2011, Adams et al. 2014) and revised, with this new taxon described (Raadik 2014). Recent on-going taxonomic work has indicated the populations of this taxon in the Cann River system in Victoria are a separate, new species, and are excluded from this assessment (T. Raadik unpublished data).

The original description appears to apply to more than one species including a putative upland form that was recognised as distinct, specifically Short Creek. This account only considers the lowland true form.

In general, purple-spotted gudgeons (genus Mogurnda, family Eleotridae) are distributed across north, east and central regions of Australia and extend up into New Guinea. Based on genetic studies and knowledge of current distributions, they are believed to have originated in Queensland, gradually moving south and west along drainage systems (Adams et al. 2013, M. Hammer pers. comm. 2016). As the climate dried, these drainage systems became disconnected and rearranged. Populations were subsequently isolated and eventually speciated (Faulks et al. 2010, Kerezsy et al. 2010). However, both M. clivicola and Southern Purple-spotted Gudgeon (M. adspersa) are both still the most widespread of all Australian Mogurnda. However, more species are believed to be distinct which have yet to be taxonomically identified and several Evolutionary Significant Units (ESUs) have been proposed (Faulks 2010, Adams et al. 2013). The distribution of M. civicola used in this assessment is based on the latest genetic assessments to confirm positive records.
This species is currently being described based on initial genetic evidence with specimens stored at the Northern Territory museum.
There is a taxonomic revision in progress, specimens have been lodged at the NT museum.
The species description has been drafted and should be submitted in 2019 (P. Unmack pers. comm. 2019).

This is a currently undescribed new taxon of Gadopsis (Percichthyidae) identified in the Gadopsis marmoratus species complex (Hammer et al. 2014, Unmack et al. 2017). Work to date has defined this taxon as a valid candidate species based on genetic divergence, and identified some morphological and biological differences between it and the congeners in the complex (T.A. Raadik unpublished data). Detailed morphological work is progressing which will eventuate in a formal taxonomic description.
This is a split from the broader concept of Leptolalax hamidi (Matsui et al. 2014) – currently Leptobrachella hamidi.
The Pezichthys species as a group are relatively newly described, and further taxonomic resolution is needed (Last and Gledhill 2009).
The Pezichthys species as a group are relatively newly described, and further taxonomic resolution is needed (Last and Gledhill 2009).
The genus Thymichthys is relatively new, and needs further taxonomic resolution (Last and Gledhill 2009).

The new species of diplodactylid gecko, Bavayia nubilaa, was described in 2012 from forests at two sites in the drainage of the Tontouta Valley in the ultramafic ranges of southeastern New Caledonia. The new gecko is the sister species of the much smaller Bavayia goroensis; both species are basal within the B. cyclura clade.

Caledoniscincus constellatus (described in 2012) has a bold, white midlateral stripe, a feature which distinguishes it from most other species of Caledoniscincus except the regionally sympatric C. haplorhinus (Günther) and C. austrocaledonicus (Bavay), from which it can be distinguished by a unique pattern of contact between the pale midlateral stripe and the ear. DNA sequence data for the ND2 mitochondrial gene identifies a high level of genetic differentiation between the new species and all other Caledoniscincus, further supporting its distinctiveness as an independent evolutionary lineage.

Caledoniscincus pelletieri was described in 2014 from the far north-west region of New Caledonia. It has a bold, white mid-lateral stripe on the body, a feature which distinguishes it from most other species of Caledoniscincus except the regionally sympatric Caledoniscincus haplorhinus (Günther) and Caledoniscincus austrocaledonicus (Bavay), and the recently described Caledoniscincus constellatus Sadlier, Whitaker, Wood & Bauer just to the south. The new species can be distinguished from these taxa in features of scalation and colouration, most notably in lacking an extension of the pale midlateral stripe between the ear and forelimbs and in having more lamellae on the underside of the fourth toe. The differences in morphology between C. pelletieri and the other members of the genus are complemented by a high level of genetic differentiation, further supporting its distinctiveness as an independent evolutionary lineage warranting recognition as a distinct species. The DNA sequence data for the ND2 mitochondrial gene identifies the new species as the sister to C. constellatus and these two taxa as the sister to all Caledoniscincus.

Correlophus belepensis (described in 2012) is a large diplodactylid. It differs from C. sarasinorum in possessing a prominent crest of spinose scales extending from behind the orbit to the shoulder region. It is most similar to its sister taxon C. ciliatus, but may be distinguished from it by its homogeneous dorsolateral trunk scalation and (in three of the four types) its possession of a series of small whitish tubercles on the lower back and/or tail base.

A molecular phylogenetic analysis of the New Caledonia giant geckos has revealed that Rhacodactylus is not monophyletic (Bauer et al. 2012). The results show that R. chahoua forms a distinct clade separate from Rhacodactylus sensu stricto and hence a new genus, Mniarogekko has been created to accommodate R. chahoua. Genetic data showed that the subpopulations in northern Grande Terre and on the Iles Belep are highly distinct and are hence described as M. jalu (Bauer et al. 2012).

Mniarogekko jalu is a large (to 140 mm SVL) diplodactylid. It may be distinguished from its sister taxon M. chahoua by its much lower number of precloacal pores in males typically arranged in three, rather than four rows. Colour comparisons between the two species of Mniarogekko are difficult to make. There are relatively few wild caught M. chahoua in museum collections and captive animals have been bred for particular colour patterns (de Vosjoli et al. 2003) so ‘wild type’ coloration, which is itself already quite variable is difficult to characterize. Ventral body coloration in the new species seems to be uniformly a pale yellowish green, whereas M. chahoua is often white or cream, with a greenish tinge localized to some parts of the venter.

The genetic study (Sadlier 2014) revealed the presence of two clearly differentiated lineages within what has previously been regarded as a single species, Nannoscincus mariei. Although the geographic range of samples available for the genetics study is not as extensive as that represented by museum specimens it is, in combination with morphological data, sufficient to indicate that the samples represent two parapatric taxa: Nannoscincus mariei Bavay (Mt. Koghis south to the Goro Plateau, including Rivière Bleue), and Nannoscincus fuscus (the Chaîne Centrale at Mt. Mou, Mt. Dzumac and Mt. Vulcain).

Nannoscincus koniambo was described in 2014 (Sadlier, 2014). It is part of the N. mariei group, is restricted to the Koniambo massif and can be distinguished from all other members of the genus a combination of characters: frontoparietals fused; loreal single; left oviduct lost in females; lower labials five; lower eyelid with a semi-translucent window; ear opening minute; body scales smooth; adult dorsal color two toned; ear opening positioned two scales posterior to lower secondary temporal; presacral vertebrae 32; phalangeal formula for manus 2.3.4.4.3.; phalangeal formula for pes 2.3.4.5.3.

Bauer et al. (2012) in a molecular phylogenetic analysis of the giant geckos found that the few subpopulations of Rhacodactylus trachyrhynchus sampled were strongly divergent from one another and a specimen from Îlot Môrô near the Île des Pins is especially distinctive. This specimen and others examined from Îlot Môrô are morphologically assignable to the species described by Boulenger in 1878 as Chameleonurus trachycephalus (and later treated as a subspecies of R. trachyrhynchus) and is now recognized as a full species (Bauer et al. 2012).

The results from field, morphological and genetic studies (Sadlier et al. 2014) reveals a taxon formerly included within Sigaloseps deplanchei sensu lato and represented by populations from the southeast of the southern ultramafic region at Rivière Bleue and Pourina River and here described as S. conditus.

The results from field, morphological and genetic studies (Sadlier et al. 2014) reveals a taxon formerly included within Sigaloseps deplanchei sensu lato and represented by populations in the central (Tontouta valley) and northern (Pic Ningua) areas of the southern ultramafic region, and coastal ultramafic ranges in the east-central region (Nakéty) and here described as Sigaloseps pisinnus.
The taxonomic status of this nominal species requires clarification (H.K. Larson pers. comm. 2019).
Although Donnelly (1998) recorded a female of this species, the female has not been described.

Included in Manis by several authors (with Smutsia sometimes considered a subgenus) and referred to Phataginus by Grubb et al. (1998), but here included in the genus Smutsia, along with Temminck's Pangolin, S. temminckii, following Gaudin et al. (2009).
Populations in the Philippines formerly attributed to this species were separated under the name Manis culionensis by Feiler (1998) and subsequently by Gaubert and Antunes (2005).

Included in Manis by many authors (with Phataginus sometimes considered a subgenus), but here placed in the genus Phataginus following Gaudin et al. (2009). Meester and Setzer (1972) recognized two distinct subspecies, Manis t. tricuspis and M. t. mabirae (Uganda), with the latter considered distinct. No subspecies are considered here in line with Kingdon and Hoffmann (2013).

The Philippine Pangolin (Manis culionensis) was first recognized as a distinct species from the Sunda Pangolin (Manis javanica) by de Elera (1915) who classified it as Pholidotus culionensis. Subsequent literature that distinguished this species used the classifications Paramanis culionensis or Manis culionensis (Feiler 1998, Hoogstraal 1951, Sanborn 1952). Classification as a distinct species is supported based on morphological characters (Lawrence 1939, Sanborn, 1952, Feiler 1998, Gaubert and Antunes 2005), and through genetic studies (Luczon et al. 2016, Gaubert et al. 2018).
There are no obvious differences between the adults of this species and Meristogenys phaeomerus (Inger et al. 2017).

The results from field, morphological and genetic studies revealed that Sigaloseps ruficauda was a species complex containing two distinct taxa and it was therefore split it into a more restricted concept of S. ruficauda and a new species S. ferrugicauda (Sadlier et al. 2014).

Apostolepis striata was described based on its type specimen alone (Lema 2004). More data is necessary for a more complete description, including comparisons with other Apostolepis such as Apostolepis serrana Lema, 2004, A. nigroterminata Boulenger, 1896, and A. phillipsae Harvey, 1999.

Traditionally considered to be a single species with some variation in colouration, morphological and molecular phylogenetic analyses have produced conflicting results and conclusions in recent years (Blanck et al. 2006, Spinks et al. 2009). The Vietnamese and possible Laotian populations are recognized as a separate species, Cuora cyclornata Blanck, McCord & Le 2006, with two subspecies, C. c. cyclornata Blanck, McCord & Le, 2006 and C. c. meieri Blanck, McCord & Le, 2006.
This species is known from a single historical specimen that appears to have been overlooked until formally described by Hedges and Conn (2012).
Dolphins of the genus Orcaella were split into two species by Beasley et al. (2002, 2005), the Irrawaddy Dolphin (Orcaella brevirostris) and the Snubfin Dolphin (O. heinsohni).

The Sousa teuszii holotype (a skull) was recovered in the “Bucht des Kameruner Kriegsshiffhafens,” (“Bay of Warships” or ‘Man O’War Bay’), in Cameroon by the German agronomist Eduard Tëusz. The German zoologist Willy Kükenthal examined it at Jena and described it as the new species Sotalia teuszii in honour of its discoverer (Kükenthal 1891, Kükenthal 1892, Van Beneden 1892, Ayissi et al. 2014). The species was originally placed in the genus Sotalia; the genus name Sousa came into general use only in the 1960s (Fraser and Purves 1960, Fraser 1966). Thorough reviews of the species taxonomy are provided by Van Waerebeek et al. (2004) and Jefferson and Rosenbaum (2014). Photographs of the holotype are provided by Kükenthal (Table 21, Kükenthal 1892), Pilleri and Gihr (1972) and Jefferson and Van Waerebeek (2004). Illustrations of the holotype were provided by Van Beneden (1892). The holotype is now held by the British Museum (catalogue number 1893.8.1.1).

The collection details for the holotype are unclear but were of consequence. The dolphin skull and a shark-mauled carcass of a West African manatee were apparently recovered by Tëusz following a heavy rain storm and were subsequently confused (Van Waerebeek et al. 2004). The stomach of the manatee contained vegetable matter, leading Kükenthal to hypothesize that the species was perhaps riparian and vegetarian, an impression reinforced by the dolphin’s rounded (worn) teeth. The Belgian zoologist Pierre Joseph Van Beneden (with whom Kükenthal corresponded) compounded the misunderstanding in a separate publication (Kükenthal 1891, Kükenthal 1892, Van Beneden 1892, Van Waerebeek et al. 2004). Necropsies of fresh animals in the 1950s confirmed that they were piscivorous and marine (Cadenat 1956, Cadenat and Paraiso 1957).

Recent genetic and morphometric work has served to clarify the taxonomy of the genus Sousa (Jefferson and Rosenbaum 2014, Jefferson and Van Waerebeek 2004, Mendez et al. 2013). The available data indicate that there is “strong and significant genetic and morphologic differentiation between S. teuszii and all other sampling units…with no evidence of exchange or contact” (Mendez et al. 2013). Principal components analysis demonstrated that S. teuszii have significantly shorter rostra, wider skulls and lower tooth counts (an average of about 30 teeth per row vs. about 33-37 for other species) when compared with Southeast African, Arabian/Persian Gulf and Indian Sousa specimens (Jefferson and Rosenbaum 2014, Jefferson and Van Waerebeek 2004). There are no shared mtDNA haplotypes with other species in the genus, but a phylogenetic assessment of combined nuclear and mtDNA datasets indicates that S. teuszii is most closely related to Indian Ocean Humpback Dolphins (Sousa plumbea) from South East Africa (Mendez et al. 2013). The most plausible mechanism for their isolation is the Benguela upwelling system, an area dominated by cold upwelling that is located within the ~2,000 km distribution gap between S. teuszii and S. plumbea (Jefferson and Van Waerebeek 2004, Mendez et al. 2013).

Cryptotis goodwini and probably Cryptotis griseoventris are sister species. However, genetic studies to test this hypothesis are lacking (Guevara 2023). Cryptotis goodwini are larger in size; however, cranial measurements of the species broadly overlap (Woodman 2010).

This species is monotypic. Oryzomys gorgasi has been considered synonyms with O. curasoae (an extinct species based on remains obtained from limestone fissures) with priority for the name O. gorgasi (Percequillo 2015).

This is a new concept for this species which now includes C. timorensis McCord, Joseph-Ouni, and Hagen 2007a. Three subspecies of Chelodina mccordi are currently recognized (TTWG 2017): C. m. mccordi (Rhodin 1994) from western Roti (Rote) Island in the Lesser Sunda Islands of Indonesia, C. m. roteensis (McCord et al. 2007b) from eastern Roti (Rote) Island, and C. m. timorensis (McCord et al. 2007a) from Timor Leste. However, preliminary genetic work in progress suggests that there may be only one taxon on Roti (C. mccordi) and one taxon in Timor Leste (C. timorensis), possibly distinct at the species level. The common name on Roti in Bahasa Indonesian is Kura-kura Rote, and in Timor Leste the name in Tetum is Lenuk Kakorok Naruk.

Elevated from subspecies of Cuora galbinifrons by Stuart and Parham (2004), this elevation was rejected by Fritz et al. (2006), but supported by Spinks et al. (2012) and TTWG (2014). The common name in Vietnamese is  Rua hop bua-re.

Elevated from a subspecies of Cuora galbinifrons by Stuart and Parham (2004); species status was contested by Fritz et al. (2006), and reaffirmed by Spinks et al. (2012) and accepted by TTWG (2014). The common name in Vietnamese is Rua hop vietnam.

Cuora galbinifrons previously included the taxa bourreti and picturata as subspecies; the latter two were elevated to full species rank by Stuart and Parham (2004) (though see Fritz et al. 2006 for dissenting perspective) and reconfirmed as full species by Spinks et al. (2012) and accepted by TTWG (2014). Cuora serrata is generally accepted to be a hybrid between C. galbinifrons and C. mouhotii (Parham et al. 2001, Shi et al. 2005). Cuora hainanensis (Li 1958), once considered a full species, later a galbinifrons subspecies, is now viewed as so similar to C. galbinifrons as to not require a separate designation (Stuart and Parham 2004). The common name in Vietnamese is Rua hop tran vang.

authority: (Vaz-Ferreira, Sierra de Soriano & Scaglia de Paulete, 1964)
taxonomicNotes: We follow Alonso et al. (2023) in considering Garcialebias a valid genus, rather than a synonym of Austrolebias.

Lucifuga teresinarum was recently characterized as a junior synonym of L. subterranea (Lara et al. 2010, García-Machado et al. 2011). Subpopulations previously considered L. teresinarum are included within the current distribution of L. subterranea.

Glyphis gangeticus was originally described from the Hooghly River, India. Records of G. gangeticus are sparse and there have been previous issues with reporting due to misidentification with other carcharhinds, mainly the Bull Shark (Carcharhinus leucas) and Broadfin sharks (Lamiopsis spp.), resulting in historic ambiguities about the range of G. gangeticus. The taxonomic status of the species was recently resolved with Li et al. (2015) demonstrating that G. siamensis and G. fowlerae are synonyms of G. gangeticus. Based on molecular data, there is an undescribed Glyphis species currently known from Bangladesh and Malaysian Borneo (Li et al. 2015).
Haploblepharus edwardsii is commonly misidentified for other Haploblepharus species.

Mustelus mustelus and M. asterias are morphologically similar and may be misidentified. Many early works may refer to either of the two species (Wheeler 1978). Mustelus asterias is distinguished from M. mustleus by a narrower inter-narial space and the presence of white spots on the dorsal and upper lateral sides of the former.

There is a demonstrated high level of contemporary and historical misidentification of unspotted Mustelus asterias as M. mustelus in the Northeast Atlantic Ocean region (Farrell et al. 2009, Farrell 2010). There are no reliably confirmed records of M. mustelus occurring north of Portuguese waters (Farrell 2010). Quignard and Capapé (1972) described a M. mustelus specimen from Portuguese Atlantic Ocean waters based on dermal denticle morphology and this remains the most northerly reliably identified record of this species. Similarly, some misidentification can occur between M. mustelus and M. punctulatus. This is because the black spots on the body are not unique to M. punctulatus and their absence is not diagnostic for M. mustelus. The distance of the nostrils and the shape of the mouth appear to be more useful features to distinguish the two species during fieldwork (Marino et al. 2018).
The genus Hemitrygon formerly was a junior synonym of Dasyatis (Kottelat, 2013); it was resurrected by Last et al. (2016a) in their revision of the family Dasyatidae.
Formerly, Urogymnus asperrimus was the only species within Urogymmus. Last et al. (2016) added five more species to the genus.

The nomenclatural history of the species is somewhat complicated. The name Stegostoma fasciatum (Hermann, 1783) has been the standard name used, however, Dahl et al. (2019) concluded that the original name Stegostoma tigrinum Forster, 1781 is the correct senior synonym.
This species was previously considered to be conspecific with Aetobatus narutobiei, but the species have since been shown to differ genetically and morphologically (White and Moore 2013).
This species is considered distinct from Aetomylaeus milvus (Last et al. 2016).
White (2014) placed the genus Pteromylaeus Garman, 1913 into the synonymy of Aetomylaeus Garman, 1908. This species is often confused with Common Eagle Ray (Myliobatus aquila).

This species is often confused with the sympatric Persian Gulf Torpedo (Torpedo sinuspercisi), though it can be distinguished by a less reticulate dorsal pattern (Last et al. 2016). There is some taxonomic uncertainty with both species possibly part of a species complex (Last et al. 2016).

Rhynchobatus springeri is part of the Indo-West Pacific Rhynchobatus djiddensis species-complex. The name R. djiddensis has previously been used widely, leading to poorly-defined taxonomy and distributions for members of the complex (in particular, R. australiae, R. djiddensis, and R. laevis). Further investigation into the taxonomy of 'whitespotted wedgefish' is required, and additional species are likely to be described in the future. 

Rhynchobatus yentinensis Wang, 1933 (described from China) might be applicable to this species or may be a synonym of R. laevis.

This species belongs to the long-snout group of Centrophorus which includes C. harrissoni, C. isodon, C. seychellorum, and C. westraliensis. The long-snout species group requires further taxonomic attention (White et al. 2017).

The Oxynotus recorded as O. centrina from Angola, Namibia, and South Africa may not be that species but an undescribed one, differing from O. centrina in having a much shorter interdorsal space. If this is correct, the records of O. centrina from tropical West Africa need to be reexamined, but until the problem is resolved the southern and southwestern African Oxynotus is retained in O. centrina (Bass et al. 1976).

There has been much debate over the Zearaja/Dipturus complex. Concha et al. (2019) synonymised Zearaja with Dipturus, resulting in this species appearing as Dipturus maugeanus on the IUCN Red List between 2019 and 2025. However, Zearaja was then resurrected as a valid genus following acceptance of its validity by the IUCN SSC Shark Specialist Group and recent taxonomic revisions (e.g., Gabbanelli et al. 2018, Figueroa et al. 2024).
Changes to the systematics of Rhinobatos elevated the subgenus Glaucostegus to full generic status and placed this genus into a family of its own: Glaucostegidae (Compagno 2005, Last et al. 2016a).
Formerly a monotypic genus (containing only U. asperrimus), Last et al. (2016) added five more species to Urogymmus, including U. granulatus.

Squalus brevirostris from the northwest Pacific has previously been considered a synonym of S. megalops. A revision of Indo-Pacific Squalus species and molecular data revealed that this species is clearly separable from the true S. megalops from Australia (Last et al. 2007, Weigmann 2016). Misidentifications of the Squalus species in the Northwest Pacific is an issue and may affect the quality of data available for this species.
Gymnura tentaculata identified from Papua New Guinea (Kailola 1987) are a misidentification of G. australis, as G. tentaculata appears to be restricted to the Northern Indian Ocean.
Last et al. (2016) described the genus Maculabatis, comprised of nine medium to large, marine whiprays previously placed in Himantura (including pastinacoides).

Formerly a monotypic genus (containing only Urogymnus asperrimus), Last et al. (2016) moved five more medium to large species to Urogymmus, including lobistomus. This species is probably misidentified as Himantura (=Pateobatis) uarnacoides and Himantura (=Pateobatis) hortlei (M. Manjaji pers. obs. 2007).
Last et al. (2016) revised the genus Rhinobatos, elevating the former subgenus Acroteriobatus to generic status.

It is unclear whether this, or a similar, species occurs along the east and west African coasts and Mediterranean Sea. A systematic review of this species is needed in these areas. This species is sometimes confused with Duckbill Eagle Ray (Aetomylaeus bovinus).

The taxonomy of Lamiopsis has recently been resolved with White et al. (2010) resurrecting and redescribing Lamiopsis tephrodes from Southeast Asia, and Akhilesh et al. (2016) redescribing Lamiopsis temminckii from the Arabian Sea. This species is potentially endemic to the Arabian Sea, however, further taxonomic work is required in the Bay of Bengal to determine if this species occurs there or if recorded specimens refer to L. tephrodes.

Due to the lack of a formal description, the first records of this species for the Maracaibo Lake (Lago de Maracaibo) identified it as Potamotrygon magdalenae (Schultz 1949, Fernández-Yépez and Espinoza 1970, Mago 1970) and there were subsequent misidentifications as P. magdalenae (Ewald et al. 1971, Andrade 1984, Galvis et al. 1997, Maldonado-Ocampo et al. 2005).
The genus Hemitrygon formerly was a junior synonym of Dasyatis; it was resurrected by Last et al. (2016a) in their revision of the family Dasyatidae.
Iwatsuki et al. (2011) revised the genus Platyrhina, describing two new species as distinct from Platyrhina sinensis.

The Red Sea Torpedo (Torpedo suessii) is considered a valid species. It is known from only three specimens captured off Mocha, Yemen, in 1897 (Steindachner 1898) which are housed at Naturhistorisches Museum, Vienna, Austria.

Steindachner (1898) did not assign the holotype and paratypes but de Carvalho et al. (2002) nominated the lectotype (NMW 88240) and the paralectotype (NMW 78016) from Steindachner’s original specimens. Steindachner’s third specimen (NMW 87406) was recently rediscovered and assigned as a paralectotype (Palandačić et al. 2023). The Red Sea Torpedo can be differentiated from other torpedo rays in the Western Indian Ocean, the Red Sea, and nearby gulfs by its distinctive patterning consisting of eight, large brown spots (de Carvalho et al. 2002).

The Red Sea Torpedo was previously considered a junior synonym of T. sinuspersici (de Carvalho et al. 2002). Garman (1913) considered T. suessii to be a valid species but postulated that it may be synonymous with T. panthera. It is however unlikely that the ornate colour pattern of T. suessii could morph into the small, irregular whitish spots typical of both juvenile and adult T. panthera (de Carvalho et al. 2002).

This species has been referred to as Torpedo suessi, however this is a misspelling.
The genus Hypanus formerly was a junior synonym of Dasyatis (Kottelat, 2013); it was resurrected by Last et al. (2016a) in their revision of the family Dasyatidae.

This species is similar to  the Leopard Whipray (H. leoparda) (Last et al. 2016). The Honeycomb Whipray juveniles of 50 cm disc width (DW) have a well-developed main denticle band with inconspicuous primary denticles while the adults have a strong pattern of large ocelli and reticulations.

Sympatric to the Little Skate, immature specimens of the two species are often confused, especially females. However, chance of misidentification decreases as they mature. Disk is rounded, with many small dark spots on upper surface. Usually 1-4 ocelli are found on the upper surface on each side of disk, each with a dark brownish center and pale edge. Young have long middorsal row of large spines on disk and tail. Upper jaw has 72 rows of teeth. Lower surface is usually white but marked with irregular pale brownish blotches (although this colouration is found in several other species within range).

Previously this species was referred to as a subpopulation of Holohalaelurus regani (occurring off KwaZulu-Natal and southern Mozambique). A revision of the genus Holohalaelurus has shown that this species is in fact distinct from H. regani (Human 2006). Specimens of H. favus were first referred to and illustrated by Bass et al. (1975).
Ishihara et al. (2012) established the new genus Beringraja for the two species B. binoculata  (previously Raja binoculata Girard, 1855) and B. pulchra (previously Raja pulchra Liu 1932).
The revision of the genus Holohalaelurus showed that H. polystigma is a junior synonym of H. punctatus (Human 2006).
No taxonomic notes.

Himantura uarnak was recently split from other Himantura species (Last et al. 2016a). The new distribution no longer includes Australia and Papua New Guinea (Last et al. 2016b). There is some continued taxonomic uncertainty with the Himantura genus and this assessment should be updated if the current distribution changes.

Himantura kittipongi, H. oxyrhynchus, and H. signifer were placed within the newly described genus Fluvitrygon (Last et al. 2016a). This species is most similar to H. signifer, from which it differs in having thorny denticles on the tail and no pale edge to the disc (Last et al. 2016b).

Dipturus nidarosiensis may be a composite species with a small and large morphotype similar to that seen in Dipturus batis-complex (Iglésias et al. 2010) and Dipturus oxyrinchus-complex. Taxonomic resolution and further genetic research is recommended to comprehensively understand the presence of Dipturus nidarosiensis across its distribution (Ebert and Stehmann 2013, Serena et al. 2020).

Last et al. (2016) described the genus Pateobatis, consisting of five medium-size to very large, marine whiprays previously placed in Himantura (including jenkinsii). This species has probably been misidentified as Himantura (=Pateobatis) fai in the literature (W. White pers. obs). More specimens of both this species and of H. draco from South Africa need to be examined to resolve the taxonomic status of this species. Presently, H. draco is considered a synonym of P. jenkinsii. The South African species had dark spots along the posterior margin of the disc, but this form has also been reported from the Arafura Sea and off Sumatra in Indonesia. It is possible that these may in fact be colour forms of the same species. Examples of specimens with dark spots along the posterior margin of the disc are from the Arafura Sea, Sumatra (Last and Stevens 2009, Fahmi pers. obs. 2007), and from western Sri Lanka (Morón et al. 1998, Manjaji 2004), and the Sulu Sea (B.M. Manjaji-Matsumoto pers. obs. 2002).

The Leopard Whipray (Himantura leoparda) is closely related and similar in shape and dorsal disc pattern to the Reticulate Whipray (H. uarnak) and Honeycomb Whipray (H. undulata). These three species are in the ‘uarnak’ species-complex, a subgroup of mainly reticulated, ocellated, or spotted whiprays. The Leopard Whipray can be distinguished by its unique arrangement of midscapular denticles and the leopard-like markings on the dorsal surface of adults and large specimens. Juveniles of the three species are born at different sizes and vary slightly in colour patterns and denticle shapes (Manjaji-Matsumoto and Last 2008, Last and Stevens 2009). Despite these differences, it can still be difficult to visually separate the species and misidentifications in the 'uarnak' species-complex are likely.

Aetobatus ocellatus was previously considered to be an Indo-West and Central Pacific form of the wider ranging Aetobatus narinari. Comparative analysis has resulted in the re-description of A. ocellatus from the Indian Ocean and West-Central Pacific Ocean with A. narinari being restricted to the Atlantic Ocean (Richards et al. 2009, White et al. 2010). Molecular analyses suggest greater levels of speciation within the Aetobatus genus, with distinguishable groups in the Western Indian Ocean and Northwest Pacific (Schluessel et al. 2010, White et al. 2010). Samples from Qatar cluster separately—designated as A. cf. ocellatus 2 (D. Ebert pers. comm. 2017). This requires further examination to delineate species boundaries.

Raja miraletus was considered wide-ranging in the Eastern Atlantic, but is a species-complex with R. miraletus occurring in the Northeast Atlantic and the Mediterranean Sea, R. parva occurring off West Africa, and R. ocellifera off South Africa (Last and Séret 2016).

A recent systematic revision by Carranza et al. (2016), using an integrative approach including molecular (mitochondrial and nuclear markers) and morphological data, indicated that the species originally classified as Asaccus caudivolvulus (Arnold and Gardner 1994, Gardner 2013) was an assemblage of three different species that started diversifying during the Mid-Miocene. The analyses uncovered a very old diversification event that resulted in a case of microendemicity, where three morphologically and ecologically similar medium-sized species previously classified within A. caudivolvulus coexist in a very short and narrow mountain stretch.

As a result of this study, two new species – A. gardneri and A. margaritae – were described, both of them widely distributed across the northern tip of the Hajar Mountains. Conversely the third species - Asaccus caudivolvulus sensu stricto - appears to be restricted to a small coastal area of the United Arab Emirates under heavy development, where the only two localities in which the species has ever been recorded occur. The molecular analyses indicate that A. caudivolvulus has been evolving independently from its sister taxon, A. gardneri, for approximately four million years.
This is a split from the broader concept of Abavorana luctuosa (Quah et al. 2017).

authority: Brito M., Almendariz-C., Batallas R. & Ron, 2017
Rada, Jeckel, Caorsi, Barrientos, Rivera-Correa & Grant, 2017
Fouquet, Vacher, Courtois, Villette, Reizine, Gaucher, Jairam, Ouboter & Kok, 2018
taxonomicNotes: This is a split from the broader concept of Anomaloglossus degranvillei (Fouquet et al. 2018).

authority: Fouquet, Vacher, Courtois, Villette, Reizine, Gaucher, Jairam, Ouboter & Kok, 2018
taxonomicNotes: This is a split from the broader concept of Anomaloglossus degranvillei (Fouquet et al. 2018).
Synonym = Enyaliosaurus bakeri (Stejneger, 1901). Not a basionym.
Description (Santos 1965) does not fit with generic diagnosis of Acanthagrion - taxonomic revision needed (von Ellenrieder and Garrison 2007).

Mokodongan et al. (2018) found that the Oryzias woworae species group (comprising of O. asinua, O. wolasi, and O. woworae) could not be separated by their body shape using geometric morphometrics and that their phylogenetic relationships did not support the presence of three species. As such, they recommend a taxonomic re-examination of the O.woworae species group.

Two subspecies, Atrytone arogos arogos and Atrytone arogos iowa, have been identified. Distribution and ecology for the taxa are quite different, however, morphological differences are subtle (NatureServe 2010).
There are no currently recognized subspecies (Selby 2005).
This species was removed from the synonymy of Craugastor mexicanus by Hedges et al. (2008).

Even though some previous studies have treated Limia as one of the multiple subgenera in the genus Poecilia (Rosen and Bailey 1963, Meredith et al. 2010, Meredith et al. 2011), several other works have supported the generic classification for Limia (Rodriguez 1997, Hamilton 2001, Poeser 2003, Palacios et al. 2016, Weaver et al. 2016). Here we endorse the latter assessment that Limia should be considered a valid, separate genus.

The taxonomic role of genera to express relationships among species is clearly justified in the case of Limia from different points of view. From the biogeographic point of view, species of Limia show a discrete distribution since they are only found in the Antilles, specifically on Hispaniola, Cuba, Jamaica and the island of Grand Cayman. However, only three species of Poecilia sensu strictu occur in this region and they are restricted exclusively to Hispaniola. In addition, recent phylogenetic analyses have found Limia to be a monophyletic group probably originated from a South American ancestor with the three Hispaniolan Poecilia species mentioned above representing the closest sister group (Palacios et al. 2016, Weaver et al. 2016). A very detailed analysis is currently in prep, also confirming Limia as monophyletic (Spikes et al. in prep). Moreover, there is enough morphological distinctiveness in characters with adaptive value such is the case of gonopodial traits to separate species of Limia and Poecilia (Rivas 1978). Furthermore, all of the existing ecological and behavioral literature is using Limia, not Poecilia. Not recognizing Limia as a separate genus from Poecilia may obscure their similarities and cause even more confusion in a group that in fact deserves more taxonomic attention.

This species was described from a single sulfidic spring on the south side of Lake Enriquillo, La Zurza. Another population was later reported from the north side of Lake Enriquillo, La Azufrada. The latter population was apparently lost to changes in Lake level. Another record from the village of Cabral is from a freshwater habitat. Clarification of the taxonomic and conservation status of this species is urgently needed.
