From the biogeographic point of view, species of Limia show a discrete distribution since they are only found in the Antilles, specifically on Hispaniola, Cuba, Jamaica and the island of Grand Cayman. However, only three species of Poecilia sensu strictu occur in this region and they are restricted exclusively to Hispaniola. In addition, recent phylogenetic analyses have found Limia to be a monophyletic group probably originated from a South American ancestor with the three Hispaniolan Poecilia species mentioned above representing the closest sister group (Palacios et al. 2016, Weaver et al. 2016). A very detailed analysis is currently in prep, also confirming Limia as monophyletic (Spikes et al. in prep). Moreover, there is enough morphological distinctiveness in characters with adaptive value such is the case of gonopodial traits to separate species of Limia and Poecilia (Rivas 1978). Furthermore, all of the existing ecological and behavioral literature is using Limia, not Poecilia. Not recognizing Limia as a separate genus from Poecilia may obscure their similarities and cause even more confusion in a group that in fact deserves more taxonomic attention.

Even though some previous studies have treated Limia as one of the multiple subgenera in the genus Poecilia (Rosen and Bailey 1963, Meredith et al. 2010, Meredith et al. 2011), several other works have supported the generic classification for Limia (Rodriguez 1997, Hamilton 2001, Poeser 2003, Palacios et al. 2016, Weaver et al. 2016). Here we endorse the latter assessment that Limia should be considered a valid, separate genus.

The taxonomic role of genera to express relationships among species is clearly justified in the case of Limia from different points of view. From the biogeographic point of view, species of Limia show a discrete distribution since they are only found in the Antilles, specifically on Hispaniola, Cuba, Jamaica and the island of Grand Cayman. However, only three species of Poecilia sensu strictu occur in this region and they are restricted exclusively to Hispaniola. In addition, recent phylogenetic analyses have found Limia to be a monophyletic group probably originated from a South American ancestor with the three Hispaniolan Poecilia species mentioned above representing the closest sister group (Palacios et al. 2016, Weaver et al. 2016). A very detailed analysis is currently in prep, also confirming Limia as monophyletic (Spikes et al. in prep). Moreover, there is enough morphological distinctiveness in characters with adaptive value such is the case of gonopodial traits to separate species of Limia and Poecilia (Rivas 1978). Furthermore, all of the existing ecological and behavioral literature is using Limia, not Poecilia. Not recognizing Limia as a separate genus from Poecilia may obscure their similarities and cause even more confusion in a group that in fact deserves more taxonomic attention.
According to Catalog of Fishes Tor progeneius is now considered a synonym of Tor putitora (Hamilton 1822) and the concepts have therefore been merged in this reassessment.

Barbus chlorotaenia, B. bigornei and B. inaequalis also have a large number of scales and long barbels, but B. boboi is distinguished from these species by its colour pattern (in particular, the large blotch at end of caudal peduncle), as well as by the downward flexion of the lateral line below dorsal fin.

Yang et al. (2015) placed B. boboi in Enteromius.

This species is similar to Barbus cadenati, B. guineensis and B. dialonensis which also possess an ossified and serrated last unbranched fin ray.

Yang et al. (2015) placed Barbus carcharhinoides in Enteromius.
Yang et al. (2015) placed Barbus clauseni in Enteromius.
Yang et al. (2015) placed Barbus liberiensis in Enteromius.
Yang et al. (2015) placed Barbus melanotaenia in Enteromius.
Yang et al. (2015) placed Barbus nigroluteus in Enteromius.
The relationship between this species and Urocotyledon palmeri requires additional research.

authority: Harvey, O'Connell, Barraza, Riyanto, Kurniawan & Smith, 2015
Siler, Davis, Watters, Freitas, Griffith, Binaday, Lobos Amarga & Brown, 2017

taxonomicNotes: Charadrius obscurus and C. aquilonius (del Hoyo and Collar 2014) were previously lumped as C. obscurus following Sibley and Monroe (1990, 1993).

Yang et al. (2015) placed Barbus stauchi in Enteromius.

Enteromius stauchi may be conspecific with E. rubrostigma, its description was based on preserved specimens and its colour pattern in life has not been recorded. In his description of Barbus stauchi, Daget (1967) did not compare it with Barbus miolepis rubrostigma. He was either unaware of this publication, or he considered it significantly different, since it entirely lacks serration on the last simple dorsal ray that Poll and Lambert (1964) erroneously reported as present in their description of Barbus miolepis rubrostigma. None of the type specimens nor any other specimen of E. rubrostigma examined shows the presence of a serrated last simple dorsal ray.
Yang et al. (2015), placed Barbus quadrilineatus in Enteromius.

Sitana attenboroughi was described from three specimens from a single locality at the northern range limit of Sitana marudhamneydhal. This description was based on a qualitative morphological character that appears to exhibit overlap with S. marudhamneydhal, and the describing authors' genetic phylogeny suggests that it differs by 2.2% (based on ND2 alone) from a sample of marudhamneydhal and less than 2% from a second specimen which they identified as "Sitana sp." and which, based on low genetic divergence from S. marudhamneydhal, is considered also to represent this species (V. Deepak and N.S. Naruyanan pers. comm. 2019). As other members of this group have been delimited based on greater morphological evidence, larger sample sizes, and sequence data - including multiple genes - that exhibit 3% divergence using ND2, this is considered insufficient evidence to recognize Sitana attenboroughi as a valid species, and this account follows the recommendation of V. Deepak (pers. comm. 2019) in retaining it within S. marudhamneydhal pending further research.

Amarasinghe et al. (2015) revised Sitana ponticeriana, removing S. deccanensis from synonymy and describing two new species - S. bahiri and S. devakai - from Sri Lanka. Subsequently, Deepak et al. (2018) suggested that S. bahiri should be considered a junior synonym of S. ponticeriana, citing morphological and molecular evidence reported in previous papers that suggested "extensive overlap between S. ponticeriana and S. bahiri". Confusingly, Deepak et al. (2016b), the paper they cite in support of the morphological similarity, makes no reference to the two taxa being difficult to diagnose morphologically, describes distinct differences in dewlap size, and provides distribution records for S. bahiri that appear not to correspond to the known range of the species recognized under this name by Amarasinghe et al. (2015) (but may represent records of "Sitana cf. bahiri", a genetically similar form that Deepak et al. 2016a explicitly refrained from referring to S. bahiri). Due to this confusion, it is unclear whether the molecular evidence referred to by Deepak et al. (2018) correspond to the original concept of S. bahiri. As these authors provided no novel data to resolve this confusion, S. bahiri is here treated as a valid species pending further taxonomic research.

The species name calamarius was replaced by templetoni because Coluber calamarius was considered to be the legitimate type species of Calamaria (see Brongersma et al. 1966, Bull. Zool. Nomencl. 22). However, Calamaria linnaei was later declared the type species of Calamaria by ICZN Opinion 2196. This species was therefore renamed from templetoni to calamarius and Oligodon calamarius is thus the correct name for this taxon (Uetz et al. 2018).
Further taxonomic studies are recommended on similar looking populations from parts of the Eastern Ghats (Ganesh and Arumugam 2016).

This species, which had not been recorded since its original description, was synonymized with Hemidactylus depressus by Smith (1935). Following the collection of additional material, Batuwita and Pethiyagoda (2012) determined that H. pieresii represents a validly distinct taxon and redescribed both species.
This species was described from populations previously understood to represent a northern isolate of Melanophidium punctatum (Gower et al. 2016).
This species was recently resurrected from synonymy within Uropeltis arcticeps by Ganesh et al. (2014).
This species was removed from synonymy within Uropeltis ceylanica by Ganesh et al. (2014).

This species has been considered as a synonym of Trimeresurus labialis (e.g. by Wallach et al. 2014) but was revalidated by Vogel et al. 2014. It has the type locality of Nicobar Islands; restricted to “Camorta Island, in the central Nicobars”.

The taxonomic history and range limits of Liasis mackloti are complex and still not fully resolved (Rawlings et al. 2004, D. Esquerre pers. comm. 2018). Confusion over range limits in Liasis fuscus, which was long considered a junior synonym of L. mackloti following Stuhl (1932), has resulted in a lack of clarity of the eastern and southern range limits of L. mackloti. Based on limited mtDNA sampling, Rawlings et al. (2004) found that Australian water pythons are divided into two clades, representing L. fuscus in Queensland and New Guinea and L. mackloti in the Northern Territory and the Indonesian islands Babar, Timor and Semau. Nevertheless, Australian workers have continued to recognize only a single water python species in this country pending resolution of taxonomic problems (Cogger 2014, Wilson and Swan 2017). New Guinean records have been attributed either to L. fuscus (McDowell 1975, Barker and Barker 1994, Barker et al. 2018) or to L. mackloti (O'Shea 2007, Cogger 2014). Carmichael et al. (2007) regard L. mackloti as an island endemic consisting of the subspecies L. m. mackloti, L. m. savuensis and L. m. dunni, excluding New Guinea from the distribution. These authors note that the three subspecies are allopatric and exhibit no evidence of hybridization. Rawlings et al. (2004) reported molecular evidence that the Sawu population, L. m. savuensis, may warrant specific recognition. Recently, both L. savuensis and L. dunni have been recognized as valid species (Barker et al. 2015, D. Esquerre pers. comm. 2018). Following these taxonomic changes L. mackloti is restricted to Timor, Roti, Semau and Alor (Barker et al. 2015).

The previous Red List assessments for Galapagos tortoises treated the various allopatric island populations as subspecies of Chelonoidis nigra (name now Chelonoidis niger), as did several authors (Pritchard 1996, Caccone et al. 1999, Beheregaray et al. 2003, Fritz and Havas 2007, TTWG 2007, Rhodin et al. 2008). However, other authors have considered them as full species based on morphology (Bour 1980, Fritts 1983, Ernst and Barbour 1989) and more recently several researchers (Caccone et al. 2002; Russello et al. 2005, 2007; Poulakakis et al. 2008, 2012, 2015; Chiari et al. 2009) have treated most of them as full species based on congruent patterns of mitochondrial and nuclear variation. This elevated species-level taxonomy has been largely accepted by TTWG (2009, 2014) and TEWG (2015) for most phylogenetic lineages of Galapagos tortoises. This Red List assessment treats C. phantasticus as a full species, rather than retaining its previous subspecies ranking from earlier taxonomies. The species has never before been assessed for the IUCN Red List.

Preliminary genetic analysis of the single old adult male saddlebacked holotype of C. phantasticus from Fernandina has indicated its apparently closer relationship with dome-shaped C. porteri from distant Santa Cruz rather than the more proximate populations of C. vicina on Isabela or any other saddlebacked taxa (Poulakakis et al. 2012). This apparent biogeographic anomaly, in addition to the lack of any discovery to date of skeletal tortoise remains or other living tortoises on Fernandina, has created some uncertainty as to its relationships. However, tortoise scats and bite marks on cacti found in 1964 have confirmed that other tortoises have occurred and more recent sightings and signs suggest that a few tortoises might yet persist on Fernandina.

Validity of new species status accepted by Dr T.A. Raadik (Research Associate, Museum Victoria), taxonomist, expert in Australian Galaxiidae taxonomy) and Dr Martin Gomon, Curator, Fishes, Museum Victoria. Voucher specimens lodge at Museum Victoria NMV A 30583-001 Galaxias sp. nov. ‘hunter’.

Validity of new species status accepted by Dr T.A. Raadik (Research Associate, Museum Victoria), taxonomist, expert in Australian Galaxiidae taxonomy) and Dr Martin Gomon, Curator, Fishes, Museum Victoria. Voucher specimens lodge at Museum Victoria NMV A 30569-002 Galaxias sp. nov. ‘yalmy’

Validity of new species status accepted by Dr T.A. Raadik (Research Associate, Museum Victoria), taxonomist, expert in Australian Galaxiidae taxonomy) and Dr Martin Gomon, Curator, Fishes, Museum Victoria. Voucher specimens lodge at Museum Victoria NMV A 31962-001 Galaxias sp. nov. ‘morwell’

Validity of new species status accepted by Dr T.A. Raadik (Research Associate, Museum Victoria), taxonomist, expert in Australian Galaxiidae taxonomy) and Dr Martin Gomon, Curator, Fishes, Museum Victoria. Voucher specimens lodge at Museum Victoria NMV A 31963-001 Galaxias sp. nov. ‘moroka’.

Chimpanzee taxonomy remains an active area of research, particularly in population genetics. Four subspecies are commonly recognised: the Western Chimpanzee (Pan troglodytes verus); the Nigeria-Cameroon Chimpanzee (P. t. ellioti); the Central Chimpanzee (P. t. troglodytes); and the Eastern Chimpanzee (P. t. schweinfurthii). Genetic data, which include analyses of complete genomes (Prado-Martinez et al. 2013), suggest that the subspecies form two distinctive groups: one group includes P. t. verus and P. t. ellioti and the other group includes P. t. troglodytes and P. t. schweinfurthii (Fünfstück et al. 2015). Pan t. verus and P. t. ellioti separated from one another much earlier than P. t. troglodytes from P. t. schweinfurthii. Each subspecies has experienced markedly different demographic histories since their separation 100,000 years ago, with all punctuated by dramatic reductions in their effective populations size (Gonder et al. 2011, Mitchell et al. 2015, Prado-Martinez et al. 2013). The degree of connectivity between chimpanzee populations in western Nigeria and those in eastern Nigeria and western Cameroon has yet to be adequately examined. Published relationship trees based on the analysis of mitochondrial DNA do not group western Nigerian chimpanzees closely with those of either Upper Guinea or eastern Nigeria (Gonder et al. 2006); however, those analyses were based on a very small number of western Nigerian samples. A more comprehensive analysis using additional samples is urgently needed.

While taxonomic labelling is often debated and subject to revision, the relative importance of different threats faced by chimpanzees varies across Africa, making a regional approach valuable for conservation purposes.

Until recently, synonymised under Galaxias olidus Günther, 1866 by McDowall and Frankenberg (1981) until found to be a valid species (Raadik 2011, Adams et al. 2014) and revised and re-elevated (Raadik 2014).

The species was originally discovered in 2012 and thought to be a northern outlier population of the Cairns Rainbowfish Cairnsichthys rhombosomoides (Martin and Barclay 2013). The northern populations were not included in any previous status assessment of Cairnsichthys rhombosomoides. Following genetic and morphological studies, the form was determined to be a distinct species and was formally described as part of a generic revision in 2018 (Hammer et al. 2018).
Authority should be Hoese and Allen 1983

Previously (del Hoyo and Collar 2014) placed in the genus Alopecoenas (as A. erythropterus) and before that in the genus Gallicolumba but transferred to Pampusana on the grounds of the latter’s priority (Bruce et al. 2016). Closely related to P. jobiensis, P. kubaryi and P. xanthonura; this group also has somewhat more distant affinities with P. sanctaecrucis, P. stairi and P. salamonis. Birds from Hao, Hiti and probably Tahanea sometimes accorded subspecific recognition as subspecies albicollis on grounds of all-white head and neck of males, but this may be merely a colour variant; in past, name pectoralis used for white-headed birds, but this name suppressed as based on female specimens from Arakita, which appear inseparable from those elsewhere in range; in addition, all males in population recently discovered on Rangiroa Atoll (N Tuamotus) are grey-naped (previously only all-white-headed males had been recorded in this part of archipelago), and taxonomic status of these is therefore unclear. Internal taxonomy of species requires reassessment. Treated as monotypic.
This species was previously placed in the family Mussidae but is now placed under Faviidae (Budd et al. 2012, Budd et al. 2019).

Historically, it has been proposed that American Eels (Anguilla rostrata) and European Eels (A. anguilla) may be conspecific (vertebral count differences were believed to be entirely ecophenotypic); this has now been shown to be incorrect by several studies. Separate species status has been confirmed by demonstrations that American and European Eels comprise two largely separate gene pools (see Avise et al. 1990, Bastrop et al. 2000, Avise 2003, Bernatchez et al. 2011). Eels in Iceland include, in low frequency, the products of hybridisation between American and European Eels (Avise et al. 1990, Avise 2003, Albert et al. 2006, Pujolar et al. 2014). Phylogenies of the genus Anguilla based on mitochondrial DNA have shown the two species to be distinct, although more closely related than most species of the genus (Aoyama et al. 2001, Minegishi et al. 2005).
This species is going to be split into two different species. D. diaboli with distribution in the US and in Rio Conchos in Mexico.

Species name previously listed as habroptila (del Hoyo and Collar 2014) though this change due to a mistaken application of gender agreement rules and S. habroptilus remains the valid species name (Savage and Digby 2023). Monotypic.

The species has been taxonomically supported since its description (Hollingsworth 1998). Morphological data places Sauromalus hispidus as sister to the clade containing S. ater, S. klauberi, and S. slevini (Hollingsworth 1998), while molecular data place S. hispidus as a sister to S. varius (Petren and Case 1997).
Séret and Naylor (2016) described Rhynchorhina mauritaniensis (and Rhynchorhina as a new genus) from limited specimens collected off Mauritania (Eastern Central Atlantic).

The new taxonomic concept for Squatina guggenheim includes S. punctata as a junior synonym.

There is some controversy concerning the taxonomy and nomenclature of Squatina species of the Southwest Atlantic. Squatina occulta described by Vooren and da Silva (1991) has been regarded as being a junior synonym of S. guggenheim by Soto (2001) in a species-level checklist of the region. Although evidence was not given to support this statement, Bernardes et al. (2005) followed Soto’s proposal. However, later studies have re-evaluated the species’ validity. The validity of S. occulta Vooren and da Silva, 1991 as a distinct species has been confirmed through the study of mitochondrial DNA by Stelbrink et al. (2010) and in a comparative study of Southwest Atlantic Squatina neurocrania (Carvalho et al. 2012). Both studies also recognized S. argentina, S. guggenheim, and S. occulta as the only valid species in the Southwest Atlantic (preceding the description of new species since that time, S. david and S. varii).

The Sei Whale, Balaenoptera borealis is a recognized species but catch and sightings records of Sei Whales prior to 1972 tended to include Bryde's Whales (B. edeni or B. brydei) in areas where both species occur. The Society for Marine Mammalogy’s Committee on Taxonomy (Committee on Taxonomy 2017) recognizes two subspecies, B. b. borealis in the Northern Hemisphere and B. b. schlegelii in the Southern Hemisphere, as proposed by Tomilin (1957). The Sei Whales found in the North Pacific and North Atlantic have not been formally compared and so are still placed in the same nominate subspecies by default, although Huijser et al. (2018) found a high degree of genetic divergence between North Atlantic and North Pacific Sei Whales.
Geospiza pauper (del Hoyo and Collar 2016) was previously placed in the genus Camarhynchus following SACC (2005 & updates); Sibley & Monroe (1990, 1993); Stotz et al. (1996).
Most closely related to S. stilesi and S. rodriguezi. Monotypic.
Pternistis ochropectus (del Hoyo and Collar 2014) was previously placed in the genus Francolinus.
Formicivora paludicola (del Hoyo and Collar 2016) is recognized following evidence presented in Buzzetti et al. (2013).

For Crocodylus rhombifer, there is strong evidence, based on morphology and genetic analyses, that widespread hybridization is taking place with Crocodylus acutus in the Cuban Archipelago (Milian-Garcia et al. 2014).

This species was previously included in the genus Eleutherodactylus (Crawford and Smith 2005). The rediscovery of this species in 1976 showed it to be a largely montane frog, occupying an apparently small range in the borderland of Costa Rica and Panama (Savage and Myers 2002).
Further taxonomic work is needed to finally determine whether Duellmanohyla lythrodes is a synonym of Duellmanohyla rufioculis (Federico Bolaños pers. comm. 2007).

The types of this species were collected by Domínguez-Domínguez in 2010 in a pond at San Sebastián near Etzatlán. The first specimens of this species in general were probably collected by C.L. Turner in a dam about 2km N of Etzatlán and at the type location (1939), but regarded as Xenotoca eiseni for many years (Xenotoca eiseni "San Marcos"). As the species occurs in two separate drainages, two subpopulations, the Laguna de Magdalena subpopulation (type subpopulation) and the Upper Río Ameca subpopulation can be inferred. Xenotoca doadrioi differs in many parametres from Xenotoca variata, the type species of the genus, means Doadrios Redtail Splitfin will be transfered in the future into a new to be erected genus.

The types of this species were collected by Domínguez-Domínguez in 2010 in the Río Tamazula and the Río San Geronimo, an affluent of the Río Tamazula at Vista Hermosa. The first specimens of this species in general were probably collected by D.S. Erdman in 1941 from the Río Tuxpán, but regarded as Xenotoca eiseni for many years (Xenotoca eiseni "Río Tamazula"). As the species occurs in two separate drainages, two subpopulations, the Río Tuxpán subpopulation (type subpopulation) and the Río Armería subpopulation are distinguished. Xenotoca lyonsi differs in many parametres from Xenotoca variata, the type species of the genus, means Lyons Redtail Splitfin will be transfered in the future into a new to be erected genus.
Anthochaera phrygia (del Hoyo and Collar 2016) was previously listed as Xanthomyza phrygia.

Following Kennedy and Spencer (2014) the genus Phalacrocorax has been divided into seven genera, with P. magellanicus, P. bougainvilliorum, P. atriceps, P. verrucosus, P. carunculatus, P. chalconotus, P. onslowi, P. campbelli, P. ranfurlyi and P. colensoi moved into Leucocarbo (HBW and BirdLife International 2018). In past, often considered a subspecies of P. carunculatus (which see). Monotypic.
Thalasseus bernsteini (del Hoyo and Collar 2014) was previously placed in the genus Sterna.

It is believed that the San José Brush Rabbit is closely related to the Brush Rabbit (S. bachmani) from mainland Baja California; it has been considered as a full species by different authors (Chapman 1974, Thomas and Best 1994, Cervantes et al. 1999). However, recent genetic data indicate its close affiliation with the Brush Rabbit (S. bachmani), suggesting that it may represent a subspecies of that form (Álvarez-Castañeda and Lorenzo 2016). The San José Brush Rabbit may be distinguished from the Brush Rabbit by its larger ears and paler pelage (Nelson 1907). Further genetic and taxonomic studies are necessary to clarify the relationship between these two species. No subspecies are recognized.

Genetic data indicate that this species is sister to S. pachecoi (Mata et al. 2009); these two form a clade together with S. petrophilus and S. diamantinensis (Maurício 2005, Whitney et al. 2010). See also S. speluncae. Monotypic.
Ptilinopus purpuratus and P. chrysogaster (del Hoyo and Collar 2014) were previously lumped as P. purpuratus following Sibley and Monroe (1990, 1993).
Nothobranchius bellemansi was established as a species by Valdesalici in 2014

Nothobranchius capriviensis was established as a species by Watters, Wildekamp and Shidlovskiy in 2014, and that status has since been accepted by all authors. The year for the journal issue in which the description appeared is 2014 but that issue of the journal was printed in August 2015, thereby representing the year in which the species description became available.

In early evaluations, this species was first listed as Nothobranchius sp. Caprivi in 1994 and later, in 2013, it was regarded as a population and colour form of N. kafuensis, a species that is relatively widespread in nearby Zambia.
Nothobranchius flagrans was established as a species by Nagy in 2014.

At the discovery in 2002, populations of this species found in Mafia Island were referred to N. aff. melanospilus, based on some general similarities with that species but also some important differences. Morphometric and molecular analyses confirmed the distinction from N. melanospilus and the populations from Mafia Island, together with other populations from coastal mainland Tanzania, were described as N. lucius by Wildekamp et al. in 2009. Nothobranchius insularis itself was established as a species by Costa in 2017, the name applied to the island populations occurring in Mafia Island, by separating them from the mainland populations of N. lucius.
Nothobranchius milvertzi was established as a species by Nagy in 2014, and that status has since been accepted by all authors.

Fundulus mkuziensis was established as a species by Fowler in 1934. Jubb (1963) synonymized it with N. orthonotus but the description and illustration in Fowler (1934) is not consistent with that opinion. Wildekamp (2004) proposed N. mkuziensis as separate species, based on morphometric, meristic and colour pattern elements.
Nothobranchius sainthousei was established as a species by Nagy, Cotterill and Bellstedt in 2016.

Populations of this species were regarded as N. aff. neumanni in Wildekamp (2004) before morphometric and molecular analyses confirmed it to be a separate species and it was described as Nothobranchius sagittae in Wildekamp et al. 2014.
Trochalopteron ngoclinhense (del Hoyo and Collar 2016) was previously listed as Garrulax ngoclinhensis.
Zosterops ficedulinus and Z. feae (del Hoyo and Collar 2016) were previously lumped as Z. ficedulinus following Dowsett & Forbes-Watson (1993) and Sibley & Monroe (1990, 1993).

Previously (del Hoyo and Collar 2014) placed in the genus Alopecoenas and before that in the genus Gallicolumba but transferred to Pampusana on the grounds of the latter’s priority (Bruce et al. 2016). Closely allied to P. stairi and P. salamonis, and more distantly to the P. jobiensis group. Monotypic.
Pternistis castaneicollis and P. atrifrons (del Hoyo and Collar 2014) were previously placed in the genus Francolinus and lumped as F. castaneicollis following Sibley and Monroe (1990, 1993).

Previously treated as Garrulax courtoisi (del Hoyo and Collar 2016) but moved to current genus following Cibois et al. (2018). Until recently treated as conspecific with P. galbanus. Isolated population in Simao, in S Yunnan, described as subspecies simaoensis on basis of yellowish-grey breastband, but this character sometimes present in Jiangxi population. Treated as monotypic.
Chlidonias albostriatus (del Hoyo and Collar 2014) was previously placed in the genus Sterna as S. albostriata.
Chlorophoneus kupeensis (del Hoyo and Collar 2016) was previously listed as Telophorus kupeensis.
Myrmoderus ruficauda (del Hoyo and Collar 2016) was previously listed as Myrmeciza ruficauda.

Spinus cucullatus (del Hoyo and Collar 2016) was previously placed in the genus Carduelis and listed as C. cucullata following AOU (1998 and supplements); SACC (2005 and updates); Sibley and Monroe (1990, 1993); Stotz et al. (1996).
Rhabdotorrhinus corrugatus (del Hoyo and Collar 2014) was previously placed in the genus Aceros.

Korpade et al. (2018) produce strong molecular evidence that Heteroglaux is a synonym of Athene. This is accepted here, hence Forest Owlet becomes Athene blewitti (Handbook of the Birds of the World and BirdLife International 2020).
Puffinus bryani (del Hoyo and Collar 2014) was first described by Pyle et al. (2011).
Berenicornis comatus (del Hoyo and Collar 2014) was previously placed in the genus Aceros.

Hydrobates leucorhous (del Hoyo and Collar 2014) has been split into H. leucorhous, H. socorroensis and H. cheimomnestes (which see) (Handbook of the Birds of the World and BirdLife International 2018), based on the proposal of Howell (2012).

Puffinus newelli (del Hoyo and Collar 2014) has been split into P. newelli and P. myrtae (Handbook of the Birds of the World and BirdLife International 2018). Martínez-Gómez et al. (2015) found that P. (n). myrtae, ‘Rapa Shearwater’, is genetically more distinct from newelli than newelli is from auricularis. On this basis they propose that myrtae be treated as a full species. Such an arrangement is supported by Shirihai et al. (2017), who noted:

'Rapa Shearwater is much smaller (in all biometric characters) than the other two taxa…, and is furthermore highly distinctive in having a unique combination of white undertail-coverts (vs. dark in both newelli and auricularis) and whitish inner webs to the remiges, as well as a white face.'

Following revised scoring of parsed characters for the differentiation of P. auricularis, P. newelli and P. myrtae, the latter emerges as a very strong candidate for species rank, given that the effect size for wing length vs the smaller of the other two taxa (auricularis), based on Table 3 in Shirihai et al. (2017), is –6.56 (score 3); white vs dark undertail-coverts (at least 2); whitish inner webs to the remiges (at least 1); and a ‘white face’ (impossible to interpret, and in fact not apparent in several photographs online).

We will seek to confirm the characters identified as distinctive by Shirihai et al. (2017) but accept the strong likelihood of at least minor vocal differentiation. Allowing 1 for such an eventuality, and having a score of 6 already on morphological grounds, we tentatively accord myrtae species status as Rapa Shearwater.
Sometimes placed in genus Stictocarbo or occasionally in Leucocarbo. In past, often considered a subspecies of P. punctatus. Monotypic.

P. auricularis (del Hoyo and Collar 2014) is retained as a separate species from P. newelli (which see) (Handbook of the Birds of the World and BirdLife International 2018), following a revision of the scoring of parsed characters for these taxa plus P. myrtae (Handbook of the Birds of the World and BirdLife International 2018), split from P. newelli (del Hoyo and Collar 2014).

Puffinus lherminieri (incorporating baroli), P. bannermani, P. persicus, P. subalaris and P. bailloni (del Hoyo and Collar 2014) were previously lumped as P. lherminieri and baroli was subsumed under P. assimilis, following Brooke (2004), before which P. bannermani and P. persicus where split following Sibley and Monroe (1990, 1993).
Hydrobates macrodactylus (del Hoyo and Collar 2014) was previously placed in the genus Oceanodroma as O. macrodactyla.
Hydrobates homochroa (del Hoyo and Collar 2014) was previously placed in the genus Oceanodroma.

Fregetta maoriana (del Hoyo and Collar 2014), endemic to New Zealand (Brooke 2004), was not listed as part of the avifauna by Turbott (1990) as it was previously believed to be extinct. Previously placed in the genus Oceanites as O. maorianus after Sibley and Monroe (1990, 1993) based on Oliver (1955). Has alternatively been listed as the basionym, Pealeornis maoriana (Mathews 1932), by Gill et al. (2010).
Long known only from putative fossil material and from three 19th-century specimens; was assessed as extinct. Has sometimes been considered a subspecies, or even merely a plumage variant, of Oceanites oceanicus, but is morphologically quite distinct. Recent phylogenetic work using cytochrome b and nuclear genes places it closer to Fregetta than to Oceanites (Robertson et al. 2011). Monotypic.
Synthliboramphus hypoleucus and S. scrippsi (del Hoyo and Collar 2014) were previously lumped as S. hypoleucus following Sibley and Monroe (1990, 1993).
This species previously appeared on the IUCN Red List in the genus Lampetra but is now considered valid in the genus Tetrapleurodon.

This is a newly described species split from the former Naja (Boulengerina) melanoleuca complex based on an an analysis of mitochondrial and nuclear gene sequences and morphological data (Wüster et al. 2018).

Has elsewhere been placed in genus Grus; or in Bugeranus together with B. carunculatus on basis of morphological and behavioural similarities, but molecular evidence has revealed deep distinctiveness of present species (Krajewski et al. 2010); alternatively, placed in separate monospecific genus, Sarcogeranus, but present monotypic genus (Leucogeranus) has priority. Three wintering subpopulations identified, but no evidence as yet of geographical variation. Monotypic.

This species is often misidentified with the Taiwan Angelshark (Squatina formosa). Diagnostic features for this species include pelvic fin tips which extend to origin of the first dorsal fin and angular pelvic, dorsal, and caudal fins (Walsh et al. 2011).

This species is often misidentified with other regional Squatinids. Diagnostic features for Squatina japonica include pelvic fin tips which do not reach the first dorsal origin and spines along the midback line (Walsh et al. 2011).
Ceyx fallax and C. sangirensis (del Hoyo and Collar 2014) were previously lumped as C. fallax following Sibley and Monroe (1990, 1993).

Otus magicus and O. tempestatis (del Hoyo and Collar 2014) were previously lumped as O. magicus, and O. alfredi, O. siaoensis, O. enganensis, O. insularis and O. beccarii (del Hoyo and Collar 2014) were split from O. magicus following Widodo et al. (1999), Lambert and Rasmussen (1998), Andrew (1992) and Holt et al. (1999). Prior to that all these taxa were lumped in O. magicus following Sibley and Monroe (1990, 1993).

Jønsson et al. (2018) establish that Cerulean Paradise-flycatcher Eutrichomyias rowleyi, Drongo Fantail Chaetorhynchus papuensis, Natewa Silktail Lamprolia klinesmithi and Taveuni Silktail L. victoriae belong in a separate ancient family, Lamproliidae, sister to the Rhipiduridae. This finding is accepted here, but it raises the issue of the English names of the flycatcher and the fantail. At present we opt for ‘Cerulean Flycatcher’, to remove the impression that the species is a member of the Monarchidae, but retain ‘Drongo Fantail’ given the proximity of the Lamproliidae to the fantails; but neither proposal is set in stone.

This species is one of only four species in the genus that belong to a rare group of freshwater crabs endemic to Sierra Leone, West Africa. This genus was recently transferred from the Gecarcinucidae and reassigned to the Potamonautidae: Deckeniinae: Deckeniini: Globonautina by Cumberlidge et al. (2008).

This species was described by Cumberlidge (1987) as Globonautes leonensis, and was later transferred to the genus Afrithelphusa by Cumberlidge (1999). This species is one of only four species in the genus hat belong to a rare group of freshwater crabs endemic to the upper guinea forest block of West Africa. This genus was recently transferred from the Gecarcinucidae and reassigned to the Potamonautidae: Deckeniinae: Deckeniini: Globonautina by Cumberlidge et al. (2008).

Bott (1959) described this species as Globonautes monodosus. Cumberlidge (1999) transferred this species to the genus Afrithelphusa Bott, 1969. This species is one of only four species in the genus that belong to a rare group of freshwater crabs endemic to the upper Guinea forest block of West Africa. This genus was recently transferred from the Gecarcinucidae and reassigned to the Potamonautidae: Deckeniinae: Deckeniini: Globonautina by Cumberlidge et al. (2007).

Bott (1959) treated this taxon as Globonautes macropus macropus, while Cumberlidge (1996, 1999) regarded Globonautes to be a monotypic genus and treated this taxon as Globonautes macropus. This species is one of only five species in two genera that belong to a rare group of freshwater crabs endemic to the upper Guinea forest block of West Africa. This genus was recently transferred from the Gecarcinucidae and reassigned to the Potamonautidae: Deckeniinae: Deckeniini: Globonautina by Cumberlidge et al. (2007). The species is very poorly sampled and a surveys are needed to understand the extent of the species.
The only published account of this species is Cumberlidge (1999).
The only published account of this species is Cumberlidge (1999).

Cumberlidge and Sachs (1989) described this species as Liberonautes latidactylus nanoides. Cumberlidge (1999) revised the taxonomy of this taxon and treated it as the valid species Liberonautes nanoides.

Eight species, Hypsipetes affinis, H. longirostris, H. mysticalis, H. harterti, H. aureus, H. platenae, H. chloris and H. lucasi have been moved from the genus Thapsinllas (del Hoyo and Collar 2016) following a comprehensive phylogeny of the bulbuls (Shakya and Sheldon 2017). All were previously lumped as Alophoixus affinis following Sibley and Monroe (1990, 1993).

Não existem revisões recentes do gênero, nem chaves de classificação disponíveis, o que impede a identificação acurada da maior parte das espécies de Ancistrus. Neste sentido, é possível que Ancistrus minutus ocorra em outros locais e esteja disponível em lotes depositados em coleções científicas, mas sem identificação taxonômica correta (C. Chamon, C. Cramer, J. Zuanon, L. Sousa e R. Oliveira obs. pess.).
We follow Alonso et al. (2023) in considering Matilebias a valid genus, rather than a synonym of Austrolebias.
Não há limitações taxonômicas relevantes à validação do táxon.

A espécie pode ser confundida, por não especialistas, com outras espécies do grupo reticulata que ocorrem na região ou em regiões próximas (e.g. Crenicichla caetana e Crenicichla stocki) pelo padrão de colorido reticulado do corpo (P.M. Ito e H.R. Varella obs. pess.).

A princípio, não existem limitações taxonômicas relevantes à validação do táxon. Hipóteses prévias de relações filogenéticas entre os gêneros de Diapomini, Planaltina, Diapoma e Acrobrycon são discutidas com base em informações morfológicas preliminares (Menezes et al. 2011). É proposto que o grupo a que pertencem, Diapomini, seja monofilético.
Subgenus (Pauciradius).
Subgenus (Rheoglanis)
Yang et al. (2015) placed Barbus subinensis in Enteromius.

Garra trewavasae Monod, 1950 was previously synonymised on the IUCN Red List with Garra ornata (Nichols & Griscom 1917). This synonymy has been removed and Garra trewavasae Monod, 1950 reinstated as a valid species.
Until recently this species was placed in Aplocheilichthys.
Some references to Tetraodon guttifer in the literature may represent misidentifications of Tetraodon pustulatus.
There are nuclear and mitochondrial genetic markers available for the identification of this species and its hybrids.

Molecular studies strongly suggest that Russian Sturgeon (Acipenser gueldenstaedtii) is very closely related to Persian Sturgeon (Acipenser persicus), and it may be the case that these two taxa are conspecific. However, this is still under discussion, and currently, they are treated as two separate species. This also holds true for the subspecies which has been classified as A. gueldenstaedtii colchicus, as well as A. persicus colchicus, which has not successfully been characterised genetically yet.

Molecular studies strongly suggest that Persian Sturgeon (Acipenser persicus) is very closely related to Russian Sturgeon (Acipenser gueldenstaedtii) and it cannot be excluded that these two taxa are conspecific. However, this is still under discussion, and currently they are treated as two separate species. The status of the Colchic Sturgeon is unresolved.
The Adriatic population described as a subspecies and may have been a conservation unit, is now extinct.

The taxonomy of Rutilus in the Mediterranean region remains partially unresolved, and this species is sometimes placed in the genus Leucos on the basis of minor molecular and morphologic characters (e.g., Bianco and Ketmaier 2014, Fricke et al. 2024).

For the purposes of this assessment it is retained in the genus Rutilus pending additional research (Barbieri et al. 2015).

This species was for a period believed to have a relatively wide distribution in France and northern Spain, but it is currently considered endemic to its type locality in southern France (Denys et al. 2021). The former taxonomic concept of B. quignardi included individuals now classified as B. hispanica.

The taxonomy of Barbatula species in Western Europe is not fully resolved. Subpopulations inhabiting rivers in Catalonia, northeastern Spain, and a number of French systems (including the Garonne and Rhône rivers) may represent undescribed taxa (Gauliard et al. 2019, Clavero et al. 2023).

This species was previously thought to have a broader range in Aegean Türkiye. However, taxonomic research has clarified that subpopulations from the Güzelhisar Stream and the Gediz/Büyük Menderes river systems belong to the congeneric taxa O. fatmae and O. germencicus, respectively (Yoğurtçuoğlu et al. 2022, Turan et al. 2024).

Molecular analyses have demonstrated that this species is closely-related to the congeners Knipowitschia panizzae and K. radovici, leading to suggestions that they correspond to a single taxon (Geiger et al. 2014, Thacker et al. 2019).

However, K. radovici and K. mrakovcici are exclusively found in freshwater habitats, while K. panizzae is euryhaline, and the three taxa are diagnosable by morphological characters (Kovačić 2005, Miller 2009).

It is therefore possible that they diverged very recently, in which case powerful genetic markers such as microsatellies or next-generation sequencing would be required to detect any genetic variability (Milana et al. 2021).

In the absence of a definitive review, these three taxa continue to be assessed separately for the Red List.

Bolling (1965) considered just three species of this genus for Germany. By 2001, CLECOM (Check List of European Continental Mollusca; Falkner et al. 2001) listed 70 species and subspecies for Germany in this problematic genus, noting that as long as identification was based solely on shell morphology, there will be no progress on resolving the taxonomic issues. Glöer and Zettler (2005) noted that for Germany nearly 70 different "species" of this genus were originally described.

A revision of this genus based on DNA analysis was urgently needed and Richling et al. (2017) published data which reduced this and a number of other species to synonymy with B. acicula. However, Glöer (2022) re-established the species, as he considered that the molecular data was insufficiently robust.

When originally described (Blakemore and Grygier 2011) the question was raised whether this is an endemic native, a translocated native or an introduced exotic.  Its nearest congeners however, from morphological and molecular analysis, appear to be Japanese native species and the genus and family are also endemic to Japan, but not uniquely (see Blakemore 2012b: Appendix).  On balance this is most likely a native taxon that is apparently rare, although there has yet to be any systematic field survey of Japan's earthworms (Easton 1981, Blakemore 2003, 2012a).
Original and valid as Liobagrus kingi Tchang 1935.
Originally Macrones medianalis, but changed in 1989 to Pseudobagrus medianalis by Burgess.
Original and valid as Silurus mento Regan 1904.

The genus Barbus (Cyprinidae) is restricted to a small number of species mainly inhabiting the European ichthyographic region including Northeast Africa. Most of the African species that were included in the genus, taxonomically do not appear to be closely related to the genus Barbus sensu strictu.

Yang et al. (2015) transferred this species to the genus Enteromius.
This species has now been transferred to the genus Garra (Hamidan et al. 2014, Sayyadzadeh et al. 2015).

O nome Hopliancistrus tricornis tem sido utilizado erroneamente para designar espécies de aparência similar, mas ainda não descritas formalmente, das bacias dos rios Xingu e Tapajós (J.A.S. Zuanon e R.R. de Oliveira, obs. pess.).

The genus Barbus (Cyprinidae) is restricted to a small number of species mainly inhabiting the European ichthyographic region including Northeast Africa. Most of the African Barbus species that were included in the genus, taxonomically do not appear to be closely related to the genus Barbus sensu strictu (Seegers et al. 2003).

The African Barbus s.l. are further divided into two major groups, i.e. the large-size hexaploid species and the small-size 'Barbus' (diploid) (Berrebi et al. 1996). The former have since been placed in the genus Labeobarbus Rüppel, 1836 (Berrebi 1998, Skelton 2001, Berrebi and Tsigenopoulos 2003).

Yang et al. (2015) transferred this species to Labeobarbus.

The Western Gorilla (Gorilla gorilla) has two recognized subspecies: the Western Lowland Gorilla (Gorilla gorilla gorilla) and the Cross River Gorilla (Gorilla gorilla diehli: Sarmiento and Oates 2000, Groves 2001). Genetic data suggest that the two subspecies of Western Gorilla diverged approximately 18,000 years ago (Thalmann et al. 2011) and that the Cross River Gorilla population can be clearly differentiated from Western Lowland Gorillas (Prado-Martinez et al. 2013). The taxonomic status of the Gorilla populations in Ebo (Cameroon) awaits clarification; however, measurements from a single Ebo Gorilla skull indicate this may be a relict population of a previously more widespread population living north of the Sanaga River (Groves 2005).
This species was been moved from the genus Hyloscirtus to Colomascirtus (Duellman et al. 2016), and then subsequently moved back to Hyloscirtus (Rojas-Runjaic et al. 2018).
Espécie endêmica da bacia do médio rio São Francisco, que pertence ao complexo de espécie Hypsolebias flavicaudatus (Costa et al. 2014).
Outros nomes aplicados ao táxon:Simpsonichthys marginatus (Costa e Brasil 1996).
Espécie endêmica da bacia do médio rio São Francisco, que pertence ao complexo de espécie Hypsolebias flavicaudatus (Costa et al. 2014).
Não existem limitações taxonômicas relevantes à validação do táxon.
Molecular analyses suggest that this species is very closely related to the congener Cobitis hellenica (Perdices et al. 2018). The taxonomy of these species might therefore be reviewed in the future.
This species was returned to the original genus Tellia by Freyhof & Yoğurtçuoğlu (2020).
This species was transferred to the genus Apricaphanius by Freyhof & Yoğurtçuoğlu (2020).
Molecular evidence suggests that this species is very closely-related to the congener Alburnus arborella, and additional systematic research is recommended (Geiger et al. 2014).

The systematics of Alburnoides taxa from the western Balkan region remains unresolved, and they are sometimes described as belonging to the "Alburnoides prespensis complex".

In particular, the results of molecular analyses suggest that the congeners A. devolli and A. fangfangae, plus subpopulations inhabiting the Vjosa (el. Aoös), Skhumbin, Dukati and Borsh river systems in Albania and Greece, might be conspecific with A. prespensis (Geiger et al. 2014, Jouladeh-Roudbar et al. 2016, Stierandová et al. 2016, Tsoupas et al. 2022).

This species was formerly considered native to a series of river systems draining from eastern Italy to the Adriatic Sea, but these subpopulations were in 2021 reassigned to the congeneric Italian Barbel (Barbus plebejus), Samnite Barbel (B. samniticus) and Fucine Barbel (B. fucini). Subpopulations inhabiting rivers south of the Tiber system on the Tyrrhenian slope were also reassigned to the Fucine Barbel (Lorenzoni et al. 2021).
Borkenhagen and Krupp (2013) transferred this species to the genus Carasobarbus.

Molecular analyses suggest that this taxon might be conspecific with the Vardar Spined Loach (Cobitis vardarensis), albeit no explicit systematic review has yet been published (Barbieri et al. 2015, Perdices et al. 2018). Moreover, there exists unpublished data indicating that it is genetically distinct, therefore further research is required (S. Zogaris pers. comm.).

In some published literature, this taxon is treated as a subpopulation of the Bourget Whitefish (Coregonus lavaretus) or a member of the "Coregonus lavaretus species complex", and referred to by the English vernacular names "Common Whitefish" or "European Whitefish" (see below).

At the regional scale, the systematics of European ciscoes and whitefishes (Coregonus spp.) has been the subject of considerable debate since the turn of the 21st century, with little indication of a definitive outcome.

It is widely accepted that non-anadromous members of this group have repeatedly undergone adaptive radiations in boreal, subarctic and perialpine lakes (Douglas et al. 1999, Østbye et al. 2005, Kahilainen and Østbye 2006, Harrod et al. 2010, Hudson et al. 2011).

Subpopulations inhabiting these systems largely diversified in the wake of the most recent glacial period 10,000-15,000 years ago. They are typified by parallel patterns of divergence in traits associated with foraging (i.e., gill raker counts, benthic vs. pelagic feeding ecology), physiology (i.e., growth rate, habitat depth partitioning) and reproductive ecology (i.e., reproductive timing and spawning habitat).

Up to six different sympatric forms sometimes referred to as “morphs”, "ecomorphs" or "ecotypes" have been recorded in perialpine lakes, and these radiations might be even more diverse in some large systems of northeastern Europe (Præbel et al. 2013, Doenz et al. 2018, Bitz‐Thorsen et al. 2020, Öhlund et al. 2020).

However, there exist significant differences in opinion regarding the taxonomic status of these forms.

In Northern Europe, including the United Kingdom, all except a handful of Irish subpopulations are usually regarded as belonging to two widely distributed species; Coregonus albula (ciscoes) and C. lavaretus (whitefishes), with the latter also frequently referred to as the "Coregonus lavaretus species complex” (Etheridge et al. 2012, Wanke et al. 2017, Häkli et al. 2018, Crotti et al. 2020). Within this comparatively uniform taxonomic concept, the catch-all English vernacular name “Vendace” is typically used for C. albula while "Common Whitefish" or "European Whitefish" are applied to C. lavaretus.

Conversely, researchers and fisheries authorities based in Central Europe have tended to treat the different forms as distinct taxa, leading to the recognition of more than 60 species across the European region (Kottelat and Freyhof 2007, Selz et al. 2020, De-Kayne et al. 2022, Selz and Seehausen 2023). Under this diverse taxonomic concept, the name “Vendace” is restricted to its original usage for the United Kingdom endemic Coregonus vandesius and the native range of C. lavaretus is limited to its type locality of Lake Bourget in France.

With the above in mind, it appears unlikely that the striking phenotypic diversity exhibited by members of this genus will ever be adequately represented by a single accepted taxonomic system. In the absence of a region-wide consensus, the IUCN Red List broadly follows the taxonomy provided by Fricke et al. (2024).

British whitefishes
The taxonomic status of Coregonus subpopulations inhabiting the British Isles has not been definitively resolved since a series of endemic species were described between the early 19th and early 20th centuries.

Among these, C. pennantii is most closely related to C. clupeoides Lacépède 1803 from central Scotland and C. stigmaticus Regan 1908 from northwestern England. A number of recent studies have demonstrated that there is little morphological or genetic support for considering these as distinct taxa when they are compared with one another (Etheridge et al. 2012, Crotti et al. 2020).

In the United Kingdom, all three of these taxa are treated as junior synonyms of the congener Coregonus lavaretus (Linnaeus 1758), the name of which has since the mid-20th century been applied to a widespread complex of polymorphic whitefish populations occurring across northwestern Eurasia (Crotti et al. 2021). However, the systematics of this putative assemblage and the native range of C. lavaretus remain questionable (see further discussion below).

For IUCN Red List purposes, C. pennantii is currently regarded as valid following Fricke et al. (2024), and the present assessment should therefore be considered equivalent to the most recent Welsh national assessment for C. lavaretus (Nunn et al. 2023).

European ciscoes and whitefishes
At the regional scale, the systematics of European ciscoes and whitefishes (Coregonus spp.) has also been the subject of considerable debate since the turn of the 21st century, with little indication of a definitive outcome.

It is widely accepted that non-anadromous members of this group have repeatedly undergone adaptive radiations in boreal, subarctic and perialpine lakes (Douglas et al. 1999, Østbye et al. 2005, Kahilainen and Østbye 2006, Harrod et al. 2010, Hudson et al. 2011).

Subpopulations inhabiting these systems largely diversified in the wake of the most recent glacial period 10,000-15,000 years ago. They are typified by parallel patterns of divergence in traits associated with foraging (i.e., gill raker counts, benthic vs. pelagic feeding ecology), physiology (i.e., growth rate, habitat depth partitioning) and reproductive ecology (i.e., reproductive timing and spawning habitat).

Up to six different sympatric forms sometimes referred to as “morphs”, "ecomorphs" or "ecotypes" have evolved in some perialpine lakes, and these radiations might be even more diverse in a few large systems of northeastern Europe (Præbel et al. 2013, Doenz et al. 2018, Bitz‐Thorsen et al. 2020, Öhlund et al. 2020).

However, there exist significant differences in opinion regarding the taxonomic status of these forms.

With the above in mind, it appears unlikely that the striking phenotypic diversity exhibited by members of this genus will ever be adequately represented by a single accepted taxonomic system. In the absence of a region-wide consensus, the IUCN Red List broadly follows the taxonomy provided by Fricke et al. (2024).
Unpublished molecular analyses suggest that this putative species might prove to be a synonym of the European Bullhead (Cottus gobio) (Freyhof pers. comm.).

An unidentified and potentially conspecific whitefish subpopulation inhabits Lake Mond (de. Mondsee), which is located adjacent to Lake Atter. It is known to reproduce one month earlier than C. atterensis, although individuals displaying this behaviour were recorded in Lake Atter following translocation during the early 20th century.

They do not appear to have become established, and the taxonomic status of the Lake Mond subpopulation remains in need of clarification.

British whitefishes
The taxonomic status of Coregonus subpopulations inhabiting the British Isles has not been definitively resolved since a series of endemic species were described between the early 19th and early 20th centuries.

In the United Kingdom, C. vandesius is treated as a junior synonym of the congener Coregonus albula (Linnaeus 1758), the name of which has since the mid-20th century been applied to a widespread complex of polymorphic cisco populations occurring across northwestern Eurasia (Mehner et al. 2021). However, the systematics of this putative assemblage and the native range of C. albula remain questionable (see further discussion below).

For IUCN Red List purposes, C. vandesius is currently regarded as valid following Fricke et al. (2024), and the present global assessment should be considered equivalent to the most recent English and Scottish national assessments for C. albula (Nunn et al. 2023).

This species is closely-related to the Skadar Gudgeon (Gobio skadarensis), and the taxonomy of gudgeons inhabiting the Western Balkans has not been fully-resolved (Geiger et al. 2014, Shumka et al. 2018, Grapci-Kotori et al. 2020).

Some authorities, for example, the Catalog of Fishes (Fricke et al. 2024) consider this species to be a junior synonym of Gobio tauricus Vasil'eva 2005, but the description of that species was apparently published one day later than that of G. delyamurei (Kottelat & Bogutskaya 2006), hence G. delyamurei has priority.

More recently, it has been suggested that G. delyamurei might be synonymous with Gobio krymensis Bănărescu & Nalbant 1973, and that its only known subpopulation is not native (Dyldin et al. 2020).
This species was treated as a subpopulation of the congeneric Iberian Arched-Mouth Nase (Iberochondrostoma lemmingii) prior to its description (Doadrio and Carmona 2003).

There exists very low genetic distance between this species and the congeneric Corfu Dwarf Goby (Knipowitschia goerneri), leading to proposals for them to be synonymised (Thacker et al. 2019).

On the other hand, it has been demonstrated that these fishes correspond to distinct taxa based on morphology plus the results of cytochrome b and COI analyses (Vukić et al. 2016, 2017, Milana et al. 2021).

Their respective ranges in the western Ionian Sea basin remain in need of clarification, and a potentially conspecific and threatened subpopulation inhabiting the island of Zakynthos (el. Ζάκυνθος) cannot currently be assigned to either species to the ongoing uncertainty (Vanhove et al. 2016).

Molecular analyses have demonstrated that this species is closely-related to the congeners Knipowitschia panizzae and K. radovici, leading to suggestions that they correspond to a single taxon (Geiger et al. 2014, Thacker et al. 2019).

However, K. radovici and K. mrakovcici are exclusively found in freshwater habitats, while K. panizzae is euryhaline, and the three taxa are diagnosable by morphological characters (Kovačić 2005, Miller 2009).

It is therefore possible that they diverged very recently, in which case powerful genetic markers such as microsatellies or next-generation sequencing would be required to detect any genetic variability (Milana et al. 2021).

In the absence of a definitive review, these three taxa continue to be assessed separately for the Red List.

The taxonomic status of putative Epirus Minnow subpopulations inhabiting the Louros and Arachthos river systems, the southern Ambracian Gulf and Lefkada Island is in need of confirmation.

Although molecular evidence indicates that they might be conspecific (Tsaparis et al. 2021, Viñuela Rodríguez et al. 2021), they are omitted from this assessment pending a conclusive review.

Records of Phoxinus subpopulations from the upper Strymon, Loudias and Filiouris rivers have been considered to represent this species in the past, but their taxonomic status remain unresolved and they are not considered for the purposes of this assessment.

At the wider scale, The Channel Minnow (Phoxinus phoxinus), formerly Common or Eurasian Minnow, was for a considerable period of time treated as the only European member of the genus Phoxinus. However, at least 23 distinct mitochondrial lineages and 14 valid species have been identified in the region since the mid-2000s. This diversity is likely to increase further, as comprehensive information for all major European river systems has not yet been compiled. Moreover, additional sampling plus morphological analyses are required to corroborate some putative species divisions which are currently supported only by inconclusive nuclear data (Denys et al. 2020, Palandačić et al. 2017, 2020, 2022, Bogutskaya et al. 2023).

Taxonomic determination has been further hampered by natural phenotypic variability within the different lineages, plus their disparate geographic distributions which do not generally reflect patterns observed in related European fish genera. Furthermore, anthropogenic translocations linked to recreational fisheries have in some cases led to uncertainty regarding native range boundaries and driven introgressive hybridisation between congeners (Museth et al. 2007, Knebelsberger et al. 2015, Miró and Ventura 2015, Ramler et al. 2017, Vucić et al. 2018, Corral-Lou et al. 2019, Palandačić et al. 2020, 2022, De Santis et al. 2021).

As a result of the increasing species diversity, much of the published literature referring to Phoxinus phoxinus in fact pertains to other taxa and should therefore be reviewed with due care.

The taxonomy of Rutilus in the Mediterranean region remains partially unresolved, and this species is sometimes placed in the genus Leucos on the basis of minor molecular and morphologic characters (e.g., Bianco and Ketmaier 2014, Fricke et al. 2024).

For the purposes of this assessment it is retained in the genus Rutilus pending additional research (Barbieri et al. 2015).

Although the systematics of Eurasian and North African brown trouts (Salmo spp.) remain somewhat disputed, this taxon is well-established as a genetically and morphologically distinct species that is clearly differentiated from members of the Brown Trout (Salmo trutta) complex (Whiteley et al. 2019). It has previously been classified in the genera Acantholingua and Salmothymus (Sušnik et al. 2006, Snoj et al. 2009, Pustovrh et al. 2014).

There is currently no general consensus regarding the systematic classification of Eurasian and North African brown trouts, an assemblage comprising all representatives of the genus Salmo except the well-differentiated Atlantic Salmon (Salmo salar), Marble Trout (Salmo marmoratus), Softmouth Trout (Salmo obtusirostris) and Ohrid Belvica (Salmo ohridanus). While numerous, often range-restricted, members of this grouping have been described based largely on their ecological and morphological diversity, this variability is not consistently reflected by phylogenetic and phylogeographic evidence (Sanz 2018, Whiteley et al. 2019, Segherloo et al. 2021).

Despite a relatively recent diversification history spanning the period 0.5-2.5 Mya, brown trouts exhibit marked ecological and phenotypic variability throughout their large native range, which extends eastward from Europe and Northwest Africa to Russia and the Aral Sea basin. They occupy a wide range of habitats, from mountain streams and larger rivers to lakes and estuaries. Individual subpopulations can exhibit sedentary, anadromous or potamodromous life history strategies. Some freshwater systems are inhabited by multiple sympatric forms which differ in traits associated with foraging and reproductive ecology, and are sometimes referred to as "morphs", "ecomorphs" or "ecotypes" (Klemetsen et al. 2003, Kottelat and Freyhof 2007, Ferguson et al. 2019, Segherloo et al. 2021).

Some authorities have viewed this combination of factors to be representative of high species diversity and recognised around 50 nominal taxa, a number of which have been described this century (Kottelat and Freyhof 2007, Snoj et al. 2011, Sanz 2018). Alternatively, their systematics have been viewed from a phylogenetic and phylogeographic perspective based largely on mitochondrial DNA (mtDNA) analyses, with all subpopulations treated as a single polymorphic taxon customarily referred to as the “Brown Trout (Salmo trutta) complex” (Sanz 2018, Whiteley et al. 2019, Segherloo et al. 2021).

The latter approach led to brown trout diversity being defined by ten mtDNA lineages or sublineages corresponding to extensive catchments (the Danube, Atlantic, Mediterranean and Adriatic basins), specific geographic areas (the Balkan Peninsula and North Africa), individual watersheds (the Dades, Duero and Tigris rivers) and a distinctive phenotype (Marble Trout). Subsequent studies revealed that the distribution of some of these mtDNA lineages extends beyond their defined boundaries, e.g., the Adriatic lineage occurs from the Iberian Peninsula to the Republic of Türkiye, and the Marble Trout lineage is present in areas where no marbled phenotype exists, such as Corsica, central Italy, Albania and Greece (Bernatchez et al. 1992, Apostolidis et al. 1997, Bernatchez 2001, Suárez et al. 2001, Cortey and García-Marín 2004, Sušnik et al. 2005, 2007, Splendiani et al. 2006, Martínez et al. 2007, Snoj et al. 2009, 2011, Tougard et al. 2018, Schöffmann et al. 2022).

However, several studies have revealed the presence of mosaic distributions of mtDNA haplogroups among wild brown trout populations, plus mitochondrial-nuclear phylogenetic discordance in reconstructions made with both mitochondrial and nuclear trees (Snoj et al. 2009, Pustovhr et al. 2014, Splendiani et al. 2020). This suggests the presence of incomplete lineage sorting or asymmetric introgressive hybridization, which are common phenomena in rapidly diverging lineages and indicate that mtDNA genealogies might be generally unsuitable for defining phylogenetic relationships between brown trout taxa (Pustovhr et al. 2011, 2014). The validity of this taxon has been considered questionable in the past, but is unambiguously supported by a range of genetic and morphological evidence (Gigliarelli et al. 2013, Tancioni et al. 2013, Lucentini et al. 2014).

Since the mid-2000s, rudd subpopulations from the Adriatic and Ligurian/Tyrrhenian sea basins of Italy have been referred to as S. hesperidicus Bonaparte 1845 and S. scardafa (Bonaparte 1837), respectively. However, these two taxa are diagnosed only on the basis of minor meristic and molecular characters, and their taxonomy has not been definitively resolved (Ketmaier et al. 2003, 2004, Bianco 2014, Geiger et al. 2014, Lorenzoni et al. 2019).

It has also been suggested that the introduction of S. hesperidicus caused S. scardafa to go extinct throughout its native range during the 20th century. The only extant S. scardafa subpopulation thus occupies Lake Scanno in Abruzzo and purportedly originates from nearby Lake Fucine, which was artificially drained during the late 1800s (Bianco 2004, 2014).

However, there is no clear evidence that this translocation took place, and it is perhaps unlikely that the introduction of S. hesperidicus to some locations within the range of S. scardafa would have driven the latter to extinction within the space of a few decades (Lorenzoni et al. 2019).

Moreover, the results of a comparative analysis demonstrate that Italian rudd subpopulations exhibit a defined phylogeographic structure, with no significant anthropogenic influence on their native distribution (Stefani et al. 2004). In addition, older literature indicates that there exists natural variation in meristic characters, which have not been fully-investigated (Lorenzoni et al. 2019)

Molecular research also suggests that both S. hesperidicus and S. scardafa are closely-related to S. plotizza, which is native to the Neretva River system in the western Balkan Peninsula. Therefore, additional systematic research is recommended (Geiger et al. 2014).

An integrative taxonomic approach combining morphological and ecological data with next generation sequencing of nDNA to identify genomic clusters may represent the most promising option for resolving brown trout systematics (Guinand et al. 2021, Segherloo et al. 2021). However, no comprehensive morphological or nDNA analyses have yet been completed, and it is plausible that the elaborate genetic and phenotypic diversity demonstrated by these fishes may never be adequately captured by a single accepted taxonomic system (Whiteley et al. 2019).

Pending a definitive outcome to the above, the Red List broadly follows the nomenclature provided by Fricke et al. (2024).

Two phenotypically-distinct forms of this taxon inhabit the upper and middle-lower stretches of the Trebižat River, respectively. They are largely separated by the subterranean stretch of the river, and it is hypothesised that they may represent distinct species (Bogutskaya et al. 2019). Further research is required, since their splitting is not fully-supported by existing molecular analyses (Perea et al. 2010, Geiger et al. 2014, Schönhuth et al. 2018).

This taxon is currently regarded as valid for global Red List purposes, but was treated as a subpopulation of the Arctic charr, Salvelinus alpinus (Linnaeus 1758), for the most recent national assessment (Nunn et al. 2023).

The taxonomic status of Salvelinus subpopulations inhabiting the British Isles has not been definitively resolved since a series of endemic species were described between the mid-19th and early 20th centuries (Adams and Maitland 2007).

In the United Kingdom and Ireland, all of these taxa are treated as junior synonyms of the congeneric Arctic Charr, Salvelinus alpinus (Linnaeus 1758), the name of which has been routinely applied to a widespread complex of polymorphic charr populations occurring throughout the Holarctic region (Jonsson and Jonsson 2001). However, there remains considerable uncertainty regarding the systematics of the genus across the majority of this range (Reist et al. 2013, Taylor 2016, Whiteley et al. 2019).

Members of this "Arctic Charr complex" exhibit bewildering subpopulation-scale ecological and morphological variability. When such divergence occurs within a single lake system, the different sympatric forms are often referred to as “morphs”, “morphotypes”, "ecomorphs" or "ecotypes" (Snorrason et al. 1994, Adams et al. 1998, Knudsen et al. 2006, Klemetsen 2010, Muir et al. 2016).

Some of these subpopulations and sympatric forms have over time been described as nominal species, including at least 15 from North America, around 30 from Europe and 12 from Siberia and the Far East. However, these taxa encompass only a small fraction of charr distribution and diversity, and there exist significant differences in opinion regarding which of them should be considered valid (Savvaitova 1995, Adams and Maitland 2007, Kottelat and Freyhof 2007, Klemetsen 2010, Whiteley et al. 2019).

With the above in mind, there is an emerging consensus that the striking genetic and phenotypic diversity exhibited by members of this genus cannot be adequately represented by a single accepted taxonomic system (Whiteley et al. 2019).

The Red List currently follows the nomenclature provided by Fricke et al. (2024), albeit a species-oriented conservation management approach is unlikely to prove appropriate for members of this genus (Barthelemy et al. 2023; also see 'Conservation').

The taxonomy of Mediterranean trouts remains unresolved, and has been complicated by widespread anthropogenic translocation of different genetic lineages throughout the region.

Although S. cettii was described from the island of Sicily, trout subpopulations native to Sardinia and the Tyrrhenian slope of western Italy have in the past been treated as conspecific. However, molecular analyses subsequently revealed that the Sicilian subpopulations are genetically divergent from other Italian trouts, and possibly related to the Atlantic Brown Trout (Salmo trutta) mitochondrial lineage (Segherloo et al. 2021). For the purposes of this assessment, S. cettii is thus considered endemic to Sicily and northern Algeria (see 'Geographic Range' for further details).

In addition, this assessment follows the conclusions of Splendiani et al. (2019) in considering Salmo cettii to be a senior synonym of Salar (later Salmo) macrostigma Duméril 1858.

Trout subpopulations from southwestern France (Garonne, Adour and Aude rivers) and the Iberian Peninsula (Ebro River) have formerly been referred to S. macrostigma, but are likely to represent distinct taxa and thus are not considered within the framework of this assessment.

In some published literature, this species is referred to as a subpopulation of the Arctic Charr (Salvelinus alpinus), and there remains considerable uncertainty regarding the evolutionary relationships and taxonomy of the genus Salvelinus (Reist et al. 2013, Taylor 2016, Whiteley et al. 2019).

Members of this genus exhibit bewildering subpopulation-scale ecological and morphological variability throughout their global range. When such divergence occurs within a single lake system, the different sympatric forms are often referred to as “morphs”, “morphotypes”, "ecomorphs" or "ecotypes" (Snorrason et al. 1994, Adams et al. 1998, Knudsen et al. 2006, Klemetsen 2010, Muir et al. 2016).

Some of these subpopulations and sympatric forms have over time been described as nominal species, including at least 15 from North America, around 30 from Europe and 12 from Siberia and the Far East. However, these taxa encompass only a small fraction of charr distribution and diversity and there exist significant differences in opinion regarding which of them should be considered valid (Savvaitova 1995, Adams and Maitland 2007, Kottelat and Freyhof 2007, Klemetsen 2010, Whiteley et al. 2019).

With the above in mind, there is an emerging consensus that the striking genetic and phenotypic diversity exhibited by members of this genus cannot be adequately represented by a single accepted taxonomic system (Whiteley et al. 2019).

The Red List currently follows the nomenclature provided by Fricke et al. (2024), albeit a species-oriented conservation management approach is unlikely to prove appropriate for members of this genus (Barthelemy et al. 2023; also see 'Conservation').

This taxon was considered to be a synonym of the congeneric Peloponnese Chub (Squalius peloponensis) in the recent past, and its systematic status remains in need of review.

Individuals collected in the Vouraikos River system (see 'Geographic Range') have recently been identified as S. moreoticus, but molecular research suggests that this subpopulation is probably conspecific with S. peloponensis (Tsoupas et al. 2022).

Moreover, the two species cannot be differentiated by external morphology. Samples collected from Lake Stympahlia prior to the drying event in 1989 (see 'Population' and 'Threats') should ideally be reanalysed for comparative purposes, since they were in the past recovered as genetically distinct (Doadrio and Carmona 1998).

There is currently no broad consensus regarding the systematic classification of Eurasian and North African brown trouts, an assemblage comprising all representatives of the genus Salmo except the well-differentiated Atlantic Salmon (Salmo salar), Marble Trout (Salmo marmoratus), Softmouth Trout (Salmo obtusirostris) and Ohrid Belvica (Salmo ohridanus). While numerous, often range-restricted, members of this grouping have been described based largely on their ecological and morphological diversity, this variability is not consistently reflected by phylogenetic and phylogeographic evidence (Sanz 2018, Whiteley et al. 2019, Segherloo et al. 2021).

Despite a relatively recent diversification history spanning the period 0.5-2.5 Mya, brown trouts exhibit marked ecological and phenotypic variability throughout their large native range, which extends eastward from Europe and Northwest Africa to Russia and the Aral Sea basin. They occupy a wide range of habitats, from mountain streams and larger rivers to lakes and estuaries. Individual subpopulations can exhibit sedentary, anadromous or potamodromous life history strategies. Some freshwater systems are inhabited by multiple sympatric forms which differ in traits associated with foraging and reproductive ecology, and are sometimes referred to as "morphs", "ecomorphs" or "ecotypes" (Klemetsen et al. 2003, Kottelat and Freyhof 2007, Ferguson et al. 2019, Segherloo et al. 2021).

Some authorities have viewed this combination of factors to be representative of high species diversity and recognised around 50 nominal taxa, a number of which have been described this century (Kottelat and Freyhof 2007, Snoj et al. 2011, Sanz 2018). Alternatively, their systematics have been viewed from a phylogenetic and phylogeographic perspective based largely on mitochondrial DNA (mtDNA) analyses, with all subpopulations treated as a single polymorphic taxon customarily referred to as the “Brown Trout (Salmo trutta) complex” (Sanz 2018, Whiteley et al. 2019, Segherloo et al. 2021).

The latter approach led to brown trout diversity being defined by ten mtDNA lineages or sublineages corresponding to extensive catchments (the Danube, Atlantic, Mediterranean and Adriatic basins), specific geographic areas (the Balkan Peninsula and North Africa), individual watersheds (the Dades, Duero and Tigris rivers) and a distinctive phenotype (Marble Trout). Subsequent studies revealed that the distribution of some of these mtDNA lineages extends beyond their defined boundaries, e.g., the Adriatic lineage occurs from the Iberian Peninsula to the Republic of Türkiye, and the Marble Trout lineage is present in areas where no marbled phenotype exists, such as Corsica, central Italy, Albania and Greece (Bernatchez et al. 1992, Apostolidis et al. 1997, Bernatchez 2001, Suárez et al. 2001, Cortey and García-Marín 2004, Sušnik et al. 2005, 2007, Splendiani et al. 2006, Martínez et al. 2007, Snoj et al. 2009, 2011, Tougard et al. 2018, Schöffmann et al. 2022).

However, several studies have revealed the presence of mosaic distributions of mtDNA haplogroups among wild brown trout populations, plus mitochondrial-nuclear phylogenetic discordance in reconstructions made with both mitochondrial and nuclear trees (Snoj et al. 2009, Pustovhr et al. 2014, Splendiani et al. 2020). This suggests the presence of incomplete lineage sorting or asymmetric introgressive hybridization, which are common phenomena in rapidly diverging lineages and indicate that mtDNA genealogies might be generally unsuitable for defining phylogenetic relationships between brown trout taxa (Pustovhr et al. 2011, 2014). In the case of brown trouts, naturally intricate patterns of diversification and secondary contact shaped by repeated glaciations during the Pleistocene have been additionally complicated by widespread anthropogenic translocation and introgressive hybridisation since the Middle Ages (Largiadèr and Scholl 1996, Sanz et al. 2006, Lerceteau-Köhle et al. 2013).
