An integrative taxonomic approach combining morphological and ecological data with next generation sequencing of nDNA to identify genomic clusters may represent the most promising option for resolving brown trout systematics (Guinand et al. 2021, Segherloo et al. 2021). However, no comprehensive morphological or nDNA analyses have yet been completed, and it is plausible that the elaborate genetic and phenotypic diversity demonstrated by these fishes may never be adequately captured by a single accepted taxonomic system (Whiteley et al. 2019).

Pending a definitive outcome to the above, the Red List broadly follows the nomenclature provided by Fricke et al. (2024).

The taxonomic status of chub subpopulations inhabiting river systems draining to the Bay of Gibraltar east of the Miel River in southern Spain is currently unclear. Formerly identified as Squalius malacitanus, they may actually be conspecific with S. gaditanus (Doadrio et al. 2023).

Some recent studies suggest that this taxon represents a translocated subpopulation of the Black Sea Trout (Salmo labrax), which is native to the Danube River system (Marić 2019, Mrdak and Milošević 2023). However, the results of molecular analyses indicate that the Nikšić Trout is native, has been isolated for a considerable period of time, and belongs to a different mitochondrial lineage than trouts inhabiting the middle and lower Zeta River (Pustovrh et al. 2014, Simonović et al. 2017).

At the broader scale, there is currently no general consensus regarding the systematic classification of Eurasian and North African brown trouts, an assemblage comprising all representatives of the genus Salmo except the well-differentiated Atlantic Salmon (Salmo salar), Marble Trout (Salmo marmoratus), Softmouth Trout (Salmo obtusirostris) and Ohrid Belvica (Salmo ohridanus). This taxon has been regularly treated as a synonym or subspecies of the congeneric Italian Chub (Squalius squalus) since the 1980s (Bianco and Recchia 1983, Kottelat 1997, Bianco 2014, Lucentini et al. 2014). As a result, it was not included in the most recent checklist of Italian freshwater fishes or Red List of Italian Vertebrates (Lorenzoni et al. 2019, Rondinini et al. 2022). In contrast, the majority of recent molecular analyses have concluded that it represents a valid species (Perea et al. 2010, Geiger et al. 2014, Buj et al. 2019). There also exists evidence that individuals from Lake Trasimeno and nearby tributaries in the Tiber (it. Tevere) River system may together represent a distinct genetic lineage (Lucentini et al. 2014).

Valencia letourneuxi and the congeneric Peloponnese Valencia (Valencia robertae) were considered to represent a single taxon prior to 2014, thus a number of earlier studies and conservation actions refer to both species under the name of the former (Freyhof et al. 2014).

Molecular analyses suggest that this species and the congener Telestes dabar Bogutskaya, Zupančič, Bogut & Naseka 2012 might be conspecific (Buj et al. 2017). No systematic review has yet been published, therefore the Red List continues to follow Fricke et al. (2024) in treating them as separate taxa.
This species is not considered valid by authorities in France, and it was omitted from the most recent iteration of the French National Red List (UICN Comité français 2019).
Orr & Hämäläinen (2013) placed specimens from Brunei and Sarawak that had previously treated as P. triangularis into two new species, leaving only the holotype female under the name P. triangularis.

Sánchez-Vialas et al. (2018) argued that the described characters of the lost holotype of Algyroides hidalgoi fall within the range of variation in A. marchi, implying that the older name A. hidalgoi should take precedence. Doubt, however, remains over this interpretation, and whether the type locality of A. hidalgoi was correctly reported (Speybroeck et al. 2020). While noting that opinions within the Society of European Herpetology were divided on the issue, these authors recommend retaining the widely-used name A. marchi to maintain nomenclatural stability pending the results of an application to the International Commission for Zoological Nomenclature.

A subsequent commentary on taxonomic issues within the Spanish herpetofauna (Sánchez-Vialas et al. 2024) addressed this concern and concluded that evidence was sufficient to justify the use of the name Algyroides hidalgoi as proposed by Sánchez-Vialas et al. (2018), and this is followed by the Reptile Database (Uetz et al. 2025).

Successful hybridisation between endemic Macrovipera schweizerii from Milos and adjacent islands reported in Kamelin et al. (1997) and results of a recent comprehensive mitochondrial phylogeny of European vipers (Freitas et al. 2020) support suggestions that this species is conspecific with M. lebetinus. These results are however preliminary and no formal taxonomic change has yet been proposed (Speybroeck et al. 2020, W. Wüster pers. comm. 2022) and they are retained as separate species at present.

Individual islet subpopulations are likely to represent Evolutionarily Significant Units (despite shallow genetic divergence - Rodriguez et al. 2013), and as many as 28 morphological subspecies have been proposed (Rodriguez et al. 2013, Salvador et al. 2015).
The validity of this species is uncertain, as it may be a striped morph of Chalcides colosii, a species known from nearby areas that differs only in patterning (Martínez del Mármol et al. 2019).

A lineage from the northeast of Gran Canaria has been described as the distinct species Chalcides bistriatus on morphological grounds (Pasteur et al. 1988). While this arrangement has a genetic basis the same lines of evidence indicate that the divergence between the two is both shallow and incomplete, suggesting both a young divergence and a lack of reproductive isolation (Speybroeck et al. 2020). On this basis Speybroeck et al. (2020) concur with Mayer and Tiedemann (1991) that C. bistriatus is best considered conspecific with C. sexlineatus.
This taxon is considered a full species based on evidence from Odierna et al. (1996), Arribas (2001), Crochet et al. (2004), and Mayer and Arribas (1996, 2003).

This species was formerly treated as a subspecies of Iberolacerta monticola, but genetic and morphological data support a full specific rank (Mayer and Arribas 2003, Arribas and Carranza 2004, Crochet et al. 2004).

Recent literature has adopted the spelling Podarcis raffeonae for this species following a proposed emendation by Michaels and Bauer (2004). Arribas (2017) concluded that this emendation was unjustified and this assessment consequently retains the spelling raffonei, and this arrangement was accepted by Speybroeck et al. (2020).
This species exhibits high intraspecific genetic diversity based on a small number of specimens (Mendes et al. 2017).
Was under Iguanidae.
This is a split from the broader concept of Leptophryne cruentata (Hamidy et al. 2018).
Ceragrion georgiferyi was formerly considered a subspecies of Ceriagrion tenellum but is now considered to be a full species (Schneider 1986, Kalkman 2005).

Populations of Caridina holthuisi appear in two allopatric clades, with one in Lake Matano, Petea River and Lake Mahalona and the other in Lake Towuti. No morphological differences have been observed so far. The existence of two cryptic species is therefore likely.

authority: Bornschein, Baldo, Pie, Firkowski, Ribeiro, & Corrêa, 2015
Baldo, Bornschein, Pie, Firkowski, Ribeiro, & Belmonte-Lopes, 2015

taxonomicNotes: This species when described was allocated to the Brachycephalus pernix group (Pie and Ribeiro 2015), a group consisting of 18 formally recognized species (Monteiro et al. 2018). Studies with molecular data confirmed the delimitation of the species (Firkowski et al. 2016). However, Condez et al. (2017) suggest the taxonomic revaluation of B. quiririensis, due to problems in its diagnosis and original description. Pie et al. (2017) refute the arguments and point to the absence of evidence to support a taxonomic reassessment of the species. B. auroguttatus and B. leopardus occur in the same region as B. quirirensis but not in sympatry, their populations are isolated from each other by more than 3 km in Serra do Araçatuba/Serra do Quiriri, a single mountain range located on the border of the states from Paraná and Santa Catarina (Bornschein et al. 2016).
Existem incertezas quanto à atribuição ao gênero Leporinus.
Following Eschmeyer (2004) Cynolebias marmoratus was transferred to Leptolebias marmoratus.

Fricke et al. (2020) notes that this species was placed into synonymy with Cynolebias sandrii, but sandrii is no longer available and so splendens should be treated as a valid species. Costa (2016) transferred Leptolebias splendens to a new genus Leptopanchax.

Não existem limitações taxonômicas relevantes à validação do táxon. Segundo Villa-Verde et al. (2012), Listrura camposae foi registrada somente em dois pontos da bacia do Ribeira (SP), em um dos quais, a localidade-tipo, a subpopulação já foi extirpada. A subpopulação de Listrura previamente identificada como L. camposae em Florianópolis, Santa Catarina, na verdade trata-se de uma espécie não identificada. A espécie consta na portaria MMA no 445/2014 como Listrura camposi (Miranda-Ribeiro, 1957).

authority: de Oliveira, Zuanon, Rapp Py-Daniel & Rocha, 2010

taxonomicNotes: A localidade-tipo da espécie é a caverna Areias de Cima, parte do sistema Areias, na margem esquerda do rio Betari, de onde provém a maior amostra estudada. As amostras provenientes das demais cavernas dessa margem, as quais, inclusive, localizam-se em aquíferos distintos, são relativamente pequenas, insuficientes para estudos morfológicos conclusivos no que diz respeito à identidade da espécie que, em princípio, encontra-se fragmentada. Além disso, essas populações estariam isoladas daquelas da margem esquerda do rio Betari, pois este corre sobre um dique de diabásio, insolúvel, que impede a conexão subterrânea entre esses dois conjuntos de cavernas. Diferenças morfológicas foram observadas, mas, novamente, o pequeno tamanho das amostras disponíveis, condicionado pelas dificuldades de acesso e pela aparente extinção de uma das populações da margem direita devido à poluição por mineração, ainda não permite conclusões robustas sobre o status taxonômico dessas populações. No entanto, a presença do dique de diabásio intersectando a área atribuída à espécie nominal é forte evidência de que existam pelo menos duas espécies crípticas; o nome Pimelodella kronei fica para a população das Areias, resultado de colonização e isolamento independentes, seguido de evolução paralela, fenômeno constatado para outras espécies troglóbias, tais como os lambaris cegos mexicanos do gênero Astyanax (E. Trajano e M.E. Bichuette obs. pess.).
Não existem limitações taxonômicas relevantes à validação do táxon.

Stygichthys typhlops é uma espécie altamente especializada para a vida no meio freático. É o único Characiforme troglóbio da América do Sul. Gênero monotípico, descrito de um único exemplar coletado na década de 1960, foi então atribuído ao complexo Hyphessobrycon-Hasemania de Tetragonopteridae (Characidae). Com base em amostra de 26 exemplares coletados 40 anos depois, Moreira et al. (2010) redescreveram a espécie, porém a questão da posição taxonômica ainda não estava resolvida. Oliveira et al. (2011), entretanto, encontraram evidências morfológicas e filogenéticas para manter a espécie em Characidae, sendo que S. typhlops forma um grupo robusto com Coptobrycon bilineatus, tendo como grupo-irmão, o grupo composto por Probolodus heterostomus, Deuterodon iguape e Myxiops aphos. Todas essas espécies habitam formações geológicas antigas das regiões nordeste e sudeste do Brasil, que também são áreas de residência de linhagens primitivas de outros importantes grupos de peixes, como aqueles pertencentes às famílias Trichomycteridae e Loricariidae. Note-se que, a despeito da grande diversidade de Characiformes em corpos d´água epígeos, apenas S.typhlops especializou-se para a vida subterrânea, em contraste com as mais de 36 espécies troglóbias de Siluriformes na América do Sul. Tal característica, muito rara entre Characiformes, ilustra a relevância taxonômica dessa espécie singular (M.E. Bichuette ob. pess.).
There is ongoing discussion amongst workers since some hamlet species have been known to produce hybrids (Victor and Marks 2018).
This species was removed from synonymy with Stegonotus florensis on morphological and biogeographic grounds by Kaiser et al. (2018).

Two pairs of specimens were recently collected from the Salsa and Cachoeira rivers in Bahia, each pair belonging to a different lineage and provisionally classified as Achirus aff. mucuri; no genetic data for specimens from the Mucuri River are available, thus the complex of species may be of two or three species (A. Carvalho-Filho pers. comm. 2024).
There are three subspecies of the San Lorenzo Deermouse; Peromyscus interparietalis interparietalis, P. interparietalis lorenzi, and P. interparietalis ryckmany (Ruíz and Flores 2014).
This species was included on the 2000 IUCN Red List as Isothrix picta (DD). The Rodent Specialist Group note that Echimys pictus is the correct name. It is now listed by IUCN as Callistomys pictus.

This species is distinguished from all congeners (P. kiyawensis, P. gambiensis and P. chirioi) by the following unique combination of characters: males with more than 50 spots on flank forming irregular stripes, irregular spots on proximal portion of anal fin, pectoral fin with spots, females with spots on the flank; dorsal fin with 15-17 rays (Valdesalici 2013).

This species is distinguished from other congeners (P. chioroi, P. kiyawensis and P. seymouri) by the following unique combination of characters: males with about 5-20 spots on flank, each being greater in diameter than the radius of the pupil, proximal stripe on anal fin, dorsal fin tip reaching centre of caudal peduncle, pelvic fin short, not reaching anus, females with dots on flank and fins; dorsal fin with 15-17 rays and 26-31 scales in median lateral series (Valdesalici 2013).

This species is distinguished from other congeners (P. chioroi, P. gambiensis and P. seymouri) by the following unique combination of characters: males with about 30 - 50 spots on flank, single proximal stripe on anal fin, dorsal fin tip reaching the caudal fin, pelvic fin reaching anus, females with dots on flank and fins; dorsal fin with 15-17 rays and 26-31 scales in median lateral series (Valdesalici 2013).

This species was considered a synonym of Clupeonella abrau until the early 1990’s (Whitehead 1985) when it was recognized as a valid sub-species of Clupeonella abrau by three authors in the 1990’s (Mogil’chenko 1991, Reshetnikov et al. 1997, Dorofeeva 1998). It was later recognized as a valid species after 2007 (Fricke et al. 2007).

Sardinella tawilis is the only freshwater species of Sardinella and is closely related to the marine sister-species, S. hualiensis (Willette et. al. 2014). A recent genetic study indicates that S. tawilis  diverged from S. hualiensis in the late Pleistocene, prior to the formation of Taal Lake in the 18th century (Willette et al. 2014).
Coilia mystus is easily confused with the co-occurring C. nasus (Whitehead et al. 1988).

The scientific name of this species has been contested in the past. In China, it has been referred to by Coilia ectenes (Whitehead et al. 1988).

There are taxonomic uncertainties surrounding the species of Coilia present in China, Korea, and Japan. For the purposes of this assessment, we are treating C. nasus as being distributed in China, Korea, and Japan, as treated in Whitehead et al. (1988).
Castro (2011) resurrected Carcharhinus cerdale, considering it distinct from Carcharhinus porosus. Carcharhinus cerdale is restricted to the Eastern Pacific and C. porosus to the Western Atlantic.

Perea et al. (2010) transferred this species to the genus Tropidophoxinellus.

The previous Red List assessments for Galápagos tortoises treated the various allopatric island populations as subspecies of Chelonoidis nigra (now named Chelonoidis niger), as did several authors (Pritchard 1996, Caccone et al. 1999, Beheregaray et al. 2003, Fritz and Havas 2007, TTWG 2007, Rhodin et al. 2008). However, other authors have considered them as full species based on morphology (Bour 1980, Fritts 1983, Ernst and Barbour 1989) and the more recent consensus among researchers (Caccone et al. 2002; Russello et al. 2005, 2007; Poulakakis et al. 2008, 2012, 2015; Chiari et al. 2009) is to treat most as full species based on congruent patterns of mitochondrial and nuclear variation. This elevated species-level taxonomy was largely accepted by TTWG (2009, 2014) and TEWG (2015) for most, but not all, phylogenetic lineages of Galápagos tortoises, while synonymizing the four central and southern Isabela Island populations (vicina, guntheri, microphyes, and vandenburghi) under C. vicina. However, TTWG (2017) and this Red List assessment now treat these four Isabela Island taxa, previously treated as subspecies of vicina, as separate and valid species, rather than retaining their previous rankings.

The taxonomy of the C. vicina species complex remains less resolved than for other Galápagos taxa, with four taxa on central (microphyes, vandenburghi) and southern (guntheri, vicina) Isabela treated here as a series of four recognized species. These most recently diverged lineages may not have been completely sorted into reciprocally monophyletic clades in terms of mitochondrial DNA data, although their nuclear gene pools are distinct (Ciofi et al. 2006, Poulakakis et al. 2008, Edwards et al. 2014). They may possibly represent subspecies or Evolutionarily Significant Units (ESUs) rather than full species (Edwards et al. 2014). TTWG (2009, 2014), following Pritchard (1996), synonymized these four taxa under C. vicina, but in consideration of the majority of the assessors’ view that they may be full species, TTWG (2017) and the Red List now treats them as separate species.

Tortoises from southern Isabela Island have also been referred to as C. (n.) elephantopus or C. (n.) vicina for the southwestern Cerro Azul population and C. (n.) elephantopus or C. (n.) guentheri or C. (n.) guntheri for the southeastern Sierra Negra subpopulation. The guentheri and guntheri names have previously either been considered nomina dubia and referred to vicina based on type locality, or synonymized under C. (n.) vicina by morphology. Tortoises from Darwin Volcano on north central Isabela have historically been recognized as C. (n.) microphyes, a name that has previously been considered either a nomen dubium and referred to vicina based on type locality, or synonymized under C. (n.) vicina by morphology (review by Pritchard 1996). Tortoises from Alcedo Volcano on central Isabela have traditionally been recognized as C. (n.) vandenburghi, but were previously synonymized under C. (n.) vicina based on morphology (Pritchard 1996; Fritz and Havas 2007; TTWG 2009, 2014).

All these taxa have in the past variously been argued to be valid at species or subspecies level (under either elephantopus and/or nigra), and placed in the genera Testudo, Geochelone, or Chelonoidis, and these permutations, plus a variety of typographic errors, have yielded over 50 nomenclatural combinations and chresonyms.
Levin et al. (2012) transferred this species to the genus Luciobarbus.

According to recent molecular studies this species does not belong to the genus Phoxinellus or Pseudophoxinus, and is most closely related to the Iberian endemic Anaecypris hispanica (e.g., Perea et al. 2010, Geiger et al. 2014).
Experts have suggested that this species may be a spring form of Eurytides dolicaon deicoon (Tyler et al. 1994).

The latter was previously considered a subspecies of Parides panthonus, but was synonimised with P. burchellanus at the species level by both morphological observations as well as by genetic analysis (Mielke et al. 2004, Silva-Brandão et al. 2008).

Undescribed species. Affinity appears to be with Mexican golden trout in the adjacent Fuerte basin, but genetic work shows this trout to be unique. Nathaniel Thomas Lupton collected two specimens of trout  from this region in 1884 (Cope, 1886), but the location of the specimens and the collection locality remain a mystery.

Five endemic Salmo populations from northwest Africa are currently recognised as valid species. All are members of the so-called 'Salmo trutta' (brown trout) complex', which is widely distributed across much of the Palearctic region. However, taxonomic classification within this complex remains a matter of discussion due to its complicated evolutionary history and inherent phenotypic diversity. While a consensus regarding the existence of several distinct phylogeographic lineages (Atlantic, Duero, Mediterranean, Adriatic, Marmoratus, Danubian and Tigris) has been reached, the evolutionary relationships between them remain unclear. In northwest Africa, mitochondrial and nuclear DNA analyses have revealed the existence of three major clades which derived from multiple waves of ancient colonisation; (1) an Afro-Atlantic clade inhabiting Mediterranean coastal rivers of North Africa and the Sous River system in Atlantic Morocco, stemming from a colonisation wave of the Atlantic lineage from Iberia, (2) a relict and native clade inhabiting the Draa River in Atlantic Morocco and (3) a native Atlas Mountains clade which probably arose from a reticulate evolutionary event, following invasion by Atlantic trout that introgressed into local populations.

The drawing of concrete conclusions is complicated, however, because the stocking of hatchery trout appears to have driven significant introgression in some populations. Furthermore, the Salmo trutta complex reaches the southern limit of its natural range in northwest Africa, and most rivers in the region are too warm to support trout populations. Those populations that do exist are mostly confined to high altitude streams and lakes, or perennial headwaters of short Mediterranean rivers. This degree of isolation, coupled with the relatively extreme environmental conditions, has permitted localised adaptation and morphological differentiation which is not necessarily reflected in distinct genetic signatures. In fact, the population inhabiting the Draa River (currently valid as Salmo multipunctatus) constitutes the only case in which a fully preserved indigenous genetic signature has been detected. Additional research employing a broader genomic survey has therefore been recommended to more clearly elucidate the evolutionary and taxonomic relationships between northwest African Salmo populations.

Salmo pellegrini is included in the Atlas Mountains clade, but there is evidence of introgression with stocked trout. This is particularly apparent within the Rheriya River subpopulation, where one individual was found to form a monophyletic group with trouts from the geographically distant Lake Isni (this population is currently valid as Salmo viridis) and the heavily-stocked Ziz River. Other individuals from the Rheriya shared haplotypes with trouts from a hatchery in northern Morocco plus an introduced population inhabiting Lake Tamda in the High Atlas Mountains. The subpopulation from the Ourika River exhibits a more distinct genetic signature, but also share haplotypes with trouts from the Moulouya River, which empties into the Mediterranean Sea in northeast Morocco.

Wickramasinghe et al. (2019) described a new species, Aspidura desilvai sp. nov., closely resembling A. trachyprocta and the last addition to the genus, from Knuckles massif, Matale District, of Sri Lanka.

The taxonomy of Alburnoides species from the western Balkan region remains unresolved, and the results of molecular analyses suggest that A. devolli might be conspecific with the congener A. prespensis (Geiger et al. 2014, Jouladeh-Roudbar et al. 2016, Stierandová et al. 2016).

In addition, a congeneric subpopulation inhabiting the Vjosa (el. Αώος/Aoös) River system in Albania and Greece has been referred to as A. cf. devolli, but is omitted from this assessment pending resolution of its identity.

This placement has subsequently been verified by a molecular study involving all European lampreys (Pereira et al., 2021).

The taxonomic status of an unidentified and potentially conspecific whitefish subpopulation inhabiting Lake Wolfgang (de. Wolfgangsee) in Austria is in need of clarification.

In some published literature, the Austrian Whitefish is treated as a subpopulation of the Bourget Whitefish (Coregonus lavaretus) or a member of the "Coregonus lavaretus species complex", and referred to by the English vernacular names "Common Whitefish" or "European Whitefish" (see below).

At the regional scale, the systematics of European ciscoes and whitefishes (Coregonus spp.) has been the subject of considerable debate since the turn of the 21st century, with little indication of a definitive outcome.

It is widely accepted that non-anadromous members of this group have repeatedly undergone adaptive radiations in boreal, subarctic and perialpine lakes (Douglas et al. 1999, Østbye et al. 2005, Kahilainen and Østbye 2006, Harrod et al. 2010, Hudson et al. 2011).

Subpopulations inhabiting these systems largely diversified in the wake of the most recent glacial period 10,000-15,000 years ago. They are typified by parallel patterns of divergence in traits associated with foraging (i.e., gill raker counts, benthic vs. pelagic feeding ecology), physiology (i.e., growth rate, habitat depth partitioning) and reproductive ecology (i.e., reproductive timing and spawning habitat).

Up to six different sympatric forms sometimes referred to as “morphs”, "ecomorphs" or "ecotypes" have been recorded in perialpine lakes, and these radiations might be even more diverse in some large systems of northeastern Europe (Præbel et al. 2013, Doenz et al. 2018, Bitz‐Thorsen et al. 2020, Öhlund et al. 2020).

However, there exist significant differences in opinion regarding the taxonomic status of these forms.

In Northern Europe, including the United Kingdom, all except a handful of Irish subpopulations are usually regarded as belonging to two widely distributed species; Coregonus albula (ciscoes) and C. lavaretus (whitefishes), with the latter also frequently referred to as the "Coregonus lavaretus species complex” (Etheridge et al. 2012, Wanke et al. 2017, Häkli et al. 2018, Crotti et al. 2020). Within this comparatively uniform taxonomic concept, the catch-all English vernacular name “Vendace” is typically used for C. albula while "Common Whitefish" or "European Whitefish" are applied to C. lavaretus.

Conversely, researchers and fisheries authorities based in Central Europe have tended to treat the different forms as distinct taxa, leading to the recognition of more than 60 species across the European region (Kottelat and Freyhof 2007, Selz et al. 2020, De-Kayne et al. 2022, Selz and Seehausen 2023). Under this diverse taxonomic concept, the name “Vendace” is restricted to its original usage for the United Kingdom endemic Coregonus vandesius and the native range of C. lavaretus is limited to its type locality of Lake Bourget in France.

With the above in mind, it appears unlikely that the striking phenotypic diversity exhibited by members of this genus will ever be adequately represented by a single accepted taxonomic system. In the absence of a region-wide consensus, the IUCN Red List broadly follows the taxonomy provided by Fricke et al. (2024).

taxonomicNotes: Molecular data indicate that this taxon and the congeneric Striped Dace, Telestes metohiensis (Steindachner 1901), might be conspecific (Buj et al. 2017).
This species was treated as a subpopulation of the congeneric Striped Dace (Telestes metohiensis) prior to its description (Bogutskaya et al. 2012).

There is currently no broad consensus regarding the systematic classification of Eurasian and North African brown trouts, an assemblage comprising all representatives of the genus Salmo except the well-differentiated Atlantic Salmon (Salmo salar), Marble Trout (Salmo marmoratus), Softmouth Trout (Salmo obtusirostris) and Ohrid Belvica (Salmo ohridanus). While numerous, often range-restricted, members of this grouping have been described based largely on their ecological and morphological diversity, this variability is not consistently reflected by phylogenetic and phylogeographic evidence (Sanz 2018, Whiteley et al. 2019, Segherloo et al. 2021).

Despite a relatively recent diversification history spanning the period 0.5-2.5 Mya, brown trouts exhibit marked ecological and phenotypic variability throughout their large native range, which extends eastward from Europe and Northwest Africa to Russia and the Aral Sea basin. They occupy a wide range of habitats, from mountain streams and larger rivers to lakes and estuaries. Individual subpopulations can exhibit sedentary, anadromous or potamodromous life history strategies. Some freshwater systems are inhabited by multiple sympatric forms which differ in traits associated with foraging and reproductive ecology, and are sometimes referred to as "morphs", "ecomorphs" or "ecotypes" (Klemetsen et al. 2003, Kottelat and Freyhof 2007, Ferguson et al. 2019, Segherloo et al. 2021).

Some authorities have viewed this combination of factors to be representative of high species diversity and recognised around 50 nominal taxa, a number of which have been described this century (Kottelat and Freyhof 2007, Snoj et al. 2011, Sanz 2018). Alternatively, their systematics have been viewed from a phylogenetic and phylogeographic perspective based largely on mitochondrial DNA (mtDNA) analyses, with all subpopulations treated as a single polymorphic taxon customarily referred to as the “Brown Trout (Salmo trutta) complex” (Sanz 2018, Whiteley et al. 2019, Segherloo et al. 2021).

The latter approach led to brown trout diversity being defined by ten mtDNA lineages or sublineages corresponding to extensive catchments (the Danube, Atlantic, Mediterranean and Adriatic basins), specific geographic areas (the Balkan Peninsula and North Africa), individual watersheds (the Dades, Duero and Tigris rivers) and a distinctive phenotype (Marble Trout). Subsequent studies revealed that the distribution of some of these mtDNA lineages extends beyond their defined boundaries, e.g., the Adriatic lineage occurs from the Iberian Peninsula to the Republic of Türkiye, and the Marble Trout lineage is present in areas where no marbled phenotype exists, such as Corsica, central Italy, Albania and Greece (Bernatchez et al. 1992, Apostolidis et al. 1997, Bernatchez 2001, Suárez et al. 2001, Cortey and García-Marín 2004, Sušnik et al. 2005, 2007, Splendiani et al. 2006, Martínez et al. 2007, Snoj et al. 2009, 2011, Tougard et al. 2018, Schöffmann et al. 2022).

However, several studies have revealed the presence of mosaic distributions of mtDNA haplogroups among wild brown trout populations, plus mitochondrial-nuclear phylogenetic discordance in reconstructions made with both mitochondrial and nuclear trees (Snoj et al. 2009, Pustovhr et al. 2014, Splendiani et al. 2020). This suggests the presence of incomplete lineage sorting or asymmetric introgressive hybridization, which are common phenomena in rapidly diverging lineages and indicate that mtDNA genealogies might be generally unsuitable for defining phylogenetic relationships between brown trout taxa (Pustovhr et al. 2011, 2014). In the case of brown trouts, naturally intricate patterns of diversification and secondary contact shaped by repeated glaciations during the Pleistocene have been additionally complicated by widespread anthropogenic translocation and introgressive hybridisation since the Middle Ages (Largiadèr and Scholl 1996, Sanz et al. 2006, Lerceteau-Köhle et al. 2013). The combined use of multiple nuclear (nDNA, e.g., microsatellites, nuclear genes) and mitochondrial markers has already provided better insight into this complex scenario, resulting in progress towards a deeper understanding of evolutionary relationships at particular geographic scales or among subsets of putative taxa (Snoj et al. 2002, 2010, 2011, Sušnik et al. 2006, 2007, Berrebi et al. 2013, 2019, Gratton et al. 2014, Marić et al. 2017).

An integrative taxonomic approach combining morphological and ecological data with next generation sequencing of nDNA to identify genomic clusters may represent the most promising option for resolving brown trout systematics (Guinand et al. 2021, Segherloo et al. 2021). However, no comprehensive morphological or nDNA analyses have yet been completed, and it is plausible that the elaborate genetic and phenotypic diversity demonstrated by these fishes may never be adequately captured by a single accepted taxonomic system (Whiteley et al. 2019).

Pending a definitive outcome to the above, the Red List broadly follows the nomenclature provided by Fricke et al. (2024).

The presence of two forms of charr distinguished by body size were reported from Bear Island by Klemetsen et al. (1985). The taxon Salvelinus salvelinoinsularis was subsequently associated with the smaller-growing form, and its diet was described as comprising "benthic invertebrates" (Kottelat and Freyhof 2007). A specimen deriving from Lake Ellasjøen was later designated as lectotype, and the larger-growing form was assumed to be conspecific with the congeneric Artic Charr (S. alpinus), which is widespread in northern Europe (Kottelat and Freyhof 2009).

However, recent research has suggested that three forms of charr occur on the island. These consist of a large, partially piscivorous littoral benthivore and a smaller pelagic planktivore which both inhabit a number of lakes, and a small profundal benthivore currently known only from Lake Ellasjøen (Hawley et al. 2016).

For the purposes of this assessment, the latter form is assumed to represent S. salvelinoinsularis, although in most published literature all charr inhabiting Bear Island are considered to represent a single polymorphic subpopulation of S. alpinus.

At the broader scale, there remains considerable uncertainty regarding the evolutionary relationships and taxonomy of the genus Salvelinus (Reist et al. 2013, Taylor 2016, Whiteley et al. 2019).

Members of this genus exhibit bewildering subpopulation-scale ecological and morphological variability throughout their global range. When such divergence occurs within a single lake system, the different sympatric forms are often referred to as “morphs”, “morphotypes”, "ecomorphs" or "ecotypes" (Snorrason et al. 1994, Adams et al. 1998, Knudsen et al. 2006, Klemetsen 2010, Muir et al. 2016).

Some of these subpopulations and sympatric forms have over time been described as nominal species, including at least 15 from North America, around 30 from Europe and 12 from Siberia and the Far East. However, these taxa encompass only a small fraction of charr distribution and diversity, and there exist significant differences in opinion regarding which of them should be considered valid (Savvaitova 1995, Adams and Maitland 2007, Kottelat and Freyhof 2007, Klemetsen 2010, Whiteley et al. 2019).

With the above in mind, there is an emerging consensus that the striking genetic and phenotypic diversity exhibited by members of this genus cannot be adequately represented by a single accepted taxonomic system (Whiteley et al. 2019).

The Red List currently follows the nomenclature provided by Fricke et al. (2024), albeit a species-oriented conservation management approach is unlikely to prove appropriate for members of this genus (Barthelemy et al. 2023; also see 'Conservation').

This taxon was split from the congeneric Corfu Valencia (Valencia letourneuxi), hence a number of studies and associated conservation actions published prior to its description refer to both species (Freyhof et al. 2014).

A form allied to Heliogomphus olivaceus is known from Borneo, see Dow & Stokvis (2018) but it is not clear if they are the same species or not at this time. For assessment purposes the Bornean form is left out of consideration but if they do eventually prove to be the same species then re-assessment will be necessary.

Diplacina guentherpetersi appears to differ from D. bolivari Selys mostly in details of colouration and wing venation and confirmation that it is really a distinct species rather than a variant of D. bolivari is needed.
Diplacina holgerhungeri appears to differ from D. bolivari Selys mostly in details of colouration and confirmation that it is really a distinct species rather than a variant of D. bolivari is needed.
Prodasineura poncei is very similar to P. palawana Lieftinck, 1948, differing only in having a reduced or absent antehumeral stripe, and might eventually prove to be a junior synonym of P. palawana.

The Roland Müller collection contains a number of specimens identified as allied to Risiocnemis elegans by M. Hämäläinen (pers. comm. 2019: unpub. database of specimens from collection R. Müller identified by M. Hämäläinen); the status of these specimens is unresolved. The view taken for assessment purposes is that only the type series and other specimens identified as elegans in published sources are definitely R. elegans and other specimens are left out of consideration. Hämäläinen (1991) noted that R. varians Hämäläinen, 1991 might eventually prove to be a junior synonym of R. elegans but Hämäläinen and Muller (1997: 279) noted that material collected in Quirino Province after the publication of Hämäläinen (1991) “seems to indicate that R. elegans and R. varians are different species.”
The species was described from the female sex only and although male specimens subsequently became available, no description of the male has been published to the assessor’s knowledge.

Hämäläinen (1991) noted that Risiocnemis varians might eventually prove to be a junior synonym of R. elegans but Hämäläinen and Muller (1997) noted that material collected in Quirino Province after the publication of Hämäläinen (1991) “seems to indicate that R. elegans and R. varians are different species.” This issue has not yet been satisfactorily resolved, but the two are treated as distinct for assessment purposes.

authority: Villanueva, Van Der Ploeg & Van Weerd, 2011
Villanueva, Van Der Ploeg & Van Weerd, 2011

taxonomicNotes:
According to the original pictures and description, this species appears to be somewhat similar to H. melchori with regard to the sculpture of the shell (Vega-Luz and Vega-Luz 2008). However, this species is described from a locality far away, although also on the island of Tenerife. Genetically, H. modesta belongs to a clade including also species of the H. plicaria group (H. plicaria, H. bethencourtiana) and the H. consobrina group (H. consobrina consobrina, H. consobrina nivariae, H. consobrina retrodens, H. melchori, H. fulgida, H. invernicata) but appears to be more closely related to H. plicaria and H. bethencourtiana than to the other taxa (M. Neiber unpublished data).

According to Bank et al. (2002), the name modesta appeared twice as a nomen nudum in A. Férussac in 1821; in both publications, Férussac referred to plate 42 fig. 1. This plate was actually published by Férussac in 1822, but without explanations on the plate or on the covers. However, the identity of fig. 2 can be inferred from the two 1821 publications. Bank et al. (2002) considered 1822 as the publication date of modesta, as the name became for the first time nomenclaturally available in the year the plate was published, and we follow that opinion here.
Molecular analyses indicate that this species might be a member of the genus Neogobius (Geiger et al. 2014).

The taxonomy of roaches inhabiting northern Italy, the Western Balkans and southern Greece has not been definitively resolved.

At the generic level, they were included in the genus Rutilus prior to the mid-2010s, before being transferred to the resurrected genus Leucos based on a series of molecular and morphological characters (Bianco and Ketmaier 2014).

However, a number of authors have opted against support of this split, and there is a further lack of consensus regarding the validity of nominal taxa inhabiting the Western Balkans. Subpopulations occurring in this region are either treated as distinct, locally-endemic Rutilus species, i.e., R. basak from the Neretva River system, R. albus from the Lake Skadar basin, R. ohridanus from Lake Ohrid and R. prepsensis from the Prespa Lakes basin (Marić 2010, 2019, Perea et al. 2010, Milošević et al. 2011, Tsoumani et al. 2014, Barbieri et al. 2015, Tutman et al. 2018, Ćaleta et al. 2015, 2019, Freyhof et al. 2020), or lumped together under Leucos basak (Bianco and Ketmaier 2014, Pietrock et al. 2022).

In addition, molecular analyses have demonstrated a close relationship between the Skadar, Ohrid and Prespa subpopulations, and that this subgroup is somewhat divergent from the Neretva River subpopulation (Geiger et al. 2014, Petrosino et al. 2022).
The taxonomic relationship of this species to Cryptotriton monzoni needs to be investigated (D. Wake and G. Parra-Olea pers. comm. 2007).
McCranie and Rovito (2014) synonymised Cryptotriton wakei with this species.

When populations of this species were first found east of Lake Kyoga in Uganda by Tait in 1969 and 1971, they were referred to as N. cf. taeniopygus, based on some affinities shown in male colouration. The locality of the population from Uganda is, however, far-removed from the known northern limits of N. taeniopygus in central Tanzania (Wildekamp 1990) but without more recent collections, the taxonomic position of the Ugandan population remained uncertain (Wildekamp, 1994, 2004). Finally, morphometric and molecular analyses confirmed it to be distinct and Nothobranchius taiti was established as a species by Nagy in 2019, based on material collected in 2017.
This species may belong to the genus Odonteleotris; work is in progress (D. Hoese, in prep.).
Considered by Daget (1998) and by Graf and Cummings (2019) as a distinct species and not a form of Coelatura aegyptiaca.

In the former IUCN assessment (Kristensen and Stengaard 2010) this species was referred to as Mutela ovata, based on the lumping taxonomic approach of Mandahl-Barth (1988).

But the genus Pleiodon is a distinct genus that occurs in Africa since the Later Cretaceous and presently only persists in Lake Tanganyika (P. spekii) and till the early part of the 20th Century in most of West Africa (P. ovatus). All West African populations of P. ovatus appear to have become extinct during the early 20th Century. A new population of Pleiodon ovatus was discovered in Sierra Leone in 1968. However, the shells of the Sierra Leone population differ from the West African ones by the absence of a swollen, protruding central umbo, the general shape and the colour (black instead of green). For the confirmation of the taxonomic identification, additional collecting and taxonomic investigation is needed.

Eschmeyer's Catalog of Fishes (Fricke, Eschmeyer and Van der Laan 2020) has this species valid as Glossolepis incisa but here we follow Gerry Allen (pers. comm. 2020) in accepting the spelling as Glossolepis incisus.

Eschmeyer's Catalog of Fishes (Fricke, Eschmeyer and Van der Laan 2020) has this species valid as Glossolepis maculosa but here we follow Gerry Allen (pers. comm. 2020) in accepting the spelling as Glossolepis maculosus.

Mature males of O. squamipinnis are easily distinguished from the other chambo, because they have pale heads. Usually the head is blue, but sometimes it can be green or white. The genital tassels may be exceptionally long in O. squamipinnis. One 17 cm (6.7 inch) SL male, which held a territory but did not spawn, was reported to have a pair of tassels as long as 9 cm or 3½ inches (Trewavas 1983: 477).
This species previously appeared on the IUCN Red List in the genus Pseudotropheus Regan, 1922 but is now valid in the genus Chindongo Li, Konings & Stauffer, 2016.
Previously classified in Protomelas due to lack of knowledge on its melanin pattern when it was described — only individuals in full blue colouration had been observed.
This species previously appeared on the IUCN Red List in the genus Melanochromis Trewavas, 1935 but is now valid in the genus Pseudotropheus Regan, 1922.

This species has a bold mid-lateral stripe on its body. Konings (1995) regards this species as belonging to the genus Nyassachromis due to its basic pigmentation pattern as well as its habit of constructing spawning cones, since all other species in the genus Copadichromis have spots or lack pattern on the flanks and are not known to build spawning cones on sand [except for Copadichromis likomae (Iles, 1960)]. This species previously appeared on the IUCN Red List in the genus Copadichromis but is now valid in the genus Nyassachromis (Konings 2016).
This species previously appeared on the IUCN Red List in the genus Copadichromis Eccles & Trewavas, 1989 but is now valid in the genus Mchenga Stauffer & Konings, 2006.
This species previously appeared on the IUCN Red List in the genus Melanochromis Trewavas, 1935 but is now valid in the genus Pseudotropheus Regan, 1922.

Vujić et al. (2008) provide figures of the male terminalia and the key distinguishing Pipiza luteibarba from other European members of the luteitarsis group. The luteitarsis group of species may be distinguished from other European Pipiza by the lack of apicoventral ridges on the hind femora. P. luteibarba can be separated from other European species of the genus using the key provided by Vujić et al. (2013).

Pipiza laurusi cannot be separated using traditional morphological characters, only with molecular data and using geometric morphometric evidence: wing size and shape (Vujić et al. 2013). Distribution can also be used to separate this species from Pipiza lugubris.
Pipiza carbonaria can be separated from other European species of the genus using the key provided by Vujić et al. (2013).

This recently-recognized European monotypic genus has been discovered and described recently (Vujić et al. 2019). The systematic position of the taxon is within the tribe Rhingiini. Katara connexa is shiny, black species with broad, oval abdomen, which is distinctly broader than thorax in both sexes. Eyes are separated in both sexes (Vujić et al. 2019).

Claussen et al. (1994) provided the key in which Heringia adpropinquans is distinguished from H. heringi (Zetterstedt, 1843) and figured the male terminalia. In the male of H. adpropinquans the surstylus is noticeably broader in the apical half of its length, whereas in H. heringi it is almost parallel-sided, from base almost to apex (Speight 2018).

A species of the subgenus Taeniocheilosia. For identification see the key to males of European Taeniocheilosia (as Nigrocheilosia) species in StN Keys volume; and Barkalov and Ståhls (1997) who figured the male terminalia. Cheilosia venosa belongs to the Cheilosia caerulescens-group for which Ståhls and Barkalov (2017) provided the key. This species is inadequately differentiated from C. caerulescens, C. herculana and C. kerteszi in the existing keys (Speight 2018).

Based on the presence of a precoxal bridge, Cheilosia varnensis belongs to the Cheilosia angustigenis-species group. Claussen (2000) provides features separating C. varnensis from other species in this group and figures the male terminalia.

A species of the subgenus Taeniochilosia. For identification see key to males of European Taeniochilosia (as Nigrocheilosia) species in StN Keys volume; and Barkalov and Ståhls (1997) who figured the male terminalia. Both sexes can be easily confused with C. sahlbergi Becker and females can easily be confused with C. pedestris Becker (Speight 2018). Claussen and van der Weyer (2004) provided an additional key for its identification.

A species of the subgenus Taeniocheilosia. For identification see the key to males of European Taeniocheilosia (as Nigrocheilosia) species in StN Keys volume; and Barkalov and Ståhls (1997) who figured the male terminalia.

Cheilosia pini belongs to the nominal subgenus. Vujić and Claussen (2000) redescribed the male and provide a description of the female, together with figures of the male terminalia and the key separating C. pini from other species of the pini group (Speight 2020).

A species of the subgenus Taeniocheilosia. For identification see the key to males of European Taeniocheilosia (as Nigrocheilosia) species in StN Keys volume; and Barkalov and Ståhls (1997) who figure the male terminalia. The female of this species is remarkably similar to the female of C. alpestris, from which it may most easily be distinguished by its hairy metasternum (the metasternum is bare in C. alpestris; Speight 2018), and by the shape of alula on the wing.
Cheilosia montana belongs to alpina species group of the subgenus Montanocheila. Features that distinguished this species from other members of this group were given by Claussen (1998).

Cheilosia lucense belongs to the subgenus Montanocheila. It is most similar to C. chrysocoma. Males can be distinguished by using features of terminalia. Females can be separated by angular facial tubercle and pollinose sternum 2. Ricarte et al. (2014) describe both the male and female, figure the male terminalia and show how C. lucense can be incorporated into the key to the European alpina group of species (Claussen 1998).
Cheilosia limbicornis belongs to nominal subgenus. The male is described by Torp Pedersen (1971). Diagnostic features are mentioned by Pérez-Bañón et al. (1996).
Cheilosia lenta belongs to the nominate subgenus. It is extremely difficult to separate C. lenta from C. rhynchops. The main difference is absence of pilosity at mid of katepisternum.

Claussen and Kassebeer (1993) provide the key for distinguishing Cheilosia latigenis from the closely similar species C. mutabilis, C. psilophthalma and C. urbana. C. latigenis belongs to the nominate subgenus.

A species of the subgenus Taeniocheilosia. For identification see the key to males of European Taeniocheilosia (as Nigrocheilosia) species in StN Keys volume; and Barkalov and Ståhls (1997) who figured the male terminalia. Cheilosia laeviventris belongs to the caerulescens species group of the subgenus Taeniocheilosia (former Nigrocheilosia) for which Ståhls and Barkalov (2017) provided the key.

Cheilosia laeviseta is a species of the subgenus Taeniocheilosia. For identification see the key to males of European Taeniocheilosia (as Nigrocheilosia) species in StN Keys volume; and Barkalov and Ståhls (1997) who figured the male terminalia.

Cheilosia kerteszi belongs to the caerulescens species group of the subgenus Taeniocheilosia (former Nigrocheilosia) for which Ståhls and Barkalov (2017) provide the key. It can be distinguished from the most similar species (Cheilosia circassica) by the presence of a few black bristles on the postalar callus.
Cheilosia katara belongs to nominal subgenus Cheilosia. An identification key distinguishing C. katara from other related species was provided by Vujić and Claussen (2000).

Cheilosia insignis is a member of subgenus Taeniocheilosia. An identification key is provided by Claussen and Van der Weyer (2004), Barkalov and Ståhls (1997) figure the male terminalia. Verlinden (2000) points out problems in the identification of C. insignis and provides features that help to identify C. insignis with more certainty.

Cheilosia ingerae is a member of the nominative subgenus. Nielsen and Claussen (2001) described Cheilosia ingerae from northern Norway, and figured the male genitalia. They also provided characters to separate C. ingerae from C. gigantea. This species is included in the keys to northern European Cheilosia species in Haarto and Kerppola (2007) and Bartsch (2009).

Cheilosia impudens belongs to the genus Taeniocheilosia. For identification see key to males of European Taeniocheilosia (as Nigrocheilosia) species in StN Keys volume and Barkalov and Ståhls (1997) who figure the male terminalia.

Cheilosia iberica belongs to Cheilosia s.str., and is a member of the proxima species-group. Marcos-Garcia and Claussen (1989) described both sexes of this species and figured the male terminalia and the head of the female.

Cheilosia hercyniae is a member of subgenus Taeniochilosia, and shares characters with members of the Cheilosia caerulescens species group. Ståhls and Barkalov (2017) provide a key for the separation of this species from others of the caerulescens group. The female of C. hercyniae is distinguished from related species by its possession of a very large, yellow-orange, third antennal segment, with, low down on its internal face, a very distinct, straight, slit-like antennal pit. An additional distinguishing feature of this species is that, in both sexes, the postero-dorsal triangular extension of mesanepisternite 1 carries long hairs (Speight 2018).

Cheilosia herculana belongs to subgenus Taeniochilosia, and the caerulescens species group. Barkalov and Ståhls (2017) provide a key for the separation of this species from others of the caerulescens group. This species closely resembles C. caerulescens (Mg.).
Cheilosia griseifacies can be distinguished by features in the key presented in Vujić (1994). It has previously been confused with Cheilosia canicularis, but there should be less confusion now.

Cheilosia gagatea belongs to subgenus Taeniochilosia. An identification key to males of European Nigrocheilosia (=Taeniochilosia) species is provided in the StN Keys volume and by  Barkalov and Ståhls (1997), the latter study also figure the male terminalia. The length of the aristal hairs is variable in this species (Speight 2020).

Cheilosia faucis is a member of the subgenus Taeniochilosia. An identification key to males of European Taeniochilosia species is provided in StN Keys volume, and the key bare-eyed, black-legged Cheilosia species with partly red/orange antennae by Claussen and Van de Weyer (2004). Barkalov and Ståhls (1997) figure the male terminalia.

Cheilosia crassiseta belongs to subgenus Taeniochilosia. A key to males of European Nigrocheilosia (= Taeniochilosia) species is provided in StN Keys volume. An additional key, for identification of the bare-eyed, black-legged Cheilosia species with partly red/orange antennae, is provided by Claussen and Van der Weyer (2004). Barkalov and Ståhls (1997) figured the male terminalia.

Cheilosia clausseni belongs to subgenus Taeniochilosia. Identification keys include key to males of European Nigrocheilosia (=Taeniochilosia) species in StN Keys volume, and Barkalov and Ståhls (1997) who also figure the male terminalia, and a key for identification of the bare-eyed, black-legged Cheilosia species with partly red/orange antennae by Claussen and Van der Weyer (2004).

The species belongs to Cheilosia s.str. The male of this species bears a superficial resemblance to C. nebulosa Verrall, while the female resembles C. psilophthalma, though its eyes are entirely bare and its claws are paler in colour (Speight 2020). Vujić and Claussen (2000) provide information for discerning C. clama from the similar species C. pini and C. alba.

Cheilosia beckeri is a member of subgenus Taeniochilosia. Strobl (1910) included a number of males in his description of Cheilosia beckeri, but whether they all belong to the same taxon is doubtful (Speight 2018). The female lectotype was redescribed by Barkalov and Ståhls (1997) and included in their key to females, and the male was tentatively included in the key to males but no male specimen was available for study, see also key to males of European Taeniochilosia (as Nigrocheilosia) species in StN Keys volume in Speight (2018). Barcoding of recently obtained specimens links male specimens with the females (Ståhls, in prep.), which will enable the accurate inclusion of the males in identification keys based on diagnostic characteristics.

Cheilosia barbafacies was described by Vujić et al. (2013). The species is a member of the nominal subgenus and the proxima group. Vujić et al. (2013) provided descriptions of both sexes of the species, figured the male terminalia and provided an identification key distinguishing C. barbafacies from closely related species.

Cheilosia balkana belongs to the proxima group of species of the nominal subgenus, and it  morphologically closely resembles the species Cheilosia pascuorum. For identification see the key for European species of the proxima group in Vujić et al. (2013), who figured the male terminalia.

This is one of several small, black Cheilosia species of the subgenus Taeniochilosia endemic to the Alps. The thickened antennal arista is readily observable and facilitates identification (also in the field), identification key is provided by Barkalov and Ståhls (1997).

The species belongs to the nominal subgenus Cheilosia s. str. Torp Pedersen (1971) described the male of this species, and Marcos-Garcia (1987) described the female. This species is apparently similar to C. rhynchops (C. Claussen pers. comm. 2020).

Identification keys include Barkalov and Ståhls (1997; including figures of the male terminalia) and the key to males of European Taeniochilosia (as Nigrocheilosia) species in the StN Keys volume (Speight 2018). In general appearance this species is similar to C. derasa Loew and the female to the female of C. pedemontana Rondani, separating characteristics provided in Barkalov and Ståhls (1997).

Cheiloisa alba is a member of the nominative subgenus. The species is similar in general appearance to Cheilosia clama, C. flavipes (Panzer, 1798), C. nebulosa Verrall, 1871 and C. uviformis Becker, 1894. Vujić and Claussen (2000) provide details for their separation.

The year of publication of this species is given as 2018 because even though the describing paper was published online in 2017 (Radenković et al. 2017), the online publication and the species name were not registered with ZooBank, and as a result, the year of publication (2018) of the paper version of the paper is taken.

Merodon naxius belongs to Merodon aureus species group and Merodon bessarabicus subgroup and M. luteomaculatus species complex of six cryptic species distinguishable from one another using combinations of molecular analysis, geometric morphometrics and distributional characteristics given by Radenković et al. (2017).

Until recently (e.g. Peck 1988), Claussenia hispanica has been regarded as a synonym of Heringia heringi (Zetterstedt). It may be found in flight with H. heringi and the two species are virtually indistinguishable in the field, though males of C. hispanica are usually slightly larger than those of H. heringi (Speight 2018). A key distinguishing these two species is provided by Speight and Sarthou (2017). One of the features used to characterise C. hispanica is the presence of hairs at the ventral extremity of the median lobe of the lunule. These can be absent in the female (Speight 2018). Claussen et al. (1994) figured the male terminalia.

Cheilosia vujici belongs to the nominal subgenus. It is very similar in general appearance to the smallest specimens of C. psilophthalma and C. latigenis. Main diagnostic features are presented in Claussen and Doczkal (1998).

Barkalov and Stahls (1997) figure the male terminalia. An additional key, for identification of the bare-eyed, black-legged Cheilosia species with partly red/orange antennae, is providedby Claussen and van der Weyer (2004). C. clausseni is easily mistaken for C. pilifer (Speight 2020).

Cheilosia pedestris belongs to the subgenus Taeniocheilosia. It was described by Becker (1894), based on a solitary female. Claussen and van der Weyer (2004) described C. romigi from Italy. The synonymy of C. romigi with C. pedestris was established by Claussen and Speight (2007) (Speight 2018).

A species of the subgenus Taeniocheilosia. For identification see the key to males of European Taeniocheilosia (as Nigrocheilosia) species in StN Keys volume; and Barkalov and Ståhls (1997) who figure the male terminalia. Genitalia have complicated structures which can make identification more difficult than for other species of the subgenus.

Two different taxa are clearly encompassed by the names Cheilosia morio and C. scanica, but correct application of these names and consequently relative range limits remain to be established (Speight 2020). The identities of Cheilosia morio (Zetterstedt 1838) (Syrphidae, tribe Rhingiini) and of its synonyms are reviewed by Doczkal and Claußen (in prep.). This work referred Cheilosia scanica Ringdahl, 1937 to the synonymy of Cheilosia morio (Zetterstedt, 1838) and determined that the name C. morio had been widely misapplied to a distinct species, for which these authors resurrected the name Cheilosia luteicornis (Zetterstedt, 1838). This nomenclature was used in a few recent publications (Nilsson et al. 2012, van Steenis 2016).

Cheilosia morio belongs to the subgenus Neocheilosia. From the most similar species it can be distinguished by its smaller size, presence of black pile around facial knob and black pile on terga. In Bartsch et al. (2009) this species is mentioned as Cheilosia morio B.

A species of the subgenus Taeniocheilosia. For identification see the key to males of European Taeniocheilosia (as Nigrocheilosia) species in StN Keys volume; and Barkalov and Ståhls (1997) who figured the male terminalia. Not an easy species to identify, closely related to other species but the genitalia are distinct.

This species was previously referred to as Eumerus minotaurus until the discovery of three cryptic species within the Emerus minotaurus complex: Eumerus minotaurus, Eumerus karyates and Eumerus phaeacus (Chroni et al. 2018).

The identity of Callicera rohdendorfi Zimina, 1982 with respect to C. macquarti requires confirmation. Zimina (1986) described details of Callicera rohdendorfi which led to the assumption that it should be C. macquarti. Callicera rohdendorfi was considered to be "almost certainly the same" as C. macquarti by Speight (1991), however this requires confirmation based on review of the Zimina holotype and/or paratype specimens of Callicera rohdendorfi. If C. rohdendorfi is a synonym of C. macquarti, then the range of C. macquarti extends to Crimea and the Caucasus, probably to Azerbaijan.

Callicera macquarti is probably synonymous with C. fagesii according to recent results from DNA analysis, but Callicera macquarti is maintained as a valid species until its identity is formally revised.
A recent record (Mutin and Barkalov 2018) of this species from Omsk (Siberia) refer to another species, with genetic confirmation (A. Grković pers. comm. 2021).

This species was previously referred to as Eumerus minotaurus until the discovery of three cryptic species within the Emerus minotaurus complex: Eumerus minotaurus, Eumerus karyates and Eumerus phaeacus (Chroni et al. 2018).

Cheilosia thessala belongs to the longula species group closely resembling the species Cheilosia scutellata. Claussen and Ståhls (2007) provided the key to separate this species from others in the C. scutellata group and also figured the male terminalia (Speight 2018).
The species is morphologically similar to Palumbia eristaloides (Portschinsky, 1887), and further research is needed to determine whether they may be the same taxon.

authority: J. van Steenis and W. van Steenis, 2014

taxonomicNotes: This species has been reported from outside Corsica (the French mainland, Sardinia and from Italy) by other authors (Burgio et al. 2015, Speight et al. 2018), but Eupeodes vandergooti is difficult to distinguish from related species, such as E. lucasi, and has not reliably been confirmed from outside Corsica. The species is considered here to be endemic to Corsica pending further work.

There is uncertainty as to which genus Epistrophella coronata should be referred to because the only other species of this genus - E. euchroma - is consigned by some authors to the genus Meligramma (Speight 2020).

Smit et al. (2004) supports the validity of X. babyssa and placed X. parahyalinatus as a synonym of X. babyssa, following Wakeham-Dawson et al. (2004).

However, there are some doubts about the validity of X. babyssa (Speight 2020), which appears in recent literature as Xanthandrus parahyalinatus. The fact that X. babyssa is found only on Madeira island - from which other endemic hoverflies are known - provides sufficient justification to consider it valid until new studies integrating morphology and molecular biology are undertaken.

authority: Conradie, Bittencourt-Silva, Farooq, Loader, Menegon & Tolley, 2018
taxonomicNotes: No notable taxonomical issues.

authority: Conradie, Bittencourt-Silva, Farooq, Loader, Menegon & Tolley, 2018
taxonomicNotes: First recorded from Mount Ribáuè by Blake (1965) and Poynton (1996). This is a split from the broader concept of Nothophryne broadleyi (Conradie et al. 2018). No notable taxonomical issues.
This species was previously confused with Cnemaspis kumarasinghei. It was found to be genetically distinct by Agarwal et al. (2017) and formally described by Batuwita et al. (2019).

authority: Karunarathna, Bauer, de Silva, Surasinghe, Somaratna, Madawala, Gabadage, Botejue, Henkanaththegedara & Ukuwela, 2019

taxonomicNotes: The apparently isolated record of this species from Khammam, Telengana needs taxonomic validation (C. Srinivasulu and P. Mohapatra pers. comm. 2019).
This is a split from the broader concept of Scutiger nyingchiensis (Jiang et al. 2016).
This species was previously considered a subpopulation of Hynobius nebulosus prior to its description by Matsui et al. (2019).
This species was previously considered a subpopulation of Hynobius nebulosus prior to its description by Matsui et al. (2019).
This species was previously considered a subpopulation of Hynobius nebulosus prior to its description by Matsui et al. (2019).
This species was previously considered a subpopulation of Hynobius nebulosus prior to its description by Matsui et al. (2019).

This species was previously confused as a subpopulation of Hynobius dunni, but was recently elevated to species status by Sugawara et al. (2018). Hynobius dunni is now considered to be endemic to Kyushu and H. tosashimizuensis as endemic to Shikoku.

Tominaga and Matsui (2007) elevated the subspecies Hynobius naevius yatsui to full species status. Matsui et al. (2017) showed that the holotype of H. stejnegeri, long applied to another species (now H. ikioi), was the senior synonym for H. yatsui.

Tominaga et al. (2019) revised the Hynobius naevius group and described the subpopulation on Honshu as the new species Hynobius sematonotos, and the subpopulation in north-eastern Kyushu as the new species Hynobius oyamai. Thus, Hynobius naevius is now confined to the population in north-western Kyushu.

Rhampholeon tilburyi is found in the lower elevation forests on Mt Namuli. Phylogenetic analysis confirms that R. tilburyi is a separate taxon to an undescribed species (R. sp. nov. 'namuli2') that is found in the high elevation forests (K.A. Tolley unpub. data 2019). The holotype of R. tilburyi was collected from Ukalini Forest on Mt Namuli, but it was not included in the phyologenetic analysis that lead to the species description (Branch et al. 2014). The only material included was from the Cha Madal tea estate and these samples form a monophyletic clade with those from Ukalini Forest. Samples of Rhampholeon collected from Manho Forest, which is higher in elevation, are in a different clade (K.A. Tolley unpub. data 2019). Recent records from Mt Ribáuè, Mt Mepalué and Mt Socone were confirmed as Rhampholeon tilburyi using a phylogenetic analysis (K.A. Tolley unpub. data).
Populations from mainland Africa assigned to this species are now considered to be Leptopelis rufus.

Tachypleus tridentatus and T. gigas are sometimes the subject of misidentification. In China, three horseshoe crab nature reserves were established in Guangdong Province in 2004 for the protection of T. gigas (Kwan et al. 2016), even though T. gigas has not been recorded in these areas (Liao and Li 2001, Liao et al. 2001a).

In Hong Kong, the occurrence of T. gigas, T. tridentatus and C. rotundicauda was reported (Mikkelsen 1988), but recent population studies did not reveal any presence of T. gigas in Hong Kong waters (Li 2008, Shin et al. 2009, Morton and Lee 2010) and a recent literature review suggests previous reports of the presence of T. gigas in Hong Kong was a case of mistaken identity, based on the wrong labelling of photographs in a popular natural history book at the time (Laurie 2011, unpublished).

On 23 May 2012, five horseshoe crabs were seized at Sam Ratulangi International Airport, Manado, North Sulawesi, Indonesia under PP No. 7/1999 (Indonesia Government Regulation No. 7/1999). Although described as T. gigas, photographs indicate the seized animals were T. tridentatus (BKIPM 2012).

It is distinguished from all congeners (P. kiyawensis, P. gambiensis and P. seymouri) by the following unique combination of characters: males with about 20-30 spots on flank, each having a smaller diameter than the radius of the pupil, spots on proximal portion of anal fin, pectoral fin hyaline, pelvic fin reaching anal fin, dorsal fin tip reaching centre of caudal peduncle, females without spots; dorsal fin with 14-15 rays and 30-31 scales in median lateral series (Valdesalici 2013).

Former populations of this species from Sabah and Sarawak were allocated to different species (Khairuloconcha lunbawangorum Zieritz, Rahim, Taha & Pfeiffer, 2021 and Khairuloconcha sahanae Zieritz, Jainih & Lopes-Lima, 2021; Zieritz et al. 2021) and this species is now restricted to Kalimantan, Indonesia.

Analysis of DNA sequence data of recently sampled populations from the Limbang River basin in eastern Sarawak and the Kinabatangan River basin (at Gomantong) in eastern Sabah have shown that these belong to a different genus, Khairuloconcha (Zieritz et al. 2020, 2021). Thus, historical “C. borneensis” records by N.S. Haile collected in the 1950/60s (exact date not known) from Gomantong, Sabah, are misidentified Khairuloconcha sahanae.

Khairuloconcha is morphologically similar to its sister clade Ctenodesma but tends to have a sharper posterior ridge with nodulous sculpturing especially dorsally (vs. rounded and smooth posterior ridge in Ctenodesma) and has faint zigzag or irregular sculpture on the posterior slope (vs. uniform plications in Ctenodesma) (Zieritz et al. 2021). Three museum lots also exist from the Malaysian state of “Sarawak” (MNHN_MP_2723, MCZ_174117, BMNH_MP_D022). However, populations collected and sequenced from this state to date were revealed to belong to either Khairuloconcha (i.e. K. lunbawangorum from the Limbang River basin; Zieritz et al. 2021) or a new, yet undescribed Ctenodesma species (i.e. from the upper Sarawak River basin in western Sarawak; Zieritz et al. 2024), respectively.

Sudasinghe et al. (2018) reviewed the genus Labeo and confirmed, both morphologically and phylogenetically, that Labeo fisheri is a true species. Anusha et al. (2017) recorded a specimen of L. fisheri from the upstream regions of the Tamiraparani River in the southern Peninsular India. However, the meristic counts reported for this specimen differ from those of Sri Lankan L. fisheri as follows: 42 (vs. 37–39) lateral-line scales; and 21 (vs. 17–20) circumpeduncular scales. Therefore, the record of L. fisheri from India by Anusha et al. (2017) can be considered as a misidentification (Sudasinghe et al. 2018).

Recent molecular investigations in the genus Melanoides have shown that marked morphological differences are no evidence for genetic differentiation. It is advisable that the species status of M. voltae is confirmed by molecular research.

This is a poorly characterized species, known only from the original type material, described by Frierson (1913) from the Lobo (or Lobé) and Nyang (or Nyong) rivers in Cameroon. Haas (1936) created the new genus Afroparreysia for it. Recent studies (Whelan et al., 2011) indicate that the genus Coelatura is paraphyletic and that a revision, correcting the erroneous former lumping, is needed. It is possible that the genus name Afroparreysia will be reinstituted.

Brown (1994) states that 'from its size and shape, this shell could be taken for a Bellamya, but from the description of the surface as ‘granular’ it might also be a Potadoma. Operculum and anatomy unknown'.  The original description reads: 'the whole shell is covered with very small granules, which are arranged spirally'. This characteristic is absent in Bellamya species but very common in the genus Potadoma. In addition the genus Bellamya was absent from the part of West and Central Africa in the region below 10° latitude North  and 3° latitude South (Brown, 1994: fig. 69). 

The putative presence of this genus in Liberia is hence a zoogeographic anomaly.

The granulate ornamentation and the location are arguments in favour of placing this species in the genus Potadoma.

Bertola et al. (2018) reported a population related to Phyllurus gulbaru from The Pinnacles, while recognising that this represented an evolutionarily significant unit whose taxonomic status was in need of further investigation. Subsequent morphological and molecular research confirmed that this population should be regarded as a distinct species despite moderately low mtDNA differentiation Hoskin et al. (2019), and these authors accordingly described it as the new species Phyllurus pinnaclensis.

Enteromius foutensis was thought to be endemic to several river systems in the Fouta Djallon highlands in the Upper Guinean Forest. The Clown Wedgefish is a recently described small species of wedgefish (Last et al. 2016). It is a distinct species which is not likely to be confused with other Rhynchobatus species.

Praschag et al. (2007, 2008) demonstrated that the widespread species Batagur baska comprised at least two genetically distinct species, and separated out B. affinis for the southern species occupying southern Thailand, Peninsular Malaysia, and Sumatra in Indonesia, retaining the name B. baska for the more northern species from India, Bangladesh, and Myanmar. Praschag et al. (2009) further assessed the genetics and taxonomic status of a Cambodian relict subpopulation and eastern Peninsular Malaysian subpopulations and described them as the new subspecies B. affinis edwardmolli, with B. a. affinis occurring in western Peninsular Malaysia and Sumatra. Moll et al. (2015) reviewed the range-wide conservation biology and conservation status of the species.

Two subspecies are recognized, Chitra chitra chitra Nutphand, 1986 occurring in western Thailand and presumably Peninsular Malaysia, and Chitra chitra javanensis McCord & Pritchard, 2003 recorded from eastern Java and possibly extending to Sumatra.

The Gau Banded Iguana, Brachylophus gau, was newly described in 2017 (Fisher et al. 2017) as being morphologically distinct from the Lau Banded Iguana (Brachylophus fasciatus) and from the Central Fijian Banded Iguana (Brachylophus bulabula).
