Lepidochrysops littoralis is quite a variable species with populations at the limits of its range showing differences, e.g. Mossel Bay (Edge 2005). A future investigation may show some taxonomically significant infraspecific variation.

Modern taxonomic arrangements of the colobus monkeys either distinguish the red colobus monkeys and the Olive Colobus as separate genera, Piliocolobus and Procolobus, respectively (e.g., Groves 2001, 2005, 2007; Roos et al. 2011, Kingdon 2015), or consider them to belong to one genus Procolobus, with two subgenera (Procolobus for the Olive Colobus and Piliocolobus for the red colobus) (Oates et al. 1994, Grubb et al. 2003, 2013; Oates 2011). Ting (2008) placed all the red colobus as subspecies of Procolobus (Piliocolobus) badius. The 2008 IUCN Red List assessments followed the taxonomy of Grubb et al. (2003).

The arrangement of two genera in Groves (2001, 2005, 2007) is followed here: a monotypic Procolobus de Rochebrune, 1887 (Olive Colobus) and polytypic Piliocolobus de Rochebrune, 1887 (red colobus).

Although considered a subspecies of badius by Rahm (1970) and of pennantii by Delson et al. (1982), all recent taxonomic appraisals have treated the Zanzibar Red Colobus as a monotypic species (Dandelot 1971, Napier 1985, Oates 1986, Kingdon 1997, 2015; Groves 2001, 2005, 2007; Grubb et al. 2013), Groves and Ting (2013), and Siex and Struhsaker (2013).
The species has similarities with Discula tetrica (Lowe, 1852), with which it can be mistaken (Teixeira et al. 2019).
It is likely that this form consists of two separate species (G. Parra-Olea and D. Wake pers. comm.).
Listed as a separate species from Colobus polykomos following Oates and Trocco (1983) and Groves (2005).
This species was previously included in the genus Hyla but has been moved to the new genus Charadrahyla (Faivovich et al. 2005).
This species was previously included in the genus Pternohyla which was synonymized with Smilisca (Faivovich et al. 2005).

The taxonomy of Lamiopsis has recently been resolved with White et al. (2010) resurrecting and redescribing Lamiopsis tephrodes from South-east Asia, and Akhilesh et al. (2016) redescribing Lamiopsis temminckii from the Arabian Sea.
This is a split from the broader concept of Sarcohyla thorectes (Grünwald et al. 2019).
This species was previously considered a subspecies of the Pacific Bluefin Tuna (Thunnus orientalis) or Atlantic Bluefin Tuna (Thunnus thynnus).

A single subadult specimen (FMNH 64646) from the northeastern slopes of the Cordillera de Montecillos, Depto. Comayagua, was tentatively assigned to Bolitoglossa celaque sensu lato by McCranie and Wilson (2002), and, based on the presumed provenance of the specimen (ca 25 km NNE of the nearest locality for B. copinhorum), this specimen is tentatively referred to as B. copinhorum (Itgen et al. 2019).

The genus Oryctolagus appeared in the fossil record (Middle Pliocene) before any other modern leporid genus. Oryctolgus cuniculus was widespread across much of the Mediterranean area and central Europe in the Late Pleistocene, but following the maximum glacial period and Early Holocene it became confined to its current range in the Iberian Peninsula and adjoining areas in France (López-Martínez 2008).

Two subspecies are recognized: O. c. cuniculus and O. c. algirus. The latter is only present in the Iberian Peninsula and some islands, while the former occurs in most of the species’ introduced range. Based on genetic evidence they have apparently evolved independently beginning approximately 2 mya during the Quaternary glaciations (Ferrand 2008). The independence of these two subspecies has been reinforced by data on parasitology, behaviour, reproduction, and morphology (e.g. Gonçalves et al. 2002, Ferreira et al. 2015). Due to their genetic incompatibilities and reproductive isolation in their zone of contact in the middle of the Iberian Peninsula (e.g. Carneiro et al. 2010), some feel that they should be considered separate species (Delibes-Mateos et al. 2018). The domesticated rabbit originates from O. c. cuniculus.
Craterocephalus laisapi resembles but is distinct and different from C. centralis and C. cuneiceps from central and western Australia (Larson et al. 2005).

authority: Shekelle, Groves, Maryanto, Mittermeier, Salim & Springer, 2019

taxonomicNotes:
Modern taxonomic arrangements of the colobus monkeys either distinguish the red colobus monkeys and the Olive Colobus as separate genera, Piliocolobus and Procolobus, respectively (e.g., Groves 2001, 2005, 2007; Roos et al. 2011; Kingdon 2015), or consider them to belong to one genus Procolobus, with two subgenera (Procolobus for the Olive Colobus and Piliocolobus for the red colobus) (Oates et al. 1994, Grubb et al. 2003, 2013; Oates 2011). Ting (2008) placed all the red colobus as subspecies of Procolobus (Piliocolobus) badius. The 2008 IUCN Red List assessments followed the taxonomy of Grubb et al. (2003).

The arrangement of two genera in Groves (2001, 2005, 2007) is followed here: a monotypic Procolobus de Rochebrune, 1887 (Olive Colobus) and polytypic Piliocolobus de Rochebrune, 1887 (red colobus).

Kingdon (1997, 2015), following Colyn (1991), treated oustaleti as the Central African Red Colobus, with parmentieri, foai, langi, lulindicus, ellioti, semlikiensis and tephrosceles as subspecies. Grubb et al. (2003) considered tephrosceles be a subspecies but did not assign it to a species. Considering vocalizations and pelage colour patterns, Grubb et al. (2013) placed tephrosceles as a subspecies of rufomitratus, along with ellioti, foai, langi, lulindicus, parmentieri, oustaleti and tholloni.

Groves (2007) treated tephrosceles as a monotypic species, with gudoviusi Matschie as a junior synonym. For the purpose of this assessment, we follow Groves (2007) in treating tephrosceles as a monotypic species.
Acrocephalus rimitarae (del Hoyo and Collar 2016) was previously listed as A. rimatarae. See A. vaughani. Monotypic.
This species was previously included in the genus Hyla but has been moved to the genus Bokermannohyla (Faivovich et al. 2005).

This species was previously within the genus Hyla, then was moved to the resurrected genus Hypsiboas (Faivovich et al. 2005) and subsequently to the genus Boana (Dubois 2017). The taxonomy of this species remains confused and its identity is unclear (S.P. Carvalho-e-Silva pers. comm. 2004, Brazilian Red List Assessment Workshop 2021). Specimens from the state of Espirito Santo have been reassigned to Hypsiboas pombali by Weber (2009).
This is a restricted concept of Thoropa lutzi following the split of T. bryomantis by de Assis et al. (2021).

This species has taxonomic problems. It was previously confused with Melanophryniscus tumifrons prior to its description by Caramaschi and Cruz (2002). Subpopulations from Misiones, Argentina, formerly associated with this species (and included in its 2004 assessment) were believed by Baldo and Basso (2004) to pertain to Melanophryniscus aff. tumifrons and were excluded from the 2010 assessment. However, a study not yet submitted found strong evidence that this species needs to be synonymised with M. tumifrons (P. Colombo pers. comm. 2021).
This species was previously within the genus Hyla but has been moved to the resurrected genus Dendropsophus (Faivovich et al. 2005).
This species was previously within the genus Hyla but has since been moved to the genus Aplastodiscus (Faivovich et al. 2005).
This species was previously within the genus Hyla but has recently been moved to the resurrected genus Dendropsophus (Faivovich et al. 2005).
This species was resurrected from the synonymy of Phrynomedusa fimbriata by Cruz (1985).
This is a reduced concept of Phrynomedusa marginata as many individuals previously assigned to this species have now been assigned to the newly described species P. dryade (Baêta et al. 2016).
This species belongs to the Scinax ruber group of Pombal et al. (1995).

In the Scinax catharinae group of Faivovich et al. (2005). Many species in this group - including this one - require further taxonomic work to clarify the identity of certain records and the boundaries of their distributions.
This species is similar to Sphaenorhynchus orophilus, according to the original description.

Records from the Serra da Jibóia, municipality of Amargosa, Bahia State over 500 km to the southwest have been proposed as belonging to this species (V.G. Dill Orrico pers. comm. 2021). However, there are questions about the validity of these records owing to the distance from the type locality. They are excluded from this concept pending confirmation of their identity.

authority: Segalla, Berneck, Canedo, Caramaschi, Cruz, Garcia, Grant, Haddad, Lourenço, Mângia, Mott, Nascimento, Toledo, Werneck & Langone, 2021
taxonomicNotes: This concept previously bore the name Cycloramphus carvalhoi (Heyer 1983). Following the accidental confusion between Zachaenus carvalhoi and Cycloramphus carvalhoi due to changes made by Dubois et al. (2021), C. carvalhoi has been renamed as C. heyeri (Segalla et al. 2021).

Sampling efforts and the use of integrative taxonomy tools are needed to resolve the taxonomic status of this species. Records from Paraná and São Paulo States could be another species instead of Cycloramphus izecksohni (Brazil Red List Assessment 2021).
This species was removed from the synonymy of Cycloramphus asper by Heyer (1983).

The 2004 assessment reported that this concept "might be a complex of more than one species." Since that time, the 2004 distribution, which extended from the state of Rio de Janeiro to the northeastern corner of the state of Santa Catarina has been revised and records reassigned to species in the Hylodes nasus and H. lateristrigatus groups. For example, inland Rio de Janeiro state records belong to H. lateristrigatus (de Sá et al. 2015); other coastal records from Rio de Janeiro and northeastern São Paulo states belong to H. asper (Canedo and Pombal-Jr. 2007); southeastern São Paulo and eastern Paraná states belong to H. cardosoi (Lingnau et al. 2008); records from Santa Catarina state belong to H. perplicatus (Lingnau et al. 2008).
This species was previously within the genus Hyla, then was moved to the resurrected genus Hypsiboas (Faivovich et al. 2005) and subsequently to the genus Boana (Dubois 2017).
This species was described by reallocation of some specimens originally allocated to Phyllodytes tuberculosus (Caramaschi and Peixoto 2004).
Brachycephalus alipioi can be distinguished from other similar species by a combination of morphological characters (Pombal and Gasparini 2006).

A member of the Physalaemus signifer group according to the original publication. It can be differentiated from similar species by a combination of morphological characters and advertisement call features (Weber et al. 2006).
In the Scinax perpusillus group. Scinax peixotoi is diagnosed by a combination of morphological characters and distinct advertisement call (Brasileiro et al. 2007).
In the Chiasmocleis leucosticta group according to the original publication. Chiasmocleis sapiranga is diagnosed by a combination of morphological characters and colour patterns (Cruz et al. 2007).

In the Crossodactylus gaudichaudii group according to the original description. Crossodactylus cyclospinus is differentiated from other similar species by a combination of morphological and advertisement call features (Nascimento et al. 2005).

authority: Boubli, Byrne, da Silva, Silva-Júnior, Costa Araújo, Bertuol, Gonçalves, de Melo, Rylands, Mittermeier, Silva, Nash, Canale, Alencar, Rossi, Carneiro, Sampaio, Farias, Schneider, & Hrbek, 2019
Suwannapoom, Sumontha, Tunprasert, Ruangsuwan, Pawangkhanant, Korost & Poyarkov, 2018

taxonomicNotes: This species can be distinguished from similar species by a combination of morphometrics, body colouration, advertisement call features and habitat association (Rios-López and Thomas 2007).

authority: Díaz, Incháustegui, Marte, Köhler, Cádiz & Rodríguez, 2018
taxonomicNotes: It is suspected that this taxon represents a species complex (Hispaniola Red List Assessment Workshop 2020).
Two of the subspecies of Peltophryne longinasus (dunni and ramsdeni) have been elevated to species status (Hedges et al. 2019).
Two of the subspecies of Peltophryne longinasus (dunni and ramsdeni) have been elevated to species status (Hedges et al. 2019).

Haludaria pradhani was described as Puntius melanampyx ssp. pradhani from Molem, Goa (Tilak 1973). It was synonymized to Puntius fasciatus by Talwar and Jhingran (1991) and to Puntius melanampyx by Menon (1999). Pethiyagoda et al. (2012) revalidated this species as Dravidia pradhani and subsequently as Haludaria pradhani because of replacement of generic name (Pethiyagoda 2013).

authority: Pham, Sung, Pham, Le, Ziegler & Nguyen, 2019

taxonomicNotes: Anisogomphus solitaris and Heliogomphus ceylonicus have both been assessed before 2010 as Critically Endangered (Bedjanič 2006, 2009). According to Bedjanič and van der Poorten (2013) they both fall under synonymy of Anisogomphus ceylonicus (Hagen in Selys, 1878).

Van Tol and Günther (2018) excluded specimens from two locations from the type series of Rhinocypha flavipoda. One of these locations (“South Tomoni”) is not far from the locations where the type series was collected and was excluded on the basis of a small difference in colouration. A single specimen labelled from the Takala Mountains in South Sulawesi was also excluded, van Tol and Günther (2018) do not include this area in their summary of the distribution of this species and, although not explicitly stated, appear to regard the given location as mislabelling. Specimens excluded from the type series are treated as presence uncertain for assessment purposes and in particular, given their remoteness from the other known locations, the Takala Mountains do seem to be an unlikely location for this species.

Although van Tol & Günther (2018) did not exclude specimens from the Takala Mountains in South Sulawesi from the type series of Rhinocypha virgulata (in marked contrast to their exclusion of a single specimen of R. flavipoda van Tol & Günther, 2018, from the same location from the type series of that species, which has a similar distribution) it is clear from their other comments (e.g. “Probably restricted to streams running into or from Lake Poso or the Malili lakes”) that they consider the Takala Mountains location highly unlikely. Here we consider the Takala Mountains location as presence uncertain (but very unlikely) for this species, probably it is the result of mislabeling.

authority: Kompier & Yu in Ning, Kompier, Yu & Bu, 2016

taxonomicNotes: We follow Alonso et al. (2023) in considering Garcialebias a valid genus, rather than a synonym of Austrolebias.
We follow Alonso et al. (2023) in considering Matilebias a valid genus, rather than a synonym of Austrolebias.
We follow Alonso et al. (2023) in considering Garcialebias a valid genus, rather than a synonym of Austrolebias.
This species was previously within the genus Hyla, then was moved to the resurrected genus Hypsiboas (Faivovich et al. 2005) and subsequently to the genus Boana (Dubois 2017).
This species was previously within the genus Hyla, then was moved to the resurrected genus Hypsiboas (Faivovich et al. 2005) and subsequently to the genus Boana (Dubois 2017).
This species was previously within the genus Hyla but has since been moved to the genus Bokermannohyla (Faivovich et al. 2005).

In the Scinax ruber clade according to the original publication. Scinax cabralensis is differentiated from other similar species by a combination of morphological characters and a distinct advertisement call (Drummond et al. 2007).
No taxonomic issues.
This species previously appeared on the IUCN Red List with the incorrect spelling of regani.
Hypselobarbus thomassi was described by Day (1874) from South Canara (Dakshin Kannada), Karnataka State, India.
Etroplus canarensis was described by Day (1865) from South Canara (currently Karnataka, India).
Pseudeutropius mitchelli was described by Gunther (1864) from the erstwhile Madras Presidency, India.
This species is very closely related to Lampetra ninae and both might be conspecific.
Aplocheilus dayi was described as Haplochilus dayi by Steindachner in 1892.
Similar to Mystus nanus; identified as new species by Pethiyagoda et al. (2008).

Devario malabaricus (Jerdon, 1849) has been misapplied to this species. Devario annnataliae, as described by Batuwita et al. in 2017, consider this species as "species inquirendum" as the characters used to distinguish this species are self-contradictory, making it not possible to be distinguished from its congeners (Sudasinghe and Pethiyagoda 2019).

Batuwita et al. (2017) has reevaluated the species but it has been noted that the diagnosis provided for this species is ambiguous and self-contradictory by Sudasinghe and Pethiyagoda, thus, questioning the validity of the species (Sudasinghe and Pethiyagoda 2019).

Devario malabaricus was misapplied to this species and it was reevaluated to valid species by Batuwita et al. (2017). However, Sudasinghe and Pethiyagoda (2019) have questioned the validity of this species on the basis that the criteria used to distinguish this species is unusable as it is ambiguous and self contradictory.

Danio pathirana was described from southern Sri Lanka by Kottelat and Pethiyagoda in 1990 and later was placed in the genus Devario by Feng in 2001 (Batuwita et al. 2017). This species is a barred Devario, whose distinctive color pattern immediately distinguishes it from the striped Sri Lankan Devario fishes.

Originally described as subspecies of Garra ceylonensis by Deraniyagala (1933), who noted that the 76 specimens collected from stream from Mousakele estate, Gammaduwa (3,500 ft) were markedly different from specimens collected elsewhere. Later it was evaluated to species level by Pethiyagoda (1991).

This species was originally described as a subspecies of Labeo porcellus by Deraniyagala (1952). Correspondence from R. Pethiyagoda for the 1996 Red List indicates that lankae should be maintained as a distinct species endemic to Sri Lanka whereas porcellus is confined to northwest India, south of Bombay. In 2018, Sudasinghe et al. reviewed the genus and phylogenetically proved the species status.
Formerly of the genus Laubuca, now of the genus Laubuka. Laubuca was believed to be a misspelling of the genus name (Kottelat 2013).
Formerly of the genus Laubuca, now of the genus Laubuka. Laubuca was believed to be a misspelling of the genus name (Kottelat 2013).
Formerly of the genus Laubuca, now of the genus Laubuka. Laubuca was believed to be a misspelling of the genus name (Kottelat 2013).
The name of the genus has been changed to Pethia by Pethiyagoda et al. (2012)
The name of this genus was changed to Pethia by Pethiyagoda et al. (2012). Barbus cumingii  (= Puntius cumingii) was misapplied to this species by Meegaskumbura et al. (2008)
Puntius amphibius was misapplied to this species until Silva et al. (2008) re-evaluated it as a new species.
The name Puntius dorsalis was misapplied to this species (Pethiyagoda et al. 2008).

In 1958, Deraniyagala described Rasbora vaterifloris pallida. Later Batuwita et al. (2013) evaluated it as a true species. Rasboroides rohani is a junior synonym of Rasboroides pallidus (Sudasinghe et al. 2018)
Rasbora vaterifloris pallida was misapplied to this species (Batuwita et al. 2013). Rasboroides nigromarginata is a junior synonym of R. vaterifloris (Sudasinghe et al. 2018).

Populations of this species were regarded as part of a diverse complex generally lumped into Nothobranchius species ‘Lake Victoria’, from the basin of that lake in central and western Tanzania and south-western Kenya, together with populations from drainage systems in central Tanzania. Combined morphometric and molecular analyses have revealed that populations from the Moame system south of Lake Victoria in northern Tanzania represent a different species, which was described as Nothobranchius moameensis by Nagy, Watters and Bellstedt in Nagy et al. (2020).
Previously confused with the Whitespotted Whipray (Maculabatis gerrardi) from which it can be distinguished based on colour and morphometrics (Manjaji-Matsumoto and Last 2016).

Last et al. (2016) described the genus Maculabatis, comprised of nine medium to large, marine whiprays previously placed in Himantura (including gerrardi). Reports of the species are often confused with the Coach Whipray (Himantura uarnak).

Last et al. (2016) described the genus Pateobatis, comprised of five medium-size to very large, marine whiprays previously placed in Himantura (including bleekeri). Previously on The IUCN Red List bleekeri was treated as a junior synonym of uarnacoides (M. Manjaji pers. obs. 2007). However, Pateobatis bleekeri is considered a valid species by Last et al. (2016). This species was formerly referred to as Himantura alcockii/Himantura uarnacoides in the Arabian Seas region.

Last et al. (2016) described the genus Pateobatis, comprised of five medium-size to very large, marine whiprays previously placed in Himantura (including uarnacoides). Previously on The IUCN Red List, bleekeri was treated as a junior synonym of uarnacoides (M. Manjaji pers. obs. 2007). However Pateobatis bleekeri is considered a valid species by Last et al. (2016). This species is misidentified for Himantura lobiostoma, Pateobatis hortlei and Maculabatis pastinacoides (M. Manjaji pers. obs. 2007).

Trichoglossus haematodus, T. forsteni, T. capistratus, T. weberi, T. rubritorquis, T. moluccanus and T. rosenbergii (del Hoyo and Collar 2014) were previously lumped as T. haematodus following Christidis and Boles (1994), and before then were split as T. haematodus and T. rubritorquis following Sibley and Monroe (1990, 1993).
Records of this species from Guyana are here considered misidentifications of Petulanos plicatus.
Pycnonotus bimaculatus and P. snouckaerti (del Hoyo and Collar 2016) were previously lumped as P. bimaculatus following Sibley & Monroe (1990, 1993).

The apteronotid genus Megadontognathus has a remarkable apomorphic feature, up to 2-3 large and conical teeth (sometimes somewhat recurved) on the posterior region of dentary, the anterior region being edentulous (Mago-Leccia 1994). The genus has two species, M. cuyuniensis Mago-Leccia 1994 and M. kaitukaensi Campos da Paz 1999. The species M. cuyuniensis differs from M. kaitukaensi by its larger snout, 35.3-37.9% vs 29.8-33.7% of HL; somewhat smaller branchial opening, 8.7%-18.6% vs 17.4-21.0% of HL; somewhat smaller length to end of anal fin, 73.9-77.5% vs 76.9-81.2% of TL; higher counts of precaudal vertebrae, 20 vs 16-17, and anterior vertebrae 19 vs 13-14; and smaller counts of transitional vertebrae, 1 vs 3. Smaller counts of premaxillary teeth in M. cuyuniensis, 2-3 vs 4-9; maximum number of scales above lateral line, 12 vs 14-15. Upper pharyngeal tooth plates edentulous and poorly developed, vs with 5-10 teeth in M. kaitukaensis; smaller number of teeth on lower pharyngeal tooth plates of M. cuyuniensis, 7-6-7 vs 11; two anterior ossified basibranchials in M. cuyuniensis vs only anterior most basibranchial ossified in M. kaitukaensis (Campos da Paz 1999).

Sternarchorhynchus gnomus was described by de Santana and Taphorn (2006) from Venezuela, Bolivar state, Caroni River. The species can be recognized among congeners of the Orinoco River basin by its short gape, terminating posteriorly at, or slightly anterior of, vertically through the anterior naris; and mid-dorsal region of head and body without narrow, lightly coloured stripe or stripe obscure (de Santana and Vari 2010).

The type locality for Acisoma panorpoides ascalaphoides is Madagascar. It is not currently certain that the populations of Madagascar and mainland Africa are similar (K. Schütte in Dijkstra and Boudot 2010), so it is likely that the present subspecific name will have to change in the future for Africa.

This species previously appeared on the IUCN Red List as Acisoma panorpoides ssp. ascalaphoides but it has now been promoted to Acisoma ascalaphoides.
Largen (2001) emended the name from Afrixalus clarkei to Afrixalus clarkeorum. However, according to Amphibian Species of the World version 5.6, this is an unjustified emendation (Frost 2013).

This is a split from the broader concept of Alcalus baluensis by Brown et al. (2015), who recognised this species as distinct and placed it in this genus (it was previously known as Ingerana baluensis - Inger and Tan 1996).

According to Soldati F. (pers. comm. 2016), Allecula suberina might be conspecific with Allecula semilivida Pic, 1891 (now considered junior synonym to Allecula morio (Fabricius, 1787). Research is ongoing to revise the status of Allecula semilivida - type material to be studied and status changes to be published when confirmed.
This species was removed from the synonymy of Allobates brunneus by Silverstone (1971).
Most of the recent publications have treated this as a species of Spinophrynoides (Gower et al. 2012).

authority: Serrano-Rojas, Whitworth, Villacampa-Ortega, von May, Gutiérrez, Padial & Chaparro, 2017

taxonomicNotes: A member of the Trachypithecus obscurus group, that also includes T. obscurus and T. barbei, a sister clade to the T. cristatus group (Roos et al., 2007). Groves (2001) grouped T. obscurus and T. phayrei, but he was unsure of the affiliation of T. barbei and placed it in the T. cristatus group, suspecting (p.266), however, that it should in fact be allied with T. obscurus. Roos et al. (2007, 2020) defined this group phylogenetically (mtDNA haplotypes), confirming Groves’ (2001) suspicion and including T. barbei as a sister species to phayrei. Napier (1985) and Groves (2001) placed crepusculus Elliot, 1909, and shanicus Wroughton, 1917, as subspecies of phayrei. Leidigk et al. (2009) and Roos et al. (2019, 2020), however, showed that crepusculus was in fact the earliest branch of the francoisi group. Roos et al. (2020) found that phayrei comprised three clades that diverged about 1 Mya: besides phayrei, shanicus, which they synonymized with melamera Elliot, and a third previously unnamed form that they called popa—each as full species.
Singhal et al. (2018) revised Lampropholis robertsi, describing a new species (Lampropholis bellendenkerensis) that was previously considered part of L. robertsi.
Amey et al. (2019) revised Lerista storri, and described Lerista alia and Lerista parameles as new species.
This species was previously considered a subpopulation of Hynobius nebulosus prior to its description by Matsui et al. (2019).
This species was previously considered a subpopulation of Hynobius boulengeri until it was elevated to species status by Nishikawa and Matsui (2014).
Geokichla interpres (del Hoyo and Collar 2016) was previously listed as Zoothera interpres.

There is a possibility that Amphicnemis amabilis is a junior synonym of A. bicolor (Martin, 1897), in which case the known range of A. bicolor will be considerably increased and reassessment of A. bicolor will be needed.

There is a possibility that Amphicnemis amabilis Lieftinck, 1940 is a junior synonym of A. bicolor, in which case the known range of A. bicolor will be considerably increased and re-assessment of A. bicolor will be needed.

This species can only reliably be distinguished from Anodonthyla boulengerii with detailed morphological examination. It is therefore impossible to establish with certainty which historical records of A. boulengerii refer instead to this recently-diagnosed species (M. Vences pers. comm. December 2010).
Anodonthyla vallani was recognised as a distinct species by Vences et al. (2010), having previously been confused with A. nigrigularis.
This species was described from a single female. A male has never been found.

This species was treated as belonging to Arthroleptis adolfifriederici prior to its formal description (Perret, 1966; Amiet, 1975). Confusion may also have occurred with A. variabilis (Blackburn et al. 2009).
Barkudia insularis was described by Annandale in 1917 based on a single specimen collected from Barkuda Island, Chilka Lake, Orissa (Smith 1935, Das 1999).

Eschmeyer (2004) lists this as valid following Sparks 2001 (the most recent study). Sparks and Smith (2004) presents a phylogeny indicating that this is a distinct species but as there is noted spatial intraspecific variation it is possible there may be more than one species present.
This species is currently under taxonomic review, and is suspected to be a combination of many species (F. Köhler   pers.comm 2010).
This species was previously included within Callulina kreffti, formerly considered to be a monotypic species widespread throughout the Eastern Arc Mountains. (Loader et al. 2010).
This species is part of the C. nasutum/fallax complex, and there are other populations which have not yet been assigned, and might yet be referable to vohibola (Gehring et  al. 2011)
The true taxonomic status of this species requires clarification.
This taxon was formerly considered to be a subspecies of Mertensiella lushchani. It has now been elevated to species level, and assigned to the genus Lyciasalamandra by Veith and Steinfartz (2004).

There has been confusion about the generic placement of this species, which was described in Poecilurichthys, later placed in Astyanax and now Jupiaba. Adding to the confusion, a different fish and species of the same name described as Deuterodon potaroensis, was also place in Jupiaba, but now is tentatively again returned to Deuterodon. Museum specimens listed as Jupiaba potaroensis could refer to either of these two species, both of which were described by Eigenmann (1909) from the Potaro River.
Confused with C. consueta of southern Africa; taxonomy is discussed by Dijkstra (2003).

authority: Rosa, Crottini, Noel, Rabibisoa, Raxworthy & Andreone, 2014

taxonomicNotes: This species was previously included in the genus Eleutherodactylus (Crawford and Smith 2005).
In the Craugastor alfredi group. Craugastor cyanochthebius differs from all other species in the group by a combination of morphological characters (McCranie and Smith 2006).
This species was previously included in the genus Eleutherodactylus (Crawford and Smith 2005).

Cryptobatrachus remotus (Infante-Rivero et al. 2009 "2008"), could be a junior synonym of this species based on preliminary evidence (Castroviejo-Fisher et al. 2015, Meza Joya pers. comm. August 2016).
There has been no systematic revision of Pacific Island Rhytididae beyond the commentary by Solem (1959).

Subpopulations that are now recognized as belonging to Dendrotriton chujorum and Dendrotriton kekchiorum were formerly considered as part of Dendrotriton rabbi in the past, a species now considered to be restricted to Montañas de Cuilco (Campbell et al. 2010).

The genus consists of approximately 160 valid species that are distributed throughout Americas. However, the greater diversity of Dichotomius occurs in Brazil. The species that compose the genus are divided into four subgenera: Dichotomius, Homocanthonides, Selenocopris and Luederwaldtinia. Currently, Dichotomius is being revised by F. Vaz-de-Mello. Thus, the number of valid species and the internal structure of the genus may change.
Euphaea pahyapi was recently (Phan et al. 2018) recorded from Cambodia and Viet Nam, however these records are incorrect (Matti Hämäläinen pers. comm. 2018) and are not considered in the assessment.
The validity of this species has been questioned, but it is currently considered a good species (R. Jenkins pers. comm. June 2011, C. Raxworthy pers. comm. Jan 2011).
This genus has been moved from the family Hylidae (Faivovich et al. 2005).

The Fijian members of genus Gonatorhape von Moellendorff were revised by Clench (1949). However, there appears to be considerable conchological variability within and overlap among the nominal Fijian species, indicating the need for reassessment of their taxonomic status. Solem (1978) suggested that G. intercostata may be synonymous with G. stricta (Garrett, 1887) which had been described from Vatulele.

G. mukutense has been recently described and differs from all other species of the Gongylosoma genus in having a single, enlarged, posterior temporal; chinshields of equal size, and a thin nuchal band that contacts a vestigial vertebral stripe (Grismer et al. 2003).

Gymnostomus horai was described as Crossocheilus horai by Bănărescu (1986) from Inle Lake in central Myanmar. The species was considered to belong to the genus Cirrhinus by Roberts (1997) and as the name Cirrhinus horai has already been used by Lakshmanan (1966), Roberts (1997) proposed Cirrhinus lu as a replacement name for this species. However, Kottelat (2003) showed that the definition of Cirrhinus as used by Roberts (1997) is erroneous and the species that Roberts (1997) assign to Cirrhinus is congeneric with species of Henicorhynchus Smith, 1945. Furthermore, Kottelat (2003) indicated that the correct generic name for this genus should be Gymnostomus Heckel, 1843.
Horalabiosa arunachalami was described by Johnson and Sornam (2001) from Panniyar stream in Santhamparai, Iddukki district, Kerala.

Hemitriakis indroyonoi is separable from the other congeners, H. abdita, H. complicofasciata, H. falcata, H. japanica and H. leucoperiptera, by a combination of vertebral counts, morphometric characters, and juvenile colouration (White et al. 2009).

Silfvergrip (2009) described the diagnostic features of A. japonica greater than 200 mm total length (TL). These include a plain colouration, with no distinct markings, and a total number of vertebrae between 112–119. Dorsal fin anterior margin is 8.5–9.5% of TL, in advance of anus. The mouth is small, about one fourth of head length, and distance from anus to end of tail is 5.6–6.0 times longer than distance from anus to dorsal fin insertion.

Hypselobarbus pulchellus was originally described as Barbus (Barbodes) pulchellus by Day (1870) from inland streams (probably in Mangalore), India.

Hyriopsis bialata used to be regarded as fairly widespread and common in Southeast Asia (Brandt, 1974). However, molecular data (Zieritz et al. 2016) indicate that the Malaysian populations, which are restricted to the lower reaches of the Pahang River, represent a separate, cryptic species endemic to the peninsula. Since the species' type locality is Malaysia, H. bialata populations outside the Malaysian Peninsula are not considered conspecific and therefore excluded from the present assessment.

Simpson (1900, 1914)  placed Unio contritus Heude , 1881 as a junior synonym of Quadrula (Lamprotula) caveata (Heude, 1877) combining three similarly shaped taxa from China. Haas (1969) listed Unio contritus Heude , 1881 with a type locality as streams north of Po-yang, Kien -te-hien, China and listed as a junior synonym of Lamprotula leai (Gray, 1834). Dang et al. (1980) recognized this as a valid species from Ha Tay and Bac Thai provinces.

Simpson (1914) listed this species as Quadrula (Lamprotula) liedtkei  (Rolle, 1904) and the type locality as Riviere Claire, Tonkin, Vietnam. Haas (1969) used this species as a junior synonym of Mya nodulosa Wood, 1815 in the genus Lamprotula. Dang et al. (1980) recognized both L. liedtkei and L. nodulosa as separate valid species. There is taxonomic uncertainty about if this an distinct species or a synonym Lamprotula nodulosa (Wood) (Hass 1969).

Simpson (1914) used Lamprotula nodulosa (Wood, 1815)  and Lamprotula liedtkei (Rolle, 1904) as a separate species. Haas (1969) placed L. liedtkei in the synonymy of L. nodulosa. Dang et al. (1980) recognized both as valid species.
It has been used as a subspecies of Lanceolaria oxyrhyncha Von Martens, 1902 of Japan (http://fada.biodiversity.be/) (Haas 1969).

Additional work differentiating this species from nearby populations of Ambystoma velasci is needed (Shaffer and McKnight 1996).

Based on both allozymes and mtDNA, this is a very distinctive salamander (H.B. Shaffer pers. comm.). The Ambystoma salamanders occurring in other natural lakes around Alchichica are not closely related to this species.

Based on both allozymes and mtDNA, this is one of the more differentiated of the Mexican Ambystoma species; morphologically, it has evolved a superficially similar body plan to the distantly related A. dumerilii from Lago Pátzcuaro (H.B. Shaffer pers. comm.).
There is a question as to whether both Pseudoeurycea orchileucos and Pseudoeurycea orchimelas are distinct, valid species.
Specimens of this species have been included in Pseudoeurycea smithi by some previous authors (Parra-Olea et al. 2005).
The sister taxon of, and previously confused with, Oedipina gephrya according to the original publication (Frost 2013).

There has been no systematic revision of Pacific Island Rhytididae beyond the commentary by Solem (1959).

Solem (1962) suggested that in the absence of comparative study, material from Gaua (Banks Islands), Mota Island (Vanuatu) and Rotuma Island should be regarded as conspecific. While such great geographic dispersion over several disparate archipelagos in Rhytididae is likely to be associated with species level differentiation, Solem's view is accepted here pending revision of the Pacific Rhytididae. Christensen and Kirch (1981) recorded a rhytidid from archaeological sites on Tikopia Island in the southeastern Solomon Islands under the name Ouagapia perryi (Smith 1897), although this identification requires confirmation.
There has been no systematic revision of Pacific Island Rhytididae beyond the commentary by Solem (1959).

This is the only Philautus species known on Mindoro Island, but additional records of Philautus-like specimens were made during surveys at Mount Calavite, Mount Halcon, Mount Baco, Abra de Ilog, Sablayan, and Blue Mountain between 2005-2017; these are tentatively assigned to this species but require analysis and confirmation of their identity as they may represent undescribed species (Philippines Red List Assessment Workshop May 2017).

authority: Schurian, van Oorschot & Van den Brink, 1992
taxonomicNotes:
This species was first described in 1992 from four Turkish provinces (Konya, Adana, Kahramanmaras and Gaziantep). Then in 1994 Frédéric Carbonell described a new subspecies, P. (A.) theresiae larseni from Lebanon.

In 1999, a thorough review by Olivier et al. of all the theresiae specimens had the following outcomes:
-  Genetic study established that P. t. larseni should be elevated to a full species. P. theresiae was thus restored to being a Turkish endemic.
-  The specimens which had been used to establish the karyotype and chromosome number for theresiae came from Konya whilst the type specimens came from Adana. However, the Adana specimens were found to have a different chromosome number. It was thus decided to describe the Konya population as a new species: P. guezelmavi.
-  The specimens from Kahramanmaras and Gaziantep were identified to be Polyommatus poseidon.
 
 It was thus established that theresiae is an endemic species known only from the type locality in Adana.
This species was reassigned from Pristimantis to Serranobatrachus (Arroyo et al. 2022).

The type specimens of Unio messageri do not match the species recorded from Viet Nam, although they come from the same locality. These are possibly two species under the same heading. This assessment refers to the more recently described of the two species.
This species was previously considered a subpopulation of Hynobius boulengeri until it was elevated to species status by Nishikawa and Matsui (2014).
This species was previously considered a subpopulation of Hynobius boulengeri until it was elevated to species status by Nishikawa and Matsui (2014).

Even though some previous studies have treated Limia as one of the multiple subgenera in the genus Poecilia (Rosen and Bailey 1963, Meredith et al. 2010, Meredith et al. 2011), several other works have supported the generic classification for Limia (Rodriguez 1997, Hamilton 2001, Poeser 2003, Palacios et al. 2016, Weaver et al. 2016). Here we endorse the latter assessment that Limia should be considered a valid, separate genus.

The taxonomic role of genera to express relationships among species is clearly justified in the case of Limia from different points of view. From the biogeographic point of view, species of Limia show a discrete distribution since they are only found in the Antilles, specifically on Hispaniola, Cuba, Jamaica and the island of Grand Cayman. However, only three species of Poecilia sensu strictu occur in this region and they are restricted exclusively to Hispaniola. In addition, recent phylogenetic analyses have found Limia to be a monophyletic group probably originated from a South American ancestor with the three Hispaniolan Poecilia species mentioned above representing the closest sister group (Palacios et al. 2016, Weaver et al. 2016). A very detailed analysis is currently in prep, also confirming Limia as monophyletic (Spikes et al. in prep). Moreover, there is enough morphological distinctiveness in characters with adaptive value such is the case of gonopodial traits to separate species of Limia and Poecilia (Rivas 1978). Furthermore, all of the existing ecological and behavioral literature is using Limia, not Poecilia. Not recognizing Limia as a separate genus from Poecilia may obscure their similarities and cause even more confusion in a group that in fact deserves more taxonomic attention.

"T. taviuniensis may be only a subspecies of T. corallina but it attains a larger size and has more rapid whorl-increase" (Baker 1941), and is here treated as distinct following Barker & Bouchet (2010) pending a taxonomic revision. A record from Ovalau (AusMus C.34251 J. Brazier Coll.) has not been confirmed and is thus excluded from this assessment.

Fijian Trochomorphidae were last revised by Baker (1941), but with strong reliance on Bishop Museum specimen material. Mousson figured a specimen from Viti Levu as T. transarata but this is referable to T. fessonia (Baker 1941: 302).

This is the only surviving species of an ancient lineage from the early evolutionary history of the endemic family Mantellidae. It is therefore evolutionarily distinct and should be a high priority for efforts preserving Malagasy amphibian biodiversity.

Uniquely among vertebrates (except Xenopus eysoole, X. kobeli and X. ruwenzoriensis), this is a dodecaploid species, and it is therefore of considerable scientific interest. It was probably formed by both hybridization and polyploidization (Loumont and Kobel 1991).

This species was originally differentiated as a new form by Dr John A. Endler. In 1975 during his field activities in Venezuela and the Island of Trinidad, in order to study different aspects of natural selection in Poecilia reticulata (see Endler 1978, 1980, 1983 among others), Endler made collections of this new form in the Laguna de Los Patos, adjacent to the city of Cumaná, Sucre State, Venezuela. These preserved and living specimens were brought to the United States and delivered to Dr Donn E. Rosen (AMNH) for study and description, as well as to Dr. Klaus Kallman for reproduction. The species was not described by Dr. Rosen, due to his death, but Dr. Kallman took it upon himself to distribute them to numerous ornamental fish breeders in different parts of the world, and the undescribed species began to be marketed and known as Endler's Livebearer or Endler's Guppy since the 1970s (Endler 2011).

The species was described as Poecilia wingei by Poeser, Kempkes & Isbrücker (2005), with specimens captured in 2002, in the hydrographic system of the Campoma and Buena Vista lagoons, (Sucre state, Venezuela), as well as other small coastal rivers close by.

Poecilia wingei is currently a valid species, considered a sister species to P. reticulata (Morris et al. 2018). Since its description, this species has not undergone nomenclatural modifications or generic relocations.

However, it is important to know that Herdeguen et al. (2014) do not consider Poecilia wingei as a valid or separate species of Poecilia reticulata, since they did not find sufficient genetic evidence that can separate or differentiate these two species. Other authors (Alexander and Breden 2004, Alexander et al. 2006, Ward et al. 2008, Tripathi et al. 2009a, b, Watson et al. 2010) do not consider P. wingei as a valid species, although they accept Poecilia reticulata populations from the north-eastern region of Venezuela, present in the aquatic ecosystems adjacent and close to the city of Cumaná and those of the Río Campoma basin (called by them Cumaná Guppy), as a case of incipient speciation (Alexander and Breden 2004, Herdeguen et al. 2014). According to Huber (2019), P. wingei is a typical “molecular species”, not diagnosable by some pattern or combination of external characters.
This taxon was formerly considered to be a subspecies of Salamandra lushchani. It has been elevated to species level and assigned to the genus Lyciasalamandra by Veith and Steinfartz (2004).
This taxon was formerly considered to be a subspecies of Salamandra lushchani. It has been elevated to species level and assigned to the genus Lyciasalamandra by Veith and Steinfartz (2004).
This taxon was formerly considered to be a subspecies of Salamandra lushchani. It has been elevated to species level, and assigned to the genus Lyciasalamandra, by Veith and Steinfartz (2004).

The Kaputar rock skink had been recognised for more than 25 years as of Sadlier et al. (2019), and treated as an unnamed species of Egernia in field guides. Sadlier et al. (2019) confirmed that the species is both morphologically and genetically distinct from its closest relative E. striolata, and that it was the same species described as Contundo roomi by Wells and Wellington (1984). The name Contundo roomi had previously either been ignored or treated as a possible subspecies of E. striolata.
Singhal et al. (2018) revised Lampropholis robertsi, describing a new species (Lampropholis bellendenkerensis) that was previously considered part of L. robertsi.
Singhal et al. (2018) revised Lampropholis coggeri, describing two new species (Lampropholis similis and Lampropholis elliotensis) that were previously considered part of L. coggeri.
We follow Alonso et al. (2023) in considering Amatolebias a valid genus, rather than a synonym of Austrolebias.

Aguilera, Terán, Mirande, Alonso, Rometsch, Meyer & Torres-Dowdall, 2019

taxonomicNotes: Biton et al. (2013) removed this species from the genus Discoglossus and placed it into the fossil genus Latonia.

Distichodus sp. nov. "microps" is a newly described species that was initially determined to be D. notospilus. Subsequent molecular and morphological analyses revealed that this species was distinct from topotypic D. notospilus from the Ogooué River in Gabon.

Sister to H. ruddi (Alström et al. 2013a). Morphological, vocal and genetic analysis recently demonstrated that form sidamoensis, treated as a species in HBW, belongs in present species, and may not even be subspecifically distinct; a new population in E Ethiopia simultaneously shown also to belong to nominate (Spottiswoode et al. 2013).We follow Donald & Alström (in prep) in treating Liben Lark as monotypic.

Although Pericnemis bisaya is known from Cebu, Mindanao, Negros and Panay in the Philippines, Villanueva & Dow (2020) expressed some doubts over whether populations from outside of Cebu really represent the same species. However all populations are treated as a single species for assessment purposes.

Although Pericnemis yakal is known from Samar and Leyte as well as Mindanao, Villanueva and Dow (2020) expressed some doubts over whether populations from outside of Mindanao really represent the same species. However, all populations are treated as a single species for assessment purposes.
This species was previously considered a subpopulation of Hynobius stejnegeri prior to its description by Tominaga et al. (2019).

Otus magicus and O. tempestatis (del Hoyo and Collar 2014) were previously lumped as O. magicus, and O. alfredi, O. siaoensis, O. enganensis, O. insularis and O. beccarii (del Hoyo and Collar 2014) were split from O. magicus following Widodo et al. (1999), Lambert and Rasmussen (1998), Andrew (1992) and Holt et al. (1999). Prior to that all these taxa were lumped in O. magicus following Sibley and Monroe (1990, 1993).
Geotrygon purpurata and G. saphirina (del Hoyo and Collar 2014) were previously lumped as G. saphirina following Sibley and Monroe (1990, 1993).
Premnoplex tatei and P. pariae (del Hoyo and Collar 2016) were previously lumped as P. tatei following SACC (2005 & updates), Sibley & Monroe (1990, 1993) and Stotz et al. (1996).
Closely related to C. moluccensis. Monotypic.

Closely related to C. galerita and C. ophthalmica. Previous recognised taxon included subspecies citrinocristata (del Hoyo & Collar 2014), but this now split as Citron-crested Cockatoo (which see). Subspecies abbotti strikingly large (except in bill size) (3), with small, pale brownish-yellow ear-covert patch (2). Subspecies djampeana and occidentalis generally regarded as synonyms of nominate subspecies and parvula, respectively, but new evaluation indicates real morphological differences and even possible subspecific division within djampeana (Collar & Marsden 2014). Five subspecies recognized.
The taxonomy of this species remains unresolved and it may prove to be a synonym of the Smallspot Numbfish Narcine maculata (Last et al. 2016).
