Pavanelli & Oliveira (2009) suggested that this species should be considered a junior synonym of Astyanax. Preliminary molecular analyses carried out by A. Prioli (pers. comm.) further corroborates that the three morphotypes likely represent a single species. However, until this research is published, Psalidodon is considered valid (Fricke et al. 2023).

Amazilia cyanifrons and A. alfaroana (HBW and BirdLife International 2017) were previously lumped as A. cyanifrons (del Hoyo and Collar 2014), but A. alfaroana is now recognised as a good species following Kirwan and Collar (2016), who examined the sole specimen and found species-level differences from cyanifrons and no evidence of characters suggesting that it is a hybrid, with the conclusion that - subject to any future molecular analysis - it is best treated as a separate species. It differs from cyanifrons in having a slightly longer bill, paler blue crown, more continuously green chin to belly, richer russet uppertail-coverts, and bluish-black undertail-coverts with narrow whitish edges. Monotypic.

The taxonomy follows the view of the IWC Scientific Committee and the Society for Marine Mammalogy’s Committee on Taxonomy which now recognize Right Whales in the North Atlantic, North Pacific and Southern Hemisphere as three distinct species in the genus Eubalaena, namely E. glacialis (North Atlantic Right Whale), E. japonica (North Pacific Right Whale), and E. australis (Southern Right Whale) (IWC 2004), based mainly on the mtDNA phylogenetic findings of Rosenbaum et al. (2000).

The North Atlantic Right Whale was included in previous Red Lists together with the North Pacific Right Whale under the species name E. glacialis (Baillie and Groombridge 1996).

Rice (1998) classified Right Whales in the North Atlantic, North Pacific and Southern Hemisphere as the single species Balaena glacialis, in the genus Balaena along with B. mysticetus, the Bowhead Whale. While not all cetologists accept that the three Right Whale taxa qualify as full biological species, their clear geographical separation means that their treatment as separate taxa for conservation purposes is appropriate.

Previously listed as Megalurulus rufus (del Hoyo and Collar 2016) and before that as Trichocichla rufa but reassigned to Cincloramphus following Alström et al. (2018). Has been placed in monospecific Trichocichla, or sometimes with C. rubiginosus in Ortygocichla. Despite its rather long legs, however, it has many features typical of present genus. Two subspecies recognized.

Groh and Hemmen (1986) examined Geomitra coronata, and reclassified it with two other taxa (both extinct) into a new subgenus Serratorotula. Bank et al. (2002) elevated the subgenus to generic status.

This species was previously assessed under the name Serratorotula coronata, however, the valid name is Serratorotula juliformis, due to pre-occupation of the first (K. Groh pers. comm. 2019).
This species has been separated from Alytes obstetricans (Donaire-Barroso and Bogaerts 2003, Martínez-Solano et al. 2004, Fromhage et al. 2004).
Telmatobius albiventris and T. crawfordi were synonymized with this species by Benavides, Ortiz and Sites (2002).

This taxon was formerly considered a subspecies of Nycticebus coucang, with which there is a small hybridization zone in peninsular Thailand. Based on genetic evidence, it was elevated to species level by Roos (2003). Considerable variation exists in body size (850–2,100 g), genetics and coat colour (white to crimson red; absence of head forks to possession of light head forks) in this taxon, which may lead to at least three new taxa being described.
Cinnyris loveridgei (del Hoyo and Collar 2016) was previously placed in the genus Nectarinia following Sibley & Monroe (1990, 1993).

Pyrrhura picta, P. snethlageae, P. parvifrons, P. amazonum, P. lucianii, P. roseifrons, P. peruviana, P. subandina, P. caeruleiceps and P. eisenmanni (del Hoyo and Collar 2014) were previously lumped as P. picta following Sibley and Monroe (1990, 1993).

Pyrrhura picta, P. snethlageae, P. parvifrons, P. amazonum, P. lucianii, P. roseifrons, P. peruviana, P. subandina, P. caeruleiceps and P. eisenmanni (del Hoyo and Collar 2014) were previously lumped as P. picta following Sibley and Monroe (1990, 1993).
Millepora tenella is considered a synonym of this species (Randall and Cheng 1984).

Originally described by Verrill (1868), this species was at one point considered a synonym of M. alcicornis (Boschma 1948) but was subsequently considered as distinct by the same author (Boschma 1962). Millepora nitida is presently considered a valid species based on morphological (Amaral et al. 2008) and genetic evidence (de Souza et al. 2017). Millepora braziliensis is morphologically very similar to its congener, Millepora nitida - differing only in colony morphology, but not in morphometric traits of the skeleton (Verrill 1868, de Souza et al. 2017).

Millepora tuberosa is now treated as a synonym of M. exaesa (WoRMS Editorial Board 2022). Arrigoni et al. (2018) found that populations of this species from the Red Sea formed a distinct lineage with a deep genetic break with southwestern Indian Ocean populations, which suggests the occurrence of cryptic species. Since this species' type specimen was collected at the Red Sea, the name is valid for that region. However, it was pointed out that in the rest of the Indo-Pacific, this species should be referred to as Millepora cf. dichotoma and further taxonomic evaluation is warranted (Arrigoni et al. 2018, Dubé et al. 2019).

Originally described by Verrill (1868), Millepora braziliensis is considered a valid species based on morphological (Amaral 2008) and genetic evidence (de Souza et al. 2017). This species is morphologically very similar to its congener, Millepora nitida - differing only in colony morphology, but not in morphometric traits of the skeleton (Verrill 1868, de Souza et al. 2017).
Both Millepora xishaensis and M. murrayi are now treated as synonyms of this species (Razak and Hoeksema 2003, WoRMS Editorial Board 2022).

Arrigoni et al. (2018) found that populations of this species from the Red Sea formed a distinct lineage with a deep genetic break with southwestern Indian Ocean populations, which suggests the occurrence of cryptic species. Since this species' type specimen was collected at the Red Sea, the name is valid for that region. However, it was pointed out that in the rest of the Indo-Pacific, this species should be referred to as Millepora cf. dichotoma and further taxonomic evaluation is warranted (Arrigoni et al. 2018, Dubé et al. 2019).

Arrigoni et al. (2018) found that populations of this species from the Red Sea formed a distinct lineage with a deep genetic break with Southwestern Indian Ocean populations, which suggests the occurrence of cryptic species. Since this species' type specimen was collected at the Red Sea, the name is valid for that region. However, it was pointed out that in the rest of the Indo-Pacific, this species should be referred to as Millepora cf. platyphylla and further taxonomic evaluation is warranted (Arrigoni et al. 2018, Dubé et al. 2019).

Carranza and Wade (2004) showed that most of the animals from North Africa previously referred to as Pleurodeles poireti actually belong to P. nebulosus, true P. poireti being restricted to the Edough Peninsula in northern Algeria. The two species hybridize in a broad contact zone in the east of the coastal plain of El Taref (Berrihane and Lac des Oiseaux) and in the west in Guerbes-Foret des Senhaja (Djendel, Skikda) (Escoriza et al. 2016).
Pipilo maculatus and P. socorroensis (del Hoyo and Collar 2016) were previously lumped as P. maculatus following AOU (1998 and supplements); Stotz et al. (1996).
Coeligena bonapartei, C. consita and C. eos (del Hoyo and Collar 2014) were previously lumped as C. bonapartei following Sibley and Monroe (1990, 1993).
Setophaga angelae (del Hoyo and Collar 2016) was previously placed in the genus Dendroica following AOU (1998 & supplements); Sibley & Monroe (1990, 1993); Stotz et al. (1996).
Hedydipna pallidigaster (del Hoyo and Collar 2016) was previously placed in the genus Anthreptes following Dowsett & Forbes-Watson (1993); Sibley & Monroe (1990, 1993).

authority: Louis Jr., Coles, Andriantompohavana, Sommer, Engberg, Zaonarivelo, Mayor & Brenneman, 2006

taxonomicNotes: Sister to M. bicolor on molecular evidence (Burns et al. 2014, Burns et al. 2016). Monotypic.
Ammospiza caudacuta (del Hoyo and Collar 2016) was previously placed in the genus Ammodramus and listed as A. caudacutus following AOU (1998 & supplements).
Foudia eminentissima, F. consobrina and F. aldabrana (del Hoyo and Collar 2016) were previously lumped as F. eminentissima following Sibley & Monroe (1990, 1993).

Previously (del Hoyo and Collar 2014) placed in the genus Alopecoenas and before that in the genus Gallicolumba but transferred to Pampusana on the grounds of the latter’s priority (Bruce et al. 2016). A distinctive form of uncertain affinities, apparently sister to all other members of genus (Jonsson et al. 2011). Has alternatively been considered an early derivative of P. jobiensis stock. Monotypic.
This species has been split from Gymnura micra and is morphologically similar but can be distinguished on the basis of clasper morphology and disc shape (Yokota and de Carvalho 2017).

Has been treated as conspecific with A. rimitarae and A. taiti, but basic differences in sequence and pattern of leucistic plumage among the three taxa suggest differentiation at species level; present species and A. taiti are sister taxa, but A. rimitarae belongs to a separate clade (Cibois et al. 2011). Monotypic.

The validity of this species has been "seriously questioned" by recent systematic research (Leviton et al. 2018). This is based on the finding by Siler et al. (2013) that there is very little to no genetic divergence between this species and Lycodon alcalai, and Leviton et al. (2018) suggest that the two taxa may be conspecific.

This taxon was formerly considered a subspecies of N. concolor, however, the light-cheeked gibbons were subsequently separated (Dao Van Tien 1983; Ma and Wang 1986; Ma et al. 1988) and then further divided into the four taxa currently recognized with the separation of N. gabriellae (Groves 1993), then N. siki (e.g. Groves 2001; Mootnick 2006; Geissmann 2007; Van Ngoc Thinh et al. 2010a; Van Ngoc Thinh et al. 2010b; Mootnick and Fan Pengfei 2011; Van Ngoc Thinh et al. 2011) and finally with the recent description of N. annamensis (Van Ngoc Thin et al. 2010c). Nomascus leucogenys was previously considered to overlap with N. concolor in Viet Nam and Yunnan, China (Dao Van Tien 1983; Ma and Wang 1986), however, there seems to be insufficient evidence to currently conclude this (Fooden 1996; Geissmann et al. 2000; Rawson et al. 2011).

This species belongs to bicolor species group. This species was listed under Hipposideros bicolor (Temminck, 1834), but is now considered distinct (Hill et al. 1986, Srinivasulu and Srinivasulu 2012). Based on morphology and bacular structure, Srinivasulu and Srinivasulu (2018) upgraded the taxon gentilis Andersen, 1918 to specific rank, and suggested that H. pomona Andersen, 1918 sensu stricto is restricted to southern India, and H. gentilis sensu lato occurs from northeast India to Southeast Asia.
Coracornis sanghirensis (del Hoyo and Collar 2016) was previously listed as Colluricincla sanghirensis.
Monotypic.
Setophaga chrysoparia (del Hoyo and Collar 2016) was previously placed in the genus Dendroica following AOU (1998 & supplements); Sibley & Monroe (1990, 1993); Stotz et al. (1996).

Reiserer et al. (2013) proposed recognizing the four established subspecies of Heloderma horridum as full species, as these are morphologically diagnosable and have been found to exhibit relatively high levels of genetic divergence. This latter observation was based on the results of Douglas et al. (2010), although those authors did not propose elevating these taxa to specific status. While Reiserer et al. (2013) caution that the morphological diagnoses of the former subspecies are "somewhat incomplete" and that further research is needed to confirm their species-level arrangement, the four-species approach is now widely accepted and is followed here.

Hoskin (2019) revised the taxonomy of the Oedura monilis species complex and found that it consisted of four morphologically distinct species. He did not provide genetic data, but indicated that then-unpublished work demonstrated "substantial divergence" in both mitochondrial and nuclear DNA between the identified clades. He consequently described three new species: O. elegans, O. lineata and O. picta.
Monotypic.
Sitta pusilla and S. insularis (del Hoyo and Collar 2016) were previously lumped as S. pusilla following AOU (1998 & supplements), Sibley & Monroe (1990, 1993) and Stotz et al. (1996).
Lyu et al. (2021) listed this species in Xenophrys without comment, and this work was not a revision of Megophrys. This assessment retains it in Megophrys until generic boundaries are more stable.
Amazona autumnalis, A. diadema and A. lilacina (del Hoyo and Collar 2014) were previously lumped as A. autumnalis following Sibley and Monroe (1990, 1993).
The subspecies Aneides flavipunctatus niger was raised in rank to a full species by Reilly and Wake (2019).

McGuire et al. (2019) review in detail the status of the all-white taxon occidentalis, which although formerly given species status (‘Great White Heron’ [GWH]) has long been considered a subspecies of Great Blue Heron [GBH] Ardea herodias, in great part because the two taxa interbreed with regularity. However, given that the evidence points to assortative mating where possible, they conclude that occidentalis is indeed better treated as a species:

it is separated by diagnostic plumage (white vs. blue), behaviour (mate choice and timing of mating) and habitat (saltwater-island vs. fresh/brackish water mainland).

There are, in fact, a few additional morphological considerations, with GWH having a shorter occipital plume but longer bill, tarsus and middle toe than GBH (supplementary material online with measurements not accessed). Applying the Tobias criteria to the situation, the taxonomic status of GWH involves a consideration of its

·       all-white vs grey/blue plumage (an exceptional difference: score 4);
·       shorter occipital plume (seemingly fairly distinctive from the authors’ Figure 3: allow 1);
·       longer tarsus (apparently the most distinctive of the longer characters measured, but perhaps insufficient to be allowed a score: ns);
·       timing of mating or habitat choice (only one of these permitted as a score: 1);
·       narrow hybrid zone (not more than 50 km as measured on Google Earth: 2).

On this basis BirdLife accepts Ardea occidentalis (Handbook of the Birds of the World and BirdLife International 2020) as a separate species.
Hypotaenidia okinawae (del Hoyo and Collar 2014) was previously placed in the genus Gallirallus.

Tanygnathus sumatranus (with its longer-tailed subspecies sangirensis) and T. everetti (with its larger subspecies burbidgii; race duponti synonymised with nominate) (Handbook of the Birds of the World and BirdLife International 2020) have been split from T. sumatranus (del Hoyo & Collar 2014). Arndt et al. (2019) found that in both sexes Philippine forms differ by their larger bills (effect size of everetti vs sangirensis 1.62 and vs sumatranus 1.15; effect size of burbidgii vs sangirensis 1.75 and vs sumatranus 2.02; as burbidgii is here treated as conspecific with everetti, the lower values for everetti must be considered, hence score 1); blood-red or orange-red vs yellowish-white irides (3); pale matt royal blue in place of slightly glossy turquoise-blue lower back and rump (2); paler green head (ns1); and duller green underparts (ns1). Moreover, in males the Philippine forms further differ by their absence of blue in the carpal feathers and scapulars (2); and much darker green mantle (ns2). Additionally, phylogenetic reconstruction identified the Philippine and Indonesian clades as being as genetically distinct as T. lucionensis and T. megalorynchos. Three subspecies recognised.Tanygnathus sumatranus (with subspecies sangirensis) and T. everetti (with subspecies burbidgii; race duponti synonymised with nominate) (Handbook of the Birds of the World and BirdLife International 2020) have been split from T. sumatranus (del Hoyo et al. 2014).
Lyu et al. (2021) listed this species in Xenophrys without comment, and this work was not a revision of Megophrys. This assessment retains it in Megophrys until generic boundaries are more stable.

This is a split from the broader concept of Anaxyrus boreas (Gordon et al. 2017). This species should also be recognized as a distinct management unit according to microsatellite data, which demonstrated that this species exhibits one unique allele and high allele frequency (FST > 0.151) differences from any other A. boreas complex populations (Forrest et al. 2017).

Previously, this species was considered to have two subspecies: Eulemur macao macaco, the Black Lemur and E. m. flavifrons, the Blue-eyed Back Lemur or Sclater’s Lemur. Taxonomic revision of each member of the Eulemur fulvus subspecies complex to full species status (Mittermeier et al. 2006), left E. macaco as the only species in the genus that was classified as polytypic (i.e., E. m. macaco and E. m. flavifrons). However, given the degree of morphological differences between the Black Lemur and the Blue-eyed Black Lemur, retaining this subspecific taxonomy was assessed as not consistent with the available comparative data on the two taxa. Pair-wise genetic distance values between the Black Lemur and the Blue-eyed Black Lemur range from 68–72 bp -- this is in the same range as what has been found between the former E. fulvus subspecies (29–90 bp; see Pastorini 2000). In the late 1980s and early 1990s, reports of a north-south geographical morphocline in subspecific traits was reported in the region between Maromandia and the Manongarivo Special Reserve (Meyers et al. 1989, Rabarivola et al. 1991), but more recently completed survey work indicates that the hybrid zone between the two taxa is restricted to the northeastern geographic range of the Blue-eyed Black Lemur and southern portions of the Manongarivo Special Reserve (Andrianjakarivelo 2004, Schwitzer et al. 2005, 2006, Tinsman et al. 2019). In light of these findings, the Blue-eyed Black Lemur was raised to full species status (i.e., E. flavifrons) by Mittermeier et al. (2008, 2010).
Formerly considered a subspecies of Eulemur fulvus, but later raised to species level (Groves 2001).

Cytogenetic and molecular data do not distinguish Hapalemur alatorensis from H. griseus griseus (Fausser et al. 2002, Pastorini et al. 2002), although the former is differentiated based on its larger size (Rabarivola et al. 2007).
Gracula religiosa, G. venerata, G. robusta and G. indica (del Hoyo and Collar 2016) were previously lumped as G. religiosa following AOU (1998 and supplements) and Sibley and Monroe (1990, 1993).
The common name for this species in Vietnamese is Rùa Trung Bộ.
Alophoixus bres, A. frater and A. tephrogenys (del Hoyo and Collar 2016) were previously lumped as A. bres following Sibley and Monroe (1990, 1993).
Amey et al. (2019) described Lerista anyara as a new species. It is part of the Lerista allanae species group.
Amey et al. (2019) revised Lerista storri, and described Lerista alia and Lerista parameles as new species.
Amey et al. (2019) revised Lerista storri, and described Lerista alia and Lerista parameles as new species.

authority: Parra-Olea, García-Castillo, Rovito, Maisano, Hanken & Wake, 2020
taxonomicNotes: The holotype and paratypes were collected since 1969, but this species has only been recently described (Parra-Olea et. al. 2020) based on morphometrics and osteological evidence.

authority: Parra-Olea, García-Castillo, Rovito, Maisano, Hanken & Wake, 2020
taxonomicNotes: The species was suggested as a new taxon following Darda (1994), but it has only recently been described (Parra-Olea et al. 2020) based on molecular and morphological evidence.

authority: Parra-Olea, García-Castillo, Rovito, Maisano, Hanken & Wake, 2020
taxonomicNotes: The species was suggested as a new taxon following Darda (1994), but it has only recently been described (Parra-Olea et. al. 2020) based on molecular and morphological evidence.

authority: Parra-Olea, García-Castillo, Rovito, Maisano, Hanken & Wake, 2020
taxonomicNotes: The species was suggested as a new taxon following Darda (1994), but it has only recently been described (Parra-Olea et. al. 2020) based on molecular and morphological evidence.

authority: Parra-Olea, García-Castillo, Rovito, Maisano, Hanken & Wake, 2020
taxonomicNotes: The species was suggested as a new taxon following Darda (1994), but it has only recently been described (Parra-Olea et. al. 2020) based on molecular and morphological evidence. Parra-Olea et. al. (2020) hypothesize that specimens reported as C. chiropterus from 11,000 feet on Cofre de Perote by Smith and Taylor (1948) actually belong to C. perotensis.
Magumma parva (del Hoyo and Collar 2016) was previously placed in the genus Hemignathus and listed as H. parvus following AOU (1998 & supplements); Sibley & Monroe (1990, 1993).
Geospiza heliobates (del Hoyo and Collar 2016) was previously placed in the genus Camarhynchus following SACC (2005 & updates); Sibley & Monroe (1990, 1993); Stotz et al. (1996).
This is a split from the broader concept of Anaxyrus boreas (Gordon et al. 2020).
Gracula religiosa, G. venerata, G. robusta and G. indica (del Hoyo and Collar 2016) were previously lumped as G. religiosa following AOU (1998 & supplements) and Sibley & Monroe (1990, 1993).

authority: Brown, Meneses, Wood, Fernandez, Cuesta, Clores, Tracy, Buehler & Siler, 2020
taxonomicNotes: A single immature specimen from Caramoan is tentatively included in this species by Brown et al. (2020), who nevertheless note that it is genetically divergent from Sorsogon sequences and suspect that it is likely to represent an undescribed species.

Previously lumped as C. banyumas with C. whitei and C. montanus (del Hoyo and Collar 2016), but those species now split following Eaton et al. (2016) and specimen examination. Differs from C. whitei by its broad black chin vs narrow or none in male (1); continuously russet underparts vs only throat/breast (belly/vent whitish) in both sexes (3); markedly greyer vs olive-brown upperparts in female (2); greater wing length (unmeasured, but at least 1); evidently different voice (Gwee et al. 2019) (not assessed, but at least 1). For differences from C. montanus, see that species. Has been considered conspecific with C. lemprieri; songs rather similar, and separation based mainly on plumage. Until recently considered conspecific with C. magnirostris, which proves to be vocally rather close to present species. Subspecies mardii, described from Panaitan I, off Java, sometimes recognised. Two subspecies recognised.
Chloropsis cochinchinensis and C. moluccensis (del Hoyo and Collar 2016) were previously lumped as C. cochinchinensis following Wells et al. (2003).

Padda oryzivora (Handbook of the Birds of the World and Birdlife International 2022) was recently listed as Lonchura oryzivora (del Hoyo and Collar 2016), but the distinctiveness of this and P. fuscata has been demonstrated genetically (Olsson and Alström 2020) and they are returned to the genus in which they were previously placed following AOU (1998 and supplements), Christidis and Boles (2008), Dowsett and Forbes-Watson (1993), SACC (2005 and updates) and Sibley and Monroe (1990, 1993).

Quadrula intermedia has been synonymized in the past with Quadrula tuberosa and Quadrula sparsa (Ortmann 1918, Williams et al. 2008). The relationship to Quadrula tuberosa, Quadrula intermedia, and Quadrula sparsa is questionable; however, both Q. tuberosa and Q. sparsa have been found historically within the range of Q. intermedia and no intergrade specimens have been found (USFWS 1984). Q. tuberosa may be the form of Q. intermedia occurring in big rivers, where a more obese form typically occurs in many unionid species. Quadrula sparsa is currently recognized by Turgeon et al. (1998). Distributional records became confused when Ortmann (1914, 1918) lumped Quadrula sparsa and Quadrula tuberosa under Quadrula intermedia (Bogan and Parmalee 1983, Parmalee and Bogan 1998). Historical records for Q. tuberosa from the Cumberland River are therefore also included with historical records for Q. sparsa (USFWS 1984) but if these are distinct species, then Q. sparsa was not present in the headwaters of the Cumberland River (Parmalee and Bogan 1998).

This species is listed in Graf and Cummings (2007) under the genus Theliderma. However, it is thought by some that further justification is needed before this change can be confirmed.

A list of synonyms for this species can be found on The MUSSEL project web site (Graf and Cummings 2011).
Rhyticeros everetti (del Hoyo and Collar 2014) was previously placed in the genus Aceros.
This species is very similar in appearance to C. amphibius (Ranarilalatiana and Bergsten 2019).
This is a split from the broader concept of Nymphargus chancas (Guayasamín et al. 2020).

This species was revalidated in 2014, after a series of studies highlighted genetic and morphological disparities between grayling subpopulations from the northern Adriatic region and elsewhere in Europe (Sušnik et al. 2001, 2004, Geiger et al. 2014, Bianco 2014).

Earlier publications referring to graylings from the northern Adriatic as T. thymallus thus correspond to T. aeliani and/or hybrid individuals (see 'Population').
This is a split from the broader concept of Andrias davidianus (Turvey et al. 2019).

Because of the previous lack of molecular data in the past, this species has been mistakenly identified as Pristimantis riveti (e.g., collections at the QCAZ museum). Páez and Ron (2019) formally recognize it as a different species and assign it to the P. phoxocephalus species group.

This species is in need of taxonomic revision (Ortega-Andrade et al. 2021).

This is a split from the broader concept of Pristimantis phoxocephalus (Páez and Ron 2019). Pristimantis teslai has been mistakenly identified as P. phoxocephalus (e.g., collections at the QCAZ museum). This species could be a synonym of P. modipeplus (M. Yánez-Muñoz pers. comm. 2021).
This is a split from the broader concept of Pristimantis phoxocephalus (Páez and Ron 2019). Pristimantis totoroi has been mistakenly referred to as P. phoxocephalus (e.g., Hedges et al. 2008).

authority: (Reyes-Puig, Reyes-Puig, Ron, Ortega, Guayasamin, Goodrum, Recalde, Vieira, Koch & Yánez-Muñoz, 2019)

taxonomicNotes: This is a split from the broader concept of Kurixalus appendiculatus. Prior to this split, this species has been confused with K. chaseni due to superficial morphological similarities (Mediyansyah et al. 2019).

Potamotrygon jabuti is a relatively recently described species of freshwater stingray endemic to the Tapajós River. The species is part of the occelated group of freshwater stingrays formed by Potamotrygon motoro, P. ocellata and P. boesemani (Carvalho 2016).
This relatively recently described species was previously cited as Potamotrygon histrix (or P. hystrix) or misidentified as P. laticeps, P. motoro, or Disceus thayeri (Carvalho et al. 2016).
This species is in the Dryophytes immaculatus group, and is a split from the broader concept of Dryophytes suweonensis (Borzée et al. 2020).

This is a restricted concept of Gastrotheca litonedis following the split of the broader concept into this and G. cuencana based on the discrete morphological and acoustic differences between the two species (Carvajal-Endara et al. 2019).
This is the new broader concept including Centrolene scirtetes and C. gemmatum synonymised with this species, following Guayasamin et al. (2020).
Rediscovered in 2002, after more than 120 years with virtually no sightings or data of any sort (Mauro 2005). Monotypic.
In the Melanophryniscus moreirae group according to the original description (Maneyro et al. 2008).

Morgan (1997) named Euastacus dharawalus after the Dharawal aboriginal language, but did not designate an official common name. However, he did refer to specimens of Riek’s crayfish in the Australian Museum collection as Fitzroy Falls Crayfish. The common name Fitzroy Falls Crayfish has been used ever since. There are no recent taxonomic changes or doubts as to the species validity.
This species is known from both sexes, illustrated.
This species is known from both sexes, illustrated.
Revised by Griswold (1987), it is known only from the female, illustrated.
Revised by Griswold (1987), it is known from both sexes, illustrated.
It is known from both sexes and has been well-illustrated. Identification is not problematic.
This species is known from both sexes, illustrated.
It is known from both sexes and has been well-illustrated. Identification is not problematic.

Warren (1912) suggested this species may be a form of Agrotis lucicolens, which was subsequently synonymized with A. photophila, but Zimmerman (1958) disagrees with this assertion. It is therefore treated as a separate species.
This species was listed as a synonym of Agrotis cremata by Warren (1912), but Zimmermann (1958) does not agree with that assessment.
The Global Lepidoptera Names Index (LepIndex 2018) gives the spelling as Agrotis epicremma. As per the original description, this is an error, and the spelling should be Agrotis epicremna.
Lötters (1996), suggested that the Cauca localities (by Cochran and Goin 1970) were doubtful on geographic grounds.

Following the review of the genus Oedipina by Brodie et al. (2012), where the presence of Oedipina salvadorensis was confirmed for El Salvador, E. Morán and V. Henríquez reviewed nine specimens of Oedipina from the departments of San Salvador and Ahuachapán, El Salvador [deposited in the El Salvador Museum of Natural History (MUNHES)].

The taxonomic characteristics of the specimens from San Salvador corresponded to those of Oedipina salvadorensis, while the specimens from the department of Ahuachapán corresponded to Oedipina taylori (V. Henríquez pers. comm. June 2019).

authority: Formas, Northland, Capetillo, Nuñez, Cuevas & Brieva, 1999
taxonomicNotes: The species may be a synonym of T. vilamensis (Sáez et al., 2014).
May be a synonym of T. dankoi (Sáez et al. 2014).

Alouatta ululata was originally described by Elliot, 1912, but was considered one of five subspecies of Alouatta belzebul (Hill 1962).  Groves (2001, 2005) still considers A. ululata as a synonym of A. belzebul. Gregorin (2006) noted that the sexual dichromatism found in specimens from Ceará and Maranhão was not described for any individual of A. belzebul or A. discolor, and concluded that A. ululata should be considered as a distinct species. Here, we follow Gregorin (2006) in recognizing A. ululata as a distinct species. However, no genetic study has included specimens of this taxon and therefore its genetic distinctiveness from A. belzebul and A. discolor remains an open question (Cortés-Ortiz et al. 2015).

authority: Quah, Grismer, Wood, Thura, Oaks & Lin, 2019

taxonomicNotes: ‘Zirafy’ means ‘giraffe’ in Malagasy (Wood and Scharff 2018).
This species previously appeared on the IUCN Red List in the genus Tilapia but is now considered valid in the genus Heterotilapia (Eschmeyer et al. 2018).

Previously listed as Poeoptera femoralis (del Hoyo and Collar 2016) and before that as Cinnyricinclus femoralis. Also previously placed in genus Pholia. Treating Pholia and Arizelospar in Poeoptera following Lovette and Rubenstein (2007) would render Poeoptera a large entity with unusually deep branch lengths. We follow WGAC in resurrecting the monotypic genus Arizelospar Oberholser 1905 for this distinctive species. Monotypic.
Ceblepyris cinereus and C. cucullatus (del Hoyo and Collar 2016) were previously lumped as Coracina cinerea following Dowsett & Forbes-Watson (1993) and Sibley & Monroe (1990, 1993).
Treron australis and T griveaudi (del Hoyo and Collar 2014) were previously lumped as T. australis following Sibley and Monroe (1990, 1993).

The current taxonomy of spider monkeys (Ateles spp.) is based on Kellogg and Goldman’s (1944) seminal review and more recently, complemented by morphological and molecular analysis of Froehlich et al. (1991), Collins and Dubach (2000), Nieves et al. (2005) and Morales-Jimenez et al. (2015). The early classification of spider monkeys proposed by Kellogg and Goldman (1944) relied on the geographical distribution and several morphological characters. However, Ateles belzebuth comprised of several taxa characterized by the presence of a white patch on the forehead, including populations of white-bellied spider monkeys from northwestern Amazonia, the trans-Andean populations of Ateles hybridus, and the populations of spider monkeys from eastern Amazonia (Ateles marginatus). Spider monkeys without a whitish brow patch were also grouped together, including spider monkeys of the Guianas (Ateles paniscus) and spider monkeys from southwestern Amazonia (Ateles chamek). This initial taxonomic hypothesis was not parsimonious due to the geographical discordance between groups of spider monkeys with “similar” characters. In fact, Sampaio et al. (1993) had argued that the forms paniscus and chamek should be considered distinct species on the basis of the genetic distance between them.

The morphological and molecular studies conducted by Froehlich et al. (1991) and by Collins and Dubach (2000), respectively, brought up a new hypothesis that suggested that Ateles belzebuth was composed of three major Amazonian groups (Ateles b. belzebuth, Ateles b. chamek and Ateles b. marginatus), while Ateles paniscus was a sister group (and basal) to all other spider monkeys. Also, these studies provided evidence for an early divergence between Ateles hybridus in northern South America and Ateles belzebuth in the Amazon basin (Collins 1999; Collins and Dubach 2000). Later studies based on chromosomal and molecular markers supported the hypothesis that the belzebuth-chamek-marginatus group formed a monophyletic clade (Nieves et al. 2005). Since then there has been debate on the specific status of belzebuth, chamek and marginatus, and several authors have supported this new classification of Amazonian spider monkeys (e.g. Rylands et al. 2000). Recently, a new molecular study proposed that Ateles marginatus may well be the sister taxon to all other spider monkeys, and confirmed that Ateles belzebuth and Ateles chamek are indeed sister taxa that may have shared a common ancestor approximately 1.8 million years ago (Morales-Jimenez et al. 2015).

Traditionally, Ateles taxonomy is based on Kellogg and Goldman (1944) that recognize four species: Ateles paniscus (two subspecies, including Ateles paniscus chamek), A. belzebuth (3 subspecies), A. geoffroyi (nine subspecies) and A. fusciceps (two subspecies). Froehlich et al. (1991), Medeiros (1994) and Medeiros et al. (1997) argue that A. paniscus is a distinct species without subspecies. Boer and Bruijn (1990) indicated that A. chamek should be considered a full species, while Froehlich et al. (1991) and Collins (2008) classify the taxon as a subspecies of Ateles belzebuth (A. b. chamek). According to Collins (2008), genetic evidence shows that the morphological similarities that spider monkeys of Cocha Cashu National Park in Peru share with A. paniscus in Suriname and the Guiana Shield (black coat and pink face) are superficial and thus should be classified as Ateles belzebuth chamek. Rylands et al. (2000), Rylands and Mittermeier (2009) and Konstant and Rylands (2013) recognize A. chamek as a distinct species. According to van Roosmalen and van Roosmalen (2014) the species of spider monkey that occurs throughout the interfluve between the Rios Madeira, Mamoré, Guaporé and Tapajós, an area recognized for the occurrence of A. chamek, should be Ateles longimembris. In this region, Cruz Lima (1945) recognized this spider monkey as a subspecies of Ateles paniscus, while Groves (2001) considered A. longimembris as a synonym of A. chamek. Van Roosmalen and van Roosmalen (2014) report that they regard it as a distinct species of Ateles occurring between the Rios Purus and Madeira, south and west of the Rio Ipixuna (right bank tributary of the Rio Purus).

The taxonomy of spider monkeys (Ateles spp.) has been based on Kellogg and Goldman´s (1944) seminal study, and complemented by morphological and molecular studies more recently (Froehlich et al. 1991; Collins and Dubach 2000; Nieves et al. 2005; Morales-Jimenez et al. 2015b). The early studies of Kellogg and Goldman (1944) relied in the classification of spider monkeys based on the geographical distribution and several morphological characters. A. fusciceps robustus Allen, 1914 is considered a junior synonym of A. f. rufiventris Sclater, 1871 following Heltne and Kunkel (1975) (see Rylands et al. 2000). Silva-López et al. (1996) argued that Ateles geoffroyi pan was not a valid taxon. Since the earliest studies on the taxonomy of spider monkeys, A. fusciceps has been considered either within the geoffroyi clade or as a sister taxa to A. geoffroyi based on morphological characters (Froehlich et al. 1991) or molecular data (Collins and Dubach 2000).

Collins and Dubach (2000) argued that the forms fusciceps and rufiventris are subspecies of Ateles geoffroyi. The taxonomy and distributions of Ateles geoffroyi and Ateles fusciceps was reviewed by Rylands et al. (2006). Recent molecular data has suggested that the Mesoamerican spider monkeys (Ateles geoffroyi) and spider monkeys in north-western South America (Ateles fusciceps) shared a common ancestor nearly 2.2 Mya, and that both disjunct subspecies A. f. fusciceps and A. f. rufiventris shared a common ancestor nearly 1.3 Mya (Morales-Jimenez et al. 2015b). Mitochondrial DNA sequencing strongly supports Ateles geoffroyi and Ateles fusciceps being reciprocally monophyletic taxa (Morales-Jimenez et al. 2015b).

Ateles f. rufiventris Sclater, 1871, from the Río Atrato, Darien, Colombia, is listed as incertae sedis by Kellogg and Goldman (1944). Medeiros et al. (1997) indicated that, from the cytogenetic viewpoint, rufiventris Sclater, 1871, may well be reproductively isolated from A. geoffroyi.

The current taxonomy of Ateles geoffroyi is based on Kellogg and Goldman (1944), Rylands et al. 2006, and Morales-Jiménez et al. (2015). Originally, detail analyses of cranial morphology and pelage colouration by Kellogg and Goldman (1944) recognized nine distinct subspecies. Hill (1962), Hall (1981), and Konstant et al. (1985) retained all these subspecies, but morphological and genetic analyses over the past two decades challenged the validity of these taxa. For example, Silva-López et al. (1996) exemplified the enormous variation in pelage colouration of Ateles in Mexico, Guatemala and Belize and concluded that A. g. pan Schlegel, 1876 and A. g. yucatanensis Kellogg and Goldman, 1944 was within the variation of A. g. vellerosus. Napier (1976) also showed that the type specimen A. g. panamensis Kellogg and Goldman, 1944 falls within the variation of A. g. ornatus, Gray, 1870 and recommended considering the former a synonym of the latter. Based on phylogenetic analyses of small fragments of mitochondrial DNA genes, Collins and Dubach (2000) recognized a northern and a southern group of spider monkeys in Central America, but due to their fragmentary sampling could not properly evaluate subspecies distinctiveness. Using ~800 bp of the mtDNA control region, Morales-Jiménez et al. (2015) analysed the phylogenetic relationships of 49 spider monkeys sampled across Central America, and concluded that individuals from Mexico, Guatemala and Belize should be considered as part of A. g. vellerosus, while individuals from El Salvador should not be included in this taxon. They also found that individuals from southwestern Nicaragua and northwestern Costa Rica should be considered as A. g. frontatus and individuals from Azuero peninsula formed a monophyletic clade, supporting their recognition as distinct subspecies. However, despite recent efforts to improve our understanding of the phylogenetic relationships of the different subspecies of A. geoffroyi, conclusive results regarding the taxonomic validity of its subspecies still require analyses that include samples of individuals from southern Guatemala, Honduras, northern and eastern Nicaragua, central and western Panama, and eastern Costa Rica. Here, we tentatively recognized six subspecies: Ateles geoffroyi geoffroyi, Ateles geoffroyi azuerensis, Ateles geoffroyi frontatus, Ateles geoffroyi grisescens, Ateles geoffroyi ornatus, Ateles geoffroyi vellerosus.

The taxonomy and distributions of Ateles geoffroyi and Ateles fusciceps was reviewed by Rylands et al. (2006). Recent molecular data has suggested that the Mesoamerican spider monkeys (Ateles geoffroyi) and spider monkeys in north-western South America (Ateles fusciceps) shared a common ancestor nearly 2.2 Mya, and that both disjunct subspecies A. f. fusciceps and A. f. rufiventris shared a common ancestor nearly 1.3 Mya (Morales-Jimenez et al. 2015b). Mitochondrial DNA sequencing strongly supports Ateles geoffroyi and Ateles fusciceps being reciprocally monophyletic taxa (Morales-Jimenez et al. 2015b).

The taxonomy of the spider monkeys is based on Kellogg and Goldman (1944) and Hill (1962). The forms hybridus, chamek and marginatus are listed as distinct species and A. fusciceps robustus Allen, 1914 is considered a junior synonym of A. f. rufiventris Sclater, 1871 following Heltne and Kunkel (1975) (see Rylands et al. 2000).

Froehlich et al. (1991), and Medeiros (1994; Medeiros et al. 1997) argued that A. paniscus is a distinct form with no subspecies. De Boer and Bruijn (1990) indicated that chamek should be considered a full species, and likewise Sampaio et al. (1993) argued that the forms paniscus and chamek should be considered distinct species on the basis of the genetic distance between them. Ateles chamek was found to be closer to belzebuth than to paniscus (see also Nieves et al. 2005).

M.G.M. van Roosmalen reported (5 August 2003) an undescribed species of spider monkey within the supposed range of A. marginatus. It "occurs in the most south-eastern limit of the genus' distribution and inhabits the dry savanna forests and cerradão of northern Mato Grosso. Its western limit is the Rio Teles Pires and eastern limit is the Rio Culuene, a headwater tributary of the Rio Xingú. Its distribution is disjunct with that of Ateles marginatus along the lower and middle Rio Xingú, Pará, west as far as the Rio Tapajós and east as far as the left bank of the lower Rio Tocantins." It differs from A. marginatus by "the dark chestnut brown black fur, the pink instead of pitch-black muzzle and circumocular rings, and larger white whiskers and triangular blaze." According to van Roosmalen, it can be seen along the headwater effluents of the Rio Xingú, such as the Rios Culuene, Curisevo, Batoví, Ronuro, and Von den Steinen.

Vieira (1944) recognized two subspecies of Brachyteles. Recent evidence provided by Lemos de Sá et al. (1990), Fonseca et al. (1991) and Lemos de Sá and Glander (1993) indicated that Vieira’s original (1944) standing was valid, but that differentiation is even more extreme and justifies the classification of the two forms as separate species (see also Coimbra-Filho et al. 1993). Groves (2001, 2005) lists the two muriquis as separate species.

Vieira (1944) recognized two subspecies of Brachyteles. Recent evidence provided by Lemos de Sá et al. (1990), Fonseca et al. (1991) and Lemos de Sá and Glander (1993) indicated that Vieira’s original (1944) standing was valid, but that differentiation is even more extreme and justifies the classification of the two forms as separate species (see also Coimbra-Filho et al. 1993). Groves (2001, 2005) lists the two muriquis as separate species.

In the past, the eastern Brazilian marmosets (penicillata É. Geoffroy, 1812; geoffroyi É. Geoffroy in Humboldt, 1812; aurita É. Geoffroy in Humboldt, 1812; and flaviceps Thomas, 1903) of the ‘jacchus group’ were considered to be subspecies of Callithrix jacchus, following Hershkovitz (1977). All taxa were later elevated to full species level (Coimbra-Filho 1984, Mittermeier et al. 1988, Marroig et al. 2004, Coimbra-Filho et al. 2006).

Coimbra-Filho (1986a, b, 1990, 1991) has argued that the similarity in dental morphology (Natori 1986), behaviour, pelage (infants of the two forms are practically identical in appearance), vocalizations (Mendes 1997b,c) and the recent discovery of wild groups of hybrids between C. aurita and C. flaviceps at Carangola, Minas Gerais (Ferrari and Mendes 1991), suggest subspecific status (Coimbra-Filho et al. 1993). Ferrari et al. (1996) studied and reviewed the ecology and behaviour of C. aurita and C. flaviceps groups and concluded that although undoubtedly very similar, the “comparison appears to have done more to re-emphasize the enormous flexibility underlying the behavioural ecology of the marmosets as a whole than clarify the relationships between these two taxa in particular”. Here, Rylands’ (2012) proposed taxonomy is followed; it considers C. aurita and C. flaviceps as full species.

In the past, the eastern Brazilian marmosets (penicillata É. Geoffroy, 1812, geoffroyi É. Geoffroy in Humboldt, 1812, aurita É. Geoffroy in Humboldt, 1812, and flaviceps Thomas, 1903) of the “jacchus group” were considered to be subspecies of Callithrix jacchus, following Hershkovitz (1977). All are now considered to be full species (see Coimbra-Filho and Mittermeier 1973, Coimbra-Filho 1984, Mittermeier et al. 1988, Mendes 1997b, Marroig et al. 2004, Coimbra-Filho et al. 2006).

Coimbra-Filho (1986a,b, 1990) and Coimbra-Filho et al. (1993b) argued that Callithrix flaviceps should be considered a subspecies of C. aurita. The similarities between C. flaviceps and C. aurita in pelage patterns such as the ear tufts and the skull-like facial mask, ecological adaptations, ontogeny (the infants are extremely similar), vocalizations and clinal variation in overall pelage colour, based this opinion (Coimbra-Filho 1986a,b; Coimbra-Filho et al. 1993). However, Ferrari et al. (1996) rejected the subspecies status of C. flaviceps based on ecological and behavioural studies of groups of C. flaviceps and C. aurita. Rylands (2012) corroborates this view and considers C. flaviceps as a full species.

To date there have been only preliminary and non-conclusive molecular genetic assessments of Cebus aequatorialis (Boubli et al. 2012; Ruiz-García et al. 2010, 2018), and so its provisional status as a species is based on its geographic isolation from other Cebus albifrons populations and its unique morphological characteristics such as pelage colouration. Jack and Campos (2012) suggest elevating C. aequatorialis to species level following the phylogenetic species concept. Albuja et al. (2018) provide a preliminary cranio-dental and morphological analysis showing differences between Cebus aequatorialis and another Ecuadorian capuchin species, Cebus yuracus; however, their sample size is too small to be conclusive in terms of species distinction.

Described by Hershkovitz (1988, 1990) as a subspecies of Callicebus personatus. Kobayashi and Langguth (1999) argued that the titis of the Atlantic forest should be considered species rather than subspecies of C. personatus. They listed C. nigrifrons, C. personatus, C. melanochir and C. coimbrai besides C. barbarabrownae. According to Printes (2007), the name Callicebus barbarabrownae (Hershkovitz, 1990), may prove to be a junior synonym of Callicebus gigot (Spix, 1823), according to the principle of priority.

Hill (1960) listed three species of bearded saki: Chiropotes chiropotes, C. satanas and C. albinasa [sic]. Hershkovitz (1985) described a new species, utahickae, and classified satanas and utahickae as subspecies of C. chiropotes. Groves (2001, 2005) followed the taxonomy of Hershkovitz (1985). The three subspecies of Chiropotes satanas (satanas, chiropotes and utahicki) are here listed as full species following the recommendation of Silva-Júnior and Figueiredo (2002), Figueiredo et al. (2006), and Silva-Júnior et al.(2013). Bonvicino et al. (2003) resurrected the name Chiropotes israelita (Spix, 1823) for the bearded saki discovered by Boubli (2002) to the west of the Rio Branco, a tributary of the Rio Negro, Amazonas, Brazil. Genetic and pelage differences, notably a greenish brown (olive) dorsum as opposed to light golden brown as is typical of C. chiropotes, distinguish it from the bearded sakis of the Guianas to the east of the Rio Branco. There is however, a possible confusion concerning the correct names. Silva-Júnior and Figueiredo (2002) argued that the form west of the Rio Branco is, correctly Chiropotes chiropotes (Humboldt, 1811), according to its type locality, and the form to the east of the Rio Branco, extending through the states of Pará and Amapá, and into the Guianas (Guyana, Suriname, French Guiana) is C. sagulatus (Traill, 1821) from Demerara (see Silva-Júnior et al. (2013). Doubts still remain, however, concerning Hershkovitz’s interpretation of Humboldt’s type locality of C. chiropotes, which would accordingly alter the nomenclature (J. P. Boubli, pers. comm). With the further research and phylogenetic analysis, both Traill’s sagulatus and Spix’s israelita may be valid species names for different populations of this wide ranging genus. Barnett et al. (2012) proposed the black cuxiú for the common name.

Cebus kaapori was originally described by Queiroz in 1992, as who pointed out its resemblance with Cebus olivaceus. It had been previously recorded from Pará and Maranhão (Goeldi and Hagmann 1906 apud Silva Jr. 2001, Vieira 1957) as Cebus nigrivittatus and overlooked in subsequent assessments of the range of the species. Harada and Ferrari (1996), based on genetic data, argued that Cebus kaapori should be considered as a subspecies of C. olivaceus. However, Cebus kaapori was considered a valid species by Groves (2001, 2005) and Silva Jr. (2001) in regard of morphological evidence. Later on, new molecular evidence (Alfaro et al. 2012a,b) supported the current understanding that it is a valid species, and this is the current prevailing systematic arrangement (Paglia et al. 2012).

Previously listed as Amazilia lilliae (Handbook of the Birds of the World and BirdLife International 2020) and before that as Lepidopyga lillae (del Hoyo and Collar 2014), but moved to current genus following a recent overhaul of all genera by NACC, based on McGuire et al. (2014), Stiles et al. (2017a, b) and others. Status uncertain: sometimes considered a subspecies, morph or age stage of C. coeruleogularis. Monotypic.

The most detailed taxonomic treatment for this species remains that of Fooden (1963) who placed it in the genus Lagothrix. However, as a result of his comparative studies of cranial morphology in the atelines, Groves (2001, 2005) and Paredes (2003) concluded that the Peruvian Yellow-tailed Woolly Monkey should properly be considered a monotypic genus, Oreonax Thomas, 1927, quite separate from the woolly monkeys. More recent genetic studies by Di Fiore et al. (2014) and Ruiz Garcia et al. (2014) provide support for its position within the genus Lagothrix.

The lion tamarins, Leontopithecus, are listed as separate species following Della Serra (1951), Rosenberger and Coimbra-Filho (1984), Mittermeier et al. (1988), Natori (1989), and Rylands et al. (1993). They have been listed as subspecies of L. rosalia by Coimbra-Filho and Mittermeier (1972, 1973), Hershkovitz (1977), Mittermeier and Coimbra-Filho (1981), Forman et al. (1986) and Seuánez et al. (1988), the latter two publications on the basis of identical chromosome morphologies.

The lion tamarins, Leontopithecus, are listed as separate species following Della Serra (1951), Rosenberger and Coimbra-Filho (1984), Mittermeier et al. (1988), Natori (1989), and Rylands et al. (1993). They have been listed as subspecies of L. rosalia by Coimbra-Filho and Mittermeier (1972, 1973), Hershkovitz (1977), Mittermeier and Coimbra-Filho (1981), Forman et al. (1986) and Seuánez et al. (1988), the latter two publications on the basis of identical chromosome morphologies.

Orr and Hämäläinen (2007: 66) note that two forms of this species exist, differing in size, and that "Further field studies are required to clarify the biological status of these two possibly seasonal forms."

Byrne et al. (2016) split the genus Callicebus into three new genera, namely:  Cheracebus (torquatus-group sensu Roosmalen et al. 2002), Plecturocebus (cupreus-, moloch-, donacophilus-groups sensu Roosmalen et al. (2002) and Callicebus (personatus-group, sensu Roosmalen et al. 2002). According to Byrne et al. (2016), Plecturocebus includes 22 species.

Hershkovitz (1977) considered Saguinus geoffroyi to be a subspecies of S. oedipus. Comparative morphological studies by Hanihara and Natori (1987), Moore and Cheverud (1992) and Skinner (1991) argued for them being separate species. Eisenberg (1989), Rylands (1993), Hernández-Camacho and Cooper (1976), Mittermeier and Coimbra-Filho (1981), Hernández-Camacho and Defler (1989), Mittermeier et al. (1988), Rylands et al. (1993), Groves (1993, 2001, 2005), Mast et al. (1993) and Defler (1994) all list S. geoffroyi and S. oedipus as separate species.

Hershkovitz (1977) considered Saguinus bicolor to have three subspecies: the nominate, S. b. ochraceus and S. b. martinsi. Groves (2001, 2005) and Rylands et al. (1993, 2000) listed the forms ochraceus and martinsi as subspecies of S. martinsi.

Silva Jr (2001, 2002) argued that the tufted capuchins and the untufted capuchins (sensu Hershkovitz 1949, 1955) are so distinct in their morphology that they should be considered separate genera. Cebus Erxleben, 1777 for the untufted group, and Sapajus Kerr, 1792 is the name available for the tufted capuchins.

With a unique karyotype (Seuánez et al. 1986) and evidence for relatively early divergence from other Sapajus species (Lynch Alfaro et al. 2012; Nascimento et al. 2015), Sapajus xanthosternos is considered a distinct species based on morphological characteristics by both Groves (2001, 2005) and Silva Jr. (2001).

Silva Jr (2001, 2002) argued that the tufted capuchins and the un-tufted capuchins (sensu Hershkovitz 1949, 1955) are so distinct in their morphology that they should be considered separate genera: Cebus Erxleben, 1777 for the un-tufted group; and Sapajus Kerr, 1792 for the tufted capuchins.

Taxonomy of the tufted capuchins (sensu Hershkovitz 1949, 1955) follows Silva Jr (2001), who did not recognise any subspecific forms. Groves (2001, 2005) presented an alternative taxonomy for the tufted capuchins as follows: C. apella apella Linnaeus, 1758; C. apella fatuellus Linnaeus, 1766; C. apella macrocephalus Spix, 1823; C. apella peruanus Thomas, 1901; C. apella tocantinus Lönnberg, 1939; C. apella margaritae Hollister, 1914; C. libidinosus libidinosus Spix, 1823; C. libidinosus pallidus Gray, 1866; C. libidinosus paraguayanus Fischer, 1829; C. libidinosus juruanus Lönnberg, 1939; C. nigritus nigritus Goldfuss, 1809; C. nigritus robustus Kuhl, 1820; C. nigritus cucullatus Spix, 1823; C. xanthosternos Wied-Neuwied, 1826 (see Fragaszy et al. 2004; Rylands et al. 2005).

Groves (2001) listed three subspecific forms for C. nigritus: C. nigritus nigritus Goldfuss, 1809; C. nigritus robustus Kuhl, 1820, considered a distinct species by Silva Jr. (2001); and C. nigritus cucullatus Spix, 1823, considered a junior synonym of C. nigritus by Silva Jr. (2001).

The species was transferred to Sapajus (Lynch Alfaro et al. 2012).

Georg Marcgrave (1648) described a capuchin monkey from the northeastern Atlantic Forest of Brazil as "caitaia". This monkey had bright yellow long fur, a rounded head and a not prominent forehead. At least three names have been used for this primate species: Simia flavia Schreber 1774, Cebus flavus Geoffroy 1812 and Cebus apella libidinosus (Spix 1823). Until recently, specimens of Simia flavia did not exist in scientific collections. For this reason the animal depicted in plate 31-b of Schreber (1774) had never been properly identified. A specimen collected in the state of Pernambuco, Brazil was considered the neotype of Simia flavia Schreber, 1774 and was named Cebus flavius (Schreber, 1774) (Oliveira and Langguth 2006). There is a junior synonym: Cebus queirozi (Pontes et al. 2006).

Two former subspecies, H.s. hungarica and H.s. austriaca and two synonyms (Helicopsis lunulata (Krynicki, 1833) and Xerocrassa rhabdota Sturany, 1901) have now been promoted to species level (Duda et al. 2018, Balashov et al. 2020).

authority: Siler, Linkem, Cobb, Watters, Cummings, Diesmos & Brown, 2014

taxonomicNotes:
Honda et al. (2014) revised Japanese members of Goniurosaurus, previously considered either the full species G. kuroiwae, G. toyamai, G. yamashinae, G. orientalis and G. splendens, or a single taxon G. kuroiwae with five subspecies. These authors' results supported the recognition of two full species, G. splendens and G. kuroiwae, and they proposed treating G. orientalis, G. toyamai and G. yamashinae as subspecies of G. kuroiwae.

Further molecular research into this species complex is ongoing, and has confirmed that there are at least five species-level clades in the Ryukyus, corresponding to subspecies of G. kuroiwae recognized by Honda et al. (2014) plus G. splendens (H. Ota pers. comm. 2016). As a revision of this group is due to be submitted for publication shortly, this account adopts a traditional five-species treatment of this complex, while recognizing that both G. kuroiwae and G. orientalis as presently recognized may be further subdivided in the near future.
Thryophilus nicefori (del Hoyo and Collar 2016) was previously listed as Thryothorus nicefori.
Zapornia olivieri (del Hoyo and Collar 2014) was previously placed in the genus Amaurornis.

This species is sometimes placed within the genus Parias. It was formerly recognized as a subspecies of Trimeresurus flavomaculatus. Genetic work is required to clarify whether it is a valid species, and it may well prove not to be distinct (R. Brown pers. comm. 2022).
There is very little difference between the two subspecies, and indeed Nasalis larvatus orientalis is not recognized by all authorities (M. Richardson pers. comm).

This species is considered distinct from Philautus nanus following Manamendra-Arachchi and Pethiyagoda (2005), and from Pseudophilautus sordidus following Ellepola et al. (2021). However, further taxonomic studies on this species are required.

authority: Vigle, Coloma, Santos, Hernandez-Nieto, Ortega-Andrade, Paluh & Read, 2020
Townsend, Herrera-B., Hofmann, Luque-Montes, Ross, Dudek, Krygeris, Duchamp & Wilson, 2020
taxonomicNotes: This is a split from the broader concept of Atlantihyla spinipollex (Townsend et al. 2020).

The use of this name has been clarified by McCranie and Wilson (1993). This species was moved from Ptychohyla to Atlantihyla (Faivovich et al. 2018). This is a restricted concept of Atlantihyla spinipollex following the split of the broader concept into this and A. melissa (Townsend et al. 2020).
Centrolene bacatum (Wild 1994) and C. guanacarum (Ruiz-Carranza and Lynch 1995) have been placed in synonymy under C. sanchezi (Ruiz-Carranza and Lynch 1991) by Guayasamín et al. (2020).

The taxonomy of untufted capuchin monkeys (Cebus spp.) has been recently debated and although there is a better understanding of its phylogeny, some phylogenetic and biogeographical aspects of this genus remain unresolved (Ruiz-Garcia et al. 2010, Boubli et al. 2012, Lynch et al. 2012). The varied white-fronted capuchin (Cebus versicolor) currently comprises all populations of untufted capuchins that occur in northern Colombia, east of the Magdalena River and in the middle Magdalena River valley, excluding those populations east of the Eastern Andes mountains (Cebus leucocephalus). Cebus cesarae comprises populations in northern Colombia (i.e. in the northern parts of the Magdalena and Cesar departments, and southwestern part of La Guajira Department), except for the northern Colombian coast, where it is replaced by Cebus malitiosus. The geographic delimitation between the Varied White-fronted Capuchin (C. versicolor) and the Rio Cesar White fronted Capuchin (C. cesarae) is not well understood, and further research is needed in this region.

Genetic analyses by Boubli et al. (2012) and Lima et al. (2017) have indicated that the following forms of white-fronted capuchins that were recognized as subspecies of Cebus albifrons by Hershkovitz (1949) should be considered distinct species: albifrons, yuracus, unicolor, cuscinus, cesarae, malitiosus, leucocephalus, aequatorialis, and versicolor

The extremely complex nomenclatural history of this species - resulting from a type series consisting of two species, the lack of an unambiguously identified holotype, and a long history of inconsistent usages of the names Ogyris halmaturia and O. waterhouseri - was discussed iin detail by Braby and Douglas (2008), who referred O. halmaturia to the synonymy of O. otanes. 

Braby et al. (2011) subsequently reviewed the evidence presented in favour of multiple proposed treatments and upheld the lectotype designation by Tindale (1923). The consequence of this action is that O. halmaturia represents a valid species, of which O. waterhouseri is a junior synonym following Grund (2010), and this treatment is followed here.

Two Australian Museum specimens from the area north of Mount Hyland, within the distribution of the similar Diorypogyx niger, appear to represent D. duplodentatus. Further taxonomic research is required to clarify their assignment, and it is possible they represent an undescribed species (C. Reid pers. comm. 2021). These records are excluded from this assessment pending resolution of this issue.
Harvey et al. (2015) found that Moggridgea tingle was a complex of two species, one of which they described as the new species Bertmainius monachus.

While it has been suggested that this species likely hybridizes with the Red-shanked Douc Langur, Pygathrix nemaeus, where their distributions meet in Viet Nam and Cambodia this has yet to be confirmed by genetic analysis.

This species is here considered monotypic (Groves 2001).

The different records of Trachypithecus hatinhensis across its range show wide variation in morphology in terms of the pattern of white hairs on the head (especially so in Lao PDR). Published description of the different species included in the François’s-group vary (the morphologically closest being T. francoisi, T. hatinhensis, [T. ebenus], and T. laotum). It has been suggested that [T. ebenus] was a distinct species from T. hatinhensis (Brandon-Jones, 1995) but has also been considered to be a melanistic variant of T. hatinhensis (Nadler 2009). The taxonomic position of [T. ebenus] remains unresolved due to lack of evidence from DNA analysis.

The two named forms living closest by are T. hatinhensis and [T. ebenus] (see Timmins and Khounboline 1996; Walston and Vinton 1999; Nadler et al. 2003). The former is conventionally seen as having a bold white facial stripe including moustache and white marks on the nape, the latter an all-black head (e.g. Brandon-Jones 1995, Francis 2008). Moreover, Nadler et al. (2003) stated that T. (f.) hatinhensis shows a white moustache but T. (f.) francoisi, otherwise similar in head pattern, lacks one.
Each of the three river catchments in which this species occurs holds a genetically distinct subpopulation (J. Mynott pers. comm. 2021).

Differences in morphological, ecological and behavioural traits suggest that the current five-species arrangement does not accurately reflect species diversity within Kosciuscola (Tatarnic et al. 2013, Umbers et al. 2021). Molecular work has identified species-level clades within K. cuneatus, with strong evidence that K. cuneatus proper is restricted to the Australian Capital Territory and northern New South Wales mountains (Umbers et al. 2021) and the remaining clades within K. cuneatus being undescribed species. 

The recent emergence of major threats from climate change and bushfire, combined with the narrow ranges of these taxa as now understood, is here considered sufficient to justify assessing the reduced species concept of K. cuneatus pending formal publication of the forthcoming taxonomic revision, and to assess the undescribed congeners.

scientificName: Kosciuscola sp. nov. 1 'K. tristis Bogong Clade'
authority: K. Umbers et al., in prep.
taxonomicNotes:
Differences in morphological, ecological and behavioural traits suggest that the current five-species arrangement does not accurately reflect species diversity within Kosciuscola (Tatarnic et al. 2013, Umbers et al. 2021). Molecular work has identified species-level clades within K. tristis.

The recent emergence of major threats from climate change and bushfire, combined with the narrow ranges of these taxa as now understood, is here considered sufficient to justify assessing the reduced species concept of K. tristis pending formal publication of the forthcoming taxonomic revision, and to assess the undescribed congeners.

scientificName: Kosciuscola sp. nov. 3 'K. cuneatus Sth NSW Clade'
speciesName: sp. nov. 3 'K. cuneatus Sth NSW Clade'
authority: K. Umbers et al., in prep.
taxonomicNotes:
Differences in morphological, ecological and behavioural traits suggest that the current five-species arrangement does not accurately reflect species diversity within Kosciuscola (Tatarnic et al. 2013, Umbers et al. 2021). Molecular work has identified species-level clades within K. cuneatus, with strong evidence that K. cuneatus proper is restricted to the Australian Capital Territory and northern New South Wales mountains (Umbers et al. 2021) and the remaining clades within K. cuneatus are undescribed species.

The recent emergence of major threats from climate change and bushfire, combined with the narrow ranges of these taxa as now understood, is here considered sufficient to justify assessing the reduced species concept of K. cuneatus pending formal publication of the forthcoming taxonomic revision, and to assess the undescribed congeners.

authority: K. Umbers et al., in prep.

Molecular work has identified species-level clades within K. cuneatus, with strong evidence that K. cuneatus proper is restricted to the Australian Capital Territory and northern New South Wales mountains (Umbers et al. 2021) and the remaining clades within K. cuneatus are undescribed species.

The recent emergence of major threats from climate change and bushfire, combined with the narrow ranges of these taxa as now understood, is here considered sufficient to justify assessing the reduced species concept of K. cuneatus pending formal publication of the forthcoming taxonomic revision, and to assess the undescribed congeners.

Molecular work has identified species-level clades within K. cuneatus, with strong evidence that K. cuneatus proper is restricted to the Australian Capital Territory and northern New South Wales mountains (Umbers et al. 2021) and the remaining clades within K. cuneatus are undescribed species.

The recent emergence of major threats from climate change and bushfire, combined with the narrow ranges of these taxa as now understood, is here considered sufficient to justify assessing the reduced species concept of K. cuneatus pending formal publication of the forthcoming taxonomic revision, and to assess the undescribed congeners.

authority: Roos, Helgen, Miguez, Thant, Lwin, A.K. Lin, A. Lin, Yi, Soe, Hein, Myint, Ahmed, Chetry, Urh, Veatch, Duncan, Kamminga, Chua, Yao, Matauschek, Meyer, Liu, Li, Nadler, Fan, Quyet, Hofreiter, Zinner & Momberg, 2020
Schüßler, Blanco, Salmona, Poelstra, Andriambeloson, Miller, A., Randrianambinina, Rasolofoson, Mantilla-Contreras, Chikhi, Louis Jr., Yoder, & Radespiel, 2020
Louis Jr., Engberg, Lei, Geng, Sommer, Randriamampionona, Randriamanana, Zaonarivelo, Andriantompohavana, Randria, Prosper, Ramaromilanto, Rakotoarisoa, Rooney & Brenneman, 2006
taxonomicNotes:
The original Latin species name (ahmansoni) stems from the naming of the species after a man, Robert Ahmanson. The species has occasionally been incorrectly referred to as ahmansonorum (e.g. Hoffmann et al. 2009) and ahmansorum but the species should be referred to using the original Latin name.

Tattersall (2007) has cautioned that the Lepilemur species reported by Louis et al. 2006 (that were diagnosed exclusively on genetic distance and all allopatric) be treated with extreme caution until supporting data are forthcoming. However, Lei et al. (2016) subsequently established through delimitation analyses of mitogenomic data, that this species is valid despite allopatry.

The three presently recognized subspecies (roxellana, hubeiensis, qinlingensis) differ from one another mainly in tail length, as well as in certain features of skeletal structure and width of dental arch (Groves 2001). No genetic separation into distinct clades and therefore the sub-specific designation.

There are several localities in Mozambique (Mt. Namuli, Mt. Mabu) which were originally suspected to contain populations of Rhampholeon chapmanorum (Tilbury 2010). However, molecular phylogenetic analysis confirms that R. chapmanorum is endemic to remaining forest fragments on Malawi Hill, and the Mozambican localities are home to separate species of Rhampholeon (Branch et al. 2014).
No subspecies or geographic variants have been described.

Recent genetic studies show that this species is part of the Bradypodion melanocephalum species complex. Based on mitochondrial DNA markers, there is low but diagnosable differentiation between these species (da Silva and Tolley 2017).
Although there are no notable taxonomic issues, the range of Lygodactylus methueni may overlap with that of L. nigropunctatus, and these two species can be difficult to distinguish from each other.

Although there appears to be genetic divergence between this species and the morphologically similar Scelotes montispectus (Bauer et al. 2003), further investigation of the species boundaries between these taxa and related forms is required. A molecular phylogeny (Heideman et al. 2011) suggested that the subpopulation of S. kasneri at Elands Bay may represent a distinct species, requiring a taxonomic revision.
No taxonomic issues.
Genetic studies show that subpopulations from all three forests (Dlinza, Ntumeni and Ngoya) belong to this taxon (Tilbury and Tolley 2009).

The closely related subpopulation at Mkambati in the Eastern Cape Province appears to be a grassland ecomorph of this otherwise forest-dwelling species (K.A. Tolley unpubl. data 2019), but more research is needed to assess the taxonomic status of this population.

Raw (2022) argued that the species name should be caffrum rather than caffer as it is a neuter adjective, and this usage has since been adopted by the Reptile Database (Uetz et al. 2025).
No outstanding taxonomic issues.

Recent phylogenetic analyses suggest that Bitis albanica, B. armata, B. inornata and B. rubida are within a closely related species complex (Barlow et al. 2019) and the relationships between these taxa are worthy of further investigation.

Although there are no notable taxonomic issues (Tolley et al. 2004, 2006; Tolley and Burger 2007), this species is sometimes confused with Bradypodion caffer, which occurs further north along the coastline.

authority: Guayasamin, Cisneros-Heredia, Maynard, Lynch, Culebras & Hamilton, 2017

taxonomicNotes: Further research is needed to determine whether this species is identical to the Japanese species, Inversiunio yanagawensis (Sayenko et al. 2020).

The genus Pycnopodia is monophyletic, and Pycnopodia helianthoides is the only species in the genus. Pycnopodia helianthoides is one of only two species in the sub-family Pycnopodiinae, the other is Lysastrosoma anthosticta from Japan (Accessed through: WoRMS, World Register of Marine Species at: http://www.marinespecies.org/aphia.php?p=taxdetails&id=240764 on 2020-08-04).

This species belongs to the silenus species group, and was formerly known as Macaca maurus. It hybridizes with M. tonkeana in areas where their ranges overlap at the base of the Toraja Highlands (Tempe depression).
We depart from the taxonomy on Amphibians of the World, and consider Pseudophilautus silvaticus to have been synonymised with this species as in Ellepola et al. (2021).
We depart from the taxonomy on Amphibians of the World, and consider Pseudophilautus silus to have been synonymised with this species as in Ellepola et al. (2021).
This is a broader concept of Psudophilatus hankeni that now includes all the records from P. dilmah, as these species have been synonymized (Ellepola et al. 2021).

authority: Firneno, Itgen, Jacobs, Mcdaniels, Luque-Montes, Wilson & Townsend, 2021
taxonomicNotes: This is a split from the broader concept of Hypopachus barberi based on molecular and morphological differences (Firneno Jr. et al. 2021).

This species is assigned to Dendropsophus minuta species group based on its phylogenetic position, and characterized by its combination of the molecular synapomorphies, and some morphological characteristics (Faivovich et al. 2005). It is distinguished from other species of the genus by its noticeably smaller size (2.26 cm), homogeneously dotted back, presence of interdigital palms, smooth dorsal skin, and cloaca with fleshy fold, among others. A survey conducted by the Ministry of Ecosocialism in 2011 reports D. minutus, D. minusculus, D. microcephalus and D. luteoocellatus for the locality (González-Fernández and Nieves 2011), species that noticeably differ from the description of the holotype. However, updated data on taxonomic and genetic traits are still necessary.
This species was elevated to species status by La Marca (1983).

The nomenclatural history of this species was detailed by Lötters et al. (1998) and Lötters and La Marca (2001). This species was redescribed by Lötters, La Marca and Vences (2004) after the discovery that the original type material represented Atelopus varius of Central America. The former junior synonym Atelopus vogli is considered to be a distinct species following (Lötters et al. 2004)
In the Centrolene prosoblepon group according to Ruíz-Carranza and Lynch (1991), and Cisneros-Heredia and McDiarmid (2006).
Based on a molecular phylogeny, Guayasamin et al. (2009) reallocated this species in the genus Vitreorana.

Based on a molecular phylogeny, Guayasamin et al. (2009) described the new genus Celsiella to reallocate two endemic species – C. revocata and C. vozmedianoi - from the northern mountain system in Venezuela.
This species had been included in phylogenetic studies by Guayasamin et al. (2008, 2009), and Castroviejo-Fisher et al. (2014).

In the genus Hyalinobatrachium based on morphological and behavioral characteristics (Señaris and Ayarzagüena 2005, Guayasamin et al. 2009). Until now this species has not been included in any phylogenetic study.

Originally assigned to Prostherapis by Rivero (1961) and later transferred to Colostethus by Edwards (1971). Grant et al. (2006) did not include this species in their phylogenetic hypothesis, however, agree with La Marca (2004) in that its phylogenetic relationships remain enigmatic, and refer it as insertae sedis within Aromobatidae.
Misidentified specimens of Allobates humilis reported by Barrio-Amorós and García Porta (2003) from Táchira State actually represent A. algorei (Barrio-Amorós and Santos 2009).

Originally described as a member of Phyllobates (Schmidt 1932) but subsequently transferred to Prostherapis (Rivero 1961), Colostethus (Edwards 1971), and finally to Allobates (Grant et al. 2006) where is currently allocated (Frost 2020). Although the last change was made in the contexts of an exhaustive phylogenetic study (Grant et al. 2006) this species was not included in the analyses and its reallocation was only based on its overall morphological similarity with other Allobates. Therefore, its phylogenetic relationships remain unknown and its generic position should be considered provisional and pending corroboration.

Barrio-Amorós and Santos (2012) provide new information for this species and suggest that the subpopulation in Colombia referred as Aromobates saltuensis by Grant et al. (2006) is an unnamed species most closely related to Aromobates cannatellai. Also, these authors have doubts about the Colombian references of Anganoy-Criollo (2012) of A. cf. saltuensis.

This species is recovered in various places throughout the dendrobatine phylogeny, either as sister to the genus Adelphobates or to all other dendrobatines aside from Phyllobates and C. ruthveni (see Frost 2020 for a complete review of the systematic of this taxon). Currently, Minyobates is a monotypic genus, but additional phylogenetic research is needed (La Marca 2016).
It has not undergone taxonomic changes. Its phylogenetic position, as part of Mannophryne, has been corroborated by Manzanilla et al. (2009), Grant et al. (2017), and Rojas-Runjaic (2019).

Originally described as Hyloxalus collaris by Boulenger (1912) (misspelled Hylixalus). Successively moved to Prostherapis and Colostetus by Rivero (1961) and Edwards (1971), respectively, and finally allocated in Mannophryne by La Marca (1992), where still remains. Its taxonomic position has been corroborated in two recent phylogenetic studies (Grant et al. 2017, Rojas-Runjaic 2019).
It has not undergone taxonomic changes. Its phylogenetic position, as part of Mannophryne, has been corroborated by Manzanilla et al. (2009), Grant et al. (2017), and Rojas-Runjaic (2019).

Originally described as Prostherapis neblina by Test (1956) and subsequently transferred to Colostethus by Edwards (1971) and to Mannophryne by La Marca (1992). Its phylogenetic position, as part of Mannophryne, has been recently corroborated by Rojas-Runjaic (2019).
Mannophryne larandina is suspected to be a junior synonym of this species (Manzanilla et al. 2009, Barrio-Amorós et al. 2019, J. Manzanilla pers. comm. 2020).

Previous to Grant et al. (2006) the genus Aromobates was known only by a single species: A. nocturnus (Myers et al. 1991). Then, Grant et al. (2006) moved all Colostethus of the alboguttatus group (sensu Rivero 1988) or Nephelobates (sensu La Marca 1994) to Aromobates and provided a well-corroborated phylogeny of the genus. Barrio-Amorós and Santos (2012) provide a phylogeny and a list with current threats for all species of the genus. Rivero (1982a) included Colostehus inflexus Rivero, 1978 in the synonymy of C. alboguttatus. Myers, Paolillo and Daly (1991) and La Marca (1997) revive the species as Nephelobates inflexus in their works without explanation. Recently, Barrio-Amorós and Santos (2012) consider inflexus different from A. alboguttatus and a valid species, but without supporting evidence.

Originally described under the genus Colostethus by Rivero (1980). It was transferred to Nephelobates by La Marca (1994) and most recently reallocated to Aromobates by Grant et al. (2006). Its phylogenetic position (as part of Aromobates) has been corroborated by Rojas-Runjaic (2019).
