Originally described under the genus Colostethus by Dole and Durant (1972). It was transferred to Nephelobates by La Marca (1994) and lastly reallocated to Aromobates by Grant et al. (2006). Its phylogenetic position (as part of Aromobates) has been corroborated by Barrio-Amorós and Santos (2012), Grant et al. (2017), and Rojas-Runjaic (2019).

Barrio-Amorós and Santos (2012) suggested that the type material of Aromobates orostoma requires additional examination to confirm its status, and its relationship with A. inflexus should be evaluated.

Originally described as a subspecies of Hyla vilsoniana by Rivero (1961) and later elevated to the species level by Duellman (1989), who also assigned it to the labialis group. It was moved to the genus Dendropsophus by Faivovich et al. (2005) on the basis of molecular phylogenetic evidence. According to Guarnizo et al. (2012), D. pelidnus could be a junior synonym of this species but it is still considered valid for this assessment.

Originally described under the genus Hyla by Mijares-Urrutia and Rivero (2000). It was transferred to Dendropsophus by Faivovich et al. (2005) based only on morphological similarity and remained unassigned to any species group. Barrio-Amorós et al. (2019) assigned it to the Dendropsophus parviceps species group, but also on the basis of its overall similarity with the other species in that group. Its phylogenetic relationships remain unknown.

This species was transferred to Agalychnis by Faivovich et al. (2010), but was not assigned to any species group. Until now this leaf frog is still missing in phylogenetic analyses and its evolutionary relationships are unknown.

In the Pristimantis (Pristimantis) unistrigatus species group by Hedges et al. (2008). This species has not been included in any phylogenetic study, and has not been assigned to any species group in the last molecular analysis of the New World direct-developing frogs of Padial et al. (2014). In the family Strabomantidae sensu Heinicke et al. (2018).

La Marca (2007) erected the genus Mucubatrachus to accommodate species that have a dermal fold posterior to the urostyle and before the cloaca. Hedges et al. (2008), however, subsequently placed Mucubatrachus in the synonymy of Pristimantis. Further genetic studies are needed to determine the degree of differentiation of this group of species which share a morphological synapomorphy (E. La Marca pers. comm. 2020).

La Marca (2007) recently erected the genus Mucubatrachus to accommodate species that have a dermal fold posterior to the urostyle and before the cloaca. Hedges et al. (2008), however, subsequently placed Mucubatrachus in the synonymy of Pristimantis.

Originally described as a member of Eleutherodactylus (Walker and Test 1955) and later transferred to Pristimantis (Heinicke et al. 2007). Hedges et al. (2008) and Padial et al. (2014) also listed this species as part of Pristimantis but did not assign it to any species group as it was not included in their phylogenetic analyses. Its evolutionary relationships remain unknown.

This species is assigned to the unistrigatus species group by Hedges et al. (2008), based on its morphological characteristics, although it has not been assigned to any species group by Padial et al. (2014).

There are two available descriptions for this frog: Eleutherodactylus vanadise by La Marca (1984) and that of its synonym Eleutherodactylus cerasoventris (Rivero 1984 “1982”). Even though these descriptions were based on 29 and 36 specimens respectively, the variation in nature was not fully represented. Barrio-Amorós et al. (2013) reported four chromotypes, based on 98 specimens. In the Pristimantis unistrigatus species group according to the original publication (La Marca 1984) and Hedges et al. (2008); not assigned to any species group by Padial et al. (2014). In the Family Strabomantidae by Heinicke et al. (2018).

This species is assigned to Leptodactylus melanonotus species group based on its phylogenetic position, and characterized by its combination of the molecular synapomorphies, and some morphological characteristics (de Sá et al. 2014). This species is known only from the Cerro Socopó region in Venezuela. There are no other described species having toe fringes and lacking dorsolateral folds in the area where the Venezuelan states of Falcón, Lara and Zulia converge (de Sá et al. 2014). However, updated data on taxonomic and genetic traits are still necessary, considering several populations in Venezuela and Colombia (Magdalena and Maracaibo basin) could not be assigned to diagnosable species (Heyer 1994).

Schargel et al. (2002) mentioned that this species is morphologically more similar to B. adspersa, B. borburata, B. savagei, B. taylori, and B. vallecula than the geographically close species, B. orestes. Apparently, such difference is also applicable to the most recently described species B. mucuyensis, which is the sister species of B. orestes (García-Gutiérrez et al. 2013). This species was tentatively assigned to the B. adspersa group by Schargel et al. (2002). Parra-Olea et al. (2004) placed B. guaramacalensis in the B. (Eladinea) adspersa group based on its South American distribution.

This species was originally described in the genus Mucubatrachus by La Marca (2007). Hedges et al. (2008), however, placed Mucubatrachus in the synonymy of Pristimantis. Further genetic studies are needed to determine the degree of differentiation of this group of species which share a morphological synapomorphy (E. La Marca pers. comm. 2020).

La Marca (2007) erected the genus Mucubatrachus to accommodate species that have a dermal fold posterior to the urostyle and before the cloaca. Hedges et al. (2008), however, subsequently placed Mucubatrachus in the synonymy of Pristimantis. Pristimantis thyellus can be distinguished from other similar species by a combination of morphological characters and colour patterns (La Marca 2007).

This species was originally described as Colostethus caribe, but a year later was moved to the genus Allobates based on its morphological characteristics (Lötters et al. 2007).

Lötters et al. (2007: 60) in an apparent transcription mistake made reference to this species as Anomaloglossus caribe.
It has not undergone taxonomic changes. Its phylogenetic position within Mannophryne has been corroborated by Rojas-Runjaic (2019).

Originally described by Rivero (1968) as Centrolenella orocostale. Cannatella and Lamar (1986) considered this species as a junior synonym of Centrolenella orientalis, and later Ruíz-Carranza and Lynch (1991) placed it in the genus Hyalinobatrachium. Based on phylogenetic analysis and new natural history data, Castroviejo-Fisher et al. (2009) removed this species from the synonymy of Hyalinobatrachium orientalis. More recently Jowers et al. (2014) reanalyzed the phylogenetic relationships of this species and its closely related sister species Hyalinobatrachium fragile, and H. orientale (from Venezuela mainland, and Trinidad and Tobago).

Fermin et al. (2012) synonymized Bolitoglossa spongai (described by Barrio-Amorós and Fuentes-Ramos in 1999), also from Sierra La Culata, under B. orestes based on molecular evidence (16S, cytb and cox genes sequence analysis) and morphological data. Specimens from Sierra Nevada de Mérida, previously considered as B. orestes, are actually specimens of its sister species, B. mucuyensis (García-Gutiérrez et al. 2013). Bolitoglossa orestes was placed in the B. (Eladinea) adspersa group by Parra-Olea et al. (2004).

It has not undergone taxonomic changes. Its phylogenetic position was inferred from molecular data by Barrio-Amorós and Santos (2012) and then corroborated in the total evidence analyses of Grant et al. (2017) and Rojas-Runjaic (2019).

It has not undergone taxonomic changes. Its phylogenetic position was inferred from molecular data by Barrio-Amorós et al. (2011) and then corroborated by Barrio-Amorós and Santos (2012), and in the total evidence analyses of Grant et al. (2017) and Rojas-Runjaic (2019).

Since its description (Barrio-Amorós and Santos 2012) it has not undergone taxonomic changes. Its taxonomic position has been corroborated in two recent phylogenetic studies (Grant et al. 2017, Rojas-Runjaic 2019).

authority: García-Gutiérrez, Escalona, Mora, de Pascual & Fermin, 2013
taxonomicNotes: Specimens of this species were previously identified as B. orestes (Barrio-Amorós and Fuentes-Ramos 1999, García-Gutiérrez et al. 2013). The molecular and morphological analyses of Fermin et al. (2012) shed light on the species diversity and its known distribution in the vicinity of Mérida city. Thus, B. mucuyensis was described from La Mucuy region, at Sierra Nevada de Mérida, and B. orestes was restricted to the Sierra La Culata, the close parallel mountain chain (García-Gutiérrez et al. 2013). It is unclear if specimens from the road Mérida-El Morro, at Sierra Nevada de Mérida (stored at the Museo de Historia Natural La Salle, MHNLS), belong to B. mucuyensis. B. mucuyensis is the sister species of B. orestes and it is member of the B. adspersa group (García-Gutiérrez et al. 2013).
It has not undergone taxonomic changes. Its phylogenetic position, as part of Mannophryne, has been corroborated by Grant et al. (2017) and Rojas-Runjaic (2019) based on total evidence analyses.
In the Pristimantis unistrigatus group according to the original description (Kaiser et al. 2015).
In the Pristimantis unistrigatus group according to the original description (Kaiser et al. 2015).

In the Pristimantis unistrigatus group according to the original description (Kaiser et al. 2015). With the adult male measuring 13 mm, this species may be the smallest frog species in Venezuela and rank among the smallest in South America.

Pristimantis phoxocephalus was formerly considered a single highly polymorphic species (e.g., Lynch 1979, Lynch and Duellman 1997, Duellman and Lehr 2009). Páez and Ron (2019) showed that most subpopulations previously ascribed to P. phoxocephalus represent other species (e.g., Pristimantis atillo, P. jimenezi, P. teslai sp. nov., P. torresi sp. nov., P. totoroi sp. nov., P. verrucolatus sp. nov., Pristimantis sp. (CCS2), Pristimantis sp. (UCS2), Pristimantis sp. (UCS3)). This is the new narrower concept of Pristimantis phoxocephalus.

authority: Alves da Silva, Magalhães, Thomassen, Leite, Garda, Brandão, Haddad, Giaretta & Carvalho, 2020
taxonomicNotes: Split from Leptodactylus spixi by Alves da Silva et al. (2020), who placed it in the L. mystaceus complex, L. fuscus group.

Hofmann et al. (2015) reviewed the evidence used by Peng et al. (2014) to describe Thermophis shangrila, and found that both morphological and molecular evidence were insufficient to reliably distinguish this species from Thermophis zhaoermii. These authors took no taxonomic action due to the small sample sizes available, but further sampling may reveal that T. shangrila is a junior synonym of T. zhaoermii (Hofmann et al. 2015).
The two known specimens of Indotyphlops lazelli were originally attributed to the widespread Ramphotyphlops albiceps (Wallach and Pauwels 2004).

Hofmann et al. (2015) reviewed the evidence used by Peng et al. (2014) to describe Thermophis shangrila, and found that both morphological and molecular evidence were insufficient to reliably distinguish this species from Thermophis zhaoermii. These authors took no taxonomic action due to the small sample sizes available, but further sampling may reveal that T. shangrila is a junior synonym of T. zhaoermii (Hofmann et al. 2015).

Concha et al. (2019) synonymised the genus Zearaja with Dipturus based on morphological and molecular analyses, thus assigning species formerly in the genus Zearaja to Dipturus.

The species Dipturus chilensis was formerly considered occurring in both the Southeast Pacific and the Southwest Atlantic. However, Gabbanelli et al. (2018) resurrected Raja brevicaudata Marini, 1933 from Argentina out of synonymy with D. chilensis and Concha et al. (2019) described Dipturus lamillai Concha, Caira, Ebert & Pompert, 2019 as a distinct species from the Falkland Islands (Malvinas), Southwest Atlantic. Therefore, D. chilensis is restricted to the Southeast Pacific, from central Chile to the Strait of Magellan, and references to D. chilensis in the Southwest Atlantic refer either to D. brevicaudatus or D. lamillai.

This species was described in a self-published manuscript by James E. Lloyd (Lloyd 2018). Photuris forresti is a member of the P. versicolor species complex which contains many closely related species, distinguishable primarily by observed flash pattern, as there are few morphological differences (Barber 1951, McDermott 1967, Lloyd 2018). This species is morphologically indistinguishable from Photuris tremulans. It can only be identified by its flash pattern, which is typically a series of 4-11 pulsed flashes. It is also very similar to P. walldoxeyi, though the ranges, elevations, and habitat preferences of these two species differ markedly (Faust and Davis 2019).
Rheinardia nigrescens formerly regarded as a subspecies of this species but is split following Davison et al. (2020). Monotypic.

authority: Patton, Apodaca, Corser, Wilson, Williams, Cameron & Wake, 2019
taxonomicNotes: This is a split from the broader concept of Aneides aeneus (Patton et al. 2019).
Presbytis canicrus was previously included as a subspecies of P. hosei, but it is now recognised as a separate species (Roos et al. 2014). This is a monotypic species.

Blue-throated Hillstar, described by Sornoza-Molina et al. (2018), is accepted as a species diagnosable and scorable against O. stolzmanni on the Tobias criteria by its blue vs green throat in male (4); greener, brighter upperparts in male (1); dark greyish vs whitish base colour to chin and throat in female [see Sornoza-Molina et al. (2018) Appendix Figure 11] (1); slightly smaller size, albeit sample sizes are small (1). Monotypic.

Edolisoma tenuirostre, E. insperatum, E. nesiotis, E. monacha, E. rostratum, E. meyerii, E. obiense, E. grayi, E. remotum and E. salomonis (del Hoyo and Collar 2016) were previously lumped as Coracina tenuirostris following Christidis & Boles (2008) and Sibley & Monroe (1990, 1993).
This is a split from the broader concept of Hynobius leechi (Borzée and Min 2021).
This is a split from the broader concept of Hynobius leechi (Borzée and Min 2021).

Schembri (2003) reported that "Only two freshwater hydrobiids occur, Mercuria cf. similis and Pseudamnicola moussonii; the exact identity of the former is somewhat problematic as it belongs to a group of very similar forms occurring all along the coasts of the Mediterranean and in the absence of genetic studies it is not possible to say whether these are sibling species or simple morphs”.

Miller et al. (2022) provide molecular research demonstrating that there is a distinct cluster on Malta and Gozo and MolluscaBase (2025) retain Mercuria melitensis as a distinct species.
This species was previously confused with both Atelopus pulcher and Atelopus spumarius (both of which differ in morphology and advertisement call), according to Jorge et al. (2020).

Previously considered a subspecies of C. sulphurea. Collar & Marsden (2014) used the Tobias criteria to evaluate the taxonomic status of the subspecies citrinocristata from Sumba of the Yellow-crested Cockatoo Cacatua sulphurea. They found that it met the criteria (scoring 7) against every other taxon except C. s. parvula, against which it scored only 6 as a result of the latter’s surprisingly long tail (by comparison with other Wallacean yellow-crests). This may have been a case of an overly strict application of the criteria, and the decision was ignored by Eaton et al. (2016) who readily split the ‘Sumba Cockatoo’. With the further disclosure that fledgling citrinocristata have black bills and fledgling sulphurea of all known forms have pale bills, we have further evidence of a distinction between Sumba birds and the others, we reverse the decision in Collar & Marsden (2014) to keep citrinocristata lumped and now regard it as a full species. Monotypic.

The Whitespotted Eagle Ray (Aetobatus narinari) (Euphrasen 1790) was originally restricted to the Western Central and Southwest Atlantic (Sales et al. 2019) but the range now also extends to the eastern Atlantic (Fricke et al. 2020).

Previously split by del Hoyo & Collar (2016) into three species, Black-winged Myna A. melanopterus in most of Java except the far east, Grey-backed Myna A. tricolor in far eastern Java and Grey-rumped Myna A. tertius on Bali. However, genetic analysis by Sadanandan et al. (2020) established that these taxa are very weakly distinguishable genetically, and that the darker coloration of tertius may be due to introgression from Javan Myna A. javanicus. Their recommendation to lump the three species is accepted here. Sometimes placed in genus Sturnus, but appears behaviourally and vocally closer to present genus. Three subspecies recognised. Prior to the 2016, this concept, including the three subspecies as detailed above, was recognised following Sibley & Monroe (1990, 1993).

Wiemers et al. (2018) distinguish three closely related allopatric species of the group of the so-called anomalous blue butterflies, also known as ‘brown complex’ of the subgenus Agrodiaetus Hübner, 1822 on the Southern Balkans: P orphicus, P timfristos and P aroaniensis. Parmentier, Vila & Lukhtanov (2022) show that P. aroaniensis sensu stricto is restricted to the Peloponnese Peninsula, P. timfristos occurs in Central Greece around Attika, and P. orphicus (including former P. eleniae as proposed by Vishnevskaya et al. 2016) is presumably the more widespread species occurring in isolated areas in Bulgaria, N Greece, North Macedonia, and Albania. The distribution of P. orphicus is putative and requires further investigation.

Although, Correa et al. (2017) synonymized Eupsophus altor with E. migueli extending the range of E. migueli south to the Lingue River, Suárez-Villota et al. (2018) hypothesized that both are good species restoring the previous taxonomic arrangement. The taxonomic criteria used by Cuevas and Sanhueza (2020) agrees with the results obtained by Suárez-Villota et al. (2018), recognizing this taxon as a specific taxonomic unit different from E. altor.

Eupsophus queulensis was synonymized with E. septentrionalis by Blotto et al. (2013). Correa-Quezada et al. (2017) then placed E. septentrionalis in synonymy with E. roseus, but the more recent proposal by Suárez-Villota et al. (2018) restored the previous taxonomy, resurrecting E. septentrionalis as a valid species.

Several taxonomic experts suspect that the species as it exists now is not valid. Should the invalidity of the existing species be published, this species assessment should be invalidated. Additional taxonomic research should be undertaken to verify, and is included as a research recommendation within the assessment.

For more than a century, Xixuthrus terribilis Thomson, 1877 was erroneously considered a junior subjective synonym of the Giant Fijian Longhorn Beetle (Xixuthrus heros (Gräffe, 1868)) until it was restored to full species status (Yanega et al. 2004). For nearly sixty years, the Taveuni Beetle was known by the name X. heyrovskyi Tippmann 1945, a junior synonym of X. terribilis (Yanega et al. 2004).

The species was originally described by Heer in 1868 in a work published by Gräffe (Gräffe 1868), but Heer was not appropriately credited for the description. Thus, the scientific name cannot be attributed to Heer under the International Code of Zoological Nomenclature (ICZN 1999) and authorship is instead attributed to Gräffe (Evenhuis 2007). A larva of Xixuthrus heros was also described in the same work but has since been determined to belong to the genus Olethrius Thomson (Yanega et al. 2004).
Xixuthrus ganglbaueri was originally described by Lameere in 1912 (Lameere 1912) and a neotype was designated by Yanega et al. (2004) after the holotype could no longer be located.

Nishikawa et al. (2021) have clarified the taxonomic identification of Hynobius sonani and H. formosanus . Based on the descriptions and measurement data in the original description, Nishikawa et al. (2021) they confirmed that H. sonani has been treated as H. formosanus and vice versa. As the holotypes could not be found, neotypes have been assigned to avoid future taxonomic confusion.

Nishikawa et al. (2021) have clarified the taxonomic identification of Hynobius sonani and H. formosanus . Based on the descriptions and measurement data in the original description, Nishikawa et al. (2021) they confirmed that H. sonani has been treated as H. formosanus and vice versa. As the holotypes could not be found, neotypes have been assigned to avoid future taxonomic confusion.
Records of this species off Argentina refer to Mustelus schmitti.

This taxon was classified as a subspecies of the Phayre’s Langur (Trachypithecus phayrei). Genetic data suggest that this species is a product of ancient hybridisation between the ancestors of the francoisi- and the obscurus-group (Liedigk et al. 2009). More recent genetic studies indicate that the genetic material acquired from the obscurus-group is specifically from the Tenasserim Langur (T. barbei) and not from T. phayrei (Roos, unpubl.).

There is some geographic variation. Individuals from the northeastern part of the distribution tend to be darker, particularly on the limbs and tail.

Although it is now accepted as a valid species (T. crepuscula), the taxon is still listed as a subspecies on The IUCN Red list because the revised taxonomic concept of T. phayrei has not yet been assessed by the IUCN SSC Primate Specialist Group.

Three elephant taxa remain from the sixteen elephant-like species that are known from across the planet in the Pleistocene: Asian Elephant (Elephas maximus), African Savanna Elephant (Loxodonta africana), and African Forest Elephant (Loxodonta cyclotis) (Faurby and Svenning 2015, Malhi et al. 2016). The Asian and African ancestral lineages diverged approximately seven million years ago, and the African Savanna and African Forest ancestral lineages began diverging approximately one million years later (Rohland et al. 2010, Brandt et al. 2014, Roca et al. 2015, Meyer et al. 2017, Palkopoulou et al. 2018). The Third Edition of ‘Mammal Species of the World’ (Wilson and Reeder 2005) was the first to formally designate the African elephant as these two separate species. Recent genetic findings also support this designation (Roca et al. 2007, Ishida et al. 2011, Mondol et al. 2015, Palkopoulou et al. 2018, Kim and Wasser 2019). Hybridization between the two species appears restricted and evident at only 14 of the more than 100 localities recently examined across the vast forest-savanna ecotone. In nine of these 14 localities, hybrid individuals occurred alongside non-hybrid individuals of either one species or the other and not both (i.e., three localities had hybrids and African Forest Elephants only and assigned as this species; six localities had hybrids and African Savanna Elephants only and assigned as this species). For the IUCN Red List assessments, a distribution map published in Mondol et al. (2015) and recent data by Kim and Wasser (2019) are used to assign localities as range of either L. africana or L. cyclotis.

authority: Neira-Salamea, Ofori-Boateng, Kouamé, Blackburn, Segniagbeto, Hillers, Barej, Leaché & Rödel, 2021

taxonomicNotes: Gyps rueppelli (del Hoyo and Collar 2014) was previously listed as G. rueppellii.
This is a split from the broader concept of Litoria littlejohni (Mahony et al. 2020).
Proposed subspecies pileatus differs only in size, which varies clinally, increasing from small in W to large in E and S; no longer recognised (Barlow et al. 2020). Monotypic.
Fringilla teydea and F. polatzeki (del Hoyo and Collar 2016) were previously lumped as F. teydea following AERC TAC (2003); Cramp et al. (1977-1994); Sibley and Monroe (1990, 1993).

Presbytis percura was described by M.W. Lyon, Jr. in 1908 based on specimens collected from near Siak Kecil River, Makapan, Kompei, Pulau Rupat, and Salat Rupat (Lyon 1908). It was later regarded as a subspecies of P. femoralis, along with P. f. femoralis and P. f. robinsoni (Groves 2001, Meyer et al. 2011, Groves and Roos 2013, Roos et al. 2014). However, genetic data suggested that at least P. f. femoralis and P. f. robinsoni were different species (Ang 2010, Nijman 2019) which was also in agreement with their morphological characters.

Resolving all subspecies-level boundaries within banded langurs required data for P. f. percura, which was the least studied among the three taxa and only found in the Riau Province in Sumatra (Rizaldi et al. 2019). Complete mitochondrial genomes were later obtained for P. f. percura in the wild, and based on multiple species delimitation algorithms (PTP, ABGD, Objective Clustering) applied to a dataset covering 40 species and 43 subspecies of Asian colobines, all three subspecies of banded langurs were resurrected to species (Ang et al. 2020).

Presbytis percura is mainly blackish above, with a grey head and a white underside (Lyon 1908, Aimi and Bakar 1992). White underside is narrow but extends horizontally across chest, down inner sides of limbs in thin stripes to wrists and ankles, and to cheeks and chin. The tail is black and not bicolour, but part of the ventral surface of the basal third of the tail is indistinctly white. Its throat and face are grey, and there are narrow, pale eye-rings. One (sometimes two) whorls are set well back on its crown. Hairs on the chest are directed backward.

The foetuses are white with a black stripe from the head to the tail, intersected by another black stripe along the outer surfaces of both forearms, forming a distinctive cruciform (cross-like) black pattern on the dorsum.

Macaca leucogenys was first described by Li et al. (2015) based on photos captured by camera traps, and its full species status was confirmed based on DNA extracted from skin specimens (Fan et al. 2017).

Macaca leucogenys is distinguished from all potential sympatric macaque species (M. mulatta, M. thibetana, M. assamensis, and M. munzala) based on a suite of pelage characteristics including relatively uniform dorsal hair pattern, hairy ventral pelage, relatively hairless short tail, prominent pale to white side- and chin-whiskers creating a white cheek and round facial appearance, dark facial skin on the muzzle, long and thick hairs on its neck, and a round rather than arrow-shaped male genitalia.

This taxon was considered a subspecies of Nycticebus coucang but elevated by Groves and Maryanto (2008). This is further supported by morphological studies by Nekaris and Jaffe (2007) and Nekaris and Munds (2010) and genetic studies by Roos (2003) and Wirdateti et al. (2006). Two distinct forms regularly occur in bird markets and further research is needed (Nekaris and Jaffe 2007). Care should be taken in taxonomic studies however due to translocation of individuals from markets into forests and gardens throughout the island (Schulze and Groves 2004). The distinct black and white long-haired appearance of subadults and juveniles also might be mistaken as separate species, for example, in museum specimens or from photos only (KA. Nekaris et al. pers. comm. 2012).
Phapitreron amethystinus, P. maculipectus and P. frontalis (del Hoyo and Collar 2014) were previously lumped as P. amethystinus following Sibley and Monroe (1990, 1993).

Four species of Lion Tamarins are recognized. Coimbra-Filho (1990) suggested that Leontopithecus caissara was a subspecies, or colour variant of L. chrysopygus. Burity et al. (1999) and Perez-Sweeney et al. (2008) concluded that this was not so, and that L. caissara is a valid species. Perez-Sweeney et al. (2008) concluded that there are three unambiguous clades revealed by phylogenetic analysis of mtDNA control region sequences: Leontopithecus chysomelas, L. caissara and L. chrysopygus/L. rosalia. According to these authors, L. chysomelas occupies a basal phylogenetic position. Here we follow the taxonomy proposed by Rylands (2012).

Recent changes to the systematics of Rhinobatos have elevated the subgenus Glaucostegus to full generic status and placed this genus into a family of its own: Glaucostegidae (Compagno 2005, Last et al. 2016a). Reports of G. obtusus from South Africa are considered invalid and based on other species (Compagno and Last 1999).

Traditionally treated as a subspecies of P. bicalcaratum, but differs in size and morphology. Male katsumatae differ from males of other taxa in P. bicalcaratum in their smaller size (effect size on wing 8.57, score 3); brilliant red and more extensive vs dirty grey to pale yellow and less extensive facial skin (3); green vs purplish ocelli, with a somewhat different shape (2); lack of elongate crown feathers (1); darker crown (1); more densely and neatly vermiculated body plumage (ns1); browner vs greyer overall coloration (ns1) (Collar 2009, Davison et al. 2012, Pilgrim et al. 2009). Iris colour may also be a character, and the taxa are in fact not each other’s closest relatives, bicalcaratum being sister to P. chalcurum with P. inopinatum sister to this pair (Chang Jiang et al. 2008, Davison et al. 2012). Monotypic.
Lophura erythrophthalma and L. pyronota (del Hoyo and Collar 2014) were previously lumped as L. erythrophthalma following Sibley and Monroe (1990, 1993).
This species was described by Hernández et al. (2000), but the name first appeared in Barbadillo et al. (1999) and this is therefore the correct authority.

The Vipera ursinii species complex has a large distribution area from eastern France to western China, which was shaped by the last three glacial periods (Ferchaud et al. 2012). In the last two decades some V. ursinii subspecies have been elevated to distinct species level based on molecular and morphological analyses (V. renardi, V. anatolica, and V. eriwanensis). The Greek Meadow Viper (Vipera ursinii graeca) was described as a subspecies of Vipera ursinii by Nilson and Andrén (1988) based on morphological analyses. Ferchaud et al. (2012) proposed that V. u. graeca should be elevated to species level, according to the results of mitochondrial DNA phylogenetic analyses. Based on a mitochondrial and nuclear DNA analyses Mizsei et al. (2017) provided further support for this arrangement in an analysis of combined mitochondrial and nuclear DNA, recognising it as a full species Vipera graeca in order to resolve polyphyly in the Vipera ursinii–renardi complex.

This species was transferred from the genus Chamaeleo to the genus Calumma by Klaver and Böhme (1986). Subsequently the new monotypic genus Archaius was established for this species by Townsend et al. (2010). The species is found on three oceanic islands that are separated by 20-50 km of open water. It is expected that migration between islands is extremely infrequent, and that populations are divergent, possibly at the species level. These populations should therefore be investigated in a phylogenetic framework.

authority: Gehring, Pabijan, Ratsoavina, Köhler, Vences & Glaw, 2010
taxonomicNotes: Genetic data is required to needed to confirm the identity of four specimens reported from southeast Madagascar, as these are geographically distant from other known populations (Rakotondrina et al. 2024).

Recent changes to the systematics of Rhinobatos have elevated the subgenus Glaucostegus to full generic status and placed this genus into a family of its own: Glaucostegidae (Compagno 2005, Last et al. 2016a).
Myotis lucifugus does not include occultus. This species hybridizes with M. yumanensis in some areas; see Parkinson (1979). Apparently it is closely related to M. thysanodes.
Pternistis camerunensis (del Hoyo and Collar 2014) was previously placed in the genus Francolinus.

Rice’s Whale (Balaenoptera ricei) is a distinct evolutionary lineage closely related to Eden’s Whale (Balaenoptera edeni edeni), Bryde’s Whale (B. e. brydei) and Sei Whale (Balaenoptera borealis) (Rosel et al. 2021). Previously considered an undescribed subspecies of Balaenoptera edeni and referred to as the Gulf of Mexico Whale or the Gulf of Mexico Bryde’s Whale, a recent morphological study of Rice’s Whale has revealed diagnostic differences in the shape and size of bones in the skull in comparison to the two subspecies of B. edeni. This morphological evidence reinforced earlier findings of significant genetic divergence and fixed DNA differences found in several mitochondrial DNA genes; together, these data supported the description of the new species B. ricei. With elevation of Rice’s Whale to species status, there is still uncertainty as to whether Eden’s Whale and Bryde’s Whale, each of which is clearly distinct, represent subspecies of B. edeni or full species.

authority: Barr, Chaple, Hitchmough, Patterson & Ngātiwai Trust Board, 2021

taxonomicNotes: Until rather recently this species was regarded as subspecies of Pyrrhosoma nymphula. Kalkman and Lopau (2006) illustrated the differences between the two taxa and confirmed that they should be treated as two full valid species, which is the widely accepted taxonomic rank now.

Formerly, Boyeria cretensis was confused with—and regarded as a subpopulation of—Boyeria irene (Fonscolombe, 1838), but both species are clearly distinct (Boudot 1989, Peters 1991, Kohli et al. 2014).
Mirafra africana and M. sharpii (del Hoyo and Collar 2016) were previously lumped as M. africana following Dowsett & Forbes-Watson (1993).

authority: Chaves-Portilla, Salazar, Gil-Acero, Dorado-Correa, Márquez, Rueda-Almonacid & Amézquita, 2021

taxonomicNotes: Artisornis moreaui and A. sousae (del Hoyo and Collar 2016) were previously lumped as A. moreaui following Dowsett & Forbes-Watson (1993) and Sibley & Monroe (1990, 1993).

Rhinolophus montanus was originally described as a subspecies of philippinensis (Goodwin 1979), but was recently elevated to species status (Csorba 2002). This taxonomy is generally accepted and followed (Csorba et al. 2003, Simmons 2005).

This species was previously considered a subspecies of Chaerephon jobensis (Felten 1964, Hill 1983) but has been recognised as a distinct species since its treatment by Flannery (1995). The taxonomy is considered stable (Simmons 2005) despite the absence of full taxonomic enquiry.

Woodman (1993) notes that many mammalian generic names ending in -otis use the wrong gender for specific names. If this advice is to be followed, the species name should be M. pruinosa. The Chiroptera SG advise keeping the names as they are for now while a decision is still to be made on these.
This species is closely related to Myotis pruinosus that occurs in mainland Japan (Kawai et al. 2003, Abe et al. 2005).
Aratinga solstitialis and A. maculata (del Hoyo and Collar 2014) were previously lumped as A. solstitialis following Sibley and Monroe (1990, 1993) and Stotz et al. (1996).

Chrysocolaptes lucidus, C. guttacristatus, C. stricklandi, C. strictus, C. haematribon, C. erythrocephalus and C. xanthocephalus (del Hoyo and Collar 2014) were previously lumped as C. lucidus following Sibley and Monroe (1990, 1993).

Cercopithecus dryas is one of Africa’s least known and most enigmatic monkeys. Its nomenclature has been disputed, and its phylogenetic affinities are uncertain. The type specimen, the skull and skin of a captive juvenile male from the early 1930s, is from an unknown source population in central Democratic Republic of Congo (DRC), most likely in the Lomela region in what is currently Sankuru Province (Schwarz 1932). The species was not reported again until 1977, when Kano and Kuroda acquired two hunter-killed animals in the Wamba research area 300 km north of Lomela town (Kuroda et al. 1985). One of these animals became the type specimen for a new species: Cercopithecus salongo Thys van den Audenaerde 1977. Subsequent re-examination of the C. dryas type and Wamba specimen indicated that these represented the same species with age-related differences in colouration, thus sinking salongo into dryas (Colyn et al. 1991). Sarmiento (2002) disputed this assessment. However, the current treatment follows Groves (2001, 2005), Grubb et al. (2003), Butynski (2013), and Healy (2013) in accepting the synonymy of the two forms. Following the collections by Kano and Kuroda, C. dryas was confirmed in the Kokolopori area to the east of Wamba (Lokasola 2008). Lokasola (2008) suggests that Kano and Kuroda’s 1977 specimen likely came from this same population.

In 2014, the species reappeared again, 400 km southeast of Kokolopori/Wamba near Bafundo, in Maniema Province, less than 15 km west of the Congo (Lualaba) River. Field staff from the Lukuru Foundation’s TL2 Project collected photos of a fresh hunter-killed adult female and later collected a hunter-killed adult male from this same area (Hart 2014). Subsequently, the species was seen at two sites ~31 km west of Bafundo near the Lomami River, and two sites ~75 km west of the city of Kindu (Alempijevic et al. 2018). Both sexes of the Lomami-Lualaba interfluve population are characterized by striking rufous cheeks and upper ventrum, a feature not mentioned in the published descriptions of C. dryas sensu lato, as all publications describe specimens with white- or cream-coloured facial hairs and ventrum (Schwarz 1932, Thys van den Audenaerde 1977, Kuroda et al. 1985). Nevertheless, a photo of a monkey taken in 2011 at Iyondji in the Kokolopori/Wamba region by R. Mittermeier clearly shows these animals have rufous cheeks. Subsequently, S. Kuroda (comment posted online in 2016 in Hart 2014) confirmed that his 1977 Wamba specimens looked like the Bafundo animals. Tail colouration, in particular the shape and location of the triangular dark grey ventral patch at the base of the tail, in both sexes, has also been a source of confusion (e.g. C. dryas illustration, Gautier-Hion et al. 1999). The prominent blue perineum and scrotum in adult males (Hart 2014) has not been mentioned in previous published accounts of the species, but this feature likely fades in death, as in C. lomamiensis, and may not be evident in all hunter-killed animals and dry museum skins (Hart et al. 2012). Additional field surveys and population genetics studies are needed to assess the level of variation present among known populations, as this monkey occurs in very small, widely-separated populations over a very large area, and with no evident contact between them.

Cercopithecus dryas is currently assigned to its own species group, the Cercopithecus dryas-group (Groves 2001, Butynski 2002), where it is the smallest species within the genus Cercopithecus (Butynski 2013). However, recent phylogenetic analyses based on the mitochondrial genome of the type specimen (finger tip) show C. dryas clustering within the genus Chlorocebus (Guschanski et al. 2013). Groves suggested possible affinities between C. dryas and Chlorocebus after initial assessment of the skin and skull of the type specimen (1972 pers. comm. cited in Thys van den Audenaerde 1977), however he later concluded that C. dryas is a distinct species within the genus Cercopithecus based on the cranial morphology of the type specimen and a photograph of an adult skull in Kuroda et al. (1985).

A study of the morphology by Gilbert et al. (2021) confirmed the results of Guschanski et al. (2013) in treating the dryas monkey as a member of the genus Chlorocebus, not Cercopithecus (as per Groves 2001). Note also that the phylogenomic study of Guschanski et al. (2013) placed Allochrocebus solatus (Harrison, 1988) of Gabon as basal to the Chlorocebus clade.

Modern taxonomic arrangements of the colobus monkeys either distinguish the red colobus monkeys and the Olive Colobus as separate genera, Piliocolobus and Procolobus, respectively (e.g., Groves 2001, 2005, 2007; Roos et al. 2011, Kingdon 2015), or consider them to belong to one genus Procolobus, with two subgenera (Procolobus for the Olive Colobus and Piliocolobus for the red colobus) (Oates et al. 1994, Grubb et al. 2003, 2013; Oates 2011). Ting (2008) placed all the red colobus as subspecies of Procolobus (Piliocolobus) badius. The 2008 IUCN Red List assessments followed the taxonomy of Grubb et al. (2003).

The arrangement of two genera in Groves (2001, 2005, 2007) is followed here: a monotypic Procolobus de Rochebrune, 1887 (Olive Colobus) and polytypic Piliocolobus de Rochebrune, 1887 (red colobus).

This taxon was formerly a subspecies of a wider concept of P. pennantii that included as subspecies parmentierorum, bouvieri, tholloni, powelli Matschie, nigrimanus Trouessart, lulindicus, langi, foai and semlikiensis (Gautier-Hion et al. 1999). Kingdon (1997), Groves (2001, 2005) and Butynski et al. (2013) considered bouvieri and epieni to be subspecies of pennantii; Grubb et al. (2003) likewise, and also included preussi.

With the revisions and reviews of sportive lemurs located in northwestern Madagascar between 2006 and 2007, the distribution of Lepilemur dorsalis and other sportive lemurs in this region remained uncertain. Zinner et al. (2007) showed that the three teams that described new Lepilemur species in 2006 and 2007, Andriaholinirina et al. (2006), Louis et al. (2006) and Craul et al. (2007) had each submitted three alternative concepts of the holotype locality for L. dorsalis and that the validity of L. tymerlachsoni and L. mittermeieri depended on which concept turned out to be correct. The type locality of Lepilemur dorsalis was thought to be either Mourountsang (=Anorontsangana, 13°55’S, 47°55’E) or Passandava (=Ampasindava, about 13°40’S, 48°15’E). If the former was correct, then L. tymerlachsonorum is a synonym of L. dorsalis and L. mittermeieri is valid; and if the latter, then L. mittermeieri is the synonym and L. tymerlachsoni is valid. The species found at Nosy Bé is also found on Anorontsangana (Zinner et al. 2007). Further, the holotype L. grandidieri (Forsyth Major 1894) was attributed to a specimen from northern Madagascar, which may prove to be a senior synonym of one of the species described in 2006/2007. Therefore, it was recommended that as a priority research and conservation study, genetic material from the holotypes for L. dorsalis and L. grandidieri be acquired and comparable sequence data generated to determine the taxonomy and distribution of L. dorsalis and the other sportive lemurs of north-west Madagascar.

Recent phylogenetic studies by Roos et al. (2021) confirmed that L. mittermeieri and L. grandidieri Forsyth Major, 1894, are both junior synonyms of L. dorsalis Gray, 1871. The authors sequenced the complete mitochondrial genomes of the L. dorsalis and L. grandidieri (currently recognized as a synonym of L. dorsalis) type specimens and compared the sequences with those of samples with known provenance from the different Inter-River Systems (IRSs). The two type specimens of L. dorsalis and L. grandidieri are identical and cluster closely with samples collected in IRS V (between the rivers Andranomalaza and Sambirano), indicating that the type locality can be fixed to IRS V. Consequently, L. dorsalis occurs in IRS V, and L. grandidieri and L. mittermeieri are junior synonyms of L. dorsalis.

Until recently, Trachypithecus margarita was included in T. germaini. Genetic data (mtDNA studies) and phenotypical characters supports separate species status, though additional study will be needed for confirmation, since the study was based on a few samples from Cambodia and Viet Nam (including samples from animals of unknown origin) and no samples from Lao PDR (Roos et al., 2008). It is considered to be part of the cristatus group (T. cristatus, T. auratus, T. mauritus, T. germaini and T. selangorensis) (Hoang Minh Duc et al. 2012, Nadler and Brockman 2014, Nadler et al. 2005, Roos et al. 2008).
Bradypus pygmaeus has only recently been described by Anderson and Handley (2001) as a separate species.

Zanda baudinii (del Hoyo and Collar 2014) was previously placed in the genus Calyptorhynchus. Original note from Illustrated Checklists: Closely related to Z. funerea and Z. latirostris. Long considered conspecific with latter, but currently treated as separate species because ecology quite different, and bill significantly longer; difficulty in field identification, compounded by long history of conspecific treatment, has led to uncertainty regarding limits of range in area of overlap, and to publication of some inaccurate maps. Present species has also, on occasion, been lumped in Z. funerea. Confusion over bill size of specimen used in original description led to proposal of subspecies tenuirostris, but this name shown to be synonymous with baudinii (Schodde and Mason 1997). Monotypic.
Unidentified Pipistrellus bats found in the Azores have recently been proposed to belong to this species, but this awaits confirmation.
Identified as a new species by genetic analyses, this species combines morphological features of both P. austriacus and P. auritus.

Here we refer to P. teneriffae as the population of the Canary Islands. Northwest African populations previously assigned to P. teneriffae are currently included in P. gaisleri, and Balkan populations are assigned to P. austriacus. The taxonomic status of the species was confirmed by Spitzenberger et al. (2006). There are no significant genetic differences between populations on the islands of the Canary Archipelago where this species is found (Juste et al. 2004).
Cyclopsitta diophthalma and C. coxeni (del Hoyo and Collar 2014) were previously lumped as C. diophthalma following Sibley and Monroe (1990, 1993).

authority: Mahony, Hines, Mahony, Moses, Catalano, Myers & Donnellan, 2021
taxonomicNotes: This is a split from the broader concept of Assa darlingtoni (Mahony et al. 2021). The original description also includes some localities for which the identity of specimens are still uncertain. For this assessment they have been kept within Assa darlingtoni.
The Madeiran Brimstone used to be considered a subspecies of the Cleopatra (G. cleopatra).

The Canary Brimstone used to be considered a subspecies of the Cleopatra (Gonepteryx cleopatra). According to Wiemers et al. (2018), the year of the publication of the name Anteos cleobule is 1831, not 1830 (the original plate [79], published in 1824, carried no names).
Recently the subpopulations in the Sierra de la Sagra (formerly described as Polyommatus sagratrox) have been assigned to this species.
Following Vila et al. (2010), the taxon subbaeticus (from the Sierras de Cazorla y Segura and Sierra de la Sagra) is treated as a subspecies of P. violetae.
This Grayling used to be considered a subspecies of Brown's Grayling, or Buff's Asian Grayling (P. mamurra), which occurs in the Asian part of Türkiye.

The taxon is often considered as a subspecies of the Asian Pseudochazara hippolyte. However, the type species of P. hippolyte was described from the Ural Mountains and showed clear morphological differences from Iberian specimens described by Romei in 1927 (Olivares et al. 2011). Subsequently, Kudrna et al. (2011, 2015) and Wiemers et al. (2018) accepted full species status of P. williamsi.
There are no taxonomic concerns with relevance to the validity of the species and no taxonomic revisions underway.

The genus Lychas included several species that were thought to belong to other genera. The relationships have been resolved and the two African species transferred to a new genus Afrolychas (Kovařík 2019).
The species is very close to Purpuricenus globulicollis and its status (valid species or subspecies of P. globulicollis) is unclear, pending molecular data (Rapuzzi and Sama 2013).
Molecular studies confirmed the close relationship of this species with the endemic Lake Prespa pea clam Pisidium maasseni and the widespread P. nitidum (Schultheiß et al. 2008).
Recent molecular studies confirmed the close relationship of this species with the endemic Lake Ohrid pea clam P. edlaueri and the widespread P. nitidum (Schultheiß et al. 2008).

This is a new concept of the species that includes the former species Pseudanodonta elongata (Holandre, 1836) and Pseudanodonta middendorffi (Siemaschko, 1849), which are now placed as a synonym of Pseudanodonta complanata (Rossmässler, 1835) by MolluscaBase (2022).
Lophura erythrophthalma and L. pyronota (del Hoyo and Collar 2014) were previously lumped as L. erythrophthalma following Sibley and Monroe (1990, 1993).

Differences in morphological, ecological and behavioural traits suggest that the current five-species arrangement does not accurately reflect species diversity within Kosciuscola (Tatarnic et al. 2013, Umbers et al. 2021). Molecular work has identified species-level clades within K. tristis (Umbers et al. 2021), and provided strong evidence supporting the elevation of K. tristis restrictus to species level as K. restrictus (Song et al. 2021).

Differences in morphological, ecological and behavioural traits suggest that the current five-species arrangement does not accurately reflect species diversity within Kosciuscola (Tatarnic et al. 2013, Umbers et al. 2021). Molecular work has identified species-level clades within K. tristis, and provided strong evidence that K. tristis sensu stricto is restricted to the southern New South Wales mountains (Umbers et al. 2021), with the remaining clades within K. tristis representing undescribed species.

The recent emergence of major threats from climate change and bushfire, combined with the narrow ranges of these taxa as now understood, is potentially sufficient to justify assessing the reduced species concept of K. tristis, and to assess the undescribed congeners.

Metapocyrtus ginalopezae was discovered in Mindanao in 2019 and was collected through sheet beating and hand picking (A. Cabras pers. comm. 2022). Metapocyrtus (Orthocyrtus) ginalopezae sp.n. belongs to the subgenus Orthocyrtus and superficially resembles the general shape of Metapocyrtus (Orthocyrtus) mansaka but differs for its unique elytral ornamentation, less pronounced medial groove and absence of scales on basal half of rostrum and shape of the aedeagus. Metapocyrtus can be easily distinguished from Pachyrhynchus for having antennae reaching behind the hind eye and for having a transverse groove between the head and rostrum (Cabras and Medina 2019).

Metapocyrtus (Artapocyrtus) willietorresi was described based on two female specimens collected from May to July of 2017 at an elevation of 1,000 m at the slopes of Mount Apo (Cabras and Medina 2018).

authority: Coloma, Duellman, Almendáriz, Ron, Terán-Valdez & Guayasamin, 2010

taxonomicNotes: This species may be a junior synonym of Schizothorax davidi.
This species is commonly referred to under Barbodes (Barbodes) daliensis Wu & Lin in China.
The taxonomy of this species is questionable. The type specimens have been destroyed.

Gallirallus conditicius (Sibley and Monroe 1990, 1993), known only from the type reputedly collected in the Apiang Group of the Gilbert Islands, Kiribati, in 1861, is not recognised as a separate species but is treated as a synonym of H. sylvestris (Greenway 1952, Olson 1992).


Hypotaenidia sylvestris (del Hoyo and Collar 2014) was previously placed in the genus Gallirallus.
Psephotellus chrysopterygius (del Hoyo and Collar 2014) was previously placed in the genus Psephotus.

The species has been previously identified as Ctenodesma borneensis (Zieritz et al. 2020). However, molecular phylogenetic analysis revealed that Khairuloconcha is a divergent lineage sister to C. borneensis and can morphologically be discriminated from C. borneensis by its sharper posterior ridge with nodulous sculpturing (especially dorsally) and a faint zigzag or irregular sculpture on the posterior slope rather than uniform plications (Zieritz et al. 2021).

The species has been previously identified as Ctenodesma borneensis (lot FMNH 116932 [FMNH = Field Museum of Natural History, Chicago, USA]). However, molecular phylogenetic analysis revealed that Khairuloconcha is a divergent lineage sister to C. borneensis and can morphologically be discriminated from C. borneensis by its sharper posterior ridge with nodulous sculpturing (especially dorsally) and a faint zigzag or irregular sculpture on the posterior slope rather than uniform plications (Zieritz et al. in 2021).

Zanda latirostris (del Hoyo and Collar 2014) was previously placed in the genus Calyptorhynchus. Original note from Illustrated Checklists: Closely related to Z. funerea and Z. baudinii. Long considered conspecific with latter, mainly on account of identical plumages (Juniper and Parr 1998), but currently treated as separate because present species’ ecology quite different and bill significantly shorter than that of Z. baudinii; difficulty in field identification, compounded by long history of conspecific treatment, has led to uncertainty regarding limits of range in area of overlap, and to publication of some inaccurate maps. Exhaustive analysis of all available specimens of taxa in present genus suggested that latirostris was so similar to South Australian Z. funerea that it might perhaps be treated as subspecies of latter. Monotypic.

Due to the high morphological plasticity of shells within the genus Anodonta, Haas (1969) decided to combine the multiple names available for the European forms into a single species, i.e. Anodonta cygnea. Since then, in addition to A. cygnea, another Anodonta species, i.e. A. anatina, has been widely recognised in Europe (Lopes-Lima et al. 2017). More recently, with the help of molecular and morphological work, Anodonta exulcerata has been recognised as a separate and valid species for populations in the Adriatic basin of Italy and the Western Balkan countries (Froufe et al. 2017, Riccardi et al. 2020).

Unio ionicus was considered by Haas (1969) as one of the Unio crassus subspecies occurring in Santa Maura and Corfu Islands, western Greece, and has been included in Unio crassus in recent decades (Lopes-Lima et al. 2017). However, recent molecular work evaluating the Unio crassus species complex has considered Unio ionicus as a separate and valid species (Araujo et al. 2018, Lopes-Lima et al. 2023 submitted).

Formerly placed as a synonym of Unio mancus Lamarck, 1819 (Haas 1969), U. elongatulus has been split from U. mancus by numerous studies (e.g. Prié and Puillandre 2014, Froufe et al. 2017, Marrone et al. 2019).

Haas (1969) believed that the species Unio elongatulus C. Pfeiffer, 1825 (= Unio mancus Lamarck, 1819, see below) consisted of a group of 17 regional subspecies distributed mainly throughout the Mediterranean and Asia Minor.

The taxonomy of these subspecies was first unravelled by Araujo (2005), who considered the Iberian populations of U. elongatulus to be U. mancus, a separate and valid species. This was later revalidated by Prié et al. (2012), who also included the French populations in U. mancus and separated them from the true U. elongatulus for the populations in northern Italy.

Since then, both U. mancus and U. elongatulus have been recognised as valid with mutually exclusive distributions in Europe (Araujo et al. 2018, Froufe et al. 2017, Marrone et al. 2019).
Some authors regard this as the same species as Poecilium fasciatum. However, Sama (2002) is one of the leading European authorities on the family and treats them as separate species.
Also known as Aleurostictus decempunctatus.

The Osmoderma species complex is here treated as five separate species (barnabita, eremita, cristinae, italica and lassallei), following Audisio et al. (2007, 2008). Distribution limits of these different forms remain poorly resolved, but for the purpose of these assessments, we follow the approximate distribution limits outlined in Audisio et al. (2007, 2008). There is ongoing debate as to whether or not these forms constitute valid species, but for the purpose of this assessment, we are assessing each form separately, following Bezděk (2016).
This species was included in the subgenus Stereosa (Verdugo 2005) but more recently, it was changed to the subgenus Cypriacis (Kubáň 2006, 2016).

Based on morphological observations, Zlatarski and Estalella (1982), Bouchon and Laborel (1986) and Fenner (1993) concluded that Colpophyllia breviserialis should not be treated as a valid species separate from C. natans but it is considered valid according to Reyes et al. (2010) and the WoRMS online database (accessed February 2022). According to Spathias (2017), a taxonomic revision of the Colpophyllia genus that is underway is intended to resolve the uncertain status of C. breviserialis.
The group was revised in Mordan and Martins (2001).

Has frequently been considered a subspecies of H. unicolor or of H. ostralegus; differentiation relatively weak, and species status perhaps questionable. Recent study of mtDNA, however, supported treatment as a separate species (Banks and Paterson 2007). Form H. ostralegus finschi has on occasion been associated with present species. Monotypic.

Based on a growing body of molecular and morphological evidence, tiger beetles are now placed under Cicindelidae (Vogler and Pearson 1996, Maddison et al. 1999, Pearson and Vogler 2001, Putchkov and Cassola 2005, Bouchard et al. 2011, Duran and Gough 2020). Thopeutica petertaylori Medina, Cabras and Wiesner (Coleoptera: Cicindelidae) was described under Cicindelidae from 16 specimens collected in December 2019 (Medina et al. 2019).

The species was originally described as Helix pittae by Paiva in 1866, and not as Helix (Discula) polymorpha var. δ pittae as stated in Wollaston (1878) (refer to Actinella fecundaerrata in Teixeira et al. 2019). In Bank et al. (2002), it was referred to as Caseolus (Caseolus?) innominatus pittae, but the basis of record was amended to Caseolus (Caseolus?) pittae (Paiva, 1866) in Teixeira et al. (2019) where lectotypes where designated. That nomenclature was then followed in Cameron et al. (2021).

The evolutionary relationship of Tomistoma with other crocodilians was debated for many years, and the species was usually aligned with the true crocodiles (Crocodylidae) based on morphological evidence (Norell 1989, Tarsitano et al. 1989, Brochu 1997). Molecular studies since the 1980s suggest a closer relationship to Gavialis (Densmore 1983, Densmore and Dessauer 1984, Gatesy and Amato 1992, Harshman et al. 2003, McAliley et al. 2006). A recent molecular study found that Tomistoma shares gene sequences with Gavialis which are absent from Crocodylus, Mecistops (see Shirley 2010) and Osteolaemus, suggesting Tomistoma should be placed within the family Gavialidae (Willis et al. 2007).

Combined morphological and molecular data led Last et al. (2016) and White et al. (2017) to conclude that Mobula eregoodootenkee is a junior synonym of M. kuhlii. However, Hosegood et al. (2019) suggested these were separate species, which was supported by Notarbartolo di Sciara et al. (2020), who also clarified the nomenclature as M. eregoodoo.

The previously, monotypic genus Manta was re-evaluated with two species identified, Reef Manta Ray (Manta alfredi) and Oceanic Manta Ray (Manta birostris) (Marshall et al. 2009). Genetic evidence supported the separation (Kashiwagi et al. 2012). Both species have broad global distributions and are sympatric in some locations and allopatric in others (Kashiwagi et al. 2011, Lawson et al. 2017). A third species of manta ray has also been proposed (Marshall et al. 2009) with increasing genetic support (Hinojosa-Alvarez et al. 2016, Kashiwagi et al. 2017, Hosegood et al. 2018). Genetic evidence has also resulted in the genus Manta being subsumed within the genus Mobula (Poortvliet et al. 2015, White et al. 2018).

Due to the recent taxonomic changes, both present day and historical reports can often be unclear and without adequate descriptions, photographs or geographic locations, it can be difficult to conclusively attribute fisheries data to a single species. Care should be taken when using reports or accounts of the Oceanic Manta Ray. Melanistic (black) and leucistic (white) colour morphs occur in all species of manta ray (Marshall et al. 2009). Variant colour morphs often contribute an added degree of confusion when attempting to visually discriminate between species of manta rays in the field or in photographs, especially when close examination is not possible. It should be noted that these colour morphs could be a source of error, resulting in misidentifications in past or future studies or surveys of distribution.

White et al. (2017) concluded that Mobula rochebrunei (East Atlantic Pygmy Devil Ray) is a junior synonym of M. hypostoma (Atlantic Pygmy Devil Ray), with the latter species being confirmed as occurring in both the Western and Eastern Atlantic.

Combined morphological and molecular data led Last et al. (2016) and White et al. (2017) to conclude that M. eregoodootenkee is a junior synonym of M. kuhlii. However, Hosegood et al. (2019) suggested these were separate species, which was supported by Notarbartolo di Sciara et al. (2020), who also clarified the nomenclature as M. eregoodoo.

This species was recently described by Habib and Islam (2021). It is similar to, and sympatric with, Glaucostegus granulatus in Bangladesh, but the description of the new species does not change the taxonomic concept of G. granulatus.
Many lake species were identified as this species in this past, but later separated into independent species. Therefore, some historical identifications will be incorrect.

Genetic (Rosel et al. 1995) and morphological (Brownell et al. 1987) data suggest that Vaquitas are most closely related to porpoises in South America. Genetic data suggest divergence from two sister taxa (Burmeister’s Porpoise, Phocoena spinipinnis and Spectacled Porpoise Australophocaena dioptrica) in the Pleistocene (i.e., at least 2.5 million years ago).

The intraspecific taxonomy of the Tiger (Panthera tigris) has been recently debated due to inconsistent results from various methodologies (Luo et al. 2004, Wilting et al. 2015, Kitchener et al. 2017, Liu et al. 2018). Luo et al. (2004) identify six subspecies on the analysis of mitochondrial DNA (mtDNA) while Wilting et al. (2015) classified Tigers into two subspecies based on data sets of several traits [morphological (craniodental and pelage), ecological, molecular]. These inconsistencies were partly attributed to the lack of genetic samples across the Tiger range. However, Liu et al. (2018) used whole-genome sequencing analyses from 32 voucher specimens that resolve six statistically robust monophyletic clades. The higher resolution of data obtained from nuclear genomes highlights little admixture and gene flow between the six subspecies. Nevertheless, there is incomplete congruence between mitochondrial and nuclear DNA results for P. t. amoyensis, suggesting the inclusion of more samples in future analysis to confirm its relationship with P. t. jacksoni.

Liu et al. (2018) concluded that six extant subspecies exist based on genome-wide analysis:

  • Amur Tiger (P. t. altaica): Russian Far East and northeastern China.
  • Northern Indochinese Tiger (P. t. corbetti): Indochina north of the Malayan Peninsula.
  • Malayan Tiger (P. t. jacksoni): Peninsular Malaysia.
  • Sumatran Tiger (P. t. sumatrae): Sumatra.
  • Bengal Tiger (P. t. tigris): Indian sub-continent.
  • South China Tiger (P. t. amoyensis), although this subspecies has not been directly observed in the wild since the 1970s and is possibly extinct.


Three subspecies previously recognised based on morphology are extinct:

  • Bali Tiger (P. t. balica Schwarz, 1912): Bali.
  • Javan Tiger (P. t. sondaica (Temminck, 1844)): Java.
  • Caspian Tiger (P. t. virgata (Illiger, 1815)): dry river valleys of the Takla Makan, western slopes of the Tianshan mountains, Amudarya and Syrdarya river valleys, shores of the Caspian Sea, Elburz mountains, eastern Turkey, Tigris and Euphrates River valleys.


However, given the varied interpretations of existing data, the taxonomy of this species is currently under review by the IUCN SSC Cat Specialist Group.
Microcebus berthae is the name given to the diminutive Rufous-colored Mouse Lemur from Kirindy/CFPF, originally called M. myoxinus by Schmid and Kappeler (1994).

Presbytis femoralis formerly was regarded as a subspecies of P. melalophos (Thorington and Groves 1970, Medway 1983, Napier 1985, Oates et al. 1994), but later was recognized as a species with three subspecies: P. f. femoralis, P. f. percura and P. f. robinsoni (Groves 2001, Meyer et al. 2011, Groves and Roos 2013, Roos et al. 2014).

Genetic data suggested that at least P. f. femoralis and P. f. robinsoni were different species (Ang 2010, Nijman 2019), which also agreed with their morphological characters. However, resolving all subspecies-level boundaries within banded langurs required data for P. f. percura, which was the least studied and only found in the Riau Province in Sumatra (Rizaldi et al. 2019). Complete mitochondrial genomes were later obtained for P. f. percura in the wild, and based on multiple species delimitation algorithms (PTP, ABGD, Objective Clustering) applied to a dataset covering 40 species and 43 subspecies of Asian colobines, all three subspecies of banded langurs were resurrected to species (Ang et al. 2020).

Presbytis femoralis was described by Martin (1838) based on specimens collected by Raffles (1821) from Singapore. However, Martin (1838) gave the distribution as "Sumatra etc.", not mentioning Singapore explicitly, resulting in some confusion over the actual type locality (Miller 1934, Low and Lim 2015). Miller (1934) determined Singapore as the actual type locality, and the secretariat of the International Code of Zoological Nomenclature confirmed the authorship of P. femoralis as Martin, the year of publication as 1838, and the type locality as Singapore (Low and Lim 2015).

Presbytis femoralis is uniform black on the dorsum with a white band traversing down the chest and abdomen, the inside of the humeri from the axilla, and the inside of the thighs (Raffles 1821, Martin 1838, Groves 2001). In contrast to the adult pelage, infants are born with a white coat with a broad black band from the head, along the spine, to the tail, and intersected by a black band passing along the shoulders to the outer surfaces of both forearms, forming a distinctive cruciform (cross-like) black pattern on the dorsum. A faint black line runs from the dark spinal stripe along the hind legs to the feet. The crown and facial hair are completely white from the frontal view. The forehead is slightly dark compared to the pale colour of the face (Pocock 1928, Ang et al. 2010).

Muona (2021) found Corticaria cucujiformis to be a complex of two species: Corticaria andreasi (=C. strandi) in northern Europe, and C. cucujiformis in the Mediterranean area (described from Corsica).

The previous concept of C. cucujiformis was assessed for the European Red List in 2010 and at that time, C. planula was considered as a synonym, and was included in Annex II of the European Habitats Directive. However, recent investigations indicate that C. planula is an North-American species (probably itself a complex of two species), closely related to the north European C. andreasi (Muona 2021).

authority: López-Luna, Cupul-Magaña, Escobedo-Galván, González-Hernández, Centenero-Alcalá, Rangel-Mendoza, Ramírez-Ramírez & Cazares-Hernández, 2018
taxonomicNotes: López-Luna et al. (2018, 2021) described and characterized this range-restricted mud turtle (Kinosternon vogti) from the coastal Bahía de Banderas region of Jalisco, Mexico. Based on morphologic and genetic analysis it is most closely related to K. cora, K. chimalhuaca and K. hirtipes.
This species was described from Lanzarote, but it actually occurs only on Fuerteventura. This is probably due to a confusion of museum labels.
This group was revised in Mordan and Frias Martins (2001).

The use of Lutra for four New World otter species (L. felina, L. canadensis, L. longicaudis, L. provocax) has been widespread in the past. Van Zyll de Jong (1972, 1987) separated this group into the genus Lontra based on morphological criteria, which was confirmed by Wozencraft (1993). Koepfli and Wayne (1998) and Koepfli et al. (2008) re-confirmed this classification based on molecular data. Thus, nowadays the generic name Lontra should be accepted as valid for all New World otters (except Pteronura).

Haas (1969) recognised eight races/subspecies of Unio crassus. Subsequently, all of these subspecies were considered junior synonyms of U. crassus.

However, recent integrative approaches, including molecular analyses, have changed the taxonomy and systematics of several of these races of the U. crassus complex). This includes Unio nanus which was considered a valid species distinct from Unio crassus (Lopes-Lima et al. 2024).

Unio nanus was described by Lamarck in 1819 based on a specimen from the Franche-Comté region of France. The species was considered by Haas (1969) to be a synonym of U. crassus batavus and distinct from the subspecies U. crassus cytherea, but due to the overlapping distribution between these two nominal taxa in the Rhône basin, where a single molecular lineage was found (Lopes-Lima et al. 2024), U. c. cytherea was considered to be a junior synonym of U. nanus.

U. crassus crassus sensu Prié and Puillandre (2014) belongs to U. nanus (Lopes-Lima et al. 2024).

Previously, Unio desectus was considered by Haas (1969) a synonym of Unio crassus bruguierianus, one of the Unio crassus races occurring in Greece and Türkiye, and over the last decades, it has been included within Unio crassus (Lopes-Lima et al. 2017).

However, a recent molecular study re-evaluating the taxonomy and evolution of the Unio crassus species complex has considered U. desectus as a separate and valid species (Lopes-Lima et al. 2024).

Haas (1969) recognised eight races/subspecies of Unio crassus. Subsequently, all of these subspecies were considered junior synonyms of U. crassus. More recently, however, integrative approaches, including molecular data, have changed the taxonomy and systematics of several of these races of the U. crassus complex.

The Iberian subpopulations of U. crassus batavus were considered to be the valid species U. tumidiformis, separate from U. crassus (Reis and Araujo 2009).

Unio crassus mongolicus was placed in a separate genus as Middendorffinaia mongolica (Lopes-Lima et al. 2020).

U. crassus bruguierianus, U. crassus carneus, Unio crassus cytherea, Unio crassus ionicus, and Unio crassus gontierii were considered as valid species U. bruguierianus, U. carneus, U. ionicus, U. nanus, and U. gontierii, respectively, separate from U. crassus (Araujo et al. 2018, Lyubas et al. 2022, Lopes-Lima et al. 2024).

U. crassus crassus sensu Prié and Puillandre (2014) belongs to U. nanus, while U. crassus courtillieri belongs to U. crassus (Lopes-Lima et al. 2024).
Rhinopithecus brelichi was formerly considered a subspecies of Rhinopithecus roxellana, but is now accepted as specifically distinct (Groves 2001).

authority: Cusi, Gagliardi-Urrutia, Brcko, Wake & von May, 2020
taxonomicNotes: Based on molecular phylogenetic analysis, Bolitoglossa awajun is the sister taxon to the clade composed of B. altamazonica and B. peruviana (Cusi et al. 2020). Additionally, B. awajun is morphometrically similar to these species yet it exhibits several morphological and color differences.
This species was described by Hausdorf et al. (2021) on the basis of genetic evidence.
The genus Obelus was revised by Ibáñez et al. (2003).

Unio tumidiformis was considered by Haas (1969) to be a junior synonym of Unio crassus and to belong to the subspecies Unio crassus batavus, which joined the French and Iberian populations of Unio crassus. It was considered a junior synonym of Unio crassus until the end of the 20th century. Since then, the taxonomy of the Iberian populations of Unio crassus has been revised based on molecular, morphological and reproductive characters, showing that they are well differentiated from those of northern and central Europe (Reis and Araujo 2009). Therefore, the Iberian populations of Unio crassus were included in a species redescribed as Unio tumidiformis, a valid and separate species from Unio crassus (Reis and Araujo 2009).

Changes to the systematics of Rhinobatos elevated the subgenus Glaucostegus to full generic status and placed this genus into a family of its own: Glaucostegidae (Compagno 2005, Last et al. 2016a). This species is often confused with Glaucostegus typus and Pacific Ocean records of G. granulatus are probably G. typus. A newly-described species, Glaucostegus younholeei Habib & Islam, 2021, is sympatric with G. granulatus in Bangladesh but the description of the new species does not change the taxonomic concept of G. granulatus.
Probably sister to S. petrophilus (Whitney et al. 2010); see also S. novacapitalis and S. speluncae. Monotypic.

Scytalopus gonzagi (del Hoyo and Collar 2016) is recognized as a species following work by Maurício et al. (2014). Appears to be closest to S. speluncae (which see) and perhaps S. iraiensis (Maurício et al. 2014). Monotypic.

A second species of Hypogeomys is known from the subfossil record. Hypogeomys australis was still extant 4,400 years ago, and once occurred from southeastern Madagascar north to at least the Antsirabe region (Goodman and Rakotondravony 1996).

Previously (del Hoyo and Collar 2014) placed in the genus Alopecoenas and before that in the genus Gallicolumba but transferred to Pampusana on the grounds of the latter’s priority (Bruce et al. 2016). Affinities unclear: may be close to P. beccarii and P. johannae, and the three appear also to be related to the P. jobiensis complex; alternatively, may be related to P. hoedtii, and more distantly to P. beccarii, P. johannae, P. sanctaecrucis and P. stairi. Monotypic.

Previously (del Hoyo and Collar 2014) placed in the genus Alopecoenas and before that in the genus Gallicolumba but transferred to Pampusana on the grounds of the latter’s priority (Bruce et al. 2016). Closely related to P. jobiensis, P. xanthonura and P. erythroptera; this group also has somewhat more distant affinities with P. sanctaecrucis, P. stairi and P. salamonis. Formerly considered a subspecies of P. xanthonura. Monotypic.
Otidiphaps nobilis, O. aruensis, O. cervicalis and O. insularis (del Hoyo and Collar 2014) were previously lumped as O. nobilis following Sibley and Monroe (1990, 1993).

Mandahl-Barth (1950) and Bank et al. (2002) treated this as a separate species. Waldén (1983) noted the status as a distinct species from nitidiuscula was uncertain. M.B. Seddon (pers. comm. 2010) noted that detailed surveying may allow the recognition of the species limits between A. nitidiuscula and A. giramica.

authority: Simmons, Flanders, Fils, Parker, Suter, Bamba, Douno, Keita, Morales, & Frick, 2021
taxonomicNotes: Species first described in 2021 after two individuals were caught in the Nimba Mountains in January 2018 as part of a biodiversity assessment. While Myotis nimbaensis shares similar features with three other large Myotis (subgenus Chrysopteron) species with orange dorsal fur occur in Africa (M. tricolor, M. welwitschii, and M. morrisi), it is easily distinguishable due to its body size and various color patterns (Simmons et al. 2021).
<span id="docs-internal-guid-70da6615-7fff-3bee-a83e-74382b0f5da8">Females and larval stages have not yet been formally described (for a general description of a larva, see Fanti 2022, p. 393).

Ballantyne and Lambkin 2013 defined Luciola Laporte 1833 s. str., based on specimens of the type species from Pisa, Italy, and gave extensive diagnosis mainly relying on features of male genitalia. Some of the Luciola species in McDermott (1966) were assigned to Luciola Laporte 1833 s. str.; some others, regarded as heterogeneous, were assigned to Luciola s. lato sensu Ballantyne et al. 2015. Ballantyne et al. 2019 proposed to assign a new genus for Luciola s. lato, but no further taxonomic treatment has yet been published. The generic diagnosis relies largely on features of male genitalia, which have not yet been studied for this species (see Müller 1946). M. Novák (pers. comm.) has compared the male genitalia of Luciola novaki with other European Luciola species (unpublished data) and he states that aedagi of L. novaki look completely different from those of L. italica and L. mingrelica (= East European “L. lusitanica”) while these two latter species show very similar looking male genitalia between them. The difference seems to such extent that it might even lead to a possible change of genus for this species in the future (M. Novák pers. comm.).

Pica pica, P. mauritanica, P. asirensis and P. hudsonia (del Hoyo and Collar 2016) were previously lumped as P. pica following AERC TAC (2003), AOU (1998 and supplements), Christidis and Boles (2008), Cramp et al. (1977-1994) and Sibley and Monroe (1990, 1993).

The taxonomic treatment of Petauroides volans is contested and not fully resolved. A single species has long been accepted. However, Jackson (2015) and Jackson and Groves (2015) recognised three species: Petauroides volans, P. minor and P. armillatus, based on limited published evidence. A subsequent genetic study provided some support to this treatment (McGregor et al. 2020), and the three species are currently listed in the American Society of Mammalogists Mammal Diversity Database (version 1.13). In its most recent conservation assessment, the Australian government recognised two species: P. volans (including P. armillatus) and P. minor (Threatened Species Scientific Committee 2022a,b). However, that split is not recognised in a recent taxonomic review of Australasian marsupials (Baker et al. 2023), by the Australasian Mammal Taxonomy Consortium (as at August 2024) and, following this, by Jackson (2023). The previous Red List account was for the single species, P. volans; until the taxonomy is fully resolved, and the IUCN SSC Australasian Marsupial and Monotreme Specialist Group continues to follow this treatment.

Spalax istricus was previously listed as a subspecies of S. graecus but has since been recognized as a separate species due to recent molecular genetic and detailed morphological studies (Németh et al. 2013).

The three known subpopulations, separated by the Carpathian Mountains, can be regarded as separate species, and assigned to already described taxa, this includes the Oltenia Blind Mole-rat.
This species is closely related to Ogilbia deroyi and further taxonomic work is needed to clarify the differences (Møller et al. 2005).

M. portoricensis and M. grandis were previously lumped and listed as Pyrrhulagra portoricensis (del Hoyo and Collar 2016) and before that in the genus Loxigilla following AOU (1998 and supplements); Sibley and Monroe (1990, 1993); Stotz et al. (1996). However, Pyrrhulagra now appears to be a junior synonym of Loxigilla, so the name Melopyrrha is adopted. P. grandis is again recognised as a full species following Garrido and Wiley (2003) and formal approval by the North American Classification Committee in 2021. Monotypic.

Edolisoma tenuirostre, E. insperatum, E. nesiotis, E. monacha, E. rostratum, E. meyerii, E. obiense, E. grayi, E. remotum and E. salomonis (del Hoyo and Collar 2016) were previously lumped as Coracina tenuirostris following Christidis and Boles (2008) and Sibley and Monroe (1990, 1993).
