Until recently, the silvered langurs of Indochina were considered conspecific with the Sundaic Silvered Langur (Trachypithecus cristatus), which was thought to be widely distributed across parts on Indonesia, Malaysia, Thailand, Viet Nam, Cambodia, Lao PDR, and Myanmar. During the past few years, genetic analyses have supported the assignment of all non-Sundaic populations to the species T. germaini, and subsequently called for a further division of the Indochinese taxon into two species (Groves 2001, Nadler et al. 2005, Roos et al. 2008). The Indochinese populations occurring west of the Mekong River in Viet Nam, Cambodia, Lao PDR, Thailand, and Myanmar have been  attributed to T. germaini, or the Indochinese Silvered Langur, while populations occurring east of the Mekong River in Viet Nam, Cambodia, and Lao PDR have been attributed to T. margarita, or the Annamese Silvered Langur (Hoang Minh Duc et al. 2012a, Nadler and Brockman 2014, Nadler et al. 2005, Roos et al. 2008, Roos et al. 2014). Recent data based on a study of populations separated by the Mekong River provide support for the species split but indicate that the Mekong, while limiting gene flow, is not an impermeable barrier (Moody 2018). Furthermore, this analysis revealed that a population in north-eastern Cambodia was distinguishable compared to other study populations, both west and east of the Mekong. This may be indicative of a species barrier farther east of the Mekong that extends into Viet Nam but additional sampling of populations in both Lao and Viet Nam are critical to better understanding the limits between these species (Moody 2018). Thus, taxonomic analyses should be expanded outside of Cambodia with the aim of clarifying the boundary between these taxa and the limits of their distribution.

Hill (1953) listed six subspecies of Loris tardigradus. Groves (1989) recognized a single species, L. tardigradus, with three “sharply distinct” subspecies in Sri Lanka and two in India (see Schulze and Meier 1995). In 1998, however, Groves argued for two species: the very small and narrow snouted monotypic red form (L. tardigradus) from the wet zone of Sri Lanka, and the larger grey or ashy form (L. lydekkerianus) in India (two subspecies) and Sri Lanka (two subspecies). This taxonomy was followed in Groves (2001, 2005) and Brandon-Jones et al. (2004).

A phylogenetic analysis by Pozzi et al. (2015) and a morphological study by Gamage et al. (2017) also confirmed the validity of the two species. In 2004, Nekaris and Jaywardene showed that nycticeboides, also from the wet zone of Sri Lanka, is a subspecies of tardigradus, contra Groves (2001, 2005) and Brandon-Jones et al. (2004), who placed it as a subspecies of lydekkerianus. Gamage et al. (2017) argued that the slender loris from Mirigama, Gampaha District, Western Province of Sri Lanka was unique in its facial features, and cranial and post-cranial morphology, and distinct as such from L. t. tardigradus south of its range. They named it L. t. parvus. They also found evidence for a distinct, high-altitude (above 800 m) taxon in the southwestern wet zone in the Deniyaya-Rakwana mountains, which they declined to name pending more information. As such in 2017, L. tardigradus was believed to comprise three subspecies: L. t. tardigradus, L. t. nycticeboides, and L. t. parvus all from Sri Lanka. This is the taxonomy that is followed by the Primate Specialist Group.

Gamage et al. (2019) subsequently carried out a phylogenetic analysis, which they believed largely confirmed this arrangement for the Sri Lankan lorises, but this was shown to be insufficient by Nijman et al. (2020). As such, more robust genetic studies are needed.

This species was referred to the genus Platemys since its description (Freiberg, 1945), but McBee et al. (1985) demonstrated the marked karyotypic difference of the four species P. spixii, P. macrocephala, P. pallidipectoris, and P. radiolata from Platemys platycephala. As a result, Iverson (1986) separated these four species out from Platemys and placed them in the resurrected genus Acanthochelys. This was corroborated by Derr et al. (1987) based on allozymes. No subspecies have been described for Acanthochelys pallidipectoris.

The previous Red List assessments for Galápagos tortoises treated the various allopatric island populations as subspecies of Chelonoidis nigra, as did several authors (Pritchard 1996, Caccone et al. 1999, Beheregaray et al. 2003, Fritz and Havas 2007, TTWG 2007, Rhodin et al. 2008). However, other authors have considered them as full species based on morphology (Bour 1980, Fritts 1983, Ernst and Barbour 1989) and more recently several researchers (Caccone et al. 2002; Russello et al. 2005, 2007; Poulakakis et al. 2008, 2012, 2015; Chiari et al. 2009) have treated most of them as full species based on congruent patterns of mitochondrial and nuclear variation. This elevated species-level taxonomy has been largely accepted by TTWG (2009, 2014) and TEWG (2015) for most phylogenetic lineages of Galápagos tortoises. This Red List assessment therefore now treats C. darwini as a full species, rather than retaining its previous subspecies ranking from earlier Red List assessments.

This species was described based on a record from Phong Nha-Ke Bang misidentified as Trimeresurus kanburensis (Orlov et al. 2004). Grismer et al. (2008) suggested that the species should be transferred to Viridovipera on the basis of morphological evidence. This change is supported by the molecular analysis of Dawson et al. (2008), who specifically investigated the phylogenetic position of this species and provided more detailed justification for transferring the species to Viridovipera. Subsequently moved back to Trimeresurus.
This species was long recognized in the genus Trimeresurus. It has sometimes been confused with T. venustus and T. purpureomaculatus (see David et al. 2004; Malhotra and Thorpe 2004).

Liu et al. (2012) conducted a molecular phylogenetic analysis of Protobothrops maolanensis and concluded that genetic evidence supports its recognition as a full species by Yang et al. (2011). Guo et al. (2016) subsequently conducted a phylogenetic study of the genus which supported its recognition as a species distinct from, but sister to, the morphologically similar Protobothrops trungkhanensis (which had not been included in the genetic analysis of Liu et al. 2012).
This species was formerly classified as Atropoides occiduus following recommendations by Castoe et al. (2005) but was designated as a separate species following Smith and Ferrari-Castro (2008).

Arnold et al. (2008) revised the taxonomy of this species, formerly considered the only endemic species of Hemidactylus on Cape Verde and including the subspecies Hemidactylus bouvieri boavistensis (Boavista and Sal) and H. b. razoensis (Santo Luzia and Raso) in addition to populations from Fogo and São Nicolau unassigned to any subspecies. These authors re-elevated H. b. boavistensis to full species status and described the Fogo population as a new species, H. lopezjuradoi. The São Nicolau population was retained within H. bouvieri on the basis that available material is too poorly-preserved to permit morphological description, but was found to be genetically distinct from typical H. bouvieri. Arnold et al. (2008) consider that once better material becomes available this form will warrant description as a distinct taxon.
Thrasops schmidti was elevated to specific status by Broadley and Wallach (2002) on morphological and biogeographical grounds, having previously been considered a subspecies of T. jacksonii.

The status of Shinisauridae, erected by Ahl (1930), as a distinct, monotypic family was supported by Macey et al. (1999). Ziegler et al. (2008) determined that morphological and mitochondrial genetic differentiation among extant subpopulations was insufficient to justify the recognition of Chinese and Vietnamese subpopulations as separate taxa or subspecies, despite an apparent geographical separation of at least 500 km.

Synonym = Testudo leithii Günther, 1869 (non Testudo leithii Carter, 1852)). The species was classified into the subgenus Pseudotestudo by Loveridge and Williams (1957). This subgeneric name is nonapplicable for T. kleinmanni because the taxon was based on juvenile characters not retained into adulthood (Bour 1989).

Specimens from the Wolkberg, an isolated mountain, appear to represent this species, and morphological differences are likely to represent intraspecific variation (Deschodt and Davis 2019). The Wolkberg record is treated as belonging to Gyronotus glabrosus for the purposes of this assessment, but a molecular study is however needed to confirm this (C. Deschodt pers. comm. 2023).
This species is "very close" to the more recently-described Frankenbergius opacus (Davis et al. 2020).

Specimens from the two northern localities exhibit morphological differences from those from the southern sites, and their correct identification was unclear when Davis et al. (2020) went to press. Abdalla et al. (2018) subsequently resolved this issue, confirming that one of these - at Kommandokraal Farm - corresponds to Macroderes foveatus (A. Davis pers. comm. 2023).

A dead female specimen of uncertain identity has been recorded from the coast close to Vredendal (C. Deschodt pers. comm. 2023), very close to Kommandokraal. Fresh specimens, or diagnosable males, are needed from this locality to confirm whether this represents M. foveatus.

Davis et al. (2020) note that "By virtue of very small body size and mostly close morphological similarity" species of Odonotoloma are difficult to distinguish, and that the entire genus is in need of taxonomic revision. This species was until recently known only from female specimens. The first male was collected in August 2021, and a redescription is pending (G. Daniel pers. comm. 2023).
Usually considered to be closest to D. pacifica, D. oceanica and D. aurorae. Monotypic.
This species was described from material that included records attributed to Cnemaspis dickersonae by Spawls et al. (2018).
This species was described from material that included records attributed to Cnemaspis africana by Spawls et al. (2018).

Nisaetus philippensis and N. pinskeri (del Hoyo and Collar 2014) were previously lumped as N. philippensis following Haring et al. (2006), which before then was placed in the genus Spizaetus following Sibley and Monroe (1990, 1993).
This species is part of a species complex (Veron et al. 2016).

According to Veron et al. (2016), this species is a synonym of Acropora retusa. It is accepted according to the WoRMS online database (accessed June 2022) and the revision of Acropora species by Wallace et al. (2012). Molecular work is recommended to clarify the taxonomic status of the record in southeast Australia.

According to Veron (2000) and Veron et al. (2016), this species is a synonym of Acropora appressa. This species was considered ‘unresolved’ by Wallace (1999) and was not included in the revision and catalogue of Acropora by Wallace et al. (2012). It is valid according to the WoRMS online database (accessed June 2022). In WoRMS, A. appressa is taxon inquirendum.

Cyrtodactylus jeyporensis was described by Beddome in 1877 based on specimens collected from Pathinge Hill, Jeypore, Orissa (Smith 1935). Molecular phylogenetic data reveal that Geckoella is embedded within Cyrtodactylus, and this placement is now widely accepted and hence followed here.

The species' assessment change between 2007 and 2022 was the result of (i) systematic work: the species was previously published as Hydrobia marocana (Pallary 1921) and was reported from four localities two of them were in Algeria (Ghamizi 1998). Boulaassafer et al. 2021 reported recent systematic findings which led to assigning the species to the genus Corrosella (), since the genus is very restricted to freshwater springs, it is highly endemic and unlikely to be the same species in Algeria. Boulaassafer et al. 2021 restrict the presence of C. marocana in Morocco. The species is now restricted to ditches and irrigation canals where individuals were found in very low abundance attached to stones or dead branches.

The Chinese Sturgeon Acipenser sinensis is restricted to the main channel of the Yangtze and the Pearl rivers and the East and South China Seas. Though there is still disagreement about the taxonomy of the Pearl and Yangtze River populations, Chinese scholars commonly divide Chinese Sturgeon into two populations; one is the Pearl River Chinese Sturgeon, which spawns in spring, and is close to dying out. The other is the Yangtze River Chinese Sturgeon, which spawns in autumn and still maintains a certain amount below the Gezhouba Dam.

Three regional subspecies originally described by Wilson et al. (1991) have been confirmed by Cronin et al. (1996): Enhydra lutris lutris (Linnaeus, 1758) from Japan in the southwest Pacific throughout the Asian range of the Kuril Islands to the Kamchatka Peninsula and the Commander Islands, E. l. kenyoni from the western Aleutian Islands to Prince William Sound, Alaska, USA, along the Pacific coast of Canada and into Oregon state in the continental USA, and E. l. nereis (Merriam, 1904) from central to southern California, USA.

This species was established as Micropanchax petnehazyi by Nagy and Vreven in 2018. Based on molecular, morphological and osteological data, Bragança et al. (2020, 2021) identified that species in southern Africa belong to the polyphyletic genus Lacustricola. Until a new generic name for the southern African group will be available in the future, we will use Lacustricola in this assessment. The species is also referred to as "Lacustricola" petnehazyi in current literature, awaiting the new generic name.

Specimens of this species were first collected in 2013. The species was retrieved in van der Merwe et al. (2020) as a phylogenetically distinct lineage and referred to there as N. sp. Northern Mozambique. ​Nothobranchius balamaensis was established as a species by Bragança and Chakona in Bragança et al. (2022).

This cryptic species was resurrected from synonymy with Pocillopora damicornis by Schmidt-Roach et al. (2014). Additional taxonomic research that applies molecular genetics methods is recommended (Smith et al. 2017).
This is an unrecognized species according to Veron et al. (2016). It is an accepted species in the WoRMS online database (accessed March 2023).
This cryptic species can be easily mistaken for a young colony of other species (Forsman and Birkeland 2009).
Taxonomic research may be warranted (B. Cowburn pers. comm. 2024).

Acropora pagoensis, which was previously published as Data Deficient on the Red List in 2008, is now a synonym of A. eurystoma. It is considered a valid species in WoRMS (accessed November 2023). However, further research is needed to clarify the differences between this species and the resembling species Acropora tenuis (Veron et al. 2016).

Acropora copiosa and A. formosa, which were previously published on the Red List in 2008 as Data Deficient and Near Threatened, respectively, are now synonyms of A. muricata. This is the type species for the genus Acropora (Wallace 1999), and it has many synonyms.
Montipora hirsuta, which was previously published on the Red List in 2008 as Near Threatened, is now a synonym of M. carinata (Veron et al. 2016, WoRMS online database accessed April 2023).

This species has been treated as a variation of Canariella hispidula by Ibanez et al. (1995). However, currently, this form is considered as a valid species (MolluscaBase 2024).

MolluscaBase (2024) recognises two subspecies: the nominate subspecies Canariella berthelotii ssp. berthelotii (d'Orbigny, 1836) Canariella berthelotii ssp. beata (Wollaston, 1878); the latter taxon was formerly included as a subspecies of Canariella fortunata which is itself now included in the synonymy of Canariella berthelotii.

Bank et al. (2002) placed this species in genus Caseolus, but did not give any grounds for this revision. Seddon (2008) noted that this species is apparently allied to Caseolus calva rather than to Lemniscia michaudi. Wollaston (1878) and Pilsbry (1893) also commented on the lack of similarity to the type species from Porto Santo.

We follow MolluscaBase (2022) retaining the species in genus Caseolus.
This is a distinct taxon related to the D. oxytropis group, with one further extant taxon and several fossil species. It is recognised on size, morphology and distribution.

This species was listed as Actinella Form II in Waldén (1983), whereas other authors considered the species to be part of Actinella fausta. Bank et al. (2002) listed the taxa, suggesting that the type locality must be Estreito de Camara de Lobos.

Seddon (2008) noted that it appeared closely allied to the Quaternary fossil species Actinella (Faustella) promontoriensis, which also has a bisinuosity, which is more strongly developed, and a shell which appears more globose, with a more shouldered whorl (not as acutely keeled). A. promontoriensis is a common fossil species on the peninsular at the eastern end of the island.
The status of this species has been confirmed by phylogenetic studies based on DNA sequences (Elejalde et al. 2009).
The family Vitrinidae in the Canary Islands was treated by Valido et al. (1999).
The family Vitrinidae in the Canary Islands was treated by Valido et al. (1999).
The genus Obelus was revised by Ibáñez et al. (2003).

Traditionally, this species is placed within Ramusculus (e.g. Schileyko 1998, Balashov 2016a), and morphologically the species is not correctly placed in Brephulopsis (table by R. Bank in Gümüş and Neubert 2012), however, we currently follow MolluscaBase (Bank and Neubert 2017) in placing the species within the genus Brephulopsis until genetic confirmation is obtained.

From Seddon (2008): "There are differing views on the status of the Truncatellina species on Madeira; Cameron et al. (1993), Seddon (1995) and Cameron et al. (2006) all treat the dentate form as T. cf. callicratis and the edentate species as T. linearis. Waldén (1983) suggests that T. linearis might be conspecific with T. atomus, and an edentate form of T. cf. callicratis. Whereas Hutterer & Groh (1993) added 3 new species, one from Madeira and two from Porto Santo. They treat the edentate and dentate fossils from Madeira and Porto Santo as different taxa. Without a widespread review of recent and fossil material of Truncatellina from Madeira, Canary islands and North Africa these differing views will not be resolved, hence all taxa are figured to add to further research."

Waldén (1983) also suggested that the species might be conspecific with T. molecula. Hutterer and Groh (1993) rejected these associations of Waldén.
A polytypic species.  Some variations may represent distinct species or subspecies.

This is a polytypical species; in addition to the nominotypical subspecies, three further subspecies are listed by MolluscaBase (2024): M. m. castellana (Monterosato, 1892), M. n. impervia (Monterosato, 1892), and M. n. silvestrii (Cafici, 1885). The nominotypical subspecies was defined by a lectotype designation by Giannuzzi-Savelli et al. (1986).

The Spanish subpopulations of Orculella bulgarica have been assigned by Arrébola et al. (2012) to a separate species O. aragonica Westerlund, 1897, closely allied to O. bulgarica. This separation was based on anatomical (mostly by the particular structure of the penial caecum) and molecular differences, although they are sibling species in shell morphology. Only one subpopulation from Turkey was included in this study. The incorporation of more samples of O. bulgarica from different localities, and particularly DNA sequences of O. bulgarica lamellata, could give more information about the singularities of the Spanish subpopulations.

F. Welter-Schultes argues in AnimalBase (http://www.animalbase.org/ accessed 17.03.2016) that the name Orcula dolium f. aragonica Westerlund, 1897 used by Arrébola et al. (2012) could be unavailable without providing evidence that the name had been used for a species or subspecies before 1985 (Art. 45.6.4.1).

This genus originally contained two species, one from the Canary Islands, the other from Madeira, and characterised by large orthostyle shells. Further research on the Canarian taxa L. cuticula, has shown this to be a member of the family Vitrinidae (Alonso et al. 2000, Bank et al. 2002). Hence, Lampadia is now a monotypic genus, with one species restricted to Porto Santo (Seddon 2008).
This species is also known as Ochthephila spirulina (amendation by Waldén 1983).

This species was originally described as a subspecies of L. sphinctostoma, but Waldén (pers. comm. 1986) believed that the taxa merited specific status. Bank et al. (2002) treat this as a separate species in their checklist and Seddon (2008) also treated it as a distinct species.

Fauna Europaea lists this taxon as a separate species. According to Fiorentino et al. (2010), it is a junger synonym of T. villicus. Earlier K. L. Pfeiffer (1946) proposed that T. sardonius should be treated as a subspecies of T. villicus. According to the assessors of this species, this view should be retained. However, as the taxon is listed as a species in its own rights in Fauna Europea, it is here assessed as such.

The genus Plutoria was created by Stabile. The group was revised by Mordan and Martins (2001) and then again by Giusti et al. (2011), who, based on a phylogenetic study, reported that the genus Plutonia was not supported and created a new genus, Azorivitrina. Following Giusti et al. (2011) this species was moved from the genus Plutonia to the new genus, Azorivitrina.

This is a polytypic species with two subspecies:
  • T. romagnolii romagnolii (Dutailly, 1867), and T. r. deschampsiana (Hagenmüller, 1888).
  • T. romagnolii romagnolii: Hélice de Corte
T. romagnolii deschampsiana: Hélice de la Tartagine [note: the subspecies does not occur in the Tartagine valley as could be presumed from the common name].

Seddon (2008) reported that Wollaston (1878) and Waldén (1983) noted that var. subdubia is apparently intermediate between L. wollastoni and L. nivosa; both authors nonetheless treated wollastoni as a distinct species. The intermediate is only found in a very small area on Pico do Concelho, whereas in most parts of their ranges, the two species are clearly and distinctly separable. The subfossil shells from the Quaternary deposits below Pico do Concelho are larger than the living shells.

Mandahl-Barth (1950) treated the population of Ilheu de Fora off the eastern coast as a distinct subspecies. MolluscaBase (2025) recognises two subspecies, Leptaxis wollastoni ssp. forensis (Wollaston, 1878) and Leptaxis wollastoni ssp. wollastoni (R.T. Lowe, 1852)

Waldén (1983) recognized a varietal name used by Wollaston, Caseolus commixtus var. pusilla, as meriting specific status, giving it the name baixoensis. Seddon (2008) reported difficulty separating Caseolus baixoensis Waldén, 1983 and Caseolus compactus vigiae Waldén, 1983, and noted that in the absence of type material of the latter species, considered that these two species may be conspecific.

Arianta chamaeleon is a polytypic species with a number of currently accepted subspecies
  • Arianta chamaeleon chamaeleon (L. Pfeiffer, 1868).
  • A. chamaeleon carnica (Ehrmann, 1910).
  • A. chamaeleon wiedemayri (Kobelt, 1903).
  • A. chamaeleon subglobosa (Ehrmann, 1910).
  • A. chamaeleon tullina (Ehrmann, 1910).

However, the taxonomic status of these subspecies is problematic.
The taxon has been defined by a Lectotype designation by Giannuzzi-Savelli et al. (1986).
Theba grasseti was previously named Helix grasseti.

authority: Neiber, R. Vega-Luz, Ro. Vega-Luz & Koenemann, 2011
taxonomicNotes: This is a recently described species.
Kneubühler et al. (2022) created the new genus Raeticella Kneubühler, Baggenstos & Neubert, 2022 for this species.

This species was previously assessed under the name Leiostyla laevigata. Bank et al. (2002) proposed Leiostyla falknerorum nom. nov. as a new name for Pupa laevigata Lowe, 1852 as they considered it to be preoccupied by Pupa laevigata von Gallenstein, 1848. This name was known to Pilsbry (1922-26) and Waldén (1983), who both considered it a forgotten name.

According to Gittenberger (1991), the status of Helicella (Jacosta) syrensis carinatoglobosa (Haas 1934) remains unclear. Gittenberger (1991) supports that 'this is most probably a separate species, restricted to a small area in the southern part of the [Cyprus] island. Its generic position is unclear'.
Fauna Europaea is classifying the taxon under Trochoidea (Trochoidea) carinatoglobosa (F. Haas 1934).

MolluscaBase (2021) is classifying the taxon as Xerocrassa carinatoglobosa (F. Haas, 1934)
The species is cited as Xerosecta sp. in the Italian check-list.

According to Fauna Europaea this species is a synonym of Mastus grandis grandis (Mousson 1859). Nevertheless, according to Parmakelis (2003) and Parmakelis et al. (2005) this is a well separated species and thus here is treated as a separate species. Further taxonomic work is needed for this species, particularly to confirm that the Styra population is definitely conspecific with the type population (E. Neubert pers. comm, 8 July 2023).

Morelet (1860) extended the range of L. azorica to Santa Maria as variety γ minor, which Backhuys (1975) elevated to subspecific level. Van Riel et al. (2005), using molecular data, found evidence for specific separation of the populations from Santa Maria. Backhuys (1975) states the type locality as “Azores, Santa Maria, Ribeiro, Almagreira”; however, just after that statement, the author mentions the two specimens from Pico Alto as “paratypes”. Ribeiro, Almagreira is at the footsteps of Pico Alto; for that reason, although confusing, this locality imprecision is not worth correcting. On-going research has extended the range of L. minor to the entire Pico Alto complex.

The taxonomic identity of this species, as Leptaxis minor Backhuys, 1975, is considered 'uncertain' by MolluscaBase (2022).

This is a polytypic species with two subspecies besides the nominotypical subspecies: T. acropachia ssp. pollonerae (Caziot, 1903) and T. acropachiassp. garciai (Hagenmüller, 1888).

According to recent research (Falkner ined.), the subpopulations in the northwest of the main distribution area adjacent to the Monte Cardo, which have been described as Helix (Campylaea) venacensis Caziot, 1903, form a well characterised distinct species which has wrongly been treated as a synonym of T. a. acropachia.

The Lauriidae of the Azores were revised by Martins (2023). The very few specimens referred to Leiostyla rugulosa collected on Pico could not be identified with certainty as this species on a morphological basis alone; hence, they were considered Leiostyla cf. rugulosa and are assessed here. Similar specimens from São Jorge and Terceira islands were provisionally treated as Leiostyla affn. rugulosa, and are provisionally assessed separately.

Morelet and Drouët’s (1857) original description does not mention type locality, so “The Azores” is currently accepted. Morelet (1860) only found it in Caldeira das Sete Cidades, São Miguel, thus restricting the type locality. Molecular studies by Van Riel et al. (2005) indicated that it is a separate species.
Closely related with M. abundans in molecular analyses (Parmakelis et al. 2005)
The generic/subgeneric status of this species must be confirmed. Comparative anatomic and genetic studies including closest species and genera (even North Africans) could be explanatory.
Neubert et al. (2014) placed this species in genus Maltzanella Hesse, 1917. However, based on the review of Korábek et al. (2022) the species was returned to genus Helix.

Iberus is an Iberian Peninsula endemic genus, that comprised 20 morphospecies of uncertain validity (traditional classifications conflicted with each other mostly in the number of species), some of them occupying a very restricted geographical area. They were characterized mostly on the basis of the shell morphology because the reproductive system shows no diagnostic features at the specific level (Puente 1994, Arrébola 1995). Recently, Elejalde, Madeira, Arrébola, Muñoz and Gomez-Moliner (2008) have studied partial DNA sequences of two mitochondrial genes (COI and 16S rRNA) from different specimens of Iberus, to test the validity of the described morphospecies of the genus. The analysis have revealed 11 main phylogroups, being genetically unique lineages that are allopatrically distributed and considered to have full species status. One of them is I. ortizi. Previously, I. ortizi had been considered a minor form of  I. alcarazanus (Ortiz de Zárate in García San Nicolás 1957) although García San Nicolás (1957) assigned it a subspecific status: I. alcarazanus ortizi. However, the data provided by a recent taxonomic revision (Martínez-Ortí et al. 2004) indicate that I. alcarazanus should be considered as a synonym of I. alonensis. Consequently, I. ortizi is a valid name (Elejalde et al. 2008).

Chueca et al. (2017a 2017b) confirm through molecular studies that Helix moraguesi Kobelt, 1883 is a junior synonym of Xerocrassa homeyeri homeyeri (Dorhn & Heynemann, 1962), valid name for this taxon.

C. grovesiana is taxonomically inhomogeneous and seems to represent a group of distinct species or at least a Rassenkreis, which is in urgent need of revision; the well differentiated form C. scissa (Paulucci, 1881), originally described as a variety of C. cavannae (Paulucci, 1881), belongs anatomically to the grovesiana-group (unpublished data of A. Hallgass).

There are four subspecies currently accepted: Tacheocampylaea cyrniaca cyrniaca, T. cyrniaca omphalophora, T. cyrniaca faucicola and T. cyrniaca arusalensis. Tacheocampylaea revelierii (Debeaux, 1867) [Helix] is a synonym to this species. In 2009, a fourth subspecies, T. c. arusalensis (Hagenmüller, 1888) – up to now forgotten – was found back near its type locality by G. Falkner, C. Régnier and B. Recorbet.
Manucerthia mana (del Hoyo and Collar 2016) was previously placed in the genus Oreomystis following AOU (1998 & supplements); Sibley & Monroe (1990, 1993).

authority: Varela-Soto, Abarca, Brenes-Mora, Aspinall, Leenders & Shepack, 2022

taxonomicNotes: This species was previously included within Pristurus rupestris, but was found to be a separate species, with its description in preparation (S. Carranza pers. comm. September 2022). The species is sympatric with P. rupestris.

Fernholm treats the genus Paramyxine Dean, 1908 as a synonym of Eptatretus Cloquet 1819 (Fernholm 1998, Fernholm and Quattrini 2008), while other authors treat Paramyxine as a valid genus (Kuo et al. 2010, McMillan and Wisner 2004, Wisner 1999). Eschmeyer and Fricke (2011) followed Fernholm's advice on this and place all Paramyxine species within Eptatretus. This results in some duplicate names: E. wisneri McMillan 1999 and E. wisneri (Kuo, Huang & Mok 1994) (formerly Paramyxine wisneri Kuo, Huang & Mok 1994); and E. fernholmi McMillan & Wisner, 2004 and E. fernholmi (Kuo, Huang & Mok, 1994) (formerly Paramyxine fernholmi Kuo, Huang & Mok, 1994). To resolve this issue, E. wisneri McMillan 1999 and E. fernholmi McMillan & Wisner, 2004, as the more recent descriptions (i.e., junior homonyms), were renamed as E. bobwisneri and E. luzonicus, respectively (Fernholm et al. 2013).
Loxops coccineus, L. ochraceus and L. wolstenholmei (del Hoyo and Collar 2016) were previously lumped as L. coccineus following AOU (1998 & supplements); Sibley & Monroe (1990, 1993).

The taxonomy of all the canids, including Lycaon pictus, is currently under review by the Canid Specialist Group. Standard taxonomic texts recognise five subspecies: L. pictus pictus in southern Africa, L. pictus lupinus in eastern Africa, L. pictus somalicus in north-eastern Africa Africa, L. pictus sharicus in central Africa, and L. pictus manguensis in West Africa (Wilson and Reeder 2005). Morphological and genetic data from southern and eastern Africa initially tended to support the pictus/lupinus division (Girman et al. 1993); however, later analyses suggested greater mixing between the two regions (Girman et al. 2001). Ongoing genomic analyses over a broader geographical area (Ramage et al. in prep) will inform the Canid Specialist Group assessment.
Hemignathus wilsoni (del Hoyo and Collar 2016) was previously listed as H. munroi following AOU (1998 & supplements); Sibley & Monroe (1990, 1993).

This species was described in some detail (including anatomical dissections and drawings) by Pilsbry and Cooke (1934). Additional dissections and drawings were made by Y. Kondo and appear in his unpublished Ph.D. thesis (1955).

This species seems closely related to Samoana conica, but its shells are dextral, whereas those of S. conica are sinistral. Its shell is generally yellowish-green in colour. It is the least common of the three Tutuila partulids.

It has been argued, on the basis of mandibular measurements, that Crocidura canariensis should be treated as a subspecies of C. sicula (Sarà 1996). However, other morphological, ecological, palaeontological and molecular evidence supports specific status (Michaux et al. 1991, Hutterer et al. 1992). Genetic distances suggest that C. canariensis and C. sicula diverged approximately five million years ago (Vogel et al. 2003), and both share karyotype with C. tarfayaensis (Vogel et al. 2006).
Formerly considered as a subspecies of Sicista subtilis, but now recognized as a separate species based on caryotype and DNA data (Kovalskaya et al. 2011, Lebedev et al. 2020).

For a long time, the westernmost representatives of the genus Spalax were identified as a single species, Spalax graecus. According to the recent studies (Németh et al. 2013, Csorba et al. 2015) the three known subpopulations, separated by the Carpathian Mountains, represent separate species which can be assigned to described taxa. One of them is Spalax antiquus Méhely’s Blind Mole-rat. The separation of Méhely’s Blind Mole-rat as a distinct species is highly supported by the combined evidences of morphology, multilocus phylogeny, biogeographical evidences and species congruence with past tectonic and climatic events (Németh et al. 2013).

According to present taxonomic knowledge, four subspecies are distinguished by Helgen et al. 2009: S. c. citellus; S. c. gradojevici with synonyms karamani and macedonicus; S. c. istricus with synonyms laskarevi and S. c. martinoi which has balcanicus and thracius as synonyms. In a detailed revision of palearctic rodents (Kryštufek and Vohralík 2012) distinguish only three subspecies: s. c. citellus with synonyms istricus, laskarevi and probably also martinoi and balcanicus; s. c. gradojevici with synonyms thracius and macedonicus; and s. c. karamani. The validity of some subspecies should be considered as uncertain until detailed genetic studies are performed.

There are two chromosome races of this species: Spermophilus suslicus odessanus (36 chromosomes) and Spermophilus suslicus sensu stricto (34 chromosomes). These are separated by the Dnieper River. Sometimes considered as separate species, genetic and acoustic data support the integrity of both races into one species (Matrosova et al. 2016).

The species was described as Sminthus loriger Nathusius in Nordmann, 1840, but for a long time was considered as subspecies of Sicista subtilis sensu lato (Sicista subtilis nordmanni). Now this taxon elevated to species level following Zagorodniuk and Selyunina (2009) and Cserkész et al. (2016).

Sicista nordmanni (Keyserling & Blasius, 1840) is synonym as it was pointed at by Chaworth-Musters (1934). Nomenclature was revised by Zagorodniuk (2009), and current name “Sminthus loriger Nathusius in Nordmann, 1840” listed as synonym of “Sicista nordmanni Keyserling et Blasius, 1840. Sicista loriger is geographically separated from Sicista subtilis s. str. (Zagorodniuk 2011).
Erroneous records from Israel were removed by Pisanty et al. (2022). The species is endemic only to Cyprus.

Originally considered as a subspecies of Bombus lapponicus, then as a subspecies of B. monticola, the taxon konradini Reinig was finally promoted to species rank by Martinet et al. (2018) based on genetic and semio-chemical evidence.
This taxon requires modern taxonomic investigation with molecular to confirm its status.

Stelis hispanica constitutes a taxonomically controversial and rare species. It was established (Dusmet 1921) from a single individual (“holotipo: 1♀, Valladolid, 14-VI-1892, Martínez Escalera leg., Colección Dusmet, MNCN”), parasitized by “un Stylops, que aparece bajo el cuarto segmento ventral”, as Dusmet (1921) reported.

Later, Warncke (1992), without examining the type, proposed it to be a synonym of Stelis annulata (Lepeletier, 184l). This was also the opinion followed by Ornosa et al. (2008, 2009), who did study the holotype with the parasite. Schwarz and Gusenleitner (2010) restored Stelis hispanica to the species level and added a new Iberian record. Curiously Schwarz and Gusenleitner (2010) said they did not see the parasite on the holotype; it does not really appear in the photos that the paper enclosed.

The species was originally assessed with a broad northern Mediterranean distribution from Spain, east to Italy, Croatia, Bulgaria and Türkiye. The species is now considered to be restricted to the Iberian Peninsula, with records from elsewhere now considered to refer to other species.
The taxon is a member of the Hylaeus nivalis group (Dathe 2000).
A monotypic genus.
The taxon is a member of the H. nivalis group (Dathe 2000).
A record of this species from Mont Ventoux is excluded at present as recent data suggest that the specimens from this region belong to a different, as yet undescribed, species (T. Brau pers. comm.).
The species is closely related to Lasioglossum minutulum.

In Gusenleitner and Schwarz (2002), the species is named Andrena roscipes Alfken, 1933. The correct name is A. roseipes, not A. roscipes. In the original description the name indeed is spelled A. roscipes, but this was a printer's error. It is obvious, that in choosing the species name Alfken refers to the red colour of the hind legs, thus according to ICZN § 32.5.1 the name A. roseipes is valid.
Ammobates melectoides is often understood as a subspecies of A. oraniensis. Taxonomy of this species needs to be studied in detail in the future.

Andrena heterodoxa was described from Sicily, first by Pérez (1903) and then later by Warncke (1980), as A. siciliana. However, Warncke did not examine Pérez's material and synonymised his taxon with A. bicolor Fabricius, 1775 based on the description.

Recently, it was found that A. siciliana Warncke, 1980 is a junior synonym of A. heterodoxa Pérez, 1903 (Wood 2023).

The taxonomic status of Ammobates handlirschi (Friese, 1895), a close north African relative to A. dusmeti, needs to be confirmed by more comprehensive study, especially by methods of population biology based on DNA.
The taxon is a member of the Hylaeus nivalis group (Dathe 2000).

Warncke (1975) described Andrena chelma from Mount Chelmos from female specimens. In the same publication, Warncke described Andrena pulicaria Warncke, 1975 (males and females) from Türkiye, but also included a paratype from Mount Chelmos from the same collecting event as the type series of A. chelma. The 'species' are almost identical, with only an extremely small and subtle difference in the shape of the process of the labrum. It would therefore seem highly likely that A. chelma is conspecific with A. pulicaria, but genetic data is required to establish species boundaries in this problematic subgenus.

This species was previously within the genus Pseudoeurycea but has recently been moved to the genus Aquiloeurycea (Rovito et al. 2015). The species has not been seen since the 1940s and is only known from the type locality, which is somewhat vague and now seems to all be converted to sugarcane fields (S. Rovito pers. comm. October 2019).

This species was described in 2017, based on a reanalysis of material from the type locality. The species from this location was originally identified as the Sardinian species Mercuria zopissa in Falkner et al. (2002), and occupies a habitat that has changed since first collection and hence population was considered to be declining and maybe now be extinct (Boeters and Falkner 2017).
This subterranean snail is known only from its type locality (Callot-Girardi 2012). It was described only from shells and has never been collected alive. Its taxonomic status is uncertain.

Bithynia hispanica was originally considered a synonym of B. leachii in Spain, but was elevated to species status by Jaume-Ramis & Martínez-Ortí (2021) and this is now reflected in MolluscaBase (2022).

Regnier et al. (2009) suggested that Horatia hadei (now placed as Daphniola hadei (E. Gittenberger, 1982)) was a synonym of this species, however Fauna Europea (Bank et al. 2006) and MolluscaBase (2024) list both species, Islamia epirana and Daphniola hadei, and as such, until further research is undertaken they are maintained as separate species.

The synonymisation of I. epirana with Islamia graeca (Schütt 1980) is not accepted by MolluscaBase and thus the distribution of I. epirana does not include Lake Amvrakia where I. graeca is distributed.

Szarowska and Falniowski (2011) showed that the three known subpopulations of this species, on the basis of phylogenetic analysis using individuals from three different Pseudamnicola populations in this region, are distinct species and only the subpopulation from the western part of the Taigetos Mountains corresponds to the previously described P. exilis.
The first assessment of this species was published under the incorrect species epithet, 'wolfischeri'.

This species was previously regarded as a subspecies of Grossuana euxina in 2011.  A new species concept of Grossuana euxina has been created to deal with the revised species concept of  Grossuana euxina which separated Grossuana vurliana from Greece as a distinct species.
According to Hauswald et al. (2008), this species shows no significant differences within the COI gene fragment to the Balkan widespread Valvata sp.1.

There is a disagreement regarding the generic placement of this species. Some retain the species in genus Trichonia (e.g. Boeters et al. 2017, Glöer 2022), whilst others place the species in genus Radomaniola, based on morphology and molecular data (Radea et al. 2013, 2017, Falniowski et al. 2012, Delicado and Hauffe 2022). Currently placed in genus Radomaniola by MolluscaBase (2025) following Delicado and Hauffe (2022).

authority: G.A. Holyoak, D.T. Holyoak & da Costa Mendes, 2017

taxonomicNotes:
Considering molecular, morphological and biogeographic evidence, Byrne et al. (2016) proposed new genus-level systematics for the former arrangement of Callicebus species from Hershkovitz (1990): Cheracebus Byrne et al. 2016 genus for the species in the Orinoco, Negro and upper Amazon basins; Callicebus Thomas, 1903 genus for the species in the Atlantic Forest; and Plecturocebus Byrne et al. 2016 genus for the species in the Amazon basin and Chaco.

Plecturocebus vieirai was described in 2012 from three localities in Pará State, Brazil (type locality: Renato River, a tributary of the right bank of the Teles Pires river, near the city of Cláudia, Mato Grosso; 11° 33' 00.15" S, 55° 10' 59.98" W) (Gualda-Barros et al. 2012), in an area formerly attributed to P. moloch sensu Hershkovitz (1990). The Vieira’s titi monkey is currently known to occur west of the Xingu River, east of the Iriri and Teles Pires rivers, southward to the headwaters of the Xingu and Teles Pires rivers, until the border between Amazonia–Cerrado biomes (Costa-Araújo et al. in press), and it is sister taxon to P. moloch (Boubli et al. 2019).

authority: Costa-Araújo, Silva-Jr., Boubli, Rossi, Hrbek & Farias, 2021
taxonomicNotes: Mico schneideri was described in 2021 for pseudo-cryptic marmosets endemic to the northern Juruena–Teles Pires interfluve, Mato Grosso State, Brazil. Marmosets from this region were stored in the Museu Paraense Emílio Goledi and known to scientists since 1995. They were, however, confounded with M. emiliae due to inadequacy of previous taxonomic assessments––entirely based on pelage colour of a few specimens––until new morphological, molecular and distribution data had shown that they are in fact morphologically diagnosable, form a fully supported clade and are allopatric, without evidence of distribution, morphological or genomic overlap with M. emiliae or another known species of Mico (Costa-Araújo et al. 2021).

Three elephant taxa remain from the sixteen elephant-like species that are known from across the planet in the Pleistocene: Asian Elephant (Elephas maximus), African Savanna Elephant (Loxodonta africana), and African Forest Elephant (Loxodonta cyclotis) (Faurby and Svenning 2015, Malhi et al. 2016). The Asian and African ancestral lineages diverged approximately seven million years ago, and the African Savanna and African Forest ancestral lineages began diverging approximately one million years later (Rohland et al. 2010, Brandt et al. 2014, Roca et al. 2015, Meyer et al. 2017, Palkopoulou et al. 2018). The Third Edition of ‘Mammal Species of the World’ (Wilson and Reeder 2005) was the first to formally designate the African elephant as these two separate species. Recent genetic findings also support this designation (Roca et al. 2007, Ishida et al. 2011, Mondol et al. 2015, Palkopoulou et al. 2018, Kim and Wasser 2019). Hybridization between the two species appears restricted and evident at only 14 of the more than 100 localities recently examined across the vast forest-savanna ecotone. In nine of these 14 localities, hybrid individuals occurred alongside non-hybrid individuals of either one species or the other and not both (i.e., three localities had hybrids and African Forest Elephants only and assigned as this species; six localities had hybrids and African Savanna Elephants only and assigned as this species). For the IUCN Red List assessments, a distribution map published in Mondol et al. (2015) and recent data by Kim and Wasser (2019) are used to assign localities as range of either L. africana or L. cyclotis.
Chlorodrepanis stejnegeri (del Hoyo and Collar 2016) was previously placed in the genus Hemignathus and listed as H. kauaiensis following AOU (1998 & supplements); Sibley & Monroe (1990, 1993).

This taxon was formerly treated as a subspecies of the Macaca nemestrina group, which comprised M. n. nemestrina, M. n. leonina, and M. n. pagensis (Fooden 1975, Rosenblum et al. 1997).

The boundary zone between M. leonina and M. nemestrina is not well defined and lies at the Surat Thani-Krabi depression (8-9°30'N) where ranges overlap and hybridization is likely to occur (Malaivijitnond et al. 2012). Formerly, there were reports of hybridization with M. leonina in a small area in southern mainland Thailand and on the islands of Phuket and Yao Yai (Fooden 1975, in Groves 2001), however more recent reports do not provide conclusive evidence of presence of a M. nemestrina population on Phuket (iNaturalist 2022, S. Malaivijitnond, pers. comm.). Hybridization of M. nemestrina with M. fascicularis also occurs in Sepilok, Sabah (Gilhooly et al. 2021) and is occasionally observed in other parts of Malaysia (N. Ruppert, pers. obs.).

Molecular clock analysis suggests that M. nemestrina arrived in the Malay Peninsula about 0.32 million years ago; and the Bornean populations are suggested to be the first extant lineages to have separated from the other examined lineages of M. nemestrina, M. leonina, M. pagensis, and M. siberu in Southeast Asia (Abdul-Latiff and Md-Zain 2021).

The population from Perak (west Peninsular Malaysia) is reported to differ genetically from other extant populations based on phylogenetic and population genetic analyses, indicating a possible subspecies distributed in the state of Perak (Abdul-Latiff and Md-Zain 2021).

Gracupica contra and G. jalla (del Hoyo and Collar 2016) were previously lumped as Sturnus contra following Sibley and Monroe (1990, 1993). Differs from G. contra in its uniform whitish-yellow bill vs two-tone orange/whitish-yellow bill (3); large naked orange face patch vs orange eyering (2); bare reddish vs white feathered lores (2). Differs further from G. c. contra in its browner back (ns1); whiter underparts (ns1); and shorter tail (effect size -1.63, score 1); from G. c. superciliaris in its lack of white streaks on frontal crown (ns2); whiter underparts (ns1); and longer wing (effect size 2.45, score 2). Differs from G. floweri in its lack of white streaks on crown (3 consequently discounting score of 2 for lores); browner back (ns1); and shorter tail (effect size -2.56, score 2). Monotypic.

The family Gammaridae is a large monophyletic group with a complicated taxonomy. A nomenclatural review of the taxonomic status of Pectenogammarus Reid, 1940 was conducted based on Hou & Sket (2016) and Sket & Hou (2018) phylogenetic proposals. These authors used the name Homoeogammarus Schellenberg, 1937 for a set of morphologicallydiverse taxa previously included mainly within Echinogammarus Stebbing, 1899. The use of the name Homoeogammarus Schellenberg, 1937 is problematic. As a result of a detailed bibliographic review, García-París et al. (2023) proposed the name Pectenogammarus Reid, 1940 (stat. nov.) for the “Homoeogammarus” clade (Sket & Hou 2018). As a result Echinogammarus platvoeti (Pinkster, 1993) is now accepted as Pectenogammarus platvoeti (Pinkster, 1993). For more details see García-París, M.; Jurado-Angulo, P.; Rodríguez-Flores, P. C.; Rosas-Ramos, N. (2023). Nomenclatural changes in a taxonomically complex group (Amphipoda: Gammaridae). Zootaxa. 5230(5): 595-600. https://doi.org/10.11646/zootaxa.5230.5.7

This species was described from a newly discovered population within the Saurodactylus brosseti complex (Javanmardi et al. 2019).

The distribution ranges of these proposed species remain poorly-characterised, and morphological characters are not reliable in identifying specimens (P.-A. Crochet and D. Salvi pers. comm. 2024). Nevertheless the available evidence suggests that they are likely to correspond to genuinely valid species (D. Salvi pers. comm. 2024), and this arrangement has subsequently been accepted in an updated checklist of the Moroccan fauna (Bouazza and Rihane. 2021), and by the Reptile Database (Uetz et al. 2025).
Until recently Potamon kretaion was treated as a subspecies of Potamon potamios (as Potamon potamios kretaion).
Zosterornis nigrorum (del Hoyo and Collar 2016) was previously listed as Stachyris nigrorum.
Trochalopteron yersini (del Hoyo and Collar 2016) was previously listed as Garrulax yersini.
The taxonomic description of this species is incomplete, since the description of the female is still missing.
Bosmans and Gavalas (2023) transferred Malthonica spinipalpis to a newly described genus, Hellamalthonica, which apparently is endemic to Greece.

authority: A. Reischütz, N. Reischütz & P.L. Reischütz, 2012

taxonomicNotes: The date of description of this species is sometimes cited as 1972, however Brandt published the species description in 1962 (Brandt 1962).
The species status of Pelophylax cretensis is "confirmed" by short mtDNA sequences and a large number of private allozyme alleles not found in any other water frog (P. Beerli pers. comm.).

authority: Lanza, Leo, Forti, Cimmaruta, Caputo & Nascetti, 2001
taxonomicNotes: Lanza et al. (2001) described this taxon as a subspecies of Speleomantes imperialis, but it was elevated to species status by Carranza et al. (2007).
The taxonomic status of the species has only recently been clarified. Previously, it was considered conspecific with North American Green Sturgeon (A. medirostris) (Shmigirilov et al. 2007).
Psophia viridis, P. dextralis and P. obscura (del Hoyo and Collar 2014) were previously lumped as P. viridis following Sibley and Monroe (1990, 1993).
This species was described as a valid species by Kwon et al. (2020) who separated it from Gammarus zeongogensis.

A. cygnoid (del Hoyo and Collar 2014) was previously listed as A. cygnoides but internal evidence does not justify this emendation (Dickinson and Remsen 2013). Sometimes placed in monospecific genus Cygnopsis. Monotypic.
The genus Anonychonitis is monotypic and occupies a small range in southeast Africa.
While this species is presently accepted as valid, Davis et al. (2020) note that it is very close to Frankenbergerianus nanus.

This species was formerly regarded as being endemic to the island of Karpathos (Greece); However, recent evidence based on complete mitogenome sequencing showed that individuals from Karpathos and Rhodos form a well-supported monophyletic group with very low genetic distance between them, thus placing the Rhodos population to P. cerigensis and not to P. bedriagae (Toli et al. 2023). The species should be considered endemic to the islands of Karpathos and Rhodos.

Although neither Speybroeck et al. (2020) nor Frost (2024) consider the Karpathos Frog as a separate species (considered a synonym of Pelophylax ridibundus (Pallas, 1771)), recent phylogenetic analysis based on complete mitogenome sequences support the taxonomic distinctiveness of P. cerigensis (Toli et al. 2023). The taxonomy of Pelophylax is complex and further studies are ongoing.
Prosobonia cancellata and P. parvirostris (del Hoyo and Collar 2014) were previously lumped as P. cancellata following Sibley and Monroe (1990, 1993).
It is considered by some experts to be a distinctive population of Jansenia rugosiceps (D. Pearson pers. comm. 2023).

More research is required into the systematic position and validity of this species; see Kornilios et al. (2009), who found that the morphological differentiation between this and some other members of the genus was not reflected at the genetic level.
This species is extremely closely related to Pachysoma hippocrates. Morphologically, the two species are virtually indistinguishable except by comparison of the aedeagi.

Sousa plumbea has been recognized as a species since taxonomic revision of the genus Sousa in 2014 (Committee on Taxonomy 2014, Jefferson and Rosenbaum 2014). Previously this species was lumped together with the Indo-Pacific Humpback Dolphin (Sousa chinensis), but animals occurring in the western part of the Indian Ocean from South Africa to western coastline of India are now recognized as taxonomically distinct from those that occur further east, based on genetics, skeletal morphology, external morphology and colour. There is uncertainty about the taxonomic affinities of the Humpback Dolphins that occur in the Bay of Bengal. Animals in this area do not appear to be S. plumbea or S. chinensis, or to be hybrids of the two, and may be a separate taxonomic entity (Amaral et al. 2017; 2020). Additional sampling should confirm the eastern limits of the S. plumbea species, but for the purposes of this assessment we assume it to be Sri Lanka and the southern tip of India in the Gulf of Mannar.
This distinctive new species was described from the island of Wangi-Wangi in the Wakatobi Archipelago, Indonesia, by Irham et al. (2023). Monotypic.
Malacoptila striata and M. minor (del Hoyo and Collar 2014) were previously lumped as M. striata following Sibley and Monroe (1990, 1993).

Petroica multicolor, P. pusilla and P. boodang (del Hoyo and Collar 2016) were previously lumped as P. multicolor following Sibley & Monroe (1990, 1993). Conventionally considered conspecific with P. boodang, and until recently with P. pusilla, but recently shown to be most closely related to distinctive P. goodenovii (Kearns et al. 2015). Differs from P. pusilla in its (from limited available material) much larger size (significantly longer bill, wing and tail; also not sexually dimorphic in size) (allow 3); larger white forehead patch in male (1); and (from limited available recordings) much lower-pitched and differently structured song (allow 3); further research, involving comprehensive review of evidence, needed to confirm this diagnosis. Monotypic.
This is the only representative of its genus.
Oxypogon guerinii, O. cyanolaemus, O. lindenii and O. stuebelii (del Hoyo and Collar 2014) were previously lumped as O. guerinii following Sibley and Monroe (1990, 1993).

This new species was described by Melo et al. (2022). It differs from congeners most conspicuously in its distinctive call, which is most similar to that of the widely allopatric O. ireneae. Monotypic.
Taxonomic revision resulted in Amaurobius atticus becoming a synonym of A. pelops (Thaler and Knoflach 1995)

Has been separated in Natalornis, on basis of sexual dimorphism, distinctive plumage (without rufous, no white in tail), nest structure and egg coloration; within present genus, however, these features (apart from nest structure) are variable, suggesting that separate genus is not warranted. DNA data (Dor et al. 2010) indicate sister relationship with H. nigrorufa, despite striking morphological differences. N population, with plumage washed violet, sometimes separated as subspecies lynesi, but differences insignificant. Monotypic.
The species is closely related to Rattus hoffmanni. Further taxonomic research is needed as there is some uncertainty surrounding its status.
A espécie é descrita a partir de 3 subespécies: Glossoscolex riograndensis ssp. riograndensis, G. riograndensis ssp. nativus e G. riograndensis ssp. pollulus (Bartz et al. 2018).

authority: Hernández-García, Burgos-Guerrero, Dos Santos, Rousseau & James, 2018

taxonomicNotes: Mercuria vindilica (Paladilhe, 1870) is now placed as a subspecies of this species (MolluscaBase 2022).

Charadrius mongolus and C. atrifrons were previously lumped as C. mongolus (Handbook of the Birds of the World and BirdLife International 2022); see under C. atrifrons. Subspecies name stegmanni is a replacement for litoralis, latter name being invalid, as preoccupied. Two subspecies recognised.

In the Pristimantis unistrigatus group according to the original description (Kaiser et al. 2015). The karyotype of Pristimantis nubisilva consisting of 36 exclusively telocentric chromosomes (2n = 36, FN = 36), is considered to represent the ancestral condition in the taxon Terrarana (Schmid et al. 2010).

As part of a revision of the species complex then referred to as "Tympanocryptis pinguicolla", Melville et al. (2019) made the unexpected finding that the lectotype of Tympanocryptis lineata (assigned by Wells and Wellington 1985) was applicable to a member of the T. pinguicolla complex, and that its universal association with a striped earless dragon was in error.

These authors' work identified four species-level lineages within the group they consequently referred to "temperate grassland earless dragon group", and that the names T. lineata and T. pinguicolla referred to distinct species. The remaining two populations were described as the new species T. osbornei and T. mccartneyi.

authority: Melville, Chaplin, Hipsley, Sarre, Sumner & Hutchinson, 2019
taxonomicNotes: A taxonomic revision of the "temperate earless grassland dragon" group by Melville et al. (2019) identified four species-level lineages within the group that they consequently referred to as the "temperate grassland earless dragon group", and described the population from Bathurst in New South Wales as the new species Tympanocryptis mccartneyi.

The name Tympanocryptis pinguicolla was previously used to describe the greater grassland earless dragon complex across New South Wales, the Australian Capital Territory (ACT) and Victoria (Robertson and Evans 2012), and briefly included a species in southern Queensland (Melville et al. 2007). 

As part of a revision of this species complex, Melville et al. (2019) made the unexpected finding that the lectotype of Tympanocryptis lineata (assigned by Wells and Wellington 1985) was applicable to a member of the T. pinguicolla complex, and that its universal association with a striped earless dragon was in error. 

These authors' work identified four species-level lineages within the group they consequently referred to "temperate grassland earless dragon group", and that the names T. lineata and T. pinguicolla referred to distinct species, the latter being applicable to the population from Victoria. The remaining two populations were described as the new species T. osbornei and T. mccartneyi.

authority: Melville, Chaplin, Hutchinson, Sumner, Gruber, MacDonald & Sarre, 2019
taxonomicNotes: A taxonomic revision of the "temperate earless grassland dragon" group by Melville et al. (2019) identified four species-level lineages within the group they consequently referred to "temperate grassland earless dragon group", and described the population from Cooma in New South Wales as the new species Tympanocryptis osbornei.

Aphrastura masafuerae (Handbook of the Birds of the World and BirdLife International 2021) was previously listed as Aphrastura masafucrae (del Hoyo and Collar 2016). The latter spelling was subject to a later attempt to correct the name by the author of the type description, and has been recognised as an accidental error: the type description had intended to use the name of the island on which the species occurs in the original description.

A recently published taxonomic review (Miranda et al. 2023) proposes to split the northern (in southern Bahia and Sergipe) and southern subpopulations (Espírito Santo and Rio de Janeiro) into two species - Bradypus torquatus (Bahia and Sergipe) and Bradypus crinitus (Rio de Janeiro and Espírito Santo), respectively. The subpopulations were already considered genetically distinct, according to previous studies (Lara-Ruiz et al. 2008, Schetino et al. 2017). This recent analysis (Miranda et al. 2023), based on coalescent delimitation using mitochondrial and nuclear DNA, morphological analyses and field observations, recognizes that they are in fact two species, B. torquatus and B. crinitus.

The Maned Sloth was occasionally classified as a subgenus of Bradypus or placed in his own genus, Scaeopus (Hayssen 2009, Wetzel and Ávila-Pires 1980) due to its morphological distinctiveness. A growing number of molecular studies supports this differentiation (Gibb et al. 2016, Schetino et al. 2017, Ruiz-Garcia et al. 2020), but additional studies are needed to confirm this.

A recently published taxonomic review (Miranda et al. 2023) proposes to split the northern (in southern Bahia and Sergipe) and southern subpopulations (Espírito Santo and Rio de Janeiro) into two species - Bradypus torquatus (Bahia and Sergipe) and Bradypus crinitus (Rio de Janeiro and Espírito Santo), respectively. The subpopulations were already considered genetically distinct, according to previous studies (Lara-Ruiz et al. 2008, Schetino et al. 2017). This recent analysis (Miranda et al. 2023), based on coalescent delimitation using mitochondrial and nuclear DNA, morphological analyses and field observations, recognizes that they are in fact two species, B. torquatus and B. crinitus.

The Maned Sloth was occasionally classified as a subgenus of Bradypus or placed in his own genus, Scaeopus (Hayssen 2009, Wetzel and Ávila-Pires 1980) due to its morphological distinctiveness. A growing number of molecular studies supports this differentiation (Gibb et al. 2016, Schetino et al. 2017, Ruiz-Garcia et al. 2020), but additional studies are needed to confirm this.

Myosorex eisentrauti was previously considered to include M. okuensis and M. rumpii (see Hutterer and Peters 2010), though both are now recognised as distinct species (Mammal Diversity Database 2023).
Artisornis moreaui and A. sousae (del Hoyo and Collar 2016) were previously lumped as A. moreaui following Dowsett & Forbes-Watson (1993) and Sibley & Monroe (1990, 1993).

Formerly considered conspecific with B. pavonina (lumped species sometimes erroneously listed as B. regulorum), but differences revealed by electrophoresis, together with those in vocalizations, bare parts and plumage, as well as in genetic evidence (Krajewski and King 1996, Krajewski et al. 2010), advocate recognition of two species. Two subspecies recognized.

Previously placed in genus Rimator (del Hoyo and Collar 2016) but moved to current genus following Cai et al. (2019). Formerly treated as conspecific with N. malacoptila and N. albostriata following Sibley and Monroe (1990, 1993). Monotypic.
Altrichthys alelia closely resembles Altrichthys azurelineatus, with several specimens previously identified as the former (Bernardi et al. 2017).
This species was erroneously synonymized with Gillichthys mirabilis in 1907, and resurrected by Swift et al. (2011).
Eucyclogobius newberryi was split into two and the southern portion of its range is now attributed to Eucyclogobius kristinae (Swift et al. 2016).
Taxonomic work is needed on the genus Egglestonichthys (Fujiwara et al. 2020).
This species was previously misidentified as Hypleurochilus fissicornis (Pinheiro et al. 2013).

Some specimens of this species were previously identified as Malacoctenus triangulatus (Carvalho-Filho et al. 2020). Populations from Rocas Atoll and Pernambuco differ in several meristic counts and coloration, but molecular data confirms that they represent the same species (Carvalho-Filho et al. 2020).
Acrocephalus luscinius, A. hiwae, A. yamashinae, A. astrolabii and A. nijoi (del Hoyo and Collar 2016) were previously lumped as A. luscinius following Sibley & Monroe (1990, 1993).

Hybridization of Sternotherus depressus with Sternotherus peltifer occurs in some areas along the periphery of the range of S. depressus, notably in the southern portion of its range (Mount 1981, Ernst et al. 1989, Scott and Rissler 2015, Scott et al. 2019).
Archaelogical remains suggest that Acipenser sturio colonised the Baltic Sea about 3,000 years ago from the North Sea, and vanished from the Baltic Sea about 800 years ago.

The Indus River Dolphin (Platanista minor Owen, 1853), is one of two recognized species in the family Platanistidae (Braulik et al. 2015a,  2021). From the late 1990s until 2021, the Indus River Dolphin and its sister species, the Ganges River Dolphin (Platanista gangetica (Lebeck, 1801)), were recognized and listed on the Red List as subspecies P. g. minor and P. g. gangetica, respectively (Rice 1998, Society for Marine Mammalogy 2021). In early 2021, evidence from a comparison of mtDNA control region sequences together with external and skeletal morphology was published, indicating substantial differences between Indus and Ganges dolphins that warranted a change in taxonomy to separate species (Braulik et al. 2015a, 2021). This change was approved by the Committee on Taxonomy of the Society for Marine Mammalogy, the generally recognized authority, in mid-2021 (Committee of Taxonomy 2021). Details on the taxonomic history of the two species are summarized in Braulik et al. (2021).
Asarcornis scutulata (del Hoyo and Collar 2014) was previously placed in the genus Cairina.

Two subspecies have been described (Duplaix 1980): (1) Pteronura brasiliensis brasiliensis (Gmelin, 1788) from Suriname, the Guianas, southern Venezuela, southern Colombia, eastern Ecuador, eastern Peru, Bolivia, Paraguay, and Brazil; and (2) P. b. paranensis (Rengger, 1830) from the Paraguay and Parana rivers in Brazil, northern Argentina and Uruguay. However, this subspecific division was rejected by Carter and Rosas (1997) and described as being of “doubtful value” by Duplaix (1980).  Wozencraft (2005) treated subspecies paranensis as a synonym of a different subspecies: P. b. paraguensis (Schinz, 1821). Subsequent mitochondrial DNA and microsatellite analysis supports subdivision of the species into four evolutionary distinct units (ESUs). These comprise: (1) the Rio Madre de Dios with the Rio Madeira (2); the Pantanal (3) the Amazon with the Orinoco and Guianas drainages (4) The Itenez/Guapore basin (Garcia et al. 2007; Pickles et al. 2011, 2012).
Atelopus epikeisthos can be distinguished from other similar species by a combination of morphological features and colour patterns (Lötters et al. 2004).
Yánez-Muñoz et al. (2010) reported this species from Parque Nacaional Podocarpus, but Frenkel et al. (2018) note that this actually corresponds to another species, Atelopus halihelos.
This is a split from the broader concept of Atelopus ebenoides (Rueda-Almonacid and Rueda-Martínez 2005)

This is a split from the broader concept of Rhacophorus gauni (Dehling 2015). It was previously confused with R. gauni because of its close morphological resemblance. The two species occur sympatrically at Poring Hot Springs on the eastern slope of Gunung Kinabalu.
Sabahphrynus anotis was synonymized under Sabahphrynus maculatus by Matsui et al. (2007).
This is a restricted concept of this species following the split of Boophis sandrae (Glaw et al. 2010).

There are taxonomic uncertainties, as outlined in Greenwood et al. (2020). They state "The taxonomic placement of populations of Phrynobatrachus cf. ambanguluensis from North Pare is uncertain. It is possible that populations from the North Pare Mountains represent a distinct new species, but they await further research to evaluate morphological and molecular variation. A series of six specimens from Amani assigned to P. krefftii show a distinct colour difference with the presence of a light brown/orangish dorsolateral light stripe, and these might also prove to be a distinct species. Although morphologically consistent with the description of P. krefftii, new DNA data might be able to demonstrate its phylogenetic distinctiveness alongside the consistent colour differences"
Buteogallus coronatus (del Hoyo and Collar 2014) was previously placed in the genus Harpyhaliaetus.

authority: Coloma, Duellman, Almendáriz, Ron, Terán-Valdez & Guayasamin, 2010
Coloma, Duellman, Almendáriz, Ron, Terán-Valdez & Guayasamin, 2010
Reissig, Major, Renk, Barlow, Paijmans, Morris, Hofreiter, Broadley & Wüster, 2023
taxonomicNotes:
A previous Red List assessment for Hemachatus haemachatus (Alexander 2022) reported that the isolated subpopulation from the Nyanga District of Zimbabwe, known only from museum material, “appear to be morphologically different with lower ventral and subcaudal counts than those in South Africa”.

The subsequent recovery of a fragment of mitochondrial DNA from a preserved specimen confirmed that the Zimbabwean subpopulation is genetically and morphologically distinct from South African Rinkhals (Major et al. 2023), and these authors thus described the Zimbabwe subpopulation as a new species, Hemachatus nyangensis.
The taxonomy of this genus is in need of revision.
The taxonomy of this genus is in need of revision.
The relationships between this and other species without toe discs requires a detailed taxonomic study using molecular and morphological methodologies.
This species was reassigned to the genus Anhydrophryne from Arthroleptella by Dawood and Stam (2006).
A deep genetic split characterises the disjunct distribution of this species.
This recently described species (Puechmaille et al. 2023) was formerly one of the species (Myotis sp. C., subpopulation endemic to Corsica) belonging to the Myotis nattereri complex.
Premnoplex tatei and P. pariae (del Hoyo and Collar 2016) were previously lumped as P. tatei following SACC (2005 & updates), Sibley and Monroe (1990, 1993) and Stotz et al. (1996).

Lophura edwardsi (del Hoyo and Collar 2014) was previously split as L. edwardsi and L. hatinhensis following Sibley and Monroe (1990, 1993). Lophura imperialis (Sibley and Monroe 1990, 1993) is considered a hybrid between Silver Pheasant Lophura nycthemera and Vietnam Pheasant L. edwardsi following Rasmussen (1998), Garson (2001) and BirdLife International (2001).
Closely related to C. alba. Monotypic.

Trochalopteron fairbanki, T. meridionale, T. jerdoni and T. cachinnans have been moved to the new genus Montecincla after they were found to be sister to a clade composed of Heterophasia, Minla, Actinodura, Leiothrix, Liocichla and Crocias (Robin et al. 2017). They have also received new common names, and are listed as Palani Chilappan Montecincla fairbanki, Ashambu Chilappan M. meridionalis, Banasura Chilappan M. jerdoni and Nilgiri Chilappan M. cachinnans.

T. fairbanki and T. meridionale (del Hoyo and Collar 2016) were previously lumped as Strophocincla fairbanki following Rasmussen and Anderton (2005).
Pristis pristis was formerly known as P. perotteti in the Atlantic and sometimes in the Eastern Pacific, and P. microdon in the Indo-West Pacific. These are both now synonyms of P. pristis.
Leiothrix argentauris and L. laurinae (del Hoyo and Collar 2016) were previously lumped as L. argentauris following Sibley and Monroe (1990, 1993).
Pareudiastes silvestris (del Hoyo and Collar 2014) was previously placed in the genus Gallinula.
Turnix varius and T. novaecaledoniae (del Hoyo and Collar 2014) were previously lumped as T. varius following Sibley and Monroe (1990, 1993).
Eurostopodus mystacalis, E. nigripennis and E. exul (del Hoyo and Collar 2014) were previously lumped as E. mystacalis following Sibley and Monroe (1990, 1993).

An isolated population of the Elaphe quatuorlineata group is known from Israel, Lebanon and Syria. In the expectation that the Anatolian Diagonal represents a barrier to dispersal for this species Jablonski et al. (2019) suggest this was likely to represent E. urartica, but that research is needed to confirm this. This population was subsequently recognised as a distinct species on the basis of combined morphological and molecular evidence, which Jablonski et al. (2023) described as E. druzei.

Photographed individuals from the Togean Archipelago, Indonesia were originally identified as Hippocampus satomiae Lourie and Kuiter 2008. They were later revealed as the Walea Soft Coral Pygmy Seahorse (H. waleananus) based on a single specimen from Walea Islands in the Togean Archipelago by Gomon and Kuiter 2009, citing differences in tail lengths, tail ring counts, and dorsal fin rays. Lourie et al. 2016 subsequently determined H. waleananus as a synonym of H. satomiae, given their lack of differences aside from those mentioned by Gomon and Kuiter (2009), and the fact that the locality of H. waleananus is completely within the distribution of H. satomiae. However, Short et al. 2018 have recognized H. waleananus as a distinct species based on its morphological characteristics, body ornamentations, host association, and behavior.

Kaipu’s Pipefish (Microphis kaipuae) was previously described as part of the genus Coelonotus (Haÿ et al. 2021). However, phylogenetic analysis by Haÿ, Mennesson, et al. (2023) revealed that five freshwater genera of the subfamily Nerophinae are polyphyletic. All freshwater genera of Nerophinae are therefore grouped under the genus Microphis Kaup 1853 as it is the first-described and most common genus of freshwater pipefish and several morphological characters common to Microphis species are shared within the monophyly (Haÿ Mennesson et al. 2023).

Specimens with low meristic counts from Lake Izabal, Guatemala were originally determined as variants of Pseudophallus mindii (Meek and Hildebrand 1923, Dawson 1982). The Lake Izabal population is later revealed as a new species — the Lake Izabal Pipefish (P. galadrielae Dallevo-Gomes, Mattox and Toledo-Piza 2020) — based on meristic counts of P. mindii specimens from neighboring drainages that were distinctly higher than those of the Lake Izabal population (Dallevo-Gomes et al. 2020).

Previously considered to encompass the Broad-headed Map Turtles inhabiting the Pascagoula and Pearl river systems, Graptemys gibbonsi was reduced in scope when the Pearl River form was described as a separate species, G. pearlensis Ennen, Lovich, Kreiser, Selman & Qualls, 2010.
There was an error in the original 2011 assessment, in that Conus lobitensis Kaicher, 1977 was placed as a synonym, but this is not correct and that name has been removed.

The Savuto Chub is morphologically divergent from other Italian Squalius species, but has only been mentioned by a handful of authors since its description (Bianco and Recchia 1983, Kottelat and Freyhof 2007, Bogutskaya and Zupančič 2010, Bianco and Delmastro 2011, Bianco 2014). No published study has explicitly demonstrated that it does not represent a distinct taxon, hence it is retained as valid.

The Pearl River Map Turtle, Graptemys pearlensis, described by Ennen et al. (2010), was originally included as part of Graptemys pulchra Baur 1893 and then Graptemys gibbonsi Lovich and McCoy, 1992, with specimens from the Pearl River drainage referenced in both original descriptions of those taxa. This taxonomy has been accepted by Lindeman (2013) and TTWG (2021).

Nisaetus philippensis and N. pinskeri (del Hoyo and Collar 2014) were previously lumped as N. philippensis following Haring et al. (2006), which before then was placed in the genus Spizaetus following Sibley and Monroe (1990, 1993).
Monotypic.
Laticilla burnesii and L. cinerascens (del Hoyo and Collar 2016) were previously lumped as Prinia burnesii following Sibley and Monroe (1990, 1993).

Originally described as Mantella pollicaris by Boettger (1913), and later synonymized with Anodonthyla boulengerii (Noble and Parker 1926). Removed from this synonymy by Vences et al. (2010) on the basis of molecular and acoustic data.
Unidentified specimens from places other than the type locality might belong to this species.
Referred to as Boophis sp. aff. rappiodes "South" by Glaw and Vences (2007).
Candidate species from the subgenus Brygoomantis (see Vieites et al. 2009 and Wollenberg et al. 2011) are not considered in this assessment (F. Glaw pers. comm. May 2016).
The taxonomic status of this species has recently been changed (Rakotoarison et al. 2017).
The taxonomic status of this species has been changed (Rakotoarison et al. 2017).

authority: Rakotoarison, Scherz, Bletz, Razafindraibe, Glaw & Vences, 2019
Scherz, Hutter, Rakotoarison, Riemann, Rödel, Ndriantsoa, Glos, Roberts, Crottini, Vences & Glaw, 2019

taxonomicNotes: This is the revised taxonomic concept of Rhynchobatus laevis, which excludes the population occurring around Japan and is now recognized as a separate species, Rhynchobatus mononoke Koeda, Itou, Yamada & Motomura, 2021.
Symposiachrus everetti (del Hoyo and Collar 2016) was previously listed as Monarcha everetti.
The taxonomic validity of this species is uncertain (Y. Zhao pers. comm. 2022).

Pygmy Lorises were deemed a distinct genus (Xanthonycticebus) from other Slow Loris taxa (Nycticebus) on the basis of have diverged approximately 11 million years ago, as well as traits including regularly giving birth to twins, having black naked ears, and a smaller skull length than Nycticebus (Nekaris and Nijman, 2022; Blair et al., 2023). Pygmy lorises are relatively small (360-580 g); black nose; the fur on the crown patch of the head is orangish to light brown and is diffuse to ears with eye patches rather than sharply forked.
